Lipless Crankbait.insight: KVD: Red Eye Shad sizes for depth control — 1/4oz for ultra-shallow (1-2ft), 3/8oz for shallow flats, 1/2oz as go-to, 3/4oz for 10-15ft deep drains. Plus tungsten Two Tap in 1/2 and 3/4oz — heavier tungsten fishes slightly deeper. Most anglers get stuck on 1/2oz and don't downsize when fish are shallow.

KVD: Line size is a major depth control tool for lipless crankbaits. Lighter line (10-12lb) = bait gets deeper (use for clear water, deeper grass, northern natural lakes). Heavier line (17-20lb) = bait stays higher and falls slower (use for shallow grass lakes like Guntersville). Rod tip up in shallow water.

KVD: CC3 medium-heavy composite (graphite/glass) cranking rod for lipless crankbaits. 6.2:1 gear ratio reel — not high-speed in cold water. The composite gives feel for detecting grass on hooks while having power to rip free.

KVD: Always change hooks to KVD/Mustad Triple Grip. Upsize: #2 on 1/2oz, #4 on 1/4oz, #1/0 on 3/4oz. Triple grip design locks fish on — critical because lipless crankbaits are known for poor strike-to-land ratio. Won the Classic catching 5-pounders hooked in the back and side thanks to these hooks.

KVD: Crawfish patterns dominate pre-spawn — reds in any water clarity (Rayburn Red, Delta Red, Fire Craw). Clear water = natural craw patterns (dark brown, baby carp, orange brim, bluegill). Stained/dirty = chartreuse, chartreuse sexy shad, brighter colors. Shad-focused systems (Tennessee River) = chrome sexy shad, gold sexy shad, straight sexy shad.

KVD: Fish the lipless crankbait like a jig in cold water. Short hop it along the bottom. Pull it around cover, deflect off rocks. Rip it out of grass, let it flutter down. Use rod tip to manipulate depth — tip up in shallow, down to get deeper. Make the bait fall as many times as possible — that's when bites happen.

KVD: Focus on large flats and spawning flats — where fish are coming and going. Look for deeper depressions, edges, drops, centers of drains within the flat. These subtle depth changes concentrate pre-spawn fish. At places like Toledo Bend and Sam Rayburn, fish deeper drains/ditches leading into spawning bays with 3/4oz.

KVD: 'For lipless on flats: 7-foot glass rod, 5:1 gear ratio reel. Slower retrieve keeps bait down in the strike zone. Line controls depth — 20lb test for shallow water above grass, 8-10lb fluorocarbon for deeper to maximize depth and bottom contact.'

KVD: 'On large flats, the key is contact, depth, and placement. Vary retrieve speed with stop-and-go movements. Let the bait sink periodically. Always aim for the bait to be hitting something — if it's not interacting with cover, it loses effectiveness.'

KVD: 'For lipless crankbaits, use a long composite rod (part glass, part graphite). Graphite gives sensitivity to feel tiny bits of weed on hooks. Glass slows the action for better deflection off cover. Longer rod gives depth control — rod tip up for shallow flats (2-4ft), rod tip down for deeper areas. Always fluorocarbon — sinks, low stretch, abrasion resistant.'

KVD: 'Early spring lipless colors: stick to natural patterns mimicking what bass already feed on — bluegill and crayfish in clear water. Stained or murky water, switch to brighter colors — chartreuse, fluorescent reds, anything that stands out more.'

KVD: 'Pump and stop retrieve — rod tip to the side, sweep and follow the bait back down keeping tension. Let the Red Eye Shad do its thing and swim to bottom. Works over grass, rocks, deeper flats. I probably catch more fish this way than any other.'

KVD: 'I throw the Red Eye Shad on a 7-7'10" fiberglass composite rod. Gives me graphite feel with the slow reaction time of glass. Enough power to snap it free from grass. Long casts cover more water. Always fluorocarbon — it sinks, great feel, good hook sets on long casts.'
KVD: 'I use the 1/2oz Red Eye Shad in the 7-12 foot zone and the 3/4oz for deeper — 15-20 feet on ledges. Use lighter line to help keep the bait down.'

KVD: Match the forage and water clarity. Spring in Texas = red (crawfish coming out, dark red). Stained water = brighter reds like fire craw or chili craw. Gold has great flash in stained water (gold sexy shad). General rule: 'Pick something that's natural and something that's visible.' Match bluegill, crawfish, or shad based on the system.

KVD: For lipless crankbaits in shallow grass (3-7ft): CC5 7'4" heavy action composite rod, 7.5:1 reel, 20lb fluoro. Multiple cranking rods with 14, 17, and 20lb line for different depths. Heavy line keeps the bait up and slows it down in shallow grass. The heavy composite rod has backbone to rip through grass.

Squarebill Crankbait.insight: KVD: 'Spring = crawfish patterns with red. Summer = shad and bluegill patterns. Fall = shad patterns in creek backs. When the 1.5 hits grass, clear hooks with a hard snap of the rod tip.'

KVD: Square bills are round-bodied with a steep angle and a square bill — this gives them a wide wobble and the best deflection qualities of any crankbait. Most weedless crankbait you can throw. Good through grass, brush, gnarly rocks, riprap. Works in cold or warm water. Triggers neutral/negative fish with deflection.

KVD: 'The KVD 1.5 can run almost 6 feet deep with light line (10-12lb fluoro) — deeper than you'd think for a shallow runner. Put it on 20lb and it runs shallow. Rod height controls depth during retrieve — tip up to stay high, tip down to dig deeper.'

KVD: 'What I look for in a squarebill is erratic swimming — kick around, don't run straight. Old wood baits had this but broke fast. The body and weighting design make every single one swim erratically out of the package. It looks like an injured baitfish — catches a lot of smallmouth over deep water.'
KVD: 'I'm a big believer in flat-sided crankbaits in winter — the KVD 1.5 Flat and Lucky Shad for cold water. Throw Lucky Shad on spinning equipment with 8lb fluoro for extra distance and depth.'

Spinnerbait (Colorado/Willow).insight: KVD: 'Simple rule: lighter spinnerbait with smaller blades in shallow water, larger bait in deep water. The KVD Finesse Spinnerbait (3/8oz, one willow + smaller Colorado) is a great all-around option for weeds, stumps, docks.'

KVD: 'Offshore spinnerbait: 3/4oz, trim wire down a third, single willow blade. Cast, let helicopter down to bottom on tight line. Rip up sharply, let flutter back down. Provokes reaction strikes from suspended bass in summer heat.'

KVD: 'Tandem willow blades for horizontal retrieves through grass — less lift, runs deeper. Single blade for vertical presentations and helicopter flutter. Colorado/willow combo for slower retrieves and longer time in strike zone.'

KVD: 'Position boat to fish parallel to edges and cover. Keeps the bait in the strike zone longer. Short accurate casts (30-40ft) for control in dense cover. Soft underhand presentations so bait lands quietly in shallow water.'

KVD: 'Fall spinnerbaits: downsize to 3/16oz in clear water with small blades to match young-of-year baitfish. For heavy grass, go bigger with willowleaf blades. To fish fast and deep, put smaller blades on a 3/4oz head — the Scorcher design lets you burn it and keep it down.'

KVD: 7'4" medium-heavy rod (KVD Elite GC7), 7.5:1 gear ratio reel, 20lb fluorocarbon. Heavy line does NOT affect spinnerbait action — it's one of the few lures where heavy line doesn't hurt. Allows accurate casts tight to cover.

KVD: Barely turn the handle. When the bait hits a limb, speed the reel up one fast turn to force the bait over the limb keeping it up and down (not turning over and hanging). The flutter on the other side of the limb is when they bite. Even without cover, intermittently speed up for a quick flare of the skirt and blades to trigger following fish.

KVD: Always use a plastic trailer when slow rolling — adds bulk and extra action fish home in on. Rage Slim Swimmer is his go-to: subtle, not too bulky (doesn't make bait ride up), has an erratic wag. In coldest months, sometimes removes the skirt entirely and just uses a swimbait as trailer.

KVD: Bright sunny + clear water = natural colors (bluegill, spot remover). Dirty water = brightest or darkest colors (chartreuse/white or black/blue) to maximize silhouette. Shad patterns (white/chartreuse) are day-in day-out staples. Spring around grass in Texas = red crawfish patterns. Match the forage and water clarity.

KVD: When slow rolling, keep the bait ABOVE the bass's line of sight — you're imitating baitfish, not crawfish. Fish cover or fish over the bass's head. Steep rocky banks, laydowns, grass — the bait should be slowly ticking the cover above where bass are positioned.
KVD: Spinnerbait is one of the few lures where heavy line (20lb fluoro) does NOT hinder the action at all. On a small crankbait or finesse jig, heavy line hurts the action — but not on a spinnerbait.

Bladed Jig.insight: KVD: Cut the head off the blade minnow trailer to shorten it slightly. The water rushing around the chatterbait blade hits the shortened tail at the perfect distance for lively action. This is his #1 trailer for the Thunder Cricket.

KVD: 'Cold water Thunder Cricket: cast toward structure or transitions, let sink just above cover/grass, lift-and-drop retrieve with brief pauses. The key isn't speed — it's triggering instinctive reaction bites. Slow enough for blade to stay in strike zone but fast enough for vibration.'

KVD: 'Medium-heavy casting rod with backbone, 6.4-7.1:1 reel, fluorocarbon leader. Match blade color to water — gold in stained, chrome in clear. Keeping hookup ratio high is essential when fish are only following.'

KVD: Clear water on grass lakes = fish can see the bait from farther away (benefit) but can also be spooky (challenge). Fish may be timid — bites are subtle, just bumping the bait. Slow down and keep working the area.

KVD: Bite the head off the KVD Magic trailer — shorten it just a bit. Combined with blue craw Thunder Cricket, it looks like a bluegill. The trailer adds a little flash and keeps the presentation subtle and natural for clear water.
KVD: CC5 composite rod with heavy action (e-glass/graphite blend) — you feel everything with the bladed jig and it absorbs shock on the bite. 7.5:1 KVD reel, 20lb fluorocarbon.

KVD: Keeps 3/8, 1/2, and 5/8 oz Thunder Crickets on the deck during pre-spawn. Uses lighter weight (3/8oz) to slow down in tough conditions. Tungsten Thunder Cricket has smaller blade with tighter/subtler vibration and runs deeper in the same size — better for finicky fish.

KVD: In cold/post-frontal pre-spawn, don't just wind the Thunder Cricket. Work it like a jig — hop, twitch, pull-and-stop. Get it down in the grass and on the bottom. Bites are extremely subtle: the bait just gets heavy or the vibration stops. 'I always move faster than the fish' — force yourself to slow down.

Drop Shot.insight: KVD: 'Drop shot rod: fast-tapering tip with backbone for long casts. Most bend in the first third for sensitivity without sacrificing control. On the bite, don't jerk the rod — just reel in and let the tip load. The sharp hook takes care of the rest. Keep finger on the line to detect strikes.'

KVD: Dice/bug baits on a drop shot are a new frontier. Lighter weight = better (1/8oz ideal, 1/4oz max in deeper water). The slow sinking action is the key — once on bottom you can still shake it traditionally, but most bites come on the fall. Game-changer for clear water smallmouth and during Mayfly hatches.

KVD: '7.5-foot rod, 3000 series spinning reel, 10lb braid with a 20-foot leader of 8lb fluorocarbon. Long leader and small-diameter braid means long casts with no twist. Critical for clear water where long casts matter.'
KVD: '1/2oz tungsten for 10-40ft depth range with the 7-6 rod. Shallow water gets 1/8 or 3/16oz weight. Use just heavy enough weight to keep good bottom contact and line tight.'

KVD: 'On a long cast, let it sink then tightline the last bit so the Baby Z-Too swims like a real minnow on the way down. Shake 3-4 times, reel a little, shake again. If you find concentrated fish, work it longer through the cast.'

KVD: 'Match bait to fish mood: super tough = wacky-rigged Ocho or finesse worm. Power fishing = something with lots of wiggle like Baby Z-Too or nose-hooked Dream Shot. Figure out the mood and give them action that generates extra strikes.'

KVD: 'For vertical deep water (50ft on Table Rock, fishing standing timber at 30ft down), use a short rod. Video gaming — looking at graph, dropping to the fish, watching bait on sonar, trying to talk them into biting.'

Swim Jig.insight: KVD: 'The swim jig handles any shallow cover — grass, bushes, lily pads, wood, rocks. Swim it through pads like you're frogging. Skip it under docks. Fish everything from the bank to the outside grass edge. The heavy cover version powers through slime and algae.'

KVD: 'The Rage Swimmer on a jig head, scooting along bottom, kicks up mud and sand. In clear northern lakes, all forage lives on the bottom — perch, bluegill, crawfish. Swim it through the column only when bluegill are suspended.'

Suspending Jerkbait.insight: KVD: 'Depth is critical in cold water jerkbait fishing — you want the bait slightly above the fish or directly in front of them. They won't chase far when metabolism is low and they certainly don't go down after a bait.'
KVD: 'Color is crucial for jerkbaits — it's a very visual technique and fish get a really good look as the bait sits still between twitches. You need the right blend of visibility and naturalness.'

KVD: 'Cold water jerkbait: solid pull (not snap), pause one second with rod pointed at bait for slack. By pulling short distances the bait runs 3-4 feet deeper. I still cover a lot of water by fishing parallel to edges — keeps the lure in the productive zone throughout the retrieve.'

KVD: 'As water temp drops, a jerkbait that floats in summer will sink in cold water. Switch to three No. 4 Mustad KVD Trebles and test balance — a little sink is fine but nothing rapid or unnatural. 6-10 medium heavy rod, 10lb fluorocarbon for long casts in clear water.'

KVD: 'Cold water jerkbait targets: drop-offs, edges, weedlines, bridge corners, riprap, bluff ends. Fish parallel to these edges and make multiple casts to high-percentage spots like laydown tips or grass points.'

KVD: 'Standard jerkbaits fish 4-5ft and 6-7ft zones. I wanted one that swims down to 12ft — the KVD 300 Series Deep-Diving Jerkbait. Longer bill, dives deeper, suspends, maintains incredible action at depth, and isn't hard to work. Fills a gap where no other jerkbait on the market could reach.'

KVD: In clear water slick calm conditions, most anglers reach for finesse. KVD's instinct is to jerk a jerkbait over the top of them — the clear water means they'll see it from a distance. Confidence in the technique matters as much as conditions.

KVD: 'Jerkbait cadence: always start and finish with slack in your line — this gives the bait its best side-to-side action. Experiment every day and let bass tell you what they want. Cold water = longer pauses. Warm water = more active and erratic retrieve. The clearer the water, the more action in the retrieve.'

KVD: 'Jerkbait rod is the most critical piece — shorter handle, longer rod, graphite with extra fast tip and sturdy backbone. Never pull the jerkbait toward the boat with the reel — work it with the rod, use reel only to pick up slack. Fast 7.5:1 gear ratio. Fluorocarbon is critical — 14lb is my favorite, still makes bait jump on long casts into wind. Below 12lb, too much stretch hurts action.'

KVD: 'Deep jerkbait for smallmouth: slower-action medium-heavy rod (not snappy like standard jerkbait rod), fast reel, 12-14lb fluorocarbon. The slower rod action works better for the deep-diving versions.'

KVD: 'Jerkbait setup: high-speed reel (not for pulling bait, just picking up slack fast). All-graphite rod with snappy tip for sharp darting action, even at end of long casts in wind. Fluorocarbon line — lighter line runs deeper, heavier stays shallow. Line choice controls bait depth with precision.'

Deep Diving Crankbait.insight: KVD: The 10XD is unique for a straight-body deep diver — it has a slightly wider wobble than expected, giving it great deflection even in 12-15ft of water. Most deep divers with straight bills lose themselves completely when hitting cover. The 10XD doesn't — that's why it's a premier deep cranking bait.

Texas Rig (Creature Bait).insight: KVD: For pitching into grass holes — 3/8oz tungsten weight with a 3/0 Mustad grip pin hook (the pin keeps the bait from sliding down). 5-inch Ocho worm. Blue craw or green pumpkin. Making it fall straight is critical — the Texas rig glides down clean into the holes.

Neko Rig.insight: KVD: Three weight tricks for Neko rigs: (1) Superglue all tungsten Neko weights — they won't fly out when fish jump. (2) Wrap lead wire from fly tying department (Bass Pro) around the hook shank for subtle extra weight without bulk. (3) Put weights in both ends of creature baits like the Sexy Bug — hook in the middle, weight in both nose and tail.

KVD: GS1 spinning rod, 6'10" medium action for accuracy around docks. Longer spinning rod for open water distance. 8lb braid to 8lb fluoro leader. Mustad Apex weedless hook for cover/docks, exposed hook in open water. Mustad Apex drop shot hook (wide gap) for creature baits.

KVD: Neko-rigged creature baits are 'deadly springtime bait around the spawn.' In clear water, most fish bite on the initial fall — if they're on a bed, the bait lands next to them and they eat it immediately. Works for both largemouth and smallmouth/spots.

Ned Rig.insight: KVD: 7'2" medium light with extra fast tip (GS4 LSE KVD rod). Uses braid for maximum feel — 'with that braid, you can feel everything.' Bites can be very subtle.

KVD: Don't set the hook traditionally on a Ned Rig. Reel into them fast — let the rod load, then pull. If you miss one, immediately drop it right back down because they'll come right back and bite it.

KVD: The Ned Rig is so popular for smallmouth because it imitates gobies. Gobies stay locked on the bottom on rocks — that's exactly where the Ned Rig sits. Find the rocks, you find the big smallmouth. Gobies have changed these fisheries — fish don't have to move because the food source is so prolific.
KVD: Use the lightest Ned head you can get away with. 1/16 oz in shallow, 3/16 oz in deep water or heavy wind. Lighter head = better action on the bait.

KVD: When fishing the Ned Rig on an edge, the main thing is keeping yourself positioned on the edge where the fish are. Spot Lock (trolling motor GPS anchor) is essential — 'without that spot lock, I would never be able to stay positioned or hold or land one.'

lure-selection.insight: Hackney: 'The end of summer is one of the most difficult times to fish in the South. Downsizing to a compact finesse jig gives pressured bass something they haven't seen.'
Hackney: 'The thinner skirt gives a sleeker presentation for finicky fish. It's also great for smallmouth and spotted bass with their smaller mouths.'

Hackney: 'Round heads are better suited to fishing rock and stumps — less likely to get wedged. Keel-style heads are ideal for fast retrieves and fishing higher in the water column. Bullet heads offer a mix but can get wedged in crevices.'
Hackney: 'In muddy water, it's more important to increase the size of the blades than to worry about what color they are.'

Hackney: 'I prefer the Strike King Rodent because it penetrates well and doesn't have a lot of unnecessary action. When the water is cold, I don't want a lot of flappy parts on my lure, but I do want a bait with a profile.'
Hackney: 'Any plastic I throw in muddy water will be big and bulky, with a lot of arms and legs to move water. My choice is a Strike King Rage Tail Lobster when the water's real bad.'
Hackney: 'Picking the right lure for muddy conditions begins with helping the bass find it. Four related factors: water movement, noise, size, and color.'
Hackney: 'I don't pick lures based on the size of bass I expect to catch in muddy water. It's all the same whether targeting 2-pounders or 5-pounders. They gotta find it before they can bite it.'

Hackney: 'Changing the sink rate of a jig does not mean tying on a heavier jig. Alter the fall rate by varying the trailer. A bigger, bulkier trailer on a 1/2-ounce jig makes it act like a 3/8 or 1/4 ouncer.'
Hackney: 'Line-tie degrees, the physics of hook penetration, and how and where a jig will outperform plastic are all base-level considerations for every jig flip.'
Hackney: 'In early spring or cold water, tip the jig with a KVD Chunk - big profile but less action. A more subtle swimming jig is better in cold water.'
Hackney: 'Once water heats into upper 60s, switch to a Rage Craw with active vibrating legs. The Rage Craw is also his first choice for swimming a jig.'
Hackney: 'You can make a lipless crankbait look and act like a shad, bluegill, or crawfish simply by changing the color.'
Hackney: 'Flipped mats with a Rodent (black/blue) and a heavy 1.5-oz weight at Lake Garcia and Kenansville. For hydrilla and hyacinth mats, heavy tungsten is essential.'
Hackney: 'Most guys flipping grass were using a punch skirt and plastic or just plastic. But I am a firm believer that when they are biting the jig, it catches the big ones.'

Hackney: 'I carry more trailers than I do jigs because conditions sometimes change hourly. I can completely change the look of a jig by changing the trailer - from no action with a Chunk to looking like the trailer is propelling the jig with a Rage Craw.'
Hackney: 'My favorite finesse baits are the wacky worm, the shakyhead worm, and a dropshot worm. I am also learning the Ned Rig and sometimes fish a hair jig.'
Hackney: 'If Rule 1 did not apply, every pro at the top of every tournament would have caught them on the same exact bait. The only thing we have in common is that we found them.'

Hackney: 'At least 75 percent of catching bass is about fishing in the right spot. All the high-dollar tackle in the world won't help if the fish aren't there. It is rare — like never — that bass are keyed on a specific bait in a specific color'

Hackney: 'I fished cypress trees with a chartreuse-black crankbait, next day another angler caught just as many from same spot on a dark tube, then Aaron Martens caught a mess on a drop shot — the bass were there and feeding, it was location'

Hackney: 'Most of the time I use a Rage Craw trailer. Sometimes I use a small swimbait if the bass are on shad. I use the standard swim jig in colder water with less cover and the heavy cover jig most other times'
Hackney: 'If you can cast and retrieve you can fish a swim jig. If you are fishing spinnerbaits now you have the ability to fish a swim jig. I always have one tied on'
Hackney: 'Check the mouths of caught fish for signs of their recent meals — shad tails, crawfish antennae, or bluegill fins. That detail often leads to the right bait choice'

Hackney: 'I let water temperature dictate which jig to throw in shallow cover. Warm water means swim jig, cold water means flipping jig. It only takes a few casts to determine which method works better'
Hackney: 'A bladed jig has the visual appeal of swimming a jig — a very natural look — plus vibration. It does not have rattles, so it is more subtle than a lipless crankbait'

Hackney: 'There are really three jigs I use year-round: the Hack Attack Flipping Jig for grass, the Structure Jig for casting on fluorocarbon, and the Denny Brauer Premier Pro-Model with a rattle for boat docks and brush piles'
Johnston: Keep rod choices simple on Lake St. Clair - two swimbaits, a tube, and a drop-shot are all you need on the deck.
Johnston: Down south with a smaller jig, you will get smallmouth, largemouth and spots. A compact jig gets just as many bites as a full-size jig, if not more, even in Florida.
Johnston: Jig box should contain 3/8-, 1/2-, 5/8- and 3/4-ounce jigs for flipping.

Johnston: Pro picks from 6th Sense include the Provoke jerkbait series, Slice Blade Bait, Divine Jigging Spoon, Party Minnow (drop shot bait), Crube tube, Stroker Craw, NedFry 4.6, Juggle Minnow, Glitch soft plastic, and Whale Swimbait 6.0 in various shad and natural colors.
Johnston: The CJ Smasher is best suited for drop shot, jig head, or Texas rig applications. It works in both freshwater and saltwater.
Johnston: For bedding smallmouth, throw basic baits like a tube, drop shot, or Senko right into the bed.

Palaniuk: 'My offshore rotation goes: 1) deep-diving crankbait to cover water fast and fire up the school, 2) swimbait for a slower moving presentation once crankbait bite dies, 3) big flutter spoon for vertical reaction bites on fish seen on electronics, 4) big worm dragged on bottom to soak bait, 5) neko rig or drop shot as last resort.'

Palaniuk: 'After you burn a crankbait through and catch a few, the ones that dont want to bite kind of move out of the way and break up. A swimbait allows them to settle back in but still gives them a moving target to chase.'

Palaniuk: 'I dont start with the spoon because the landing ratio is poor. Bass can gain leverage and throw the spoon on the jump or tear off underwater. But it triggers additional strikes once they slow down on crankbait and swimbait.'
Palaniuk: 'Drop shot can be fished shallow to deep, in open water or around cover, and in clear or dingy water by making slight changes to leader length, hook style, plastics, and weight size.'
Palaniuk: 'Deep diving crankbaits are really one of the few ways we have of generating any kind of reaction strike through much of the year on tour.'
Palaniuk: 'The more fishing pressure the bass get, the more effective finesse fishing is with little baits, light line and spinning tackle'

Palaniuk: 'My low-light system: 1) topwater walker for aggressive fish and covering water, 2) jerkbait at 5-6 feet when bass wont surface, 3) weightless wacky-rigged stick bait as finesse to slow down for non-aggressive bass. Use all three early morning, late afternoon, and on cloudy/overcast days.'

Palaniuk: 'A stick bait with a forked tail gives a slightly different action and more shimmy on the fall than regular stick baits. I believe it draws bass from further away. Rig it wacky on a No. 2 VMC hook.'

Palaniuk: 'When marking fish on electronics that won't bite big cranks or football jigs, drop a Neko rig — the first fish was a 9+. When I kept it in my hand I could go to every single brushpile and catch one'
Palaniuk: 'Match the hatch in terms of size and color with jerkbaits. Want the lure to sit right above the bass or at their level — adjust suspension with SuspenDots placed toward the front'

Palaniuk: 'The Damiki Rig (jighead with fluke-style bait) works best when fish are in a negative mood or suspended 25-30ft off the bottom, or locked to cover. The bait just sits there and piques their curiosity because it's doing nothing'

Palaniuk: 'If fish are high in the water column, pick up a jerkbait. If they're toward the bottom, pick up a crankbait. Use forward-facing sonar to see where fish are positioned and choose accordingly.'
Palaniuk: 'I fish the same areas that I throw a crankbait or jerkbait, but use the glide bait when I am after the really big ones.'
Palaniuk: 'If seeing fish high in the water column on MEGA Live 2, pick up a jerkbait or big bait. If seeing them toward the bottom, pick up a crankbait.'

Palaniuk: 'First, there has to be a decent population of 4-pounders. Second, the bass need to be feeding on larger forage -- gizzard shad, trout, or perch. Third, fish should be in less than 10 feet of water for it to be consistently good, which favors February through May.'

Palaniuk: 'A 7-inch glide is the sweet spot. With a 6-inch Magdraft you'll catch 2 and 3-pounders galore. Going up in size doesn't always mean less bites -- sometimes fish just want the most bang for their buck.'
Palaniuk: 'At Chickamauga, I had to switch from a 7-inch glide to the 8-inch Magdraft. The fish had switched from wanting a side-to-side action to something straight and more methodical.'

Palaniuk: 'For river fishing, I want a lure that will be appropriate for the type of cover I expect to find. My top three are a jig, crankbait, and spinnerbait because each can be fished in nearly any river situation.'

Palaniuk: 'Jerkbaits aren't merely a seasonal tool for the coldest months. I've made them 12-month options. Color selections aren't typically seasonal but relate to water conditions, sunlight, wind, and forage.'

Palaniuk: 'A glide bait's slow side-to-side motion has drawing power that will pull fish out of deeper water and cover like submerged brush piles or bluff ends. You can fish it fast or slow with 3/4-turns and a pause. Definitely a big fish bait.'
Palaniuk: 'Always have multiple sizes of baits in fall. A fish's mood can change day to day, even hour to hour. Having multiple offerings to present can pay off with extra bites throughout the day.'

Palaniuk: 'Use a glidebait to draw fish and identify interest, then follow up with a large jerkbait to convert followers. The jerkbait has erratic dying action that glidebaits can't replicate, triggering bites from fish that won't fully commit to the swimbait.'
Robertson: Loves throwing hollow-bodied frogs almost any time of year. The Scum Frog Launch Frog and Trophy Series Frog are his go-to frog baits.
Robertson: Hollow-bodied frogs like the Scum Frog Launch Frog and Trophy Series Frog can be thrown almost any time of year, not just during typical frog season.

Robertson: On fisheries known for shallow-water patterns, offshore fish have likely never seen a 7-inch swimbait or a big spinnerbait. Throwing big baits that fish haven't been exposed to is a major advantage.
Robertson: Loves throwing hollow-bodied frogs almost any time of year — not just a summer-only technique.

Wheeler: If limited to three baits for any season or condition, picks a DT-6 crankbait, a buzzbait, and a Biffle Bug creature bait; prefers moving/reaction baits over finesse and likes to force reflex bites.
Wheeler: For a spring pre-spawn event limited to three rods, picks a jerkbait/crankbait for deeper staging fish, a 1/2-oz jig for versatility, and a ChatterBait for stained shallow water.

Wheeler: At the end of the day crankbait selection comes down to trial and error; sometimes everything says throw a bright squarebill shallow but someone is catching them on deep-diving baits in shad patterns. Always have a starting point with favorite baits in a few colors but be willing to experiment.
Wheeler: It's time that the Carolina rig made a comeback — a technique worth revisiting
Wheeler: In fall, find the baitfish first — bass constantly move and chase baitfish, so you might catch them one day and nothing the next because they've moved on
Wheeler: One swimbait (Storm Largo Shad) can target spotted bass, largemouth, and smallmouth across the country - a single bait that does it all.

Wheeler: Bladed jigs are go-to lures for exploring new water because you can fish them at various speeds and they come through cover well. Sound and vibration add fish-drawing power, widening the strike window.

Yamamoto pro Yelas: The Cowboy is a 4-inch 'power soft plastic' — big, bulky, salt-laden with a powerful double tail and two side appendages; always rig it perfectly straight to get the most action out of it

Yamamoto pro Shryock: 'It's a power-finesse approach — the power part comes in your tackle. You don't have to downsize. You can throw it on a spinning rod but it's almost borderline heavy for a soft spinning rod. I like using a baitcaster'
Yamamoto pro Shryock: Unlike a Senko, the Yamatanuki has a fast fall rate so it excels in nearly any depth. It's non-disruptive like a Senko in shallow water but can also target deep suspended fish

Yamamoto: The Chicken Rig is weedless unlike the Neko rig so it can be fished through brush, rocks, and docks. It has big bass appeal with the large Kut Tail profile. It's durable because the worm slides up the line on hookset
A stick bait on a skirtless football head is a productive look for walking down bluff ends
Yamamoto pro Shryock: The ultra-soft PsychoDad excels over standard jig trailers when fishing pressured bass and around lighter cover where good hookups are critical
Yamamoto pro Hite: Standard Zako for ChatterBaits (subtle natural action), Kickin' Zako (paddle tail) for deep heavy ChatterBaits on ledges with poor light penetration and for swim jigs exclusively
Hudson: 'Senko is best option if you are searching for bites and not on a pattern, or when fish are overly color sensitive or not giving solid hookups'
Howell (pro): 'When Texas rig end gets torn up, flip the Senko and use the other end - no difference in catch rates. Wacky rig lures too torn on ends'
Cowboy legs are more solid and durable than most flipping baits and jig trailers, yet still pliable enough to move water well
Howell (pro): 'Daiwa/Yamamoto Neko worms are made of same material and formula as Senko but unique designs give different action and fall rate'
Senko can be rigged at least seven ways: Texas rig, weightless Texas rig, jig worm, drop shot, wacky rig, Neko rig, and Carolina rig. Keep one or more rigged up at all times

Best for pitching to isolated cover - docks, logs, laydowns, boulders, stumps. A little tough to sink into thick cover. Effective on both smallmouth and largemouth, spawning, cruising, and fry guarding fish

gear.insight: Hackney: 'The zero-degree line tie has the same effect as a Texas rig — the line goes straight down making it weedless. Perfect for going through brush and cover without getting stuck.'
Hackney: 'Usually when you get stuck with a jig, it's not because the weed guard is giving, it's because the guard is too far away from the hook point. That's how brush can get up under there.'
Hackney: 'Opening the wire spread gives the spinnerbait more lift so you can slow down without cutting vibration. Closing the wire gives more speed and keeps it down.'
Hackney: 'Split-tail trailers give the most bang for your buck — good action without changing the speed on retrieve or fall. Craw trailers increase profile and slow the bait down.'
Hackney: 'Use a trailer hook slightly smaller than the main hook, oriented the same direction. Run the main hook point through the back of the trailer hook eye.'
Hackney: 'The trailer is usually a Rage Tail Craw. Sometimes I use a small swimbait if the bass are on shad.'
Hackney: 'I don't believe bass are line shy. You need to adjust what line you use based on how a lure performs using the line, not based on water clarity.'
Hackney: 'Tungsten weights are extremely hard with hard edges and are a lot harder on your line than lead, regardless of braid or fluorocarbon. Always use a rubber insert peg to protect the line.'
Hackney: 'For primarily flipping (not pitching), I prefer the 7-6 rod over the 7-11. The 7-6 has a softer tip which makes it easier to flip lighter baits.'
Hackney: 'Uses braid even in clear water when swimming jigs. As long as it is a moving bait, line type doesn't seem to matter — bass chomp regardless.'
Hackney: 'Key tackle for punching mats: 7-11 Heavy rod, high-speed reel, 50-pound braid, 1 to 1.5 oz tungsten, 6/0 flipping hook, Rage Punch Bug in Black Blue Flake.'

Hackney: 'You want a heavy action blank with some tip and a parabolic bend from tip to handle. Not a featherweight — you need weight to help set the hook. Close to 8 feet long, never shorter than 7-6. The parabolic bend sets the hook and the strong blank pulls the fish out.'
Hackney: 'Use a high-speed 7.3:1 gear ratio reel to get slack out quickly. I don't crank fish out — I pull them out with the rod. The reel just manages slack.'
Hackney: 'Only braid for mat flipping, nothing else under any circumstances. 50 or 60 lb test. Go up to 80 lb when you want to slow the fall. No stretch, minimal resistance sliding through the mat.'
Hackney: 'Use a 6/0 heavy flipping hook — no flex. If it flexes, it'll wear a hole in the fish's mouth and you'll lose her.'
Hackney: 'Always use tungsten. I don't have a lead weight in my boat. Tungsten is more sensitive and smaller so it penetrates the mat easier.'
Hackney: 'Flipping mats absolutely requires the right rod, reel and line. Most techniques you can do with several different combinations and still catch fish. Not when you're flipping mats.'
Hackney: 'I use 55- or 60-pound braided line for flipping. It is low-stretch, cuts through vegetation well with a big bass on, and I can tie on in the morning and never have to retie all day.'

Hackney: 'An 8-foot rod gives better line control than 7 or 7.5-foot. It gives the right amount of tip action and backbone. With braided line you need a parabolic rod with softer action than most flipping sticks because the line does not stretch.'
Hackney: 'For casting jigs into deeper water, use a 7-6 with a lighter tip. The longer rod improves hook sets on fish farther away and in deeper water.'
Hackney: 'Fished a 7-foot-11 Hack Attack flip stick, Quantum 200 HD reel (7.3 gear ratio), and 50-lb Gamma Torque braid.'

Hackney: 'Braid carries no light - it is real natural-looking in the water. Fluorocarbon and mono are not natural-looking at all. If fishing cover, the fish sees the jig first, not the line. I have caught them in 18 feet of visible water in cover with braid.'
Hackney: 'Let braid color fade to match cover. If fishing green grass, use green braid. Do not paint it black because that is one of the easiest colors for fish to see.'
Hackney: 'Braid makes me a lot more efficient. I do not have to retie as much. The whole deal about line type is whatever makes the bait act natural - I am not worried about them seeing the line.'

Hackney: 'The 7-foot-11 flip stick was built for braid - not fast enough for fluorocarbon. Very parabolic bend, limber tip, and the whole rod loads. With no-stretch line you need that forgiveness in the rod.'
Hackney: 'Fish a 6.8:1 reel with 16-lb fluorocarbon on a medium-heavy spinnerbait rod. The slower gear ratio helps maintain the medium to slow retrieve needed.'
Hackney: 'For heavy-duty flipping, use heavier straight-shank hooks to prevent flexing during close powerful sets. The Gamakatsu G Power Flip and Punch Hook works for both braid and fluorocarbon.'
Hackney: 'For casting with plastics, use round bend or EWG hooks. These ensure better hook-ups during the pull set technique which is crucial when the fish is away from the boat.'

Hackney: 'Use the largest hook possible without compromising the natural presentation of the bait. This maximizes hook-up ratio by ensuring the hook occupies the right amount of space within the bait.'
Hackney: 'The size of the hook correlates with the weight being used. For heavier weights and aggressive flipping, a larger hook maintains effective hook penetration.'
Hackney: 'Modern hook coatings like Nano Alpha enhance slickness and penetration capability. The coating aids in better hook sets and offers significant rust resistance.'
Hackney: 'Flipped with a 7-foot-11 rod, EXO PT 100 reel, and 50-pound braid for deep grass jigging.'
Hackney: 'Always flip a jig on 50-pound braid for faster fall rate. When flipping an ounce or heavier weight or throwing a frog, use 65-pound test.'

Hackney: 'I typically like to go with the biggest hook I can put on a treble hook bait because it increases my chances of catching a fish. A fish on a treble hook bait is harder to land than one caught on a single hook bait.'
Hackney: 'You can manipulate hooks because of water temperature. It is easier to sink a bait in cold water than warm water because air and gas inside the bait are less buoyant in cold water.'

Hackney: 'Adding No.4 2X strong trebles to a flat-sided balsa bait (Lucky Shad) tempers buoyancy and lets it suspend on contact with cover. It acts like a jerkbait but you can crank it and cover more water.'
Hackney: 'Only carry about 10 replacement hooks of each size. Overpacking trays leads to dull hooks as masses of metal bump against one another during long runs.'

Hackney: 'I use a medium-heavy rod instead of a heavy action broomstick for flipping. With braid there is no stretch so you do not need a superstiff rod. The medium-heavy is more accurate, lets me feel the fish before it feels me, and a softer rod is better for fighting fish.'
Hackney: 'I use braid for 90 percent of my flipping. Its smaller diameter and limpness gives the jig more action. I prefer 50-pound red Cajun braid.'

Hackney: 'Use the 7-foot-11 flipping stick for standard flipping and a 7-foot-6 version for different pitching scenarios. Balance rod action, bait weight, and the need for precise efficient hook sets.'
Hackney: 'Manipulate running depth by varying the trailer. Rage Craw keeps it high, Rage Swimmer keeps it low, Rage Lobster keeps it high with bigger profile, Menace Grub for subtle smaller profile.'
Hackney: 'Use a 7-2 swim jig/frog rod with a 7.5:1 or 8.3:1 reel for fast retrieves. In winter when fishing deep and slow, switch to a 6.4:1 reel.'
Hackney: 'Use 50-pound braid most of the time for snappy response and shallow cover. Switch to 14-20 pound fluorocarbon only for the deep-and-slow presentation.'
Hackney: 'Always upsize hooks with short-shank trebles at least one size bigger than factory. 1/4-ounce gets a No.4, 1/2-ounce gets a No.2, 3/4-ounce gets a No.1.'
Hackney: 'Line test dictates running depth. Lighter line runs deeper. 20-pound fluorocarbon is the mainstay. Use a 7-4 medium-heavy composite rod for flats and a 7-foot rod for the 1/4-ounce model.'
Hackney: 'Use a Lew's 7-2 Hack Attack swim jig/frog rod, Custom Pro reel 8.3:1 gear ratio, and 50-pound braid. Three trailers: Rage Craw, Menace Grub, and Rage Swimmer.'

Hackney: 'The topographical map is the number-one screen on the depth finder I use to find bass. I mark spots where I catch a bass at a certain depth, and it instantly shows me similar depth structure elsewhere on the lake'

Hackney: 'One of the secrets to winning tournaments is controlling all the variables — sharpen hooks, have fresh line. Once those tasks are done and a bass still gets off, accept the bass just beat you'
Hackney: 'On spinning gear I use 10-pound braid tied to a 3-6 foot fluorocarbon leader material, not casting-style fluorocarbon. The leader material is much stronger and abrasion-resistant'

Hackney: 'I fix little problems before they become major problems. A bass fisherman is only as good as the equipment he uses. I change lower unit fluid regularly and keep tools in the boat to fix almost anything'
Hackney: 'A longer rod gives more distance on pitch and flip and more control over the fish. But shorter rods double as heavy casting rods for Texas rigged plastics and suit shorter anglers'

Hackney: 'Beware of lightweight rods — they feel good in the shop but beat you to death with a heavy jig or worm weight. The more the rod weighs, the less stress on your arm, wrist and hand at end of day'
Hackney: 'There is no industry standard for heavy/extra-heavy or fast/extra-fast tip — they are all a little different. Look at and mess with a rod before you buy it'

Hackney: 'A 90- or 60-degree line tie covers up half the bite on your hook point. With a big weighted jig like 3/4 oz, the line tie can catch in the roof of their mouth instead of the hook point — feels hooked but the hook never penetrated'

Hackney: 'With low-stretch line, if a lighter-wire hook flexes it will cut its way out of a fish's mouth. A heavy wire hook sticks a lot deeper because it won't flex — it is a low-stretch hook designed for a lot of pressure'
Hackney: 'Flipping gear is a 7-foot 10-inch flipping stick, Quantum 200 HD reel at 7.6 ratio, with 50 or 65-lb braid depending on fall rate desired'

Hackney: 'For winter deep-water jig fishing on lowland reservoirs with stumps and channel swings in 6-8 feet, I use 16-20 pound fluorocarbon. On hard structure like rock I go 14-16 pound. Only downsize to 12 in extremely deep clear water'
Hackney: 'I always use a super-high-speed reel for jig fishing — 8.3:1 ratio. Super fast and super strong, works with both braid and fluorocarbon'

Johnston: Rod power matters when flipping. I switched from a 4-power GLX flipping stick to a 5-power NRX and the stiffer butt section was so powerful I was ripping holes in fish mouths on the hookset, losing four 4-pounders. Going down to a 4-power NRX solved it - never lost a fish on the jig afterward.
Johnston: For tossing swimbaits up shallow for smallmouth, use a G.Loomis GLX 892S spinning rod with a Shimano Sustain 2500 reel. For deep drop-shot, use NRX 822S rods.
Johnston: For smallmouths, use a seven-foot six-inch rod that doesn't have a lot of backbone, allowing long casts with small baits on light line. That's especially important in clear water.

Johnston: Use a Daiwa 7'5" rod designed especially for a mini-jig - lighter tip action but plenty of power to handle a 1.5-ounce weight. Pair with an 8.8:1 reel spooled with 65-pound Seaguar TactX braid or 20-22-pound Seaguar Tatsu fluorocarbon. Cover dictates line choice.
Johnston: Use an EWG-style jig hook with thicker wire for braided line, and a round-bend wide-gap hook for fluorocarbon. The EWG hook is slightly harder to get full penetration on fluoro.
Johnston: For deep smallmouth drop shot, use a 7-foot medium-light Daiwa Tatula Elite Dropshot rod, size 3000 Daiwa Exist spinning reel, 15-pound braid to 8-pound fluorocarbon leader (10 feet).
Johnston: Use a larger reel like the Daiwa Tatula 150 size for swimbaits and big crankbaits — it holds more line and helps cast big baits further.
Johnston: Fiberglass rods for crankbaits allow bass an extra second to really get the bait, so you hook more crankbait fish compared to graphite.
Johnston: For big smallmouth drop shot fishing, pairs a Daiwa Ballistic 2500 reel with a Cody Meyer Tatula Elite signature drop shot rod. The parabolic bend and smooth drag are critical on 6-lb test.
Johnston: The soft rod setup was critical to letting him downsize line to get more bites at the event. Having the soft rod to absorb runs and headshakes helped.
Johnston: Fishes two drop shot rods -- a Tatula Elite 7'0" with 8-lb test and a 6'10" light-action BLX with 6-lb test. The EXIST 3000 reel drag is absolutely key when fishing big smallmouth.
Johnston: Use 10-12 lb fluorocarbon for finesse presentations and 15-20 lb braid with fluorocarbon leader for reaction baits when targeting fall smallmouth.
Johnston: Drop shot setup uses 16-lb Seaguar JDM PEX8 Braid to 8-lb Seaguar Tatsu fluoro, #2 Gamakatsu drop shot hook, 3/8-oz tungsten weight, Daiwa Exist 3000 reel, 6'10" L Daiwa BLX rod.

Johnston: Flipping setup is a 3.7-inch 6th Sense Bongo craw on a 4/0 Gamakatsu G-Finesse Heavy Cover Hook, 1/4-oz tungsten weight, 20-lb Seaguar Tatsu fluoro, Daiwa Pitch/Flip reel, 7'5" Daiwa Tatula Elite rod.

Johnston: A quiet trolling motor (Power-Pole Move) is critical for bed fishing. Fish dart off beds if your trolling motor is on full power. Being able to cover more water quicker and quieter is a big key.

Johnston: Power-Pole Drift Paddles are key for slowing drift over areas. Half paddle slows about 25%, full paddles slow 50-80%. Allows slower drop shot drags which get more bites. Can adjust angle to drift across a shoal at an angle.
Johnston: Drop shot setup is 10-lb Seaguar Smackdown Flash Green Braid to 8-lb Seaguar Tatsu fluoro, #2 Gamakatsu drop shot hook, 3/8-oz Woo Tungsten teardrop weight.
KVD: Use lighter line in cold water cranking. Lighter line allows the bait to have its liveliest action and reach maximum depth. 10lb fluoro for the 3XD deep zone.

KVD: CC4 medium-heavy with a soft tip for cranking. 6.2:1 gear ratio reel (Paramag RX braking). The soft tip throws small cold-water crankbaits well and allows long casts for maximum time in the zone.

KVD: Red Eye Shad sizes for depth control — 1/4oz for ultra-shallow (1-2ft), 3/8oz for shallow flats, 1/2oz as go-to, 3/4oz for 10-15ft deep drains. Plus tungsten Two Tap in 1/2 and 3/4oz — heavier tungsten fishes slightly deeper. Most anglers get stuck on 1/2oz and don't downsize when fish are shallow.

KVD: Line size is a major depth control tool for lipless crankbaits. Lighter line (10-12lb) = bait gets deeper (use for clear water, deeper grass, northern natural lakes). Heavier line (17-20lb) = bait stays higher and falls slower (use for shallow grass lakes like Guntersville). Rod tip up in shallow water.

KVD: CC3 medium-heavy composite (graphite/glass) cranking rod for lipless crankbaits. 6.2:1 gear ratio reel — not high-speed in cold water. The composite gives feel for detecting grass on hooks while having power to rip free.

KVD: Always change hooks to KVD/Mustad Triple Grip. Upsize: #2 on 1/2oz, #4 on 1/4oz, #1/0 on 3/4oz. Triple grip design locks fish on — critical because lipless crankbaits are known for poor strike-to-land ratio. Won the Classic catching 5-pounders hooked in the back and side thanks to these hooks.

KVD: 'Simple rule: lighter spinnerbait with smaller blades in shallow water, larger bait in deep water. The KVD Finesse Spinnerbait (3/8oz, one willow + smaller Colorado) is a great all-around option for weeds, stumps, docks.'

KVD: 'Drop shot rod: fast-tapering tip with backbone for long casts. Most bend in the first third for sensitivity without sacrificing control. On the bite, don't jerk the rod — just reel in and let the tip load. The sharp hook takes care of the rest. Keep finger on the line to detect strikes.'

KVD: 'For cold water swimbaits and finesse — Custom Lite 200 series shallow spool reel with 8lb braid + fluoro leader. Shallow spool manages line perfectly, no wind knots. Saves line since bass won't strip 100 yards.'

KVD: 'As water temp drops, a jerkbait that floats in summer will sink in cold water. Switch to three No. 4 Mustad KVD Trebles and test balance — a little sink is fine but nothing rapid or unnatural. 6-10 medium heavy rod, 10lb fluorocarbon for long casts in clear water.'

KVD: 'Standard jerkbaits fish 4-5ft and 6-7ft zones. I wanted one that swims down to 12ft — the KVD 300 Series Deep-Diving Jerkbait. Longer bill, dives deeper, suspends, maintains incredible action at depth, and isn't hard to work. Fills a gap where no other jerkbait on the market could reach.'

KVD: 'Fall spinnerbaits: downsize to 3/16oz in clear water with small blades to match young-of-year baitfish. For heavy grass, go bigger with willowleaf blades. To fish fast and deep, put smaller blades on a 3/4oz head — the Scorcher design lets you burn it and keep it down.'

KVD: In extreme cold, downsize everything. Clearer the water = smaller the bait. Coffee tube, Bitsy tube (Zona taught him this at ice-out in Michigan — 'absolute money'). Small compact minnow baits. Use just heavy enough weight to reach the zone.

KVD: 'Four depth finders on the boat: rear has MEGA side imaging (both sides while scouting), down imaging (under boat), and LakeMaster Maps (1-foot contours). Front has split screen (side + down imaging) plus a dedicated unit for MEGA 360 scanning around the boat. 360 on trolling motor lets me see structure and bass before I arrive.'

KVD: Bill angle determines everything. Straighter bill = tighter action + steeper dive angle + deeper running. More angle from the body = wider wobble + more displacement + more vibration + quicker recovery after deflection. The line tie position relative to the bill matters too.

KVD: Flatside crankbaits send off a lot of pressure waves because flat sides displace a ton of water. Best in cold water (bass rely on lateral line to hunt) and dirty water any season (visibility is down). Round body crankbaits vary widely depending on bill angle — the body shape alone doesn't tell the whole story.

KVD: The 10XD is unique for a straight-body deep diver — it has a slightly wider wobble than expected, giving it great deflection even in 12-15ft of water. Most deep divers with straight bills lose themselves completely when hitting cover. The 10XD doesn't — that's why it's a premier deep cranking bait.

KVD: Square bills are round-bodied with a steep angle and a square bill — this gives them a wide wobble and the best deflection qualities of any crankbait. Most weedless crankbait you can throw. Good through grass, brush, gnarly rocks, riprap. Works in cold or warm water. Triggers neutral/negative fish with deflection.

KVD: 'For lipless on flats: 7-foot glass rod, 5:1 gear ratio reel. Slower retrieve keeps bait down in the strike zone. Line controls depth — 20lb test for shallow water above grass, 8-10lb fluorocarbon for deeper to maximize depth and bottom contact.'

KVD: 'For lipless crankbaits, use a long composite rod (part glass, part graphite). Graphite gives sensitivity to feel tiny bits of weed on hooks. Glass slows the action for better deflection off cover. Longer rod gives depth control — rod tip up for shallow flats (2-4ft), rod tip down for deeper areas. Always fluorocarbon — sinks, low stretch, abrasion resistant.'
KVD: 'So important in clear water lakes that you've got good polarized glasses.' They let you see green grass clumps further out so you can pitch to them without getting right on top of spooky fish.

KVD: 'Punching matted grass setup: 7-10 heavy rod, 8.3:1 high-speed reel (for making more casts, not fighting fish), 1.5oz tungsten weight pegged with bobber stop, 65lb braid, 4/0 hook with Punch Bug. Tungsten's density penetrates dense mats better than lead. The loud impact often attracts fish rather than spooking them.'

KVD: 'Medium-heavy casting rod with backbone, 6.4-7.1:1 reel, fluorocarbon leader. Match blade color to water — gold in stained, chrome in clear. Keeping hookup ratio high is essential when fish are only following.'

KVD: 'Jerkbait rod is the most critical piece — shorter handle, longer rod, graphite with extra fast tip and sturdy backbone. Never pull the jerkbait toward the boat with the reel — work it with the rod, use reel only to pick up slack. Fast 7.5:1 gear ratio. Fluorocarbon is critical — 14lb is my favorite, still makes bait jump on long casts into wind. Below 12lb, too much stretch hurts action.'

KVD: 'For all four finesse techniques (drop-shot, Neko, shaky head, Ned): 10lb braid to 8lb fluorocarbon leader with FG knot as baseline. Go heavier for cover or colored water. Longer 7-2 to 7-4 rods for open water long casts, shorter 6-10 to 7-foot for tight target work.'
KVD: CC5 composite rod with heavy action (e-glass/graphite blend) — you feel everything with the bladed jig and it absorbs shock on the bite. 7.5:1 KVD reel, 20lb fluorocarbon.

KVD: 'For northern smallmouth in clear water, use longer-than-usual rods — never shorter than 7 feet. Long casts are extremely important. Light line and long rod keep bait far from the boat and help move more line on hookset. I designed a 7-4 spinning rod specifically for finesse applications.'

KVD: 'I throw the Red Eye Shad on a 7-7'10" fiberglass composite rod. Gives me graphite feel with the slow reaction time of glass. Enough power to snap it free from grass. Long casts cover more water. Always fluorocarbon — it sinks, great feel, good hook sets on long casts.'
KVD: 'I use the 1/2oz Red Eye Shad in the 7-12 foot zone and the 3/4oz for deeper — 15-20 feet on ledges. Use lighter line to help keep the bait down.'

KVD: For pitching into grass holes — 3/8oz tungsten weight with a 3/0 Mustad grip pin hook (the pin keeps the bait from sliding down). 5-inch Ocho worm. Blue craw or green pumpkin. Making it fall straight is critical — the Texas rig glides down clean into the holes.

KVD: 'Dense vegetation techniques (frogs, flipping, swim jigs): 50lb braid on lakes where bass max around 6lbs. Lakes with 10-pounders, use 65lb braid. 7-4 rod for frogs and jigs with high-speed reel. 7-10 rod for flipping and punching.'

KVD: Fluorocarbon is mandatory for cranking grass — low stretch helps rip bait free. Soft-tip composite rod (glass/graphite blend) for feel and power. Multiple rods with different line sizes. Series 5 crankbait runs 10-12ft — wants a bait that runs to the edge of the grass, not one that buries into it.

KVD: 'Upgrade treble hooks one size on favorite crankbaits and jerkbaits. No. 6 factory → No. 4 replacement. No. 4 factory → No. 2. Keep checking hook points throughout the season. Also keep a variety of replacement spinnerbait blades with split rings pre-rigged for fast swaps — different sizes, shapes, and colors.'

KVD: 'I've weighted crankbaits for a long time. Adding weight helps in three ways: extra depth, better castability (flat-sided cranks cast like potato chips without it), and allows slower retrieve speed. In winter you want to crawl a crankbait as slow as possible.'

KVD: 'The balance point on a crankbait is right by the throat or just forward of the front hook hanger — that's where you add weight. Storm SuspenDots for small amounts, clip-on lead weights on the front hook hanger for more. Open the eye of a lead drop-shot weight as alternative.'

KVD: 'Buoyancy is determined by water temperature and elevation above sea level. Tuning at home doesn't translate to the lake. Start with a fairly heavy weight, check in the water, clip off weight with side cutters until you hit that sweet spot between suspending and a very slow fall.'
KVD: 'I'm a big believer in flat-sided crankbaits in winter — the KVD 1.5 Flat and Lucky Shad for cold water. Throw Lucky Shad on spinning equipment with 8lb fluoro for extra distance and depth.'

KVD: Three weight tricks for Neko rigs: (1) Superglue all tungsten Neko weights — they won't fly out when fish jump. (2) Wrap lead wire from fly tying department (Bass Pro) around the hook shank for subtle extra weight without bulk. (3) Put weights in both ends of creature baits like the Sexy Bug — hook in the middle, weight in both nose and tail.

KVD: GS1 spinning rod, 6'10" medium action for accuracy around docks. Longer spinning rod for open water distance. 8lb braid to 8lb fluoro leader. Mustad Apex weedless hook for cover/docks, exposed hook in open water. Mustad Apex drop shot hook (wide gap) for creature baits.

KVD: 'Deep jerkbait for smallmouth: slower-action medium-heavy rod (not snappy like standard jerkbait rod), fast reel, 12-14lb fluorocarbon. The slower rod action works better for the deep-diving versions.'
KVD: 7'2" medium light with extra fast tip (GS4 LSE KVD rod). Uses braid for maximum feel — 'with that braid, you can feel everything.' Bites can be very subtle.
KVD: Use the lightest Ned head you can get away with. 1/16 oz in shallow, 3/16 oz in deep water or heavy wind. Lighter head = better action on the bait.

KVD: For lipless crankbaits in shallow grass (3-7ft): CC5 7'4" heavy action composite rod, 7.5:1 reel, 20lb fluoro. Multiple cranking rods with 14, 17, and 20lb line for different depths. Heavy line keeps the bait up and slows it down in shallow grass. The heavy composite rod has backbone to rip through grass.

KVD: 'Cranking system: composite cranking rods for casting distance and lure response. Wide-spool reel for more line/better casting. Low 5.3:1 gear ratio for power to horse fish from cover — handles everything from Series 3 to 10XD. Fluorocarbon 10-17lb for sinking and abrasion resistance. Always upgrade crankbait hooks to heavier gauge short shank trebles — stock hooks often lack stiffness and foul on each other.'

KVD: 'For power finesse, use the heaviest gear you can get away with. 10lb fluorocarbon instead of 6lb — far less visible, better hooksets, land more quality fish around cover. The lack of stretch in heavier fluoro gives better hook penetration on long casts. Fish don't notice the line with a fast-falling heavy weight.'

KVD: 'For skip casting, use the heaviest line you can get away with — heavier line makes line management easier. 50lb braid for frogs and swim jigs in colored water. 20lb fluorocarbon for sparser cover or clearer water. 14lb fluoro for finesse jigs on deep docks in clear water. Less line on the spool is better for skip casting.'

KVD: 'Jerkbait setup: high-speed reel (not for pulling bait, just picking up slack fast). All-graphite rod with snappy tip for sharp darting action, even at end of long casts in wind. Fluorocarbon line — lighter line runs deeper, heavier stays shallow. Line choice controls bait depth with precision.'

KVD: Keeps 3/8, 1/2, and 5/8 oz Thunder Crickets on the deck during pre-spawn. Uses lighter weight (3/8oz) to slow down in tough conditions. Tungsten Thunder Cricket has smaller blade with tighter/subtler vibration and runs deeper in the same size — better for finicky fish.

KVD: 'Cold water cranking setup: 6.2:1 gear ratio reel, 10lb fluorocarbon, slow to medium retrieve with frequent pauses. Make long casts, parallel the bank, and let the bait reach max depth. Bottom contact is non-negotiable.'

KVD: '7.5-foot rod, 3000 series spinning reel, 10lb braid with a 20-foot leader of 8lb fluorocarbon. Long leader and small-diameter braid means long casts with no twist. Critical for clear water where long casts matter.'
KVD: '1/2oz tungsten for 10-40ft depth range with the 7-6 rod. Shallow water gets 1/8 or 3/16oz weight. Use just heavy enough weight to keep good bottom contact and line tight.'

KVD: 'For vertical deep water (50ft on Table Rock, fishing standing timber at 30ft down), use a short rod. Video gaming — looking at graph, dropping to the fish, watching bait on sonar, trying to talk them into biting.'

KVD: 7'4" medium-heavy rod (KVD Elite GC7), 7.5:1 gear ratio reel, 20lb fluorocarbon. Heavy line does NOT affect spinnerbait action — it's one of the few lures where heavy line doesn't hurt. Allows accurate casts tight to cover.
KVD: Spinnerbait is one of the few lures where heavy line (20lb fluoro) does NOT hinder the action at all. On a small crankbait or finesse jig, heavy line hurts the action — but not on a spinnerbait.
Palaniuk: 'Uses a 7-foot-6 medium-heavy Abu Garcia Fantasista Regista rod with Revo MGXtreme reel spooled with 15-pound Trilene 100% fluorocarbon for summer worming.'

Palaniuk: 'Setup 1 (nose-hooked): X-Zone 4-inch Original Slammer, VMC Drop Shot Hook size 2, VMC Tungsten Teardrop weight 1/8 to 3/4 oz, 6-foot-10 medium Alpha Angler DSR spinning rod, Daiwa Exist 3000, 15-lb Seaguar Smackdown braid to 6-12 lb Seaguar Gold Label fluoro leader.'

Palaniuk: 'Setup 2 (threaded): X-Zone 6-inch MB Fat Finesse Worm, VMC Finesse Neko Hook size 2, VMC Tungsten Teardrop weight 1/8 to 3/4 oz, same rod/reel, 15-lb Smackdown braid to 6-12 lb Seaguar Tatsu fluoro leader.'
Palaniuk: 'For the Santee Cooper drop shot, I used a green/pumpkin/blue X-Zone Deception Worm 12 inches above the weight on an Alpha Angler DSR 6-foot-10 medium rod with a Daiwa Exist 3000 reel.'

Palaniuk: 'For punching mats, use a 7-foot-9 extra heavy Alpha Angler Mag Hitter rod with Daiwa Zillion HD 7.3:1 reel. The X-Zone Muscle Back Finesse Craw in black-blue-laminate with 1-1/4 oz weight gets through heavy mats.'

Palaniuk: 'Use a longer than average rod for deep cranking. A 7-foot-6 medium-heavy cranking rod with a 5.4:1 retrieve reel and 10-lb fluorocarbon helps make long casts and get the lure as deep as possible.'

Palaniuk: 'Braided line has the most sensitivity compared to any other line — having 15-lb braid mainline to an 8-lb fluoro leader with a Crazy Alberto knot creates a nearly same-diameter connection that runs free through the guides'
Palaniuk: 'For flipping jig in fall: 7-foot-6 heavy-action Alpha Angler Hitter rod, Daiwa Steez A reel, 20-pound Seaguar Tatsu fluorocarbon line.'
Palaniuk: 'Uses a specific braid to fluorocarbon leader knot for combining braided mainline with a fluorocarbon leader.'

Palaniuk: 'Drop to 50-lb braid for frog fishing on thin cheese mats — the smaller pound test gives much better casting distance and helps in pressured areas. If you can cast farther than others, you reach unpressured fish in the back of the mats'
Palaniuk: 'Use 10-12lb Tatsu fluorocarbon for jerkbaits which sinks and helps the bait stay down, but switch to 8-10lb monofilament when pausing more than 8 seconds to keep a direct line to the bait'

Palaniuk: 'In soft cover like grass, use braid. In harder cover like wood, use fluorocarbon because braid digs in. 22-lb Tatsu fluorocarbon is a good in-between — 20 is too light for big fish in heavy cover, 25 struggles with lighter weights'
Palaniuk: 'Use a 7ft 6in heavy-fast rod for leverage and control, with an 8.1:1 gear ratio reel. Offset the power loss from high gear ratio with a bigger 100mm handle with oversized knobs'

Palaniuk: 'Use a 1/4 or 3/8-oz Neon Moon Eye Jig Head for the Damiki Rig — originally designed for walleye but the bait sits perfectly horizontal at rest and the lighter wire hook hooks fish well even when they're just nipping'
Palaniuk: '12-pound fluorocarbon is the optimal line for the Z2 -- the lure is sensitive to different diameters. There are occasions for 10 or 15, but 12 is the sweet spot.'

Palaniuk: 'Throws the Big-M 4.0 on 15-lb Tatsu fluorocarbon -- 15 is the perfect balance of diving ability and action, though not a stretch to go to 20. Uses 12-lb Tatsu with the Kanata and mid-depth crankbaits as his go-to mid-depth zone line.'

Palaniuk: 'Uses a 7-foot medium-action spinning rod, spinning reel with 8-lb Nanofil to 8-lb fluorocarbon leader. Ties on a snap swivel to prevent line twist and give the Jigging Rap more freedom -- the smaller the swivel the better, as large snaps may get caught in the lure's hooks.'
Palaniuk: 'Uses a Super Fluke (minnow-style bait) on the dropshot with a 2/0 straight shank hook and 3/8-oz weight. 15-lb Smackdown braid to 8-lb Tatsu fluorocarbon leader.'

Palaniuk: 'Big M 4.0: 15-lb Tatsu fluoro, Daiwa Steez A reel (6.3:1), 7-foot 6-inch MH rod. Prototype IPX: 12-lb Tatsu fluoro, Daiwa Zillion reel (7.1:1), 7-foot MH Mod rod. Kanata: 12-lb Tatsu fluoro, Daiwa Steez CT SV reel, 6-foot 10-inch rod.'

Palaniuk: 'Uses 22-lb Seaguar Tatsu for big baits -- small diameter, high strength, and supple double structure. Suppleness is particularly important with glide baits because it allows them to be fluid. Most bites come on the pause, so if line coils up when you pull it, the lure won't look natural.'

Palaniuk: 'Pairs the swimbait rod with a Daiwa Tatula 300 with 7.1:1 gear ratio -- faster than most would use, but the 110mm handle, bigger gears and oversized knobs make up for any loss in power. In tournaments, big bait casts are typically target-oriented, not long retrieves.'
Palaniuk: 'Dropshot: 3/8-oz VMC tungsten weight, #2 hook, 8-lb Tatsu fluoro leader (15 feet) to 15-lb Smackdown Flash Green braid. 15-lb braid is super small, 6-lb equivalent diameter.'

Palaniuk: 'Punch rig: Muscle Back Finesse Craw (black/blue), 3/0 VMC Heavy Duty Flippin Hook, 1.25-oz tungsten weight, VMC Sinker Stop, 65-lb Smackdown Stealth Grey braid, 7-foot 11-inch Heavy rod. Best mats were 1.5-3 foot hyacinth with giant salvinia blown up against them.'

Palaniuk: 'Dropshot at Santee: 6-foot 10-inch M rod, 15-lb Smackdown Flash Green braid to 10-lb Seaguar Gold Label fluoro, 1/4-oz VMC tungsten teardrop weight, #1 VMC Finesse Neko Hook, 6-inch Deception Worm (GP blue).'

Palaniuk: 'The right rod for topwater needs a good backbone and a good light tip that allows you to control the bait very well. That creates a system where you can stay in the strike zone and take your time.'
Palaniuk: 'The palomar knot is my favorite knot for any given technique.'
Palaniuk: 'For glide baits, use a 7-foot-9 mag/heavy action rod paired with 20-pound Seaguar Tatsu fluorocarbon. You want some shock absorption when fishing this bait.'

Palaniuk: 'For winter jerkbaits, use a smaller 70-size reel with 7.1:1 gear ratio in cold water, then bump up to 8.1:1 as it warms into spring. The smaller spool means even a faster ratio isn't too fast.'

Palaniuk: 'For jighead minnow in winter, I use a 3000-size spinning reel. Smoothness of the drag from start to finish is critical, and the smoothness of the retrieve impacts your technique, especially when constantly shaking your rod and reeling in slack.'

Palaniuk: 'For winter cranking, I prefer a 7.1:1 gear ratio with Hyperdrive gears. Since you usually retrieve a crankbait fairly slowly in winter, smooth gears let you feel everything your crankbait is doing. You can lose sensitivity if gears are not smooth.'
Palaniuk: 'Keep a tight drag on a 7-foot medium-action spinning rod with 8- to 10-pound fluorocarbon leader on 8-pound braid. Never ventures above 10-pound test even fishing heavy brush.'
Palaniuk: 'I fish all jerkbaits on an Alpha Angler Slasher casting rod with a Daiwa 70-size reel and 12-pound Seaguar Tatsu fluorocarbon. The small reel palms well, reducing forearm fatigue.'
Robertson: As a ledge/Tennessee River angler, partnered with Outkast Tackle for jigs, bringing an offshore jig perspective to their lineup.
Robertson: At the 2021 Classic on Ray Roberts, fished a 3/8-oz Outkast Swim Jig (white) with Berkley Chigger Craw (white), 50-lb Berkley X5 Braid, Abu REVO STX 6.6 reel, 6'8" MH Ugly Stik rod.
Robertson: Uses 12-lb Berkley Big Game mono for all crankbaits. 7-foot Ugly Stik (MH parabolic) for squarebills and lipless; 7'11" Abu Garcia Winch (MH parabolic) for deep divers.
Robertson: The Ugly Stik Carbon rod offers lightweight sensitivity combined with legendary toughness, proving that an accessible working-man's rod can compete at the Bassmaster Elite Series level.
Robertson: As a ledge and Tennessee River specialist, bringing an offshore jig-fishing perspective to jig design helps create products tuned for deep structure fishing.

Robertson: When fishing 10-foot depths offshore, 20-pound fluorocarbon works for swimbaits, crankbaits, and spinnerbaits. The heavier line won't spook fish at that depth and prevents break-offs. Normally he'd use mono for swimbaits and lighter fluoro for cranking.
Robertson: The Ugly Stik Carbon provides lightweight sensitivity with legendary toughness, proving an Ugly Stik can be both your first rod and your last rod, even at the Elite Series level.
Robertson: Considers the Scum Frog Launch Frog and Trophy Series Frog legendary baits for catching giant bass.
Robertson: Use 65-pound braid in heavy Florida vegetation even when fluorocarbon might get more bites — avoiding heartbreak in the grass is more important than getting extra bites.
Robertson: Fishing rods don't have to cost a fortune to compete at the highest level — Robertson finished 7th at the 2021 Bassmaster Classic using Ugly Stik Carbon rods that retail under $80.
Robertson: Fishes the Bassmaster Elite Series with Ugly Stik Carbon rods, proving that the working man's rod can compete at the highest level of professional bass fishing.
Wheeler: Carolina rig setup uses 7.5-foot medium-heavy rod, 7.0:1 reel, 17-lb fluorocarbon mainline to barrel swivel, then 14-lb fluorocarbon leader of 22 inches (18-26 inch range).
Wheeler: Usually fishes the DT-6 on 12-lb Sufix Siege monofilament but varies line size to control depth; heavier mono adds buoyancy for shallower running, lighter line increases diving depth.

Wheeler: The 7'1" MH casting rod is great for jigs, spinnerbaits, and single-hook baits around tight cover like docks, laydowns, and overhangs; the 7'4" MH moderate-fast casting rod is great for offshore cranking and big swimbaits with its parabolic bend that increases landing percentage on treble hook baits.

Wheeler: Uses 7-foot medium-action rod with tip and backbone, 7.3:1 high-speed reel (no disadvantage to high-speed for topwater - ability to catch up to fish), and 17-lb Sufix monofilament that floats and has slight stretch.
Wheeler: Jerkbait setup uses 6'10" medium Duckett rod, 7.5:1 reel, and 12-lb Sufix Advance fluorocarbon; crankbait setup uses 7'2" MH composite rod with 6.8:1 reel.
Wheeler: Uses a 7-foot medium-heavy baitcasting rod with 7.3:1 reel; 14-lb Sufix fluorocarbon when jigging around brush, 12-lb in open areas to help the bait sink faster.
Wheeler: Power-Poles are the most underrated piece of equipment; locked in at 1-2 feet of water in 20+ mph wind, inching up 20-30 feet at a time and dropping Poles for stealth.

Wheeler: Bass don't see line as well as we think — the drag of heavier line diameter negatively impacts bait action, which is why fish don't bite as well. 8-lb Nanobraid has smaller diameter, better action, equals more bites
Wheeler: Pumped fluorocarbon leader from 8-lb to 10-lb on dropshot because when you fire up a school you don't want to retie constantly — broke off a few fish earlier in the week on 8-lb

Wheeler: Experimented with fluoro leaders from 4-lb up to 12-lb on smallmouth dropshot. Going down to 4-lb to check if fish were line-shy made no difference — it's trial and error figuring out what bass fishing is

Wheeler: Prefers monofilament over braid for topwater because braid's zero stretch risks jerking the bait out of the fish's mouth when anglers instinctively jerk on the blowup. Mono's stretch provides forgiveness. Only exception is Florida heavy grass where 30-lb braid with a mono leader is needed

Wheeler: Even when using braid main line for topwater in heavy cover, always connect to the bait with a 12-inch monofilament leader (Sufix Elite) — thinner, more supple, and adds stretch at the connection point

Wheeler: Use a 6.6:1 gear ratio reel for spinnerbaits, chatterbaits, and swimbaits; a 7.3:1 is the best all-around ratio if you can only have one; and 8.1:1 is ideal for flipping, frogs, and getting fish out of cover fast
Wheeler: For prespawn swimbaits use a 7' medium-fast spinning rod with 12-pound Sufix NanoBraid mainline and 8-pound fluorocarbon leader
Wheeler: For frog fishing use a 7'6" heavy-action rod with 65-pound braid and an 8:1 gear ratio reel to pick up line quickly and set the hook in heavy cover
Wheeler: For drop shot in heavy cover use a 7'2" medium-heavy spinning rod, 3000-size reel, 8-pound NanoBraid mainline with 10-pound fluorocarbon leader and 1/4-ounce cylinder weight
Wheeler: For offshore swimbaits (3-4 inch Largo Shad) targeting suspended fish around trees and on points, use a 7' medium-heavy casting rod with a slow 6.3:1 reel and 12-pound fluorocarbon
Wheeler: For general jig fishing use a 7'1" heavy-action fast-tip casting rod with an 8.5:1 reel and 15-pound fluorocarbon for sensitivity and getting fish out of cover
Wheeler: For swim jigs use the same 7'3" medium-heavy moderate-fast tip rod used for spinnerbaits and chatterbaits — the moderate-fast action absorbs the shock of aggressive swim jig strikes
Wheeler: Always check your line while jig fishing as it constantly rubs against bottom structure and invasive species like zebra mussels cause more abrasion than ever — re-tie often
Wheeler: Has a quick method for switching out hooks on crankbaits to improve hookup ratios and land more bass.

Wheeler: Uses a 7'3" heavy action rod with parabolic action for 5/16-1/4 oz weights, and a 7'6" heavy action rod for heavier sinkers in thick cover. Pairs with 8.1:1 high-speed reel to quickly retrieve slack when bass bites and runs toward you.
Wheeler: Uses 14-pound fluorocarbon for pitching to rock banks or bluffs, switches to 17-pound fluorocarbon when pitching into heavy cover.
Wheeler: Fishes Ned rigs on a 7'2" medium heavy spinning rod with a Shimano Vanford 2500 reel.

Wheeler: Uses a longer medium-heavy rod with a slower (moderate fast) taper to balance power and forgiveness on hooksets. Pairs with 17-pound fluorocarbon for strength and durability without losing sensitivity.

Wheeler: Lighter braid (8-pound NanoBraid) results in better bait action because heavier braid has more drag in wind and current, negatively impacting swimbait action. Estimates 20% more bites with small-diameter braid vs 20-pound braid.
Wheeler: For deep water swimbait, uses 8-pound Sufix NanoBraid main line with 6-7 foot leader of 8-pound fluorocarbon. For shallow rock or cover, switches to 10-12 pound fluorocarbon leader.

DeFronzo: Use a 6ft or 6'6" ML IM8 rod with a solid backbone for Senko and Ika finesse work; 6lb Silver Thread line for almost all applications; light wire hooks reduce durability problems with soft Yamamoto plastics by not tearing the bait as much; skin-hook the baits to get multiple fish per bait
Yamamoto pro Fukae: Uses 14-lb YGK sinking braid to an 8-lb fluorocarbon leader for spawn-time drop-shot and Ned Rig presentations on a Shimano Expride 170M with 2500-size spinning reel

The D Shad's extra density allows use on casting gear (15-lb fluorocarbon, medium-heavy rod) unlike most soft jerkbaits — this opens up fishing thick cover without breakoffs, and enables skip casting under docks and overhangs
Yamamoto: Use 10-14lb Sugoi Fluorocarbon leader for stained water, 6-10lb for clear water
For bank fishing Senkos: 7-foot medium-heavy spinning outfit. Use Tactical Anglers Power Clip to swap rigs without re-tying
Yamamoto: Fish the Chicken Rig on medium-heavy spinning tackle, 15-20lb braid mainline with 16 or 20lb Sugoi Fluorocarbon leader
Kevin Hawk: Owner Flashy accent (Indiana or willow blade on barrel swivel slipped onto hook bend) used in murky water or high pressure situations to draw more attention to wacky-rigged Senko

Line choice affects stickbait presentation: monofilament makes the bait float more, fluorocarbon makes it sink quicker. Braid is often overlooked but useful for fishing weightless stickbaits over thick matted weeds
Yamamoto: 'Prefers 3/0 Gamakatsu EWG on weightless Texas rig. Smaller light-wire hooks increase tail action and bait durability'
Heavy-gauge hooks pull the Senko down more quickly; fine-wire hooks allow slower sink. Vary hooks to control sink rate
Yamamoto 10-inch grub requires Gamakatsu EWG Superline 5/0 hook with screw-in 1/4oz weight (F rig). Wrong hook and rigging method causes constant missed fish

Becker (MLF pro): Uses 7ft 2in MH spinning rod, 20lb braid mainline (abrasion resistance around docks), 12-15lb fluorocarbon leader. TroKar TK137 Finesse Hook 1/0 with unique bend that locks and holds fish
Becker (MLF pro): 'Heavier fluorocarbon leader (15lb vs 12lb) facilitates faster sink rate in addition to better durability around docks'

Yamamoto: 'Started using braided line but lost numerous fish because glass-type guides cracked from it. Had six fish in a Bassmaster tournament, only brought in two. Switched to titanium guides and solved the problem'
For wacky rig Senko use #1 VMC Neko Hook. For Texas rig use 3/0 Gamakatsu EWG Hook

retrieve-technique.insight: Hackney: 'Nine times out of 10 you'll find better success with a spinnerbait fishing around isolated cover like dock posts, stumps, and boulders. Bass position near cover to ambush prey.'
Hackney: 'Once you get bit, your job becomes dropping your bait into every nearby nook and cranny. Choose a powerful and balanced flipping rod for effortless and pinpoint pitches and flips.'

Hackney: 'Do not set the hook with a jerk when flipping mats — the weight and plastic will ball up and you'll never get a good hookset. Reel down until you feel the fish, then use a steady, hard, strong, quick pull back to drive the hook home.'
Hackney: 'Pull the fish back with the rod — don't try to crank her back. The reel's only function is to keep slack out of the line.'
Hackney: 'The frog on a buzzbait floats it, enabling you to reel much slower than with a skirt. A skirted buzzbait sinks when you slow down, but the frog keeps it on the surface.'
Hackney: 'Flippin and pitchin are great techniques but you must be accurate with your bait placement.'

Hackney: 'Different trailers affect rate of fall. A bulky active trailer makes a jig fall slower. A slender trailer like a Rage Menace causes it to plummet faster. Rate of fall can be critical - experiment with trailers.'
