Hackney: 'The big key during the shad spawn is to be slow and steady and pound the place. It is easy to fish over the bass when there is so much bait. You do not see a lot of chasing because they do not have to.'
Hackney: 'Use 4.75-inch Rage Swimmer for bigger shad or baitfish. Use 3.75-inch in colder water or when fishing deeper. Both keep the swim jig down.'

Hackney: 'With a single hook I set the hook hard enough the bass shouldn't throw it. I put a lot of pressure on a fish, pulling hard and winding fast to break the fish's will to jump, keeping its head above water like it's on skis'

Hackney: 'If you are not getting hung up you are not catching anything. You have to be deflecting that bait — that is what makes a crankbait better on inactive fish than anything. It spurs defensive bites'

Hackney: 'You can manipulate a bladed jig so much just by changing trailer. A creature bait holds the lure higher, a blade minnow runs deeper. A 1/2-ounce jig with trailer change can fish shallower than a 3/8-ounce with a different trailer'

Hackney: 'In winter it is almost impossible to fish a jig too slow. Use a lighter jig — the bigger jig you throw the quicker it reaches bottom and the tendency is to move it. A lighter one naturally slows you down'

Johnston: On pressured fisheries, if fish see your bait fall they spook every time. Overshoot them and drag your bait to the fish - they'll eat it every time. The key is not letting them see your bait fall.

Johnston: Cover water quickly with a moving bait like a crankbait or swimbait. If you drifted across the whole lake you could catch bass everywhere - that's why you need to cover water and find the sweet spots where they are grouped up.

Johnston: Use a four-bait rotation for picky pressured smallmouth. Figured out something about rigging and presentation that makes the difference - it has to do with how you rig and present the bait, not just what bait you use.

Johnston: In cold water when fishing shallower 2- to 10-foot depths, anchor outside of a sand patch, boulder, or weed edge and work the area hard for multiple hookups. These bass are moving super slow — you really have to work an area over hard with repeated casts to get a strike.

Johnston: On calm days for deep smallmouth, cast the drop shot out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them. Adjust presentation to match conditions even though the bait stays the same.
Johnston: Favorite techniques are flipping and sight fishing. Favorite lakes are Lake Simcoe and Lake Ontario.

Johnston: A Flogger (cone-shaped clear-screen surface viewing device) lets you see your bait, where the fish is, and the sweet spot. You see how the fish reacts to your bait -- it speeds up the process.

Johnston: Fall smallmouth fishing is all about location and timing. Cover water fast with reaction baits to find active groups, then slow down with finesse presentations to catch the rest. If fish stop biting a tube, switch to Ned rig or drop shot.
Johnston: The key to winning on bed fish is covering a lot of water -- doing laps -- because new fish are pulling up on beds constantly. Catch them fairly quick when you find one and move on.

Johnston: Sight fish for bedding smallmouth as deep as 20 feet in clear water, or as shallow as 1 foot in the same area. Start looking in the 8-10 foot range. In tournament practice, just look without throwing so you know what you're getting into.

KVD: 'Fall retrieve progression: go fast and erratic early in fall, less erratic late. The later in fall, the more important it is to hit bottom and keep it there at a slow, steady pace — just enough speed to keep the bill thumping. Use cranks right until ice-up but switch to tighter wobble action below 50 degrees.'

KVD: In cold water cranking, use slow to medium retrieve. Start and stop often — any time during the retrieve, pausing can trigger a following bass into biting. Parallel the bank for long casts to keep the bait in the zone the entire cast.

KVD: Fish the lipless crankbait like a jig in cold water. Short hop it along the bottom. Pull it around cover, deflect off rocks. Rip it out of grass, let it flutter down. Use rod tip to manipulate depth — tip up in shallow, down to get deeper. Make the bait fall as many times as possible — that's when bites happen.

KVD: 'Offshore spinnerbait: 3/4oz, trim wire down a third, single willow blade. Cast, let helicopter down to bottom on tight line. Rip up sharply, let flutter back down. Provokes reaction strikes from suspended bass in summer heat.'

KVD: 'Tandem willow blades for horizontal retrieves through grass — less lift, runs deeper. Single blade for vertical presentations and helicopter flutter. Colorado/willow combo for slower retrieves and longer time in strike zone.'

KVD: 'Position boat to fish parallel to edges and cover. Keeps the bait in the strike zone longer. Short accurate casts (30-40ft) for control in dense cover. Soft underhand presentations so bait lands quietly in shallow water.'

KVD: 'The swim jig handles any shallow cover — grass, bushes, lily pads, wood, rocks. Swim it through pads like you're frogging. Skip it under docks. Fish everything from the bank to the outside grass edge. The heavy cover version powers through slime and algae.'

KVD: 'The Rage Swimmer on a jig head, scooting along bottom, kicks up mud and sand. In clear northern lakes, all forage lives on the bottom — perch, bluegill, crawfish. Swim it through the column only when bluegill are suspended.'

KVD: 'Wind and waves make light-line finesse nearly impossible on big water — can't feel bites with bow bouncing and line bowing. That's when crankbaits shine. Use 360 and forward-facing sonar to define bottom composition and find fish. Drift with the wind to conserve battery, make long downwind casts. Use electronics to find irregularities, then cover water with crankbaits.'

KVD: 'Cold water jerkbait: solid pull (not snap), pause one second with rod pointed at bait for slack. By pulling short distances the bait runs 3-4 feet deeper. I still cover a lot of water by fishing parallel to edges — keeps the lure in the productive zone throughout the retrieve.'
KVD: 'On a frog bite, pause a split second before the hookset. With braid and no stretch, you want to be sure the bass gets it all the way in its mouth before setting the hook.'

KVD: 'On large flats, the key is contact, depth, and placement. Vary retrieve speed with stop-and-go movements. Let the bait sink periodically. Always aim for the bait to be hitting something — if it's not interacting with cover, it loses effectiveness.'

KVD: 'Efficiency means keeping your bait in the strike zone as long as possible. Cast straight along any edge — drop-offs, grass lines, weed lines, seawalls, sandbar drop-offs. Position yourself to fish parallel so the bait stays in the zone the entire retrieve. Choose the most efficient bait for the cover and clarity, then maximize every cast.'

KVD: 'Cold water Thunder Cricket: cast toward structure or transitions, let sink just above cover/grass, lift-and-drop retrieve with brief pauses. The key isn't speed — it's triggering instinctive reaction bites. Slow enough for blade to stay in strike zone but fast enough for vibration.'

KVD: 'Jerkbait cadence: always start and finish with slack in your line — this gives the bait its best side-to-side action. Experiment every day and let bass tell you what they want. Cold water = longer pauses. Warm water = more active and erratic retrieve. The clearer the water, the more action in the retrieve.'

KVD: 'Sexy Frog technique: reel through tall grass with enough momentum to spray water, then slow down at the grass edge — walk it side to side. Watch fish come up and nail it at the edge. Also target docks: put on a skipping reel and throw it way back under coverage. The hollow soft body skips well. Fish often follow it out of thick cover into open water.'

KVD: 'Pump and stop retrieve — rod tip to the side, sweep and follow the bait back down keeping tension. Let the Red Eye Shad do its thing and swim to bottom. Works over grass, rocks, deeper flats. I probably catch more fish this way than any other.'

KVD: 'Skip casting with baitcasters: sidearm cast low to water that turns into a tight, quick roll cast. Lure should travel 70-80% of the cast just above water, skip 2-3 times, then land quietly. First cast is critical — a bad cast spooks fish and your one shot is over. It's a deadly combination of stealth and accuracy for pressured fish.'

KVD: Position boat just outside the grass line. Cast at 45° quartering angle into shallower grass. Crank down until you hit grass, then slow up. If it won't float free, snap it out. The bait follows the contour of the grass as it comes back to the deeper edge where your boat sits. This is the most effective way to cover a lot of grass quickly.

KVD: When crankbait sticks in grass — if it won't come through, snap it free. Otherwise let it float off. The moment it tears free or floats off is when you get the bite. 'A crankbait through the grass is almost as good as a finesse bait like a worm at triggering strikes from inactive fish.'

KVD: 'Biggest smallmouth cranking difference: it's NOT about bottom contact. Keep crankbait a few feet off bottom — smallmouth in clear water love to feed looking up. It's about speed and covering water, casting all directions to find roaming aggressive fish. Burns a meal over their heads and they can't resist.'

KVD: 'The KVD 1.5 can run almost 6 feet deep with light line (10-12lb fluoro) — deeper than you'd think for a shallow runner. Put it on 20lb and it runs shallow. Rod height controls depth during retrieve — tip up to stay high, tip down to dig deeper.'

KVD: 'What I look for in a squarebill is erratic swimming — kick around, don't run straight. Old wood baits had this but broke fast. The body and weighting design make every single one swim erratically out of the package. It looks like an injured baitfish — catches a lot of smallmouth over deep water.'

KVD: Dice/bug baits on a drop shot are a new frontier. Lighter weight = better (1/8oz ideal, 1/4oz max in deeper water). The slow sinking action is the key — once on bottom you can still shake it traditionally, but most bites come on the fall. Game-changer for clear water smallmouth and during Mayfly hatches.

KVD: Don't set the hook traditionally on a Ned Rig. Reel into them fast — let the rod load, then pull. If you miss one, immediately drop it right back down because they'll come right back and bite it.

KVD: In cold/post-frontal pre-spawn, don't just wind the Thunder Cricket. Work it like a jig — hop, twitch, pull-and-stop. Get it down in the grass and on the bottom. Bites are extremely subtle: the bait just gets heavy or the vibration stops. 'I always move faster than the fish' — force yourself to slow down.

KVD: 'Three swimbait styles for different conditions: Bulkier hollow-body for slower presentations — great prespawn and stained water for extra vibration. Subtle finesse swimbaits (like Swimming Shiner) for clear water and tough bites on bright days. Mid-action swimbaits like Caffeine Shad as all-around choice — weedless through grass on screw-lock hook, vary head weight from 1/8oz shallow to 3/8oz deep. If bass follow but won't bite, adjust style, action, color, or speed.'
KVD: 'In winter, fish parallel to the structure — this keeps your lure deeper in the water column longer. Use search lures that draw strikes without moving fast.'

KVD: 'On a long cast, let it sink then tightline the last bit so the Baby Z-Too swims like a real minnow on the way down. Shake 3-4 times, reel a little, shake again. If you find concentrated fish, work it longer through the cast.'

KVD: 'Match bait to fish mood: super tough = wacky-rigged Ocho or finesse worm. Power fishing = something with lots of wiggle like Baby Z-Too or nose-hooked Dream Shot. Figure out the mood and give them action that generates extra strikes.'

KVD: 'Wake Shad runs only about 3 inches deep — perfect for burning over grass flats like hydrilla without getting snagged. Reel it right through the crisp green grass, mimicking a wounded baitfish just above the vegetation. Skimming the surface while creating enticing movement triggers aggressive strikes.'

KVD: Barely turn the handle. When the bait hits a limb, speed the reel up one fast turn to force the bait over the limb keeping it up and down (not turning over and hanging). The flutter on the other side of the limb is when they bite. Even without cover, intermittently speed up for a quick flare of the skirt and blades to trigger following fish.

Palaniuk: 'When you change from horizontal action (crankbait/swimbait) to vertical action (spoon), it makes fish react. There are two things that trigger a fish to react: a fleeing baitfish or a dying baitfish. You get both with the retrieve on a spoon.'

Palaniuk: 'When idling over an area and you find structure, idle past it. The prop leaves a bubble trail on the surface. Cast back to that bubble trail and retrieve through it to speed up finding the right cast line.'
Palaniuk: 'The more you fish a lure, the more you understand its vibration pattern. That helps you know if there is grass on the lure or a fish has subtly struck it.'

Palaniuk: 'Play a 4-5 pound smallmouth for 1-2 minutes before bringing it to the boat. When they jump, don't try to pull them down or you'll pull the hooks out. Pick them up by the belly like a football — they act paralyzed'
Palaniuk: 'Parallel the edges where grass falls off to a straight wall to keep the bait in the strike zone the longest — fish roam and sit on the edge for an ambush point'
Palaniuk: 'Shake the bait in place using your forearm, not your wrist, to create subtle action while keeping it in the strike zone'

Palaniuk: '90 percent of people say they're flipping but are actually pitching. Flipping uses a fixed amount of line, never releasing more. Pitching picks a target and creates a cast while releasing line'

Palaniuk: 'Prefer wacky rig over Neko mainly when fishing shallow around docks or laydowns or when fish are suspended under deeper docks. Use Crossover Rings to quickly switch between Neko and wacky by pulling the weight out and switching hook direction'
Palaniuk: 'Walk the frog on top of thick grass to get bass to blow through it, or fish it on edges and points of the grass. Walk it 90% of the time like a Zara Spook'

Palaniuk: 'I call it floating a crankbait -- pick a crankbait that dives deeper than the depth you're fishing, reel it so it crawls up and over rocks, then back down the other side. It looks like a crawfish slowly scooting.'

Palaniuk: 'I was popping the rod almost like stroking a jig. The crankbait was trying to dive but the line was pulling up at the same time -- like a teeter-totter or a bull bucking. I don't think that fish would have hit otherwise.'
Palaniuk: 'Standard glide bait retrieve is a 3/4 handle turn that pulls the bait to the side, then a 1/4 turn and stop, which allows it to glide.'

Palaniuk: 'Cast the soft swimbait out and let it hit bottom. Reeled at a fairly decent pace in 4-6 feet of water, trying to keep it right on the bottom and bounce it off shell or stumps. When it deflected off something, he'd get bites.'
Palaniuk: 'If you slow down and work topwater in the strike zones, you'll get more bites and the bites you do get hook up a lot better.'

Palaniuk: 'I always change cadence first, then color, then profile. Cadence is the easiest thing to change quickly. Color becomes the deciding factor when cadence adjustments don't generate the bite.'

Palaniuk: 'All the things we thought were black-and-white rules about how to work a jerkbait during different times of year are not always the case. It doesn't always have to be a fast pace — you just have to read each fish. If you don't have forward-facing sonar, the general rule of thumb is move the jerkbait faster.'

Robertson: On lakes without heavy vegetation (like Kentucky Lake), slow-roll the lipless just off the bottom rather than ripping it through grass. Move the bait only 12-18 inches at a time horizontally, almost like yo-yoing.

Robertson: When following postspawn bass down ditches with a deep diver, start by casting ahead and up on top, then bring the crankbait down the side of the ditch at about a 45-degree angle. The crankbait must hit bottom.
Robertson: On offshore ledges, you won't get a lot of bites, but figuring out one or two little things to trigger bigger bites can make the difference. It's about quality over quantity.
Robertson: On offshore ledges you won't get a lot of bites, but one or two subtle adjustments that other anglers aren't doing can trigger the bigger bites that make the difference.

Robertson: On offshore ledges, you won't get a lot of bites, but there are little things you can do to trigger bigger bites that other anglers aren't doing — subtle presentation adjustments make the difference.
Robertson: In Florida grass (pencil reeds, flat reeds, Kissimmee grass, hyacinth), drop the trolling motor and cover water with swimbaits rather than sitting on one spot.
Wheeler: When deadsticking a creature bait on a football jig or Hardhead, the claws and spear-like appendage rise to almost a 90-degree angle off the bottom and quiver and undulate.
Wheeler: Uses a curly-tail worm Texas-rigged on a keel-weighted swimbait hook as a more subtle alternative when everyone else is throwing swimbaits; the keel sinker makes it run straight as an arrow.
Wheeler: Constantly moves boat in 4-8 feet of water casting shallower; covers a lot of dead water but once you find them you catch a lot of fish.

Wheeler: If struggling to find something, just drop your trolling motor and run the bank with a handful of favorite crankbaits to contact your first fish; the first bass is an essential clue to start refining your day.
Wheeler: When a bass dogs a prop bait without striking, pause it for as long as 6 seconds; the fish finally inhaled the topwater after the extended pause.

Wheeler: Uses a come-and-get-it approach by aggressively snapping the Jigging Rap well above fish rather than dropping it in their face; forces an interested bass to commit by swimming up, making it more likely to strike.

Wheeler: Snapping a football jig is different from stroking — stroking is for suspended fish, snapping is a small sharp motion that makes bottom-hugging fish in heavy current react. The higher-frequency kick action of the Cleanup Craw trailer complements the snap
Wheeler: When fishing ledges, keep baits on bottom but often burn them fast then kill the retrieve to trigger reaction strikes — this produced key fish at Chickamauga
Wheeler: Does not use a trailer hook on spinnerbaits; instead relies on a trimmed skirt with soft plastic trailer for better hookup ratio
Wheeler: Be versatile during prespawn — cycle through finesse swimbaits, Ned rigs, and heavy gliding jigs to determine the best technique for current conditions
Wheeler: When walking a popping frog, snap the rod to throw slack back in the line — the slack allows the frog to turn; if the line is tight it will only move forward and won't walk

Wheeler: His top 5 swimbait rigs are: 3-inch on ball head jig (spinning), 4-inch on jig head (casting), Tokyo rig, belly-weighted weedless (Texas rig), and as a trailer on moving baits — each serves a distinct depth and cover scenario
Wheeler: Do not overwork a jig — the slower you go the more bites you get; less is always more when fishing a jig on the bottom
Wheeler: When fishing a football jig on deep rock, make a long cast, let it hit bottom, and drag it slowly — most bites come when the bait pops off a rock as bass follow it on the drag
Wheeler: Always use a trailer on jigs — without a trailer a jig doesn't look like anything natural; match trailer size to jig size to increase bites
Wheeler: When flipping mats, select appropriate weights and baits to punch through vegetation cover effectively.
Wheeler: Casting angles are critical for deep cranking offshore structure - varying your angle of retrieve relative to the cover triggers reaction strikes from bigger fish.
Wheeler: Pitching is for being very specific with bait placement - the strike zone may be only 6-8 inches wide. Casting is for covering water, pitching is for fishing one particular thing.
Wheeler: Aggressive bass bite on the initial fall. Sometimes the strike comes after hopping a couple times. During the spawn or heavy pressure, let the bait sit still for 5+ seconds.

Wheeler: Uses four Ned rig methods - traditional exposed jig head, drop shot Ned (best for smallmouth), Texas rigged swinging Ned (for snaggy cover), and wacky rigged drop shot Ned (for deep individual fish targeting).

Cast an unweighted Senko on a 4/0 wide-gap hook and retrieve with a hopping/skipping cadence across the surface — faster than a floating worm but not burning it; the wide-gap hook acts as a keel to keep the Senko running straight and prevents line twist; 3- or 4-inch size works best
Yamamoto: A Senko rigged on a weighted screw-lock hook is a simple system that covers water faster than wacky or weightless while maintaining skipping ability

Wilson: Split-shotting a 4- or 5-inch stickbait in 3-20ft is excellent for prespawn and high-pressure situations; vary the leader length by depth — deeper water gets a longer leader (up to 2ft) so the bait sinks the last 2ft with a slow tantalizing fall after the weight hits bottom; put the shot right against the hook when sight fishing beds

Yamamoto: Gary Yamamoto's key technique on Lake Champlain was dragging a weighted Texas-rigged Senko across the bottom rather than the standard lift-reel retrieve — the dragging motion was irresistible to smallmouth, possibly imitating crawfish

DeFronzo (via Yamamoto staffer Jerry Puckett): Do not set the hook on wacky-rigged Senko — apply slow steady pressure instead; the hook will penetrate the corner of the bass's mouth; switching from a straight-shank to a splitshot hook and using pressure instead of a hookset drastically reduced lost fish

DeFronzo: Crimp a small split shot 18 inches above a size 1 hook with a 3-inch Ika Texas-rigged; keep a slow steady retrieve; effective up to 20ft — deeper than that you lose contact; add a grub guard for bottom snags and weed penetration; a glass bead above the weight adds noise and reflects light

Yamamoto pro Fukae: The Free Rig is a cross between a Texas Rig and Carolina Rig — the line runs freely through the weight's eye so the weight falls first and the soft plastic flutters down behind it with an unpredictable slow fall; use a 'lift and fall' retrieve; 1/4-oz tungsten weight is the all-around top choice
Yamamoto pro Fukae: Rigging a 3-inch Fat Senko Texas-rigged on a drop-shot with a 3-inch leader replicates a Ned Rig presentation but avoids bottom hang-ups around cover

Sealock: When the flipping/pitching bite gets tough, switch to a wacky-rigged Senko on a spinning rod with 8-lb test — fish it in the same areas (under docks, laydowns, boulders, grass lines); hook through the middle, make a long cast, let fall on slack line, pop the slack occasionally
If a bass short-strikes the D Shad, immediately 'kill' it and let it sink on slack line — the level fall looks like a dead shad and prompts follow-up strikes on the same cast

Bank fishing: 'Shooting' technique from crappie fishing — point rod at target, pull bait toward you to load rod tip with downward arc, release like bow and arrow. Great for docks, laydowns, gaps in weeds

Adding a tail spinner blade to a Senko via Owner Flashy Spin on a centering pin is effective in darker water — swimming the bait through sunlit areas throws attention-getting flashes into shadow areas where predators lurk

Yamamoto: The Chicken Rig is a modification of the Neko rig using a 7.75-inch Kut Tail worm rigged backward with a 4/0 or 5/0 straight-shank hook inserted about an inch behind the egg sack, threaded on Texas-rig style, with a drywall screw inserted in the meaty tail end for weight

Yamamoto: 'Cast it out and let it fall on slack line. Once it hits bottom, lift it up a couple feet and let it drop again. If I haven't gotten bit, I reel it back up and throw again. It's more like a power fishing tool than finesse'
Yamamoto: 'When a fish bites, they are almost always swimming with it. If you jerk really hard, you'll likely lose fish. Just reel down and pull with hard steady pressure'

Weighted wacky hooks and wacky jig heads (flick shake) take the double-ended wiggle straight to the bottom. FLW pro Roy Hawk: 'Throw it at anything — grass lines, pockets, docks, single rocks, shade lines. Vary the head weight depending on wind, current and depth'
FLW pro Roy Hawk prefers nail-weighted Neko over flick shake in deeper waters because the rig falls faster with less lateral resistance — straight down

Yamamoto pro Hite: The Fat Ika rigged backward weightless on EWG hook backslides like the old 'Flying Lure' — heavy salt-loaded plastic sinks on its own, and backward rigging makes it slide under docks and away from you
Wacky rig stickbait works best for a slightly more aggressive retrieve. Weedless/Texas rig with no weight is a much more subtle approach with a very subtle wiggle
Yamamoto: 'Employs a simple lift-and-drop retrieve - it is like working a plastic worm but not quite as fast because it takes longer for it to fall'
Howell (pro): 'Just throw them out and let them sink. They cast a mile, ideal in heavily pressured or clear water. Smallmouth come from a long way to eat one'
Howell (pro): 'Prefers wacky rig when fish are less active and less eager to chase. Texas rig when bass are more active, working it faster'
Scott (pro): 'Fish Senkos with Power Pro braided line tipped with fluorocarbon leader because braided line floats, acting like a bobber to detect strikes on the fall'

Most effective wacky style retrieve mimics crippled shad - raise and lower stick worm a couple feet, never letting it touch bottom. Pop it a couple times for herky-jerky action then return to subtle fall
Key to catching fish on Yamamoto 10-inch grub is delayed hookset with EWG hook and screw-in weight. Using straight shank hook and standard hookset results in many lost fish
Becker (MLF pro): 'Bites commonly go undetected while Senko sinks on slack. Watch line movement and use bright-colored braid to see line bites'
Card (pro): 'Reel slow enough that it just hits bottom every once in a while, barely ticking the rocks. Can also drag on bottom like a jig, or reel-reel-reel and let it fall on tight line'
Best way to see Senko's fish-catching power is wacky rig - cast out, let it sink, twitch rod to make worm shake then return to sinking. Catches fish from 6 inches to over 5 pounds

Yamatanuki catches fish on initial fall like Senko and equally well dragged on bottom. Bites are very easy to detect due to added weight. Fish it on MH baitcasting with 15-17lb Seaguar InvizX fluorocarbon
Yamamoto: Video-only content about his sweep hook set technique. No text transcript available beyond title

mental-approach.insight: Hackney: 'I like big poles, big line and big hook sets. It's easier to get a bite on a wacky worm than a 3/4-ounce Hack Attack Jig, but at the end of the day the results are the same. It boils down to how you like to fish.'
Hackney: 'Most anglers do not use anything close to what they should for flipping mats. Most of them are carrying a knife to a gunfight.'
Hackney: 'Visualize the hookset in your mind before every flip. If you don't, you'll reflexively jerk your rod when you get a bite.'

Hackney: 'I try to fish places in a different way or with a different lure or present my lure in a way other fishermen won't. Fishing weird places with various tactics that others weren't using could pay off with big bass.'
Hackney: 'Go to your favorite lake and carry one rod for jigs. That is the best way to get familiar with jig fishing and resist using other techniques.'

Hackney: 'With 1-2 hours left in a tournament, sit down, put the rod down, and mentally pretend the day is over. Critique yourself as if you are driving home. Ask what you would have done differently, then come back and make that change.'
Hackney: 'I won the MLF World Championship in March 2018 flipping a Rodent. If it is not broken, do not try to fix it.'

Hackney: 'You cannot be a one-dimensional bass fisherman. The pros who continue to cash checks are good at every form of bass fishing. I always have a spinning rod and finesse lure on my casting deck.'

Hackney: 'The single most useful thing I can say is learn how to find them. The deal is not about our lures, it is about where we are fishing. Two completely different plastics caught fish because the fish were there and we were fishing on the bottom.'

Hackney: 'If I lose a big bass today I can get over that tragedy in about 5 minutes. Instead of being upset, I think: you've found the right lure, you're in the correct depth on the best structure, so keep doing the same thing and you will catch another one'

Hackney: 'I'm a really good visual learner. As I run down a lake I can see where bass should be. If you've put the time in on the water, your brain identifies the places you need to fish based on past experiences'
Hackney: 'During practice I think about almost anything — what to do next, how to fish, where to fish. During the actual tournament, I only think about the next bite'
Hackney: 'Confidence will make you fish a lure better. Without confidence there are casts anglers simply won't make. You'll do things that get you bites when you are confident'

Hackney: 'The big thing for me that makes fishing fun is figuring them out — making those changes. Having that weather change and knowing what bait to pick up and catching a big bass. That is the part I like'
Hackney: 'Don't overcomplicate things. Bass fishing isn't brain surgery'
Johnston: When fishing an unfamiliar lake, go in with open minds rather than preconceived notions about what fish should be doing.
Johnston: We're not really guys that do a lot of research on the Internet. We just back the boat in and figure it out.

Johnston: On Lake Ontario in big waves, I prepared my whole life for that moment. All of my equipment is up to the task. Don't abandon the plan to run to the lake because of wind and waves if you've prepared properly and know what lives on those spots.
Johnston: Get me to the St. Lawrence -- familiarity with home waters breeds confidence. Being within striking distance going into familiar fisheries is the ideal AOY scenario.
Johnston: Always swing for the win rather than fishing for a check. If you don't make a check, don't let it hinder your next tournament.
Johnston: Having a sibling rivalry with brother Chris pushes both to be better. They help each other out, which is especially advantageous during practice.
Johnston: Without a doubt, my goal is to win the AOY Title.
Palaniuk: 'You can't get in a hurry and attempt to force a bass to bite. Very rarely will you get a bite if you're rushing through your cast and working your bait faster than you should'

Palaniuk: 'The only constant we have in fishing is change. The Percentage Triangle gives you baseline confidence to pivot from practice findings when conditions change — it tells you about fish movements so you can alter your game plan quickly'
Robertson: When you stop caring too much about the outcome and just go fishing, good things happen. Assumed he'd weigh 6 pounds and stopped pressing -- ended up catching 25-8.

Robertson: Sometimes when practice goes poorly, the best approach is to stop caring so much about the outcome. Just go fishing and let things happen rather than forcing it — this mental shift can unlock better bites.
Robertson: Commit to fishing your own way. If you come in with no fish, that's fine because you're fishing how you want. Fish for five bites and don't worry about the outcome.
Robertson: Commit to fishing your strengths — if you come in with no fish, that's fine because you fished how you wanted. Don't chase other patterns.
Robertson: Sometimes the best thing is to stop caring too much about results — when he stopped overthinking and just went fishing, he caught 25 pounds including a 6-14 kicker.
Robertson: Fish more deliberately and stop second-guessing. Go with your gut instead of constantly questioning whether to move.
Robertson: Fish more deliberately and stop second-guessing. Have faith in yourself and roll with your decisions instead of bouncing between spots.
Wheeler: Prefers to force fish into biting out of reflex rather than teasing them with finesse; a bait suddenly getting in their space triggers a reaction bite.

Wheeler: Keeps a list in his phone of things he's weak at or needs to clean up each fall; believes you always have to challenge yourself to be better because the fish adjust and you must adjust your techniques and strategy.

Wheeler: The difference between top anglers and those getting relegated is minimal now; it comes down to minute details like the difference between 8- or 10-lb braid on a spinning rod, or a knot or hook choice.
Wheeler: Gets very motivated by little things - a poke from a competitor or something he hears - that he channels out on the water.

Wheeler: When making a 150-mile run through unfamiliar saltwater, accept the risks — 'I knew the dangers of making that kind of run.' Self-reported a rule violation for having his marshal exit the boat on a sandbar, resulting in a DQ, because it was the right thing to do

Wheeler: Great fishing innovations are double-edged swords — like Power-Poles making you fish too slow, FFS can trap you into casting at visible fish that won't bite while your overall game plan suffers. It's up to the angler to decide when too much of a good thing is really too much

Wheeler: At Heavy Hitters on Toho, saved brushpiles with biggest dots for the final day but they wouldn't bite. Got so consumed trying to catch dots that it bogged down his intuition and locked up his decision flow — should have relied on gut and weather, not a screen
Wheeler: Regardless of what equipment you have, it doesn't make you who you are - if you work hard you can truly accomplish great things. It ain't the boat that catches them, it's the fisherman.
Wheeler: Learning electronics and bass behavior from experienced anglers, plus fishing pot tournaments alone, helped build confidence in decision-making that translated to professional success.
Wheeler: Being too versatile can hurt - trying to do too much instead of committing to one pattern leads to bad positioning. After worst tournaments, the next one is usually pretty solid.
Wheeler: If you're not enjoying it, you don't have the right mindset. Focusing on having fun rather than standings leads to better performance.

Yamamoto: 'The most common mistake is fishing too fast. This bait is very subtle. It needs to be worked slowly. From the time it splashes and falls, it only needs to be moved a little bit to get good action'
Yamamoto: 'At 67 I still know I can do real well. If I didn't think I could win this event I wouldn't be here'
Book 'Gary Yamamoto: My Life with Fish' by Steve Price contains one-page tips at end of each chapter on fishing the Senko, Hula Grub, dropshot rigs, flipping, and Neko rig
Yamamoto: 'Every year you fish you learn a few more tricks and learn how to solve some of the problems you had before. You add a few more pieces to the puzzle'

tournament-strategy.insight: Hackney: 'The first six events during the prespawn/spawn period allow power fishing because bass are most active and in the rut.'

Hackney: 'One partner needs to do 80 percent of the fishing and the other is the support person - handling the net, unhooking fish, culling. Pride causes more teams to lose buddy tournaments than any other factor.'
Hackney: 'The support person takes the bass off the hook, puts it in the livewell, and handles culling. This lets the leader fish most of the time and catch more bass.'
Hackney: 'Key spots did not replenish. You could not go back to the same place and catch another one. Had to keep the jig wet all 8 hours for 5 big bites each day.'
Hackney: 'In MLF, use Score Tracker to decide if you need to abandon a pattern or ride it out. I never got in a hurry. More slow and steady was the deal because fishing was not through the roof.'
Hackney: 'I got behind in the first round but did not worry because it is FL - the way I was fishing you could catch an 8-10 pounder. I like to go places where they have big ones.'
Hackney: 'Many times speeding up and changing lures is not the answer. It is just the opposite - slow down, mentally evaluate, then make a deliberate change.'

Hackney: 'Sometimes fishing around other people is good because you can see what they are doing differently. Make a point to avoid following anything they might be doing. I have gone in behind other anglers and caught fish doing something completely different.'

Hackney: 'Catch what you believe to be your best weight for the day and get out of there. Do not burn out an area in practice - if you catch a 3-4 pounder in practice you risk not catching it again during the tournament.'
Hackney: 'Disappear from a productive spot when the crowd shows up, then return later. The area needs time to replenish with more bass.'

Hackney: 'I have not changed my fishing strategy at all for MLF. The one thing that will change is you probably will not leave a spot producing lots of bass to go look for bigger ones. But at the end of the day the angler who catches the most big bass will win.'
Hackney: 'Slow and steady typically wins the fishing race. When within striking distance of winning, I do not want to fish too hard and spin out.'

Hackney: 'If I am struggling or close to winning and may need one or two more fish, I will slowly fish a shakyhead worm or a wacky-rigged worm thoroughly, working the cover. Time and again this tactic produces more bass than running up and down the lake chucking and winding.'

Hackney: 'I spend a ton of time watching old tournament videos to learn about structures in the lake, what bass should be doing at that time of year, what portion of the lake produces the biggest bass, and what lures are most productive.'
Johnston: After catching spawners early, chase postspawners in deeper water at spots no one else knows about. Having secret deeper-water spots for postspawn fish can produce multiple 5-pounders.
Johnston: When you have a trusted practice partner, each head in a different direction every day during practice to effectively split the lake in half, giving you double the practice coverage.
Johnston: If I find a pattern, I'll share it so my partner can replicate that pattern somewhere else. We don't split spots or fish, because then we're just competing against each other.
Johnston: Run a bunch of isolated spots where there's one or two fish, hitting upwards of 40 different spots per day. Don't be afraid to stop on new water that looks good to test it out.
Johnston: When you haven't been to a lake before, you're not going in with a preconceived notion of what the fish were doing last time. You can really figure it out and zone in on them real good.

Johnston: Run shallow rock piles starting on the east end of the lake and working west, hitting about 20 isolated piles. Spend the first two days running shallow and adjust deeper only if conditions force it.

Johnston: On the Potomac, I found fish out in the grass, not in the real thick stuff. I was casting to catch them, not flipping. When that failed due to muddy water from rain, I moved to flipping grass on the Greenway Flats.
Johnston: When pre-fishing, very rarely make a cast, because you'd only sting fish you could catch in the tournament. Instead, closely watch sonar looking for the right kind of bottom.

Johnston: Where there's a lot of angling pressure, downsize your line and hooks, and try to get off the beaten path. Look for spots on the spot - anything different like rock and weed mixed in, or bigger pockets in grass. It's something small or isolated where people aren't all over it.

Johnston: Consistency and success on tour comes from being a well-rounded angler. I don't feel like there's a technique at which I cannot excel, which means it's a matter of figuring out what I need to do and then executing to the best of my abilities.
Johnston: Won two Elite events in one season on very different fisheries — one largemouth chasing shallow fish, and the other deep smallmouth. Being able to adapt between the two is key.

Johnston: Run a milk run on Lake Ontario starting at the closest spot and working out to the farthest, then come in. Use Garmin LiveScope to cover water while drifting. Pretty simple - just cover a lot of water over rock structure in 18 to 42 feet.
Johnston: Local knowledge is invaluable. Having a lot of spots on Lake Ontario means you only need two days of pre-fishing when you know the place well.
Johnston: In team tournaments, practice separately and then merge findings for the good of the team. We made a pact to do this from our early teens and it has always worked well.

Johnston: During pre-fishing, cruise around the tournament site examining the lake bottom and looking for irregularities that might represent opportunities. Rarely make a cast during these tours — why risk snagging a fish in practice that you could catch in competition?
Johnston: Practice helps, but research makes the difference. Pool knowledge with partners, develop and test theories about how to do things differently.

Johnston: Don't abandon the plan to run to Lake Ontario because of 8- to 10-foot waves if you've prepared. Even with only about three hours of fishing time per day, the quality of the spot makes it worth it.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, 24 pounds really isn't a good day — anything over 25 is what you need to win. It's a special place.

Johnston: When a main pattern starts dwindling, have a backup plan ready. At St. Johns, flipping pads on the main river when the bed fish pattern faded was probably the best decision I could have made — it produced my biggest fish.

Johnston: Nobody remembers who finishes second in the Classic, so throw big baits and go for the win. Expect big swimbaits, deep-diving crankbaits, Carolina rigs, and football jigs to be the best chances.
Johnston: Always have a backup plan ready. At St. Johns, I had one I hadn't needed to use all week but felt the final day would be the day to deploy it — being prepared to pivot is essential.

Johnston: Managed fish well and made good decisions across four days. When the main bed-fishing pattern dwindled on the final day, shifting to flipping pads on the main river was the best decision — it produced the biggest fish of the day.

Johnston: Always verify your time against official tournament clocks, not just your boat graphs or cell phone. A mismatch cost me 7 pounds of penalties (1 pound per minute late) and dropped me from 7th to 60th, likely costing a shot at Angler of the Year and winning the tournament.
Johnston: If you can get through tough southern events and just have a shot at AOY going into the northern swing, be happy. The St. Lawrence and northern fisheries are where you can make up ground.
Johnston: Save certain previously-located fish for when you need a big bite. Start tournament days with known bed fish or saved difference-makers to build an early cushion, then go fishing.
Johnston: Don't burn too many fish -- get a limit, upgrade a little, but save fish for subsequent days. Play the long game over a multi-day tournament.
Johnston: When team fishing with a partner, practice in separate boats on official practice days to cover more water, then combine information for tournament days.
Johnston: Sharing all practice information with a trusted partner doubles your water coverage. If your partner says there are no fish in an area, you don't have to waste time checking it yourself.

Johnston: When you and your practice partner are essentially the same person when it comes to fishing, you know how they'll fish and where they'll go. That eliminates redundancy and maximizes efficiency.
Johnston: Leave resting schools of fall smallmouth and return later to catch more. Don't burn through a school in one pass.

Johnston: Prefers fishing Lake Ontario over the St. Lawrence River current. Fish on the lake are more congregated in certain little areas. Even in rough weather with 8-10 foot waves, committed to the lake because he knows what lives out there.

Johnston: In rough weather on big water, limit your spots to a few known groups of fish and rotate them. When you only have 3 hours to fish due to long runs and rough conditions, pick your best 3-4 spots and rotate.

Johnston: Early practice in flat calm sunny conditions lets you see bedding fish that later arrivals can't. If wind stains the water later, competitors won't see the fish and fewer boats will fish the area.

Johnston: On well-known fisheries, limit practice time to about a day and a half to avoid poachers. If guys see boats in the middle of nowhere, they'll go check it. Rely on experience and knowledge of where fish will be.

Johnston: Learned from a previous mistake of spending too much time in the river. Committed to fishing Lake Ontario exclusively even with only about 3 hours of fishing time per day due to long runs and rough conditions.

Johnston: First angler to earn a Bassmaster Century Club Belt with all smallmouth (100-5 in 2022). Repeated it with 102 lbs in 2024. Key is committing to known big-fish areas even when conditions are tough.
Johnston: When extreme fishing pressure pushes smallmouth off typical spots, hunt individual bass in areas with less pressure on flats with isolated structure.
Johnston: This article is identical content to johnston-jumps-into-day-1-lead-at-the-st-lawrence-river.txt, republished on thefishingwire.com. See that extraction for all opinions and knowledge.
Johnston: When your main bed fish pattern dwindles on the final day, make the adjustment to flip pads on the main river. Managing fish well and making good decisions is key.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, 24 pounds really isn't a good day and anything over 25 is what you need to win. It's a special place.
Johnston: Hit as many spots as possible until you run into the bigger fish. Quality bites often come late in the day after working through many locations.
Johnston: Having a brother/partner who also competes lets you cover twice as much water in practice. It's like having 6 days on the water with no false information.
Johnston: When one angler is in contention to win, the other should give up their best water. No sense in both trying to do okay if one has a chance to win.
Johnston: Try to be good at a lot of things rather than great at one thing and not good with the others. We can catch them deep or shallow, largemouth or smallmouth.

Johnston: The key to tournament success in Canada was figuring out smallmouth on inland lakes that had been dominated by largemouth. When zebra mussels and gobies began impacting lakes, thinking outside the box and expanding beyond traditional flipping mats, docks and milfoil was critical.
Johnston: This article is identical content to meet-elites-cory-johnston.txt, published under a different URL. See that extraction for all opinions and knowledge.
Johnston: This is a news roundup article summarizing his 2025 St. Lawrence Open win (74-15 three-day total). No new technique details beyond what is covered in the primary win article.

Johnston: This article is behind a B.A.S.S. membership paywall. The accessible text only states Johnston gives a bait breakdown of the key lure used while battling waves of Lake Ontario en route to his second Elite Series title of 2024. No technique details are accessible.

Johnston: This article is behind a B.A.S.S. membership paywall. The accessible text only states it covers baits used by the Top 10 pros at the 2024 MAXAM Tire Bassmaster Elite at St. Johns River. No technique details are accessible.

KVD: For Knoxville Classic (Fort Loudoun/Tellico, mid-March), the key baits are: Red Eye Shad, flatside crankbait (Hardliner 35), jerkbait, and Thunder Cricket. Power fishing will dominate. Most winning fish will come from Loudoun with largemouth. Fish are staging to spawn but cold winter has them behind schedule. Forward-facing sonar will add 1-2 extra fish per day but won't change the core pattern.

KVD: At Knoxville, running to Tellico costs ~3 hours of fishing time per day. In a 3-day event needing 17-18lbs/day to win, you can't afford that. The winner will likely stay on Loudoun and maximize casts. Time management wins classics.

KVD: At Toledo Bend, started each morning shallow on the shad spawn for 1-1.5 hours, then transitioned offshore to deep crank. The biggest fish all came deep cranking with 6XD, 8XD, 10XD. Didn't get a ton of bites but when he got bit, it was a giant.
KVD: With a big lead, 30+ boats follow you. Spectators fish your spots after you leave. 'It's challenging when they're fishing your stuff.' One of the toughest aspects of being the frontrunner.

KVD: 'You've got to fish the conditions and dictate your lure choice, your patterns, your style, your speed to those conditions — and as they change, you want to adjust.' This applies to any tournament or fishing day.

KVD: 'Fishing in the moment — apply your strengths and trust instincts as you see different water, weather, pressure, and wildlife changes, then adapt instantly. Start with basics of where bass should be on seasonal migration, then apply search baits to find schools and choose the right lure to make them bite. Don't rely on past reports or history — conditions change overnight.'

KVD: 'First thing at a new lake: observe water clarity and check water temperature. Combine that with where bass should be according to seasonal patterns. The angler who figures out where bass are and how to catch them fastest wins. I'm not afraid to totally change technique, lure, and approach when conditions have changed.'

KVD: 'I want minimum 10 sites on a tournament day, prefer 100. Bass quit biting then start again 1-2 hours later — current replenishes a spot within an hour, weather changes need 2-3 hours. If a spot produces 5-6 bass in 45 min, rotate back every 1-2 hours. Only 1-2 bass in 30-45 min? Move on. Also factor in size — 10 fish at 1.5lbs, leave for a spot producing 3-4lb fish.'

KVD: 'When I pinpoint a good school of big bass, I have to maximize the number from that school. Use a search bait to pinpoint them, determine if they're actively feeding and what lures to fish, then slow down my presentation to catch as many as possible before they quit biting or move. My top finesse baits: tube, shaky head, drop shot, Ned rig, wacky-rigged worm, and Neko rig.'

KVD: 'After scouring a productive spot with power approaches, drop down to lighter line and finesse to pick up the scraps. Cleaning up with finesse has become a regular part of my game — these techniques revitalize a worn-out fishing spot.'

KVD: 'I fish faster and make more casts than other anglers — but my lure presentation isn't always fast. This enables me to find bass quicker and locate productive areas faster, spending more time in the most productive zones. I analyze weather and water conditions every minute on the lake and base fishing on current seasonal patterns, not where I caught fish last year.'

KVD: 'Power-finesse: cover water fast but use finesse baits to trigger inactive fish. Adjust weight on shaky head, drop shot, or tube to control fall rate. Tube and drop shot are the most versatile power-finesse baits. Won Smith Mountain Lake Elite with this — 61lbs over 4 days in a bed fishing event.'

KVD: 'One of the things when I'm preparing for a big tournament is I really want to follow the weather — not only current conditions but what's been happening leading up to the event.' When lakes are off-limits for a month+, weather tracking is how you prepare. Water clarity, temp trends, rain history all matter.

KVD: Day 1 of practice = get a big picture view of the whole lake. Day 2 = dial in and refine. Find areas and patterns that fit your strengths. Once you find the best area, everything else is just a secondary pattern.

KVD: 'You got to find areas and patterns that fit your strengths.' As a power fisherman, KVD looks for areas where power techniques (crankbaits, bladed jigs) can produce quality bites, not just numbers.

KVD: 'Power finesse: use heavier weights (3/8 to 1/2oz) on finesse rigs to fish them faster. Heavier sinker keeps line tight, puts more action in lure, and triggers reactionary strikes. Start heavy and work down. On tubes, I'll throw 1/2 to 3/4oz heads — sinks like a bowling ball but still produces erratic fall. Cover a ton of water.'

KVD: 'I power fish year-round, even in cold water. Bass are far more active than I once believed. Strike zone is smaller but they'll crush a power bait worked in their immediate area. Power fishing is the foundation of everything I do — it helps locate fish and dial in patterns more efficiently than finesse.'

KVD: 'Hybrid approach: use jerkbait to cover water and find fish, then go back through with drop shot, tube, or jig to pick off more. The power bait located the school and gave confidence to slow down. You can no longer fish one way in most tournaments and win — use both power and finesse to maximize fish off each spot.'

KVD: 'When choosing any lure for any situation, account for: water clarity, depth, time of year, water temp, type of cover, current, rising or falling water, weather conditions, and potential mood of the fish. Example: you might throw a frog over grass mats, but after a cold front with high pressure and 10-degree temp drop, punch through instead. Every lure is a tool — use the right one for the conditions.'

KVD: 'I power fish year-round, even in cold water. Bass are far more active in cold water than I once believed. The strike zone is smaller but they'll crush a power bait worked in their immediate area. Power fishing is the foundation of everything I do — it helps locate fish and dial in patterns.'

KVD: '2008 Classic at Hartwell — brutal cold Day 1, hammered them cranking a weighted-to-suspend Series 5 over 15-25ft with stop-and-start retrieve. Day 2, post-frontal sunny skies killed that bite. Ran to the upper end with stained water and fished flat-sided cranks shallower — caught 12lbs in 2 hours.'

KVD: 'I don't cast finesse baits until I know I'm in a good area holding bass. Three triggers to go finesse: (1) really clear water where I can see bass, (2) when bass quit biting power baits to pick up a few more, (3) when a bass blows up on or short-strikes my power bait — I know exactly where it is and cast a finesse worm right there. Go-to finesse: drop shot with Half Shell or finesse worm, then Ocho stick worm, then small jig.'

Palaniuk: 'Dont hesitate to fish a spot just because another competitor fished it before you. Another smallmouth will start holding in that same site before long. Decisions on how long you fish and how quickly or slowly you move from spot to spot make the difference.'

Palaniuk: 'Set up a milk run starting with 50 different rocks/boulders in the area with the most baitfish. Over the tournament, learn which rocks and boulders consistently hold good-sized smallmouths.'
Palaniuk: 'During the last days of practice I just idle around dropping GPS pins in areas rather than catching fish. I use mapping clarity and LakeMaster charts to dial in the pattern.'

Palaniuk: 'Even on lakes I have fished many times, I keep an open mind and try not to fish the same places where I caught bass in past years. I keep tabs on water and weather conditions each day to determine where bass might be.'

Palaniuk: 'On a 170,000-acre lake with only three practice days, do as much online research as possible studying past tournament results from the same time period to determine where the most productive water will be'
Palaniuk: 'On large lakes you must eliminate a lot of water quickly — use past tournament results to guide your practice days to the most productive sections, then verify under current conditions'

Palaniuk: 'With 15 minutes left and no keeper, don't rush — you still need to fish your lure thoroughly. Switch to power baits (jerkbait, crankbait, spinnerbait) to cover water efficiently but not frantically'
Palaniuk: 'If you haven't had bites in a while, run to productive water closer to the boat ramp — being close to the ramp lets you push the clock longer and fish until the last possible moment'

Palaniuk: 'Use a balancing beam to compare your smallest livewell fish against each new catch — whichever side goes down stays, the other goes back. Knowing your exact weight is a real secret to winning'

Palaniuk: 'I dedicated myself to catching five big smallmouths each day rather than splitting time between largemouth and smallmouth. Consistent 19-20 pound bags beat the gamble of one big largemouth bite per day'

Palaniuk: 'When 3 other competitors were already on his spot, he adapted by using forward-facing sonar to gauge fish interest and differentiated his approach with oversized crankbaits and jerkbaits rather than downsizing to jig and minnow like the others.'

Palaniuk: 'At Champlain, one big-bait bite on the way to weigh-in gave me confidence to start on largemouths the next day. I saw a weather system coming in, so I tied on multiple swimbaits and glide baits.'

Palaniuk: 'Used LakeMaster charts to drive to spots -- based on contours could idle over a spot, see fish, and catch them almost every single stop. Spent the third practice day just driving and marking spots rather than fishing.'

Palaniuk: 'Took time to do research online, looking at areas that continually came up in past tournament results no matter what time of year. That way he knew a population of fish lived in that area, then could see how they were using those places that week.'

Palaniuk: 'Strategy was to figure out how to get one big bite and then fill out a limit. That one big bite was a difference-maker for guys cutting checks. The guy who'd win would get 2-3 of those in a day vs just getting 1.'

Palaniuk: Won Bull Shoals by capitalizing on a specific spot — flooded roadbed and bridge pilings in West Sugarloaf Creek — catching 20-pound bags the first two days and 19 pounds on Day Three. He was the 99th boat to launch on Day One, so he took advantage of a spot others passed over.
Robertson: After a tough start to a season, commit to fishing your strengths rather than adjusting to what others are doing. Fish for five bites and don't care if you blank -- fish how you want.
Robertson: Sometimes the bite doesn't develop until afternoon. All five keepers caught after 12:50 PM on Lake Murray in April -- don't give up when the morning is slow.
Robertson: If you're going to fail, fail doing what you do well. When a shallow approach produces only 11 pounds, switch to offshore fishing with big baits if that's your strength.

Robertson: If you're an offshore fisherman, commit to your style even on fisheries known for shallow patterns. When a shallow approach fails, go back to what you do well — if you're going to die, die doing what you do well.
Robertson: On ledge fisheries, rotate through multiple spots rather than hammering one area. This keeps the fish from getting too pressured and ensures fresh opportunities throughout the day.
Robertson: Don't give up on a tournament day even with a rough morning. All five keepers in a 25-pound bag can come after 12:50 PM when you stop overthinking and just go fishing.
Robertson: After a tough start to a season, make the decision to fish more to your strengths rather than trying to adapt to every situation. Leaning into what you do best produces better results.
Robertson: Being self-employed (pressure cleaning business) provides the schedule flexibility needed to fish multi-day tournaments and pursue a professional fishing career through the Opens path.

Robertson: After catching your limit quickly on an offshore spot, stay on the spot to protect it for the rest of the day rather than risking someone else finding it. This was a key strategic move that preserved fish for the next day.
Robertson: On lakes with big fish like Santee Cooper, commit to fishing for big bites rather than playing it safe for consistency. Fish to win rather than just survive.

Robertson: Don't avoid community holes on ledge fisheries. Fish some community spots and let other anglers fish ahead of you, but also have your own spots where you catch the bulk of your weight undisturbed.
Robertson: If you're going to fail, fail doing what you do well. Commit to your strengths — for Robertson, that's fishing offshore.
Robertson: Don't panic if the morning is slow — all five of his weigh-in fish were caught after 12:50 PM after boat problems forced him to restart his day.
Robertson: Don't avoid community holes on ledge fisheries — let other anglers fish through first, then work behind them. But save your best weight-producing spots where you won't have competition.
Robertson: Staying in a 600-yard area all three days can pay off if the fish are the right caliber — commit when practice shows big bites and wait for weather to stabilize post-cold-front.
Robertson: Won the Cherokee Lake Open with a drop shot and topwater combination during post-tropical-storm, post-frontal fall conditions.
Robertson: Be willing to completely scrap your game plan midday if the pattern dies. Set a deadline (e.g., noon) and if it's not producing, switch to something in your wheelhouse.

Wheeler: Respool with fresh line at midday on the last practice day, not the night before the tournament; this gives you the afternoon to adjust to the feel of new line and fix any issues like too much or too little line on a spinning reel before competition.

Wheeler: Invests a full week or more before arriving at a lake for practice; prepares roughly 35 pre-rigged Duckett rods with fresh Sufix line and baits matched to the seasonal pattern, spending 1-2 full days just on rod prep.

Wheeler: Never stops thinking about the next best technique; challenges himself on pitching, flipping, boat setup, and preparation; the offseason doesn't begin until November with boat prep and recon trips to next year's venues.

Wheeler: Goal on day 1 of qualifying is to catch as much as possible and build a cushion; if the cut weight is about half his day-1 total, he uses day 2 to practice and find new areas for later rounds.

Wheeler: Pre-practice information becomes irrelevant during long events; having a cushion over the cut and a chance to look around on the second qualifying day provides the most up-to-date information possible.

Wheeler: Won the 2012 Forrest Wood Cup at Lanier going shallow while the field fished deep for spotted bass; saw cruising largemouth around bluegill beds in pockets and committed to the shallow pattern with almost no competition.
Wheeler: Saved mid-lake pockets for the final day of competition; the strategy of not burning through all areas early preserved fresh spots when he needed them most.
Wheeler: Set a specific goal weight of 12 lbs on the final day knowing his competitor would need over 18 to beat him; worked methodically toward that target rather than fishing recklessly.
Wheeler: Played a 1-2 punch of smallmouth on calm days using forward-facing sonar and largemouth in reeds; caught 50+ lbs of smallmouth day 1 then pivoted to largemouth to lock up AOY.
Wheeler: Left his best largemouth stretch on the final day to let it rest, fished other areas, then returned to find the fish had reloaded and repositioned; caught 6 fish in 10 bites to win.

Wheeler: Catch fish fast enough early each day to use the remaining periods for graphing and finding new schools — that's the way to win offshore TN River events. Finding fresh fish others haven't located is the biggest advantage
Wheeler: With a big lead, stop setting the hook on 4.5-lb fish on community holes — those fish might be the ones that win the tournament for you on the final day

Wheeler: Don't dial things in during practice. Half the tournaments I've won I figured it out during the tournament. Practice really hard daylight to dark but don't fish as much — just bebop around. Events are 8-9 days long so you have to adapt

Wheeler: When a community area has boats everywhere and other anglers are cannibalizing each other, ignore it entirely — spend time in different zones with fewer bass where you can manage your own fish with less pressure

Wheeler: Had 7-8 spots he refused to hit before Championship Day — knew he couldn't win the tournament if he burned them early. Made gametime decisions with 25 minutes left, running 15 minutes across the lake to a fresh brushpile for the winning fish

Wheeler: A big key because MLF events are so long is not dialing it in during practice. Don't commit to any one thing. Have pre-spawners, spawners, fish around brush, fish suspended around bait, fish around bluff walls — lots of options
Wheeler: It's amazing how much pressure changes fish behavior — even when a competitor fishes an area before you, the fish act weird and won't bite. They know what's up

Wheeler: When leading by 30 lbs with the 2nd-place angler nearby, it's better to share the area and split the fish (he catches one, I catch one, I stay up) rather than leave for a distant spot and risk the comeback

Wheeler: Without FFS, angles are everything on an offshore school. Changing the angle on a group of fish several times let him catch 2-3 more each time. Old-school triangulating on the bank with a heading sensor to position within 1 ft of target

Wheeler: On the TN River, when you leave a spot someone might follow you and catch your fish. If you can get away with not showing your hand to 20-30 spectator boats, it makes sense not to fish your best stuff — very strategic, not emotion-based

Wheeler: On pressured fisheries like St. Lawrence, look deeper (10-15 ft) for spawners rather than the obvious 4-8 ft zone. Beds are harder to see — look for a couple cleared-off white shells on darker bottom. Troll for miles on big flats to find congregated areas with enough current
Wheeler: Prefers 2 sneaky schools no one else has found that he can manage himself over community schools you can never get on. Key is to find fish where you're the only person fishing them

Wheeler: Practice in the wind when other competitors won't — found 25 fresh spawners in 2.5 hours that likely wouldn't be pressured by Championship Day because no one else was looking in those windy areas

Wheeler: With 2/3 of the day spent without FFS, expect many more traditional patterns in play — buzzbait, cranking, conventional bass fishing. Two-thirds of the day goes back to how he's fished his whole life, and he's excited about that

Wheeler: Don't waste FFS time panning and looking for fish. Don't stop a working pattern (like cranking rocky banks) just because you haven't used your FFS period yet. Assessing when to use it will be part of practice strategy

Wheeler: On heavily pressured community holes, a bait with an action bass haven't seen produces dramatically better results — the Freeloader's unique shaking action caught fish from Guntersville's most pressured spots
Wheeler: Target smaller isolated schools rather than mega-schools that attract most anglers — the bigger fish often live in the smaller isolated schools with less pressure
Wheeler: Look for small subtle spots off the beaten path that other anglers miss, found during low-water periods; save your best spot for when you need it most in competition
Wheeler: First cast to each area with a swimbait to catch easy grouped-up fish, then switch to drop shot to pick off individuals — this two-pronged approach maximizes catches on offshore structure

Wheeler: Graph offshore spots during practice even if they have zero or few fish - bass may show up during the tournament. By the end of a Guntersville event, he had 26 schools (double what he found in practice) because fresh fish moved in that hadn't been pressured.

Wheeler: In qualifying rounds, fish hard for just the first period and a half to secure advancement, then spend remaining time keeping tabs on other spots and discovering new ones as the tournament progresses.

Wheeler: Find the most obscure offshore areas that might only have a few fish but you can manage yourself. Popular areas where multiple anglers graph will cannibalize each other - you can't win in those places.

Wheeler: Keeps an array of spinning rods rigged with jigheads from 1/8 to 5/8 oz. The right weight for the right scenario is about efficiency - heavier gets to fast-moving fish, lighter gets better action for inactive singles.

Wheeler: Spent as much time as possible during qualifying rounds in practice mode, scouting new areas and tracking where fish were moving. When wind blew and concentrated bait, groups of 100 bass schooled up in windblown offshore areas.
Yamamoto pro Yelas: 'A lot of times I will make my first few casts with the Cowboy to catch the most aggressive fish and then if the bite slows down, I'll downsize my lure'
Yamamoto: In 2011, broke the FLW single day weight record for Lake Champlain with 24lb 4oz stringer skipping docks with a Senko tail-weighted with a No. 7 finishing screw

Yamamoto: Won the US Open of Bass Fishing in 1995 at Lake Mead. Competed on Bassmaster and FLW tours after moving east in the late nineties, found major success on both tours and internationally in Japan and Europe
Yamamoto: 'I like open practices where you can go whenever you can. If somebody can afford to spend a month practicing, more power to them. That is how people get good'

seasonal-pattern.insight: Hackney: 'Fish will move under mats of grass or cattails in the afternoon after the sun has warmed them up. You can duck hunt in the morning and bass fish in the afternoon.'
Hackney: 'Big females will group up under mats during the winter-to-prespawn transition. That's when I catch some of the biggest fish of the year.'

Hackney: 'There are two phases for prespawn: pre-prespawn right out of winter and then prespawn. These two phases are your best bet at catching a personal best. Fish are stressed from spawning prep - males guard a huge territory and are really aggressive.'

Hackney: 'In the early spawn, nothing beats a piece of plastic. Use baits without a lot of action like a tube or Rodent - the only action is from your rod tip. Be patient with repetitive casts to bedding bass.'
Hackney: 'Once water hits the 70s, fish pay more attention to baits with appendages that swim. Use something like a Game Hawg or Rage Bug. Fish grab it even when reeling back in.'

Hackney: 'Quite a few fish live shallow even during the hottest time of year. They hold in shade of boat docks, overhanging trees, lily pads, and grass. Hot weather triggers grass growth that creates shade, oxygen, and ambush points.'

Hackney: 'Threadfin shad spawn happens just as bass come off beds. Gizzard shad spawn is later. There is never a gap - it is the perfect storm. Largemouth are run down from the spawn and the shad spawn is a really easy time to gorge.'

Hackney: 'Threadfins spawn all night. The only time to cash in is within the first 45 minutes of daylight or the last 45 minutes of the day. In that span, 20-25 pounds of bass is possible on the right lake.'
Hackney: 'At Stick Marsh with no underwater vegetation, fish were spawning around willow trees and cane on the shoreline - they just wanted to have something to spawn around.'
Hackney: 'As bass conclude spawning, big females seek thick heavy grass to rest, recuperate, and feed up on bluegills and crawfish.'

Hackney: 'Before each tournament I completely reorganize my boat for the expected pattern. Going from Florida spawning bass to Tennessee winter pattern means getting rid of all the heavy tackle and packing smaller tackle.'

Hackney: 'Think of fall as a faster pre-spawn in reverse. Fish migrate back to the exact same cover they use pre-spawn. They get on boat docks, cypress trees, heavy cover just outside spawning areas. They are aggressive and feeding up for winter.'

Hackney: 'As grass dies and milfoil goes away in fall, it confines bluegill to whatever cover is left. When bluegill concentrate on remaining cover, big largemouth go there too. Everybody has their feed bag on.'

Hackney: 'After bass finish spawning their primary instinct is to feed. Females shift focus to recovery and nourishment quickly after spawning — sometimes over multiple beds. Males remain shallow longer guarding fry'

Hackney: 'Bass are opportunistic feeders. During post-spawn they target dominant forage — on many lakes bluegill begin spawning right after bass, becoming the primary food source. On other lakes it may be shad, blueback herring, or crawfish'

Hackney: 'Below 55 degrees I switch from a Rage Craw to a KVD plastic chunk. I still want a big profile but not a lot of action — everything bass eat in winter is cold-blooded and moves slower, so a crawfish does not root around and shoot everywhere in the 40s'
Hackney: 'In December-January I target blown-in mats around cypress trees, dead-end canals, oilfield canals. Big females sit in those dead ends because a month later they will be spawning'

Johnston: You have to be able to judge the size of bedding fish without catching them to make sure you're going to get the right ones. Spend time gathering waypoints on spawning areas before tournament day.

Johnston: Everything from your technique to the fish themselves pretty much slows down when it gets cold in that 40-degree range. You have to adapt and change tactics — target the middle to bottom of the water column and work lures with a sluggish style.
Johnston: Where we grew up, it was all largemouth fishing — the smallmouth weren't as relevant. We grew up punching mats, flipping reeds and that kind of stuff.

Johnston: In fall, smallmouth group tightly on specific spots and feed heavily before winter. Early fall fish hold on offshore rock piles and summer haunts. Mid to late fall they push shallow to island tips, sand flats, and points with isolated cover.
Johnston: At the St. Johns River, always look for spawning bass. You can always catch one or two big spawners and fill out a big limit.
Johnston: Playing the ledge-fishing game on southern reservoirs is very much like graphing structure in search of smallmouth on northern lakes.
Johnston: Any time we've been to the St. Johns, my mind goes toward trying to find spawning bass. You can always catch one or two big spawners and fill out a big limit.

Johnston: After ice-out, smallmouth come into back bays and shallows to feed. Then big fish disappear - they go back over deep water and suspend to get ready for the spawn. Once the spawn gets close, big females pull back in.
Johnston: Smallmouth start making beds when water reaches the mid-50s. The optimal spawning temperature is between 55 and 65 degrees.
Johnston: A lot of the biggest smallmouth spawn first, but there's no set rule. You can definitely find a few big ones kicking around late into the spawn.
Johnston: Smallmouth like to spawn on something - rock, gravel, a boulder, patch of weeds, a stick, cracks of slate. Sometimes they'll be on a gravel flat in the middle of nowhere.

Johnston: Unlike largemouth, female smallmouth generally hang out shallow after they spawn because the water is warmer. They feed up post-spawn. Some stay shallow for a month or two; some go straight to deep water depending on the fishery.

KVD: 'Fall reservoirs — shad move shallow to warmest water in creek arms. Go as far up a creek as your boat allows. If it has a defined channel, bass use it for ambush points. Pods of bait near surface plus birds indicate a really good afternoon bite as sun warms water. Fall drawdown exposes cover you can't see in summer.'

KVD: 'Natural lakes in fall — start on outside weed edge of biggest flat on main lake. Crank finds irregularities in the weedline, then pitch finesse baits to those spots. Inside turns are where you find concentrations — the closer to the bank that turn is, the better. Bass can stay shallow in milfoil/coontail even in the 40-degree range.'

KVD: Pre-spawn one-two punch strategy: shallow crankbait or jerkbait on deeper edges outside pockets, then Thunder Cricket and Hardliner back in the bays where fish are moving to spawn. The combo covers both staging and migrating fish.

KVD: Bass in cold water really use their lateral line to hunt. Even in dirty water they can feel the pressure waves of a flatside bait. This is why flatsided crankbaits are 'magical' in cold water — the flat sides displace a lot of water.

KVD: 'Cold water + no wind + high bright skies = shrunk strike zone, maybe only 1-2 feet. Don't fish fast or randomly. Slow down, be thorough, put the bait right in front of them. Pick small areas apart with repeated precise casts instead of running the lake.'

KVD: 'In cold water (40s°F), bass are where the food is — locked into winter areas. Look for depressions, little ditches, anywhere slightly deeper with warm water around it. Graph for bluegill concentrations — if you see wall-to-wall bluegill, the bass will be there.'
KVD: 'Bass were locked into one corner of the hole where wind was pushing in. Anything that creates current, a funnel, or an edge leading onto a flat — that's where fish stack in cold water.'

KVD: 'Rising water — bass push shallow and spread out. Best spring days can occur when warming water rises fast. Key on runoff areas: backs of creeks, culverts, residential yards, city street drainage. Water temp can vary 5 degrees between pockets — find the warmest runoff. Don't overlook ultra-shallow water — bass will put noses on seawalls and banks in inches of water.'

KVD: 'Cold + rising + muddy is the toughest spring combo. Bass in winter pattern are less likely to move to prespawn areas. Fish a flat-sided crankbait that displaces a lot of water, close to bottom or near objects. Make parallel casts to channel swings, steep banks, and riprap. Some fish always live shallow — target them.'
KVD: Stained water compensates for tough conditions like no wind and high bright skies — you can still trigger reaction bites. In clear water under those conditions, it would be much harder.

KVD: '45 degree water at Sam Rayburn Texas is a lot different than 45 degree water in Michigan.' Shallower natural lakes and lowland reservoirs with vegetation — fish don't go deep regardless of cold, they stay shallow. Highland reservoirs (Table Rock, Dale Hollow) — fish live deeper, suspend, roam following shad. Approach must match lake type.

KVD: When water temps drop into the 30s, threadfin shad start dying off and bass gorge. Can be the best fishing you've ever had. Signs: watch for birds (seagulls, herons) hovering over the middle of the lake on frosty mornings — birds tell you where the bait is, and that's where bass will be.

KVD: 'The common misnomer is that bass slow down and don't bite in cold water. That is absolutely not true.' Growing up in Michigan, fish live under ice all winter and still feed actively. Cold water fishing is nothing to be intimidated by — it can be some of the best fishing of the season.

KVD: On lowland reservoirs with grass (Guntersville, Toledo Bend, Okeechobee), bass don't leave shallow areas even in extreme cold. They stage in slightly deeper areas adjacent to spawning flats — drains, ditches, depressions, holes in the grass. Old dead vegetation holds heat and draws bass. Fish caught in 1-2ft of water in upper 30s temps.

KVD: 'Fall transition signals: cooling nights drop water temps below summer highs, baitfish begin transitioning. Watch for pods schooling near surface, shad flipping on top, dive-bombing gulls, multiple herons on points or in creek backs.
