On natural lakes, bluegills transition from bottom to surface — bass may be on top of grass or suspended around deep humps, feeding UP not on bottom. Switch from bottom-bouncing to mid-range presentations.'

KVD: 'Punching mats is especially effective during cold fronts (bass bury deeper under mats for stable conditions) and heat of summer (mats provide cooler shade). Aggressive fish hit on the drop. If bites are sluggish, pause and jig longer. This technique is known for producing trophy bass.'

KVD: 'Shad spawn happens around 72 degrees, after bass/crappie spawn — Mother Nature helping them recover by providing a feast. Spikes on full moon, lasts 2-4 weeks. Starts in one area of lake — keep moving until you see activity. Most happens at night; first hour of daylight is crucial. Shady areas or cloud cover extend it. Look near deep-water access: rocks, riprap, laydowns, flooded trees, grass edges, floating docks.'

KVD: 'Cold water bass bites get tougher below 55°F. Morning — wind blows into shallow flats, bass edge up. Afternoon — fish move to outside bends and deeper transition breaks. Target these transition zones for reaction strikes.'

KVD: 'Summer topwater triggers: bluegill spawn and mayfly hatches. Popper has tremendous drawing power during bluegill spawn — great hook-up ratio even with smallmouth. Use larger popper in wind, dirtier water, or around big bass for more commotion. Fish topwater all day long in summer — produces some of the biggest bass of the year.'

KVD: During pre-spawn, when you find one fish on a spot, they're usually grouped. Got a bite every single cast on one little spot on a point. They stage together before moving up to spawn. Mark the spot and work it thoroughly.

KVD: Clear water on grass lakes = fish can see the bait from farther away (benefit) but can also be spooky (challenge). Fish may be timid — bites are subtle, just bumping the bait. Slow down and keep working the area.

KVD: 'I look for a 10-degree drop from average summer water temps to trigger the fall pattern. On Ozarks reservoirs that's when temps drop from low-mid 80s to mid 70s. Length of day is the most important determinant — shorter days trigger change.'
KVD: 'You don't have to be out at dawn in fall. The best shad activity often comes in the afternoon — especially later into fall, the shad seem more active when it warms up a bit.'

KVD: 'Bass don't go extremely deep immediately after spawning. They stop at secondary points, road beds, shallow ledges, main lake points in the 8-10ft range. Many anglers pull out deep divers too soon — a medium-diving crankbait in Sexy Shad matches the shad-gorging post-spawn phase better.'

KVD: 'Not every quality bass follows bait to the back of a creek in fall — there's still plenty of good fish eating bait on flat, shallow points along the main lake too. Target shallow flat main lake secondary points.'

KVD: Mild winters at Guntersville keep water in low 50s with grass still green and water exceptionally clear. Cold winters push shad into creek centers with wolf packs of bass around them. Both scenarios produce but require completely different approaches.

KVD: 'Daylight length and time of year affect spawning movements as much as anything, but conditions must be right too. Snowmelt runoff is colder AND clearer than normal spring rain — moves slower and drops water temps faster. In prespawn, target pockets and bays near creek channels or deep water access. Northerly flats with dark/muck bottoms, rock, dying vegetation are degrees warmer.'

KVD: Neko-rigged creature baits are 'deadly springtime bait around the spawn.' In clear water, most fish bite on the initial fall — if they're on a bed, the bait lands next to them and they eat it immediately. Works for both largemouth and smallmouth/spots.

KVD: As water warms in pre-spawn, fish scatter out into big spawning bays. Try to follow ditches but sometimes you just have to fan cast randomly. Wind or clouds help position fish better and make them more aggressive.

KVD: 'Don't assume you must finesse spawning bass. At the 2011 Classic, 4 of top 7 finishers power fished spinnerbaits and crankbaits on the same 150-yard spawning flat. Key conditions: hard bottom flat with stumps + grass, 10-degree water temp rise in a week, full moon. Day 1 spinnerbait (fish aggressive), Day 2 crankbait (fish on beds, needed triggering). Dirty water helped crankbait effectiveness.'

KVD: 'Winter shallow power fishing is often best on calm bluebird days with little traffic. While these conditions are typically unfavorable, they allow fish to move shallow and bask in the sun. Target stained water and shallow rock — both hold heat.'

KVD: 'Hot water dog days: bait and bass suspend off structure over deep points and humps. Clear water makes them selective and easily spooked — odds of catching several from one spot are minimal. Hit a lot of spots, catch a few, move on. Go back to the same area 3-4 times during the day while rotating with other spots. Give edgy bass time to forget what just happened.'

KVD: 'Cold-water bass respond to weather changes like warmer seasons. Strike zone largest on cloudy, rainy frontal days with wind — move lures faster. High pressure/no wind — slow down to bottom-bouncing baits but still fish faster and erratically. 2008 Classic at Hartwell: hammered them cranking Day 1 in brutal cold, switched to stained water with flat-sided cranks Day 2 when front passed.'

KVD: 'There is no magic temperature that triggers the hot bite — it's about transition and rising temperatures, not a set number. The moon has greater influence over spawning than temperature. Stable weather + full moon + water in the 50s = very active bass, some spawning. Don't wait for the 60s. Northern bass spawn at lower temperatures than southern fish.'

KVD: 'Peak prespawn: concentrate on fish staging near spawning areas — they're the most aggressive. Cover water with fast baits searching for the sweet spots. I've hit one little stretch where a school of hungry big ones pulled up and caught four or five in a row. Dragging soft plastics, I might have missed those opportunities.'

KVD: 'Winter bass move shallower than most realize — if you can reach them with crankbait, jerkbait, or slow-rolled spinnerbait in 10ft or less, power baits are first option. Fish avoid expansive flats unless warming trend pushes them to gravel points. Rocky banks and riprap are ideal — hold crawfish (primary winter forage). Big pods of shad = jerkbait or spinnerbait. No baitfish = crankbait rooting on bottom.'
KVD: Texas pre-spawn isn't just about Rayburn and Toledo Bend. Many other Texas lakes have big fish. Pre-spawn in Texas = follow ditches and drains leading to spawning flats.

KVD: 'Conditions over calendar. High, off-colored water sets the stage for shallow bass even in summer. More structure in shallows means more bass. The bluegill spawn is a coast-to-coast pattern — bass target spawning panfish for big meals.'

KVD: 'Cold-water bass respond to weather changes like in warmer seasons. Strike zone is largest on cloudy, rainy frontal days with wind — move lures faster. On high-pressure/no-wind days, slow down to bottom-bouncing baits but still fish them faster and erratically.'

KVD: 'My Seasonal Guide helps me make educated guesses about where bass are most likely at any given time. It quickly eliminates unproductive water. The concept: at any given time, the majority of bass in a lake will be on certain key types of structure.'

KVD: 'Water clarity is a major determinant of bass depth in winter. In clear lakes, bass can go 50-60 feet deep. In murky lakes, they'll be much shallower. Bass often suspend in clear water rather than hold tight to cover.'

KVD: 'Once the water hits 50°F, bass are definitely prespawn. They follow migration routes — ditches, depth contours, tapering points, fallen trees on sloping banks — toward shallow water. Northern banks are warmer and very important now.'

KVD: 'I pick up my fishing pace considerably as the water hits around 52°F. I may run down a bank quickly throwing a lipless crankbait or spinnerbait, then pause to pitch a jig at submerged logs or brushpiles.'

KVD: 'Many bass move onto spawning beds around the new or full moon, or when the sun comes out after prolonged cloudy weather. Not all spawn at once — biggest bass are first. Personally, I stay with prespawn bass as long as I can because they're more aggressive and weigh more.'

KVD: 'Postspawn bass hang around their spawning grounds near cover sticking up vertically — dock pilings, submerged bushes, standing tree trunks. Keep your presentation up high in the water column, not bumping bottom.'

Palaniuk: 'In the fall, plan to fish everything in your tackle box. Bass are moving between deep and shallow water. Not all bass will be on the same cover or depth at the same time. You must be willing to adapt and adjust to various water and wind conditions.'

Palaniuk: 'In fall, cooling temps trigger bass to feed heavily for winter and the spawn. Bass prefer to eat a bigger bait so they dont have to chase as much small bait. A big swimbait moves slowly through the water column so bass can track it easier. Thats why I start with a giant swimbait in October.'
Palaniuk: 'In fall, bigger bass set up to ambush bait on isolated cover in creek backs and adjacent to channel swings with big flats. Match jig color to water color.'

Palaniuk: 'Late August and early September are the worst times for most anglers. Bass need cooler, well-oxygenated water — find it in three places: water running into the lake (upper river/creeks), deep cover (brush/rock piles), and thick vegetation providing shade'

Palaniuk: 'When water is still really low and clearer than normal in the spring, fish want to go shallow but there is nothing for them to get on -- they are just sitting and waiting on those transitions, the first breaks. Shallow crankbaits become the top producer.'

Palaniuk: 'When the water hits the upper 40s and low 50s, fish start getting more active and willing to eat a big bait. Northern strain bass get much more aggressive sooner in the year since they are used to living in a colder climate.'

Palaniuk: 'Fall is when the difference between primary forage up north vs down south is most noticeable. The baitfish start to make their fall transitions, with bass soon to follow or already set up and waiting.'

Palaniuk: 'If you can still find vegetation in winter, get there as fast as possible. On places like Okeechobee, big prespawn females will be under those mats. Use a heavy-duty reel with bigger spool, handles, and gears to winch big ones out of cover.'

Palaniuk: 'In fall I operate under the theory that you downsize your baits. Baitfish born in spring have grown but are still smaller. These smaller baitfish school in large numbers making them easy pickings for bass. Downsizing to match the hatch ensures more consistent bites and might be your best shot at a true giant.'
Robertson: During prespawn at Chickamauga, fish an offshore pattern with a lipless crankbait in grass, then go headhunting with a big swimbait for kicker bites.
Robertson: After the spawn on Kentucky Lake, target points at the mouths of bays where fish move to after spawning. High water can bust up schools, requiring an open mind.

Robertson: Spring crankbait progression -- 1) Prespawn: slow-roll lipless on flats near channel banks in 1-6 feet; 2) Postspawn: big-bodied squarebill on tapering points near spawning flats in under 10 feet; 3) Late postspawn: deep diver following ditches and drainages 10-15 feet out to summer structure.
Robertson: Postspawners don't immediately go deep. They hold on small tapering points and bars about 150 yards from spawning flats for about three weeks. New fish keep replenishing the spot.

Robertson: During prespawn, a cold snap that slows bass from moving shallow is beneficial for offshore prespawn fishing. If fish are still wanting to spawn, cold weather keeps them staged offshore longer.

Robertson: The prespawn period is one of Robertson's strongest seasonal patterns, complementing his offshore fishing identity. He considers himself a 'heavyweight angler' who prefers big-fish fisheries.

Robertson: The shad spawn is a morning-only pattern. Spend 1-1.5 hours on it maximum, then transition to other patterns. Having limited shad-spawn locations means you must have a backup plan for the rest of the day.
Robertson: A cold snap during prespawn can work in your favor by keeping fish offshore and slowing the spawn, extending the prespawn feeding window.
Robertson: During prespawn, a cold snap slowing fish from spawning keeps them in the offshore staging pattern longer, which benefits offshore anglers.

Robertson: On Lake Okeechobee during prespawn, quantity comes first and quality follows — Day 1 produced 60 fish of lesser quality, Day 2 produced 40, and Day 3 produced fewer but bigger fish as spawning females moved in.
Wheeler: Learned to pattern bass at age 16 - the ability to go to an entirely new lake and figure it out rather than just fishing spots or chasing distant memories.

Wheeler: On flat fisheries bass stay in the shallow zone a long time before spawning, but on highland reservoirs they may stage in 6-10 feet before moving up; crankbait selection should match the lake type and depth zone.
Wheeler: In late February/early March, bass group up in underwater ditches 30-40 feet deep; targets them with underspin or small swimbait on light fluorocarbon.

Wheeler: In fall, bass follow shad to backs of creeks onto flats, but the biggest misconception is only focusing on creeks; don't overlook main lake flats, flat points on the main lake, and mouths of creeks.

Wheeler: This is the bass rut - they're heading to the bank and you need to intercept them in transit; stresses interpreting water temperature and clarity, reading the bank (different rock types), and figuring out the depth zone bass are hanging.

Wheeler: Multiple patterns usually happen simultaneously on complex reservoirs; anglers tend to rush the bass in spring - just because it's warm out doesn't mean they're all on the bank; there's a significant lag between air and water temperature so don't overlook deeper main lake bass.
Wheeler: At Santee Cooper, pre-spawn keys were 10 ft and shallower, hard places and rock (hard bottom, rock, gravel), plus brush and stumps. Better if you had both rock and wood but didn't need both

Wheeler: Post-spawn offshore on Table Rock, the 3 key elements were brushpiles, isolated cedar trees, and bluff end points/big long main-lake points where schools related to current and hard bottom — mixed species (smallmouth, largemouth, spotted bass) depending on lake section

Wheeler: Northern smallmouth fisheries like Champlain only have a few months to do their deal — 1 week up north is like a month down south. Water temps went from 68 to 75 degrees during the event and fish transitioned dramatically from 12-16 ft to 18-30 ft
Wheeler: Any highland reservoir is more of a FFS lake because bass sit on bait a lot and suspend. Dale Hollow fish ranged from 1 ft to 20-30 ft deep
Wheeler: Fish trying to move offshore can get held up by the shad spawn in 4-8 ft of water. In years with floods and cold fronts, the post-spawn transition can run behind schedule

Wheeler: Smallmouth spawn starts in creeks and flat areas with no current, then they transition toward the main river/lake — same as largemouth. Look for spawners in 10-15 ft rather than the obvious 4-8 ft most people target

Wheeler: On highland reservoirs with clean water, fall bass may be in 15-30 feet in creek pockets and drains; on lowland reservoirs like Tennessee River lakes, fish are shallower (3-5 feet) focused on gizzard shad requiring bigger baits

Wheeler: The jighead/swimbait combo is the #1 deal in winter on highland reservoirs like South Holston, Cherokee, and Norris — vertical presentation targeting smallmouth in depressions and on rockpiles
Wheeler: Determine the thermocline depth early in summer practice because it gives you a starting point for where fish can live and sets a maximum effective fishing depth

Wheeler: As temps flirt with the 50s, target traditional staging areas close to spawning pockets; deeper staging fish focus more on feeding than spawning activities, making prespawn the most consistent action of the year

Wheeler: In spring, start with a prop bait (X-Rap Prop) in colder pre-spawn water when fish aren't as active, then transition to a frog when water temps hit low 60s and fish move off beds into heavier cover
Wheeler: When you get the first rain in fall and water temps drop, fish reset into winter patterns. Temperature thresholds vary: 40s-30s up north, around 50 degrees in the south.
The surface Senko technique is a year-round pattern — works whenever there are fish up shallow, and according to this angler there are always fish up shallow
Yamamoto: The Senko works all year round but shines spring to fall. In colder months, fish deeper water on long underwater structure such as points with a small weight added
Yamamoto pro Hite: Use Kut Tail Worm on Neko rig post-spawn through summer for more aggressive action in warmer water. Switch to standard Senko on Neko when cold or when subtle action is needed
Hudson: 'Prefer Dinger in post-spawn pitching at finicky recovering bass hanging near the surface'

Card (pro): 'Single Tail Grub works all year but great during pre-spawn for smallmouth. Fish it like a small swimbait, reeling slow enough to just tick rocks. Also reel-reel-reel then let fall on tight line'
Yama Craw will work wonders during spawn with claws floating right up in bedding bass's face

structure-reading.insight: Hackney: 'During cold fronts, look for old canals, dead-end channels and northern banks sheltered from cold northern winds that retain heat from the sun.'

Hackney: 'Flooded shallow areas where grass and bushes filter the water are usually your best water. The difference in color might be slight to us, but a bass living in it sees that slight difference as huge.'

Hackney: 'If fishing a river or main lake with good current, the only thing I'm looking for is the current break. Bass have to get out of that moving water to survive. That one factor makes up for anything else.'

Hackney: 'Matted grass provides excellent surrounding and overhead protection and ambush points while heating up quicker than the surroundings. These factors make them resilient to cold fronts. Rich soils support food webs including crawfish and sunfish.'

Hackney: 'A mat can be made of anything — vegetation, pine needles, leaves piled by wind and current, even discarded lumber or trash. Bass will hold under any mat if it breaks the sunlight and there's a foot of water under it.'

Hackney: 'The most consistent spot to find a large bass is where no one else is fishing. If you are more concerned about how you will get a bass out of structure than about a bass biting, that is where you need to fish.'

Hackney: 'One of the biggest bass I ever caught was from the center of a giant cypress stump that looked solid. I threw a worm right into the hole in the top and caught a 7.5-pounder. No one else was dumb enough to fish there.'

Hackney: 'It was all about creek channels or drains. Where the drain turns, the break is steep and bass relate to that steep break. Bushes were everywhere but the deal was the bottom contour, not the cover.'

Hackney: 'In the South, shad spawn on hard bottoms, matted vegetation, cypress trees, and boat docks. Old Styrofoam marina docks are the best. On the Tennessee River, the better shad spawn is on shell beds and shallow bars in 5-6 feet of water.'
Hackney: 'Look for something different in the mat - a yellow algae mat growing in green milfoil will isolate the fish. Fish are never random, NEVER.'
Hackney: 'An abnormality in vegetation like mixed species can be key. A small area of cabbage growing outside deep milfoil or coontail growing inside will concentrate fish.'

Hackney: 'One big log sticking up on a flat - never run past that in the fall. That is about the keyest place you can find. Isolated cover on flats holds the bigger, quality fish, not the little schoolers chasing shad.'
Johnston: Fish regular rock/weed transition areas, anywhere from 12 feet deep right up to about a foot of water.

Johnston: Key on isolated rock piles off the shore in 4 feet or less. Anything that provides a transition - rock pile, patch of sand, clump of weeds - a shallow bass can key on is fair game in summer.
Johnston: If shallow water has a chalky tint to it, head back out to the lake. Water color changes indicate you need to move to cleaner water.

Johnston: Targeted an 80-yard stretch of bottom in 33 feet loaded with big smallmouth. Had about three hours of fishing time per day due to the long run, but committed to the spot and made it worth it.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, catch them shallow on sand flats, rockpiles, weed edges all the way to 35-40 feet on little high spots, rocks and deeper weed edges. There's not one specific thing — hit as many spots as you can until you run into them.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, fish a mix of rock, sand, and weed in 15 feet or less. Hit as many spots as you can and hope the right ones bite.
Johnston: The key to fall smallmouth is finding something different -- a lone weed clump, isolated rock, or small patch of gravel that holds bait and draws the school.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, fish a lot of stuff other guys wouldn't fish -- little small subtle things. Spots vary with no similarities: some rocks, some breaks, some little grass patches, depths from 15 to 42 feet.
Johnston: Catch St. Lawrence smallmouth across a wide variety of habitats in a single day: shallow sand flats, rockpiles, weed edges, and deep high spots, rocks, and deeper weed edges in 35-40 feet.

Johnston: The most important structure for smallmouth in rivers is breaks - ledges, humps, anything where the depth drops off. These aren't always adjacent to shoreline; they could be a ledge next to the main channel, a hump rising from the channel, or an underwater point extending from shore.

Johnston: Set up a milk run of big boulders for smallmouth. Each boulder typically holds one big smallmouth and maybe one small one. Stop on each, do a few short drifts, and move on. If you catch a big fish, move right away since a boulder only holds one big fish.

Johnston: Boulders used to replenish more quickly with resident gobies keeping smallmouth stationary, but now it takes longer. You generally can't return to boulders during a tournament, so you need to mark as many as possible.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, smallmouth prefer flat bottoms. Look for transitions from hard bottom to soft, or rock to sand, rather than gravel-to-rock transitions. These transitions are easy to spot on Garmin electronics via color change on the bottom.

Johnston: Drop-offs are the key structure on the St. Lawrence because bass can push bait up against them. Sonar won't mark these tight-to-bottom fish, making an underwater camera essential for confirming fish presence.

KVD: In cold water, target transition areas where bass will stage before moving to spawning bays. Channel swing ends, flat edges, steeper banks transitioning to flatter areas, where different rock types come together. Chunk rock banks, bluffs, and riprap around bridges are prime for the mid-range crankbait.

KVD: Focus on large flats and spawning flats — where fish are coming and going. Look for deeper depressions, edges, drops, centers of drains within the flat. These subtle depth changes concentrate pre-spawn fish. At places like Toledo Bend and Sam Rayburn, fish deeper drains/ditches leading into spawning bays with 3/4oz.

KVD: 'Grassy lakes have 3 depth zones: Shallow flats (0-4ft) — topwater, spinnerbaits, weedless plastics for sight-feeding fish. Weed edges (4-8ft) — the sweet spot, bass ambush from the edge, use soft plastics, jigs, creature baits. Deeper transitions (8ft+) — where grass tops out, fish suspend, use deep cranks, jigging spoons, blade baits. Cast along features, not straight across flats.'

KVD: 'Deep bream beds are the coral reefs of freshwater — bass attractors all summer. Bream bed in 10-17ft well into August/September. Use side imaging to find them around deep docks, points, shoals, humps, flats, river bars. Full/new moons are peak activity but beds attract bass between spawns too. Mark with waypoints — bream use same beds year after year.'

KVD: 'Cold water jerkbait targets: drop-offs, edges, weedlines, bridge corners, riprap, bluff ends. Fish parallel to these edges and make multiple casts to high-percentage spots like laydown tips or grass points.'

KVD: 'Target the greenest mats — they're untreated, most fertile, and hold the most bass. Avoid brown dying mats that lack ecosystem. Noise isn't a concern when punching — the loud impact of your weight often attracts fish rather than spooking them. Mats are naturally noisy environments.'

KVD: 'Summer natural lakes: weeds are where you find the majority of largemouth. Look for prime structure or irregular features — dropoff close to dense vegetation, creek channel, or where covers combine (thick weeds against a dock, two weed types mixing). Bluegill colors on all lures in summer, especially up north where bluegills are primary forage.'

KVD: The crankbait is a search tool for reading the grass line — you can feel where grass comes out farther, where it's taller, subtle points and cuts. Fish relate to irregularities in the grass edge. On natural lakes where structure is subtle, the crankbait finds what your eyes can't.
Palaniuk: 'I like to fish offshore and target shell bars or brushpiles with a 10-inch worm in summer.'

Palaniuk: 'Big fish that haven't made the full transition to main summertime structure are singles or doubles on brushpiles or standing timber with a bottom composition change (mud to hard), on straight banks leading from spawning areas to points — a midway transition point'

Palaniuk: 'The same principles applied from 4ft to 30ft: it had to have a good straight steeper edge close to a main channel — main-lake fish near the main river channel, grass fish near a creek channel'

Palaniuk: 'Fish an 8-mile stretch moving between rock piles and boulders — build confidence on spots that reload with big smallmouths daily. I had 50 spots and was sure to catch a 4-pounder on 20 of them'

Palaniuk: 'Look for a mixture of different grass types in mats — different species grow and die at different rates creating better canopy. Always seek out the cleanest, greenest grass which produces more oxygen and is crucial in summer'
Palaniuk: 'Matted vegetation provides warmth for bass early and late in the year and helps them cool down in summer — no other type of cover can do that'

Palaniuk: 'In reeds and cattails, focus on places where they create little debris mats — grass blown in, or where some have folded over. Also look for tighter clumps and areas where contour lines show quick access to deeper water'

Palaniuk: 'In lily pads, look for tight clumps and pay attention to where you flip so the line doesn't get in the cracks between pad stems — braid slices through pads but stems and stalks hang you up'

Palaniuk: 'In hydrilla and milfoil, use contour lines to find where bottom tightens from deep to shallow or makes a point in the grass. A harder edge congregates fish better than sparse grass — find subtle points or ditches in the grass'

Palaniuk: 'The Percentage Triangle: the wide base is the spawning flats where contour lines are widest, the point is where contours are steepest. It's all based off the spawn — the larger the triangle, the more fish it holds, and fish use contour lines like highways'

Palaniuk: 'Pull out a paper map and reduce the playing field by identifying percentage triangles. When you find where the fish are in one triangle, run to other triangles and focus on the same section — it eliminates home lake advantage'

Palaniuk: 'Florida is the hardest place to apply the Percentage Triangle because it lacks distinct contour changes. Grand Lake is one of the best — I could look at a map and tell you where I was going to catch a bass'
Palaniuk: 'Bass hold just off the current behind structure — trees in the water, rock piles coming up off the bottom, or other cover that breaks the current'

Palaniuk: 'Find good, healthy, green vegetation that looks vibrant — it produces the most oxygen and shade for bass and baitfish. The thickest part of the grass that makes a canopy over the water is where bass hold'

Palaniuk: 'Find the perfect mixture of a particular level of visibility mixed with a particular type of rock or rock transitions. Focus on areas where contours change from tight to wide, with something unique -- a laydown, small rockpile, or bottom composition change that concentrates the strike zone.'

Palaniuk: 'As fish progress toward the spawn, you want things to be as stable as possible. On large waters, wind might be the biggest factor. On rivers, it might be water levels and current. Find the places that are most stable and least affected by conditions.'

Palaniuk: 'Fish isolated rockpiles, boulders and grass patches anywhere from 8 to 22 feet. It just has to be the right-looking stuff -- depth didn't really seem to matter. Had a milk run of places to hit and cycle through, and at some point during the day would run into a group of fish actively feeding.'

Palaniuk: 'The spot had a riprap bank with a steep drop, then a trough that came up onto a hard bottom ridge, tapered back off into another trough. The trough made a horseshoe. A 40-yard section where the fish wanted to live, with a 25-foot hole the trough dumps into.'

Palaniuk: 'Read the entire vegetation management plan document to figure out where grass was and wasn't. Found only a 50-yard section of submerged grass. Because it was the only submerged stuff within miles, it held bait and fish. It wasn't far off the main lake so bigger fish could move in and out easily.'

Palaniuk: 'I looked for the largest spawning areas near deep water and the best smallmouth habitat. The bass were on points and steep drops near those points in 20 to 35 feet. They were relating to sand and gravel seams on some spots and boulders on others.'

Robertson: Fish exclusively offshore by idling every inch of a lake to find hard-bottom spots in 9-10 feet with no visible structure. Use electronics to see big bass stacked on these areas -- spots as small as 40x50 yards can hold massive numbers.

Robertson: On fisheries known for shallow grass fishing like the Harris Chain, look for offshore hard-bottom spots 150 yards from shore in 9-10 feet of water. Idle over the area and use electronics to spot big bass — a 40x50 yard hard spot can hold fish that replenish daily.

Robertson: Winning offshore areas can be essentially featureless — no defined contour, just a hard bottom with 'sort of a point but not really.' The distinguishing feature is simply hard bottom 150 yards from shore.
Robertson: When fishing eelgrass flats, the big bites come from areas where the grass is not dense but more isolated. Concentrate on those isolated clump areas rather than the thick grass mats.
Robertson: When idling offshore, look for hard-bottom spots that hold big bass even with no visible structure. A 40x50 yard hard spot in 9-10 feet can hold large concentrations of quality fish.

Robertson: A lifetime of experience on Tennessee River ledge fisheries (Kentucky Lake, Pickwick) is invaluable — knowing how fish set up on ledges from years of fishing them gives you an edge others can't replicate.
Wheeler: In fall, fish school up in ditches, on little flats in 2-4 feet, and especially around grass; on grassless lakes, key on shallow ditches with stumps that let bass ambush shad.
Wheeler: Targets bluff ends and transitional banks leading into pockets; looks for distinct areas with shale rock and a creek or river channel nearby.

Wheeler: Community areas are big giant points and lead-out places where ditches and creek channels come out — the highway bass use to exit spawning areas. Sneakier spots are along straight ledges where bass set up due to current swing or a little hard spot, and these change year to year
Wheeler: One of his best spots was a cedar tree in the middle of a creek channel — in 40 ft of water but came up to 12-15 ft with fish suspended in the top of it

Wheeler: Key structure elements were hard gravel places, small isolated grass patches, boulders, and some fish on nothing — just suspended and nomadic. There wasn't one pattern, you had to keep things in rotation

Wheeler: On highland reservoirs like Cherokee, smallmouth live around holes/depressions (20 ft around the side) — if rock is around them the smallies live there. If a rock vein goes into the middle, they live on those veins. The holes harbor the bait with schools in the 20-30 ft range at the bottom

Wheeler: Big fish spots are big giant flats in 22-29 ft with tiny isolated rockpiles — a boulder in the middle of a rocky flat, or a hard-bottom spot. Only good for 1-2 fish and time-consuming to find, but that's what separates you

Wheeler: Smallmouth want to be related to and adjacent to gravelly bottom (flat or chunk rock) near some depth change — even a 1 ft change matters. One spot was just hard bottom from 40 to 37 ft with isolated rock spots coming up half a foot more
Wheeler: Look for structure like drop-offs, humps, ledges and cover like brush, rockpiles, and timber that intersect the thermocline depth
Wheeler: Transitional banks and creek arms are prime prespawn locations; use electronics to scan deeper pockets and channel swings near spawning grounds for actively feeding fish
Wheeler: Best offshore spots included areas adjacent to grass lines, actual holes in the grass, and hard-bottomed shell beds in 8-35 feet of water.

Wheeler: Flat main-lake drains near island clusters are bass magnets - the islands funnel baitfish and the flatter topography serves as spawning territory. Current flow through island cut-throughs makes them prime prespawn/postspawn staging areas.

Wheeler: On deep clear reservoirs, look for sharp edges near bait concentrations - points going into creek arms, where creek channels run into bluffs, channel turns. The bait congregates there. Bass follow food and don't move much in winter because deep areas are more stable.

Wheeler: In shallow winter situations, fish push into backs of pockets and creek arms to depths as little as 2 feet, but deeper water (8-12 feet) is always close by. Target ditches, drains, secondary points, and grass lines.
Yamamoto: Fish the Chicken Rig around bridge pilings, barge tieups, docks, riprap, and other hard cover. Used it to finish 5th at Rayovac FLW Series on Upper Mississippi
Wacky rig causes Senko to fall sideways, gliding under pier platforms or foam flotation of docks where bass lurk in shadows. Especially effective when bass suspend under docks

color-selection.insight: Hackney: 'I'll use any color as long as it's black. It's almost impossible to get pure black anymore — it all has flake in it — so I go with the darkest looking stuff I can find. If black isn't working, try Summer Craw or Bama Craw.'

Hackney: 'It's a mistake to only use black and blue for mats. I frequently pick colors like I do for any other lure — match the hatch and pay attention to water color. The more natural a lure looks the more likely a fish will eat it.'

Hackney: 'I limit myself to just two colors — black/blue for dark water or dark conditions, green pumpkin for clearer water and bright days. These two catch fish from Florida to New York. I may switch during the day — black/blue early morning, green pumpkin when the sun gets up.'
Hackney: 'Bluegill and bream are the bait bass feed on most at this time of year. A bream-colored topwater looks like a bream jumping, skipping, and moving on the surface.'
Hackney: 'Black-and-blue and green-pumpkin are my two favorite colors for punching grass. These two colors you can fish in all 50 states and be very successful.'
Hackney: 'I pull a bait through the water and if it looks real, you will get bit. I do not like to fish anything that stands out. I think I am more apt to get bit on something that looks natural.'
Hackney: 'Three favorite swim jig colors are green pumpkin, black and blue, and white. Choose color depending on water cover and what the dominant forage is.'
Johnston: For fall smallmouth, use natural shad or baitfish colors for reaction baits, and green pumpkin, brown, or goby for bottom presentations.
Johnston: For bed fishing, uses green pumpkin on everything. Fish bite quicker with a more natural bait. You don't need to see the bait yourself.

KVD: Crawfish patterns dominate cold water. Clear water = natural/ghost crawfish (greens and browns). Stained = fire craw, brighter patterns like spring craw. If you see shad activity, shad patterns work too. Look at what color crawfish the fish are eating — is it green, brown, red, or purple? Match it.

KVD: Crawfish patterns dominate pre-spawn — reds in any water clarity (Rayburn Red, Delta Red, Fire Craw). Clear water = natural craw patterns (dark brown, baby carp, orange brim, bluegill). Stained/dirty = chartreuse, chartreuse sexy shad, brighter colors. Shad-focused systems (Tennessee River) = chrome sexy shad, gold sexy shad, straight sexy shad.
KVD: 'Spring = crawfish patterns with red. Summer = shad and bluegill patterns. Fall = shad patterns in creek backs. When the 1.5 hits grass, clear hooks with a hard snap of the rod tip.'

KVD: 'In stained water on tough days, Gold Sexy Shad is a go-to. High visibility, reflects light as sun gets higher, mimics shad without being too subtle. Stained water creates opportunity even with bluebird skies — bass rely on vibration and contrast for reaction strikes.'
KVD: 'Color is crucial for jerkbaits — it's a very visual technique and fish get a really good look as the bait sits still between twitches. You need the right blend of visibility and naturalness.'

KVD: 'Early spring lipless colors: stick to natural patterns mimicking what bass already feed on — bluegill and crayfish in clear water. Stained or murky water, switch to brighter colors — chartreuse, fluorescent reds, anything that stands out more.'

KVD: 'Smallmouth crankbait color: go to one extreme or the other. Start super bright — Chartreuse Sexy Shad, Powder Blue Back Chartreuse. Smallmouth are sight feeders, want a bait they can see from far off. On slick calm sunny days or when they won't commit to bright, switch to natural — Green Gizzard Shad, bluegill, or perch pattern.'

KVD: Match the forage and water clarity. Spring in Texas = red (crawfish coming out, dark red). Stained water = brighter reds like fire craw or chili craw. Gold has great flash in stained water (gold sexy shad). General rule: 'Pick something that's natural and something that's visible.' Match bluegill, crawfish, or shad based on the system.

KVD: 'Sexy Shad color system: Sexy Shad for slightly stained to clear (first choice most situations). Chartreuse Sexy Shad for dirtier water. Sexy Blue Back Herring for herring-forage lakes. Sexy Ghost Minnow for real clear/bright days when you don't want a loud profile (great for topwaters). Chrome Sexy Shad for max flash (lipless, jerkbaits). Sexy Sunfish for bluegill-dominant lakes.'

KVD: 'Cold water crankbait colors: crawfish patterns are top choice. Red/fire craw in stained water, natural greens/browns in clear water, shad patterns when baitfish are present. Pro tip: if you catch a bass with a crawfish in its mouth, note the color and match it.'

KVD: 'Summer Sexy Shad vs Sexy Shad: if creeks are clearer than normal from lack of rain or zebra mussels, Summer Sexy Shad is the better choice. It's a subtler version for clear water fall conditions.'

KVD: Bright sunny + clear water = natural colors (bluegill, spot remover). Dirty water = brightest or darkest colors (chartreuse/white or black/blue) to maximize silhouette. Shad patterns (white/chartreuse) are day-in day-out staples. Spring around grass in Texas = red crawfish patterns. Match the forage and water clarity.

Palaniuk: 'Red is the first color in the light spectrum to disappear. Bass and bait in the spring tend to be real milky or void of color. If you're looking for something muted, red, the first color to disappear, looks the most that way.'

Palaniuk: 'For southern regions the key player is the shad — I lean towards colors with more flash, silver or white hues with a touch of color. For northern regions, fish follow perch on outside weedlines and transitions to deep water, so I go more towards greenish hues, often with vertical bars to imitate perch.'

Palaniuk: 'Start with 4-5 baseline colors based on conditions and experience. If the baseline isn't working, bracket — go more natural/transparent one direction, more opaque/bright the other. Identify the strongest response, make that your new baseline, and bracket again. It may take 6-7 color switches to dial in.'

Palaniuk: 'My baseline jerkbait colors are Mat Shad, Ito Wakasagi, GP Pro Blue, HT Ito Tennessee Shad, and Table Rock SP. Rather than copying my colors, adopt the bracketing system for your specific waters.'

Palaniuk: 'A baitfish stuck in the gullet of a bass provides valuable information. Bait color and size are key factors when choosing jerkbaits. Mat shad works well for largemouth; in clear northern waters, half-tone translucent colors like pro blue and HT Tennessee shad are good starting points.'
Robertson: Use shad-type colors for lipless crankbaits on lakes without vegetation -- not the reds and oranges that work in grass.
Wheeler: ChatterBait color system is simple - black/blue for stained water, green pumpkin for slightly clearer conditions and bluegill waters, shad patterns for clear water and shad-based fisheries.
Prochnow (Berkley): Some colorants harden the plastic while others do not — Berkley adjusts the plastic formula by colorant to ensure consistent action, density, and texture across colors
Yamamoto: 'Salt-and-pepper is probably a sleeper color — a forgotten color, but I can take it any place in the world and fish will bite it'

Adding accent color to Senko's tail can turn lookers into biters. Chartreuse mimics bream fin tips. Red and orange also work. Rubbing the tail vigorously lightens the plastic as a field-expedient color change

Rubbing the tail of a Senko releases more salt and subtly changes the color — an on-the-fly alteration when fish have seen enough of one color. Chartreuse tail dip gets most carries; orange works where bluegill abound
Yamamoto: 'One of the best colors that is probably a sleeper is salt-and-pepper. I think it is a forgotten color, but I can take that color any place in the world and fish will bite it'

fish-behavior.insight: Hackney: 'Bass in real muddy water move shallow, about as deep as the light penetrates. They don't like pitch black but also don't like being near the surface. They stay close to the bottom but not on it, right at the point where the light disappears.'
Hackney: 'When the water's a little clearer they relate to cover — around it. When it's real muddy they're right on it, almost touching sometimes. Even more true in strong current.'
Hackney: 'Mats offer shade, an ambush point and a safe place from predators. The only time bass won't use a mat is when they're actually on the bed — they like sunlight then.'

Hackney: 'Fish feed more on crawfish up to the spawn, then switch to shad and bluegill diet. Shad spawns and bluegill spawns occur after the bass spawn, so bass switch because of all that fresh food right in their faces.'
Hackney: 'As the days become shorter and there has been cool weather, bass are very aggressive. I think black is a color that is easily seen by the bass in cooler weather.'
Hackney: 'Especially in the South, females like to bunch up after spawning. When you find one female, you find a group of them. Use topwater around those groups as females seem to like it more.'

Hackney: 'When water gets real high, fish do not go shallow - they go straight up. They sit where they were at normal pool and suspend. Every fish I caught was suspended 1-4 feet under the surface in 8-11 feet of water.'
Hackney: 'When the water was falling, they were not as aggressive. When it was creeping up, they were aggressive.'
Hackney: 'Anywhere I caught bass, I had to get perch bites. If I fished a stretch of grass and I did not get any perch or bluegill bites, there would not be any bass there.'
Hackney: 'Fish in mats are school oriented, not isolated. Once you get a bite, it is time to slow down.'
Hackney: 'Shad hang along the outside edge of a mat but never go inside it. Bluegill, sunfish, shiners, and crawfish will go inside. Bass in mats are short-range ambush predators, not bait chasers.'

Hackney: 'Birds of a feather flock together. Bigger fish stick together because they are on bigger bait. If you find 3-pounders, a 5-pounder could be nearby. A group of 1-pounders - consider a 3-pounder a gift.'
Hackney: 'I will never target smallmouth with a jig. Smallmouth get a soft plastic better than a jig - they are a weird fish.'
Hackney: 'Big fish relate to the perimeter, a creek channel, and hold on an isolated piece of grass on a flat. Little fish run up shallow while big fish satellite around the area.'
Hackney: 'In summer fish swim around in the abyss on nothing, but in fall as water temps drop they get back on cover - brush piles, boat docks, grass edges. Now we can pinpoint and target them.'
Hackney: 'Largemouth bass do not think. They are simple prehistoric creatures. They eat, rest, and have sex once a year. They do not hide behind rocks because they are afraid.'

Hackney: 'In Zone 1 you are dealing with resident fish — they just move in and out of close proximity from summer to fall and winter. As it cools off, fish move into places with less current and less wind, like dead ends'
Hackney: 'When the water is dirty or muddy, bass will suspend more. They don't like to be on the bottom when it is real dark because they can't see. In the end they are sight feeders'

Hackney: 'In cold water under something thick with sun shining, bass are much more likely to be up high under the mat or heavy overhead cover. I have actually seen them touching the underside of a mat — they grab whatever you pitch the second it breaks through the canopy'

Hackney: 'Bass rarely move very far in a short period of time. A far more likely explanation than movement is they simply shut down — ate their fill the day before or environmental factors turned them negative'

Hackney: 'If the water is cold and warms quickly, bass often move vertically into the upper foot or two of the water column, especially in heavily stained or muddy water. SONAR won't find them and you can fish a long time without a bite'

Hackney: 'I fished a tree top three days in a row knowing a 3.5 pounder was in it. First two days no bite. Third day she nearly ripped the rod out of my hand — same jig, nothing different. Sometimes they go negative for reasons we don't understand'
Johnston: You don't get a ton of largemouth on northern smallmouth lakes, but when you do, they're big ones. Those largemouth catches are crucial to tournament success.
Johnston: There are so many smallmouths in St. Clair that you can catch them anywhere, even in shallow pencil reeds where you'd expect largemouth. A 4-pound smallmouth in reeds is not unusual.
Johnston: You can find smallmouth on rock, you can find them on sand. It doesn't really seem to matter as long as the bottom's not covered in moss or weeds.

Johnston: Pressured St. Lawrence smallmouth are extremely sensitive. Catch one and they shut down. Time and distance are the best remedy - make a big run away from the spot between catches. It's like night and day from even two years ago.
Johnston: Smallmouth groups on sand flats are transient. You could find a group one day but the next day they were gone. Keep covering water until you come across a group of them.
Johnston: For smallmouth bass in cold water, they seem to key in on tube jigs or a swimbait better than jerkbaits. For largemouth, suspending jerkbaits and deep-drop jigs are the go-to.

Johnston: Zebra mussels clearing the water in the St. Lawrence River system led to goby proliferation, which fueled dramatic smallmouth growth. Clearer water also enabled new sight-based angling techniques.
Johnston: Heavy recreational and tournament pressure on the St. Lawrence causes fish to behave differently and not go to where they're typically at.

Johnston: Humps in current are unique structure because fish will be in front of them feeding in the current, or resting in the slack water behind. You can catch them in both states, which makes humps especially productive.

Johnston: River smallmouth sit very tight to bottom in current and won't show up on sonar. Always take the time to drop a bait even if you don't mark fish. They're more stationary now due to goby forage - why move when there's so much food right there?

Palaniuk: 'No two schools are the same, and no two fish in a school are the same. Each one is sitting down there with a little bit different personality. You have to figure out which bait pushes their buttons enough to get them to bite.'

Palaniuk: 'During the late-summer/fall transition, bream and shad hold around and under grass mats. Bigger largemouths are ambush feeders and move under those mats for a safe haven and to locate ambush points.'
Palaniuk: 'Some fish caught off rocks are resident fish, but a larger majority are roaming and following schools of baitfish like perch and alewives.'
Palaniuk: 'Standing-timber fish sit in 18-28ft suspended 5-6ft off the bottom, relating to bottom composition changes even though they aren't on the bottom'

Palaniuk: 'When it slicked off, the bait would roam wherever in the lake. But when the wind blew it pushed bait to the edges of deep flats, and smallmouth would pull up and get on rocks and stuff on those flats.'

Palaniuk: 'On the last day, realized smallmouth were just following bait across 40-foot sand flats. Made the decision to jump downwind to intercept the same group of fish again -- first drop caught almost a 4.5-pounder.'
Palaniuk: 'I often watched a bass on my graph move up to my bait as it was sinking and then followed it to the bottom. Whenever that happened, a strike was almost sure to follow.'

Palaniuk: 'The largest bass prefer cooler water. During late fall, they get more active since the water is cooling and shorter days trigger them to feed more. Big bass are very efficient at eating an easy, large meal. They usually won't waste a lot of energy chasing something down.'

Palaniuk: 'At temperature extremes (below 42F or above 90F), bass metabolism is either too slow or they conserve energy by sitting in shade. An opportunistic feeder will still take one more bite if it's an easy meal — a finesse bait is ideal.'
Palaniuk: 'Spotted bass often group by size, varying from section to section. Recognizing these patterns lets you shift quickly between areas and be selective.'
Robertson: Fish move a lot with rising water -- keep an open mind and don't get stuck on previous spots. Be willing to search for new schools.
Robertson: High water busts up schools of post-spawn bass. Keep an open mind, don't get stuck on previous spots, and be willing to search for new fish.

Robertson: On offshore hard-bottom spots, bass move to the outside edge in bright sun but hold 40 yards closer to shore under cloud cover. When you hit a lull in the action, it often means they've relocated.
Robertson: In fall, smallmouth stack up on boulder fields in 20 feet of water where baitfish are concentrated. The fish can be found within a very small 60-foot area when they're grouped up.
Robertson: Look for bass hunting bait in small feeding groups called 'wolf packs' — these aggressive groups will respond well to topwater presentations in pockets and around scattered rock.
Wheeler: Events on big fisheries won't be won catching big numbers of 1-8 and 1-9 bass; you need mid-grade fish between 4 and 6 pounds to win.
Wheeler: Bass don't all spawn at once; they spawn in waves so some will be in pre-spawn and some will be done, and both times are perfect for crankbaits.
Wheeler: Many bass spawn on the main lake, not just up in creek arms and pockets.
Wheeler: In 14-16 inches of clear water, hooking one bass spooks 4-5 more in the area; they need time to set back up and reposition before you can catch them again; requires long casts.

Wheeler: On TN River lakes, post-spawn fish move to offshore spots in waves — first a few show up, then a few more, then suddenly 100 bass are there 5 days later. If you can be the first one to find them arriving, it can be a lot of fun
Wheeler: When wind picks up, bass move from the top of brush/stumps down to the base of the cover to use it as a current break. When it goes slick calm, they slide back up higher in the cover

Wheeler: When current runs around the clock at high levels on the TN River, fish have no rest period and change positions — they move to secondary places where they can get out of the main channel. Secondary spots become the key

Wheeler: In heavy TN River current, fish don't set up consistently — sometimes behind a stump, sometimes on the front edge of the current. The pattern is never always the same, so the goal is to find as many places as possible
Wheeler: Cherokee smallmouth were feeding up, not down — a dropshot dropped in front of them wouldn't get bit. Had to keep the bait 2 ft above their heads for them to come up off the bottom and eat
Wheeler: The thermocline sets up deeper (25-30 feet) in clear-water reservoirs and shallower (8-10 feet) in murky waters; rivers generally don't have consistent thermoclines due to flowing water
Wheeler: Concentrations of baitfish gather right above the thermocline with bass feeding on them — that's the ideal summer setup
Wheeler: Post-spawn bass push fry into grass mats for protection — target those grass mats with a frog, and also skip under bushes, docks, and overhangs on shallow flats
Wheeler: Reading the mood of offshore bass is essential - sometimes a deep crankbait triggers reaction bites from bigger fish when finesse approaches are dominating.
Wheeler: Has become a bigger fan of scent recently - adding extra Bait Pop Sonar Intensifier gel when fish are inactive and won't commit helps trigger bites.

Wheeler: In winter, bass target the prior year's threadfin shad hatch, the most abundant and smallest forage. The majority of fish target the smaller profile shad, making finesse swimbaits the right match.
Prochnow (Berkley): Bass are more inclined to spit out a hard soft plastic bait faster than a softer one — softness increases how long a bass holds the bait

Yamamoto: 'When the fish hear the splash, they look to see what it is. When they see the worm, they may not grab it right away, but go over and inspect it. When they get a good look at it and it moves again, that's when most strikes occur'

Yamamoto: 'We as anglers need to show the bass something different. When that backwards weighted Kut-Tail falls in front of them, it presents an image they haven't seen before that looks really natural. They have no choice but to strike'

Scott (pro): 'Wacky rigged 5-inch Senko with 1/0 Owner Mosquito hook through egg sack is ideal for heavily pressured smallmouth. Caught 20-pound bag while everyone else throwing tubes and drop shots caught few fish'

Yamamoto: 'Using 4lb test and 1/16-1/32oz jigheads with tiny grubs for crappie, caught 7-pound bass instead. Makes me think we are missing opportunities to catch fish that are there but just won't eat a big bait'
Profile matches what Dr. Keith Jones proved was the preferred size and profile bass wanted in testing. Heavy plastics gaining popularity in US after years of use in Japan on lakes like Biwa

weather-adaptation.insight: Hackney: 'Cold, muddy water is tough but warm, muddy water isn't really that bad. Don't let it get into your head. Think water conditions rather than seasonal pattern.'

Hackney: 'Cold fronts in the fall are not as negatively effective as in the spring. I like those cold front days in fall because the cool water triggers them to feed. I do not like warm Indian summer days in the fall.'
Johnston: I need the sun and the calm. Most people want the wind to blow, but I want it to be really tough on everybody because I figured out a method to catch them when it's really slick.

Johnston: When wind creates waves and stains shallow water, move to sheltered secondary spots that aren't too close to shallow water so they aren't stained. When shallow is blown out, fish a break with a moving bait.
Johnston: On big water, commit to the lake when the wind direction gives you shelter from a point or land feature. Staying sheltered can reduce 8-foot waves to 6-footers, which is fishable.
Johnston: A late cold spring with high water isn't bad for smallmouth fishing - it can actually make it better.
Palaniuk: 'In the fall, weather can be a big factor in where you'll pinpoint bass and how you'll catch them — chilly mornings warming through the day will shift fish locations'

Palaniuk: 'In the spring you want more warm sunshine, maybe even calmer water, so it pushes big bass to docks and laydowns. In August, you might still want sunshine, but a little more wind or current will generally make things better.'
Robertson: On heavily-pressured lakes, wind is the great equalizer -- when the wind blows, the fish bite. Want it to blow hard.
Robertson: A cold snap during prespawn can work in your favor by slowing fish down and keeping them in their prespawn staging areas longer.

Robertson: On heavily pressured lakes, wind is the great equalizer. When the wind blows, the fish bite — especially on a lake with significant fishing pressure. The only thing that equalizes the pressure is the weather.

Robertson: A wind direction change can destroy a deep-water pattern by muddying previously clear water. When visibility drops from 6 feet to 2 feet, bass stop moving through the area and the deep bite dies. Be ready to switch to a shallow backup plan.
Robertson: On heavily pressured Tennessee River lakes, wind is the great equalizer — when the wind blows, the fish bite. Root for bad weather on pressured fisheries.
Robertson: Wind equalizes fishing pressure on Tennessee River lakes — the only thing that overcomes heavy pressure is weather. Root for it to blow hard.
Wheeler: On large open fisheries, wind dictates where you can fish; plans areas for different wind directions and uses calm days to explore the whole fishery.
Wheeler: Wind direction changes on the Great Lakes change the way smallmouth position big time; a shift from practice to tournament conditions can mean never relocating them.

Wheeler: On highland reservoirs, 15-25 mph wind with 40 mph gusts blows bait and nutrients into certain areas. This gets bass to group up more in schools and actually hunt — wind can amplify a pattern you thought was going away

Wheeler: Bass are often most active on wind-blown spots when the food chain is riled up; less pressure, bass are less spooky, and they're often more aggressive, making the inconveniences worth the effort

Wheeler: Wind congregates bait in protected areas like island funnels, which causes bass to school up in massive numbers. A spot with a few small groups can explode to groups of 100 bass after a windy day.

Wilson: 'If it's breezy, the wind will pull your line and the lure so you don't get the slow fall — a split shot crimped above your line will help you stay in contact with the bait and help it fall straighter'

depth-strategy.insight: Hackney: 'Muddy water bass are target oriented. Look for something shallow, out of the current, with cover in it — stump, laydown, rock, or flooded dock. If there's something that filters the water, so much the better.'
Hackney: 'Shad will not spawn deeper than light penetration - it takes light for their eggs to hatch. Same reason bass do not spawn in 20 feet when water is dirty.'
Hackney: 'There were more numbers of fish shallow, but bigger ones were on the deeper edge of that grass.'

Hackney: 'During winter drawdown, larger fish will be on the flat out front holding on a stump, even in 2-3 feet of water, always relating to solid structure like a drop-off, rock pile, piece of wood, or isolated grass.'

Hackney: 'Use your knowledge, your electronics and anything else. Once you find them figure out the details — are they suspended, on the bottom, breaking the surface? Then make an intelligent decision on what bait to throw'

Hackney: 'After you find the bass, figure out whether they are on the bottom or suspended. At Okeechobee in 2 feet of water, smaller bass were on the bottom and bigger ones were suspended — hitting the lure as soon as it hit the water'

Hackney: 'Sometimes different size fish hold at different depths in the same place. If you are catching smaller bass at one depth, try a different depth. There is only a short time in the spring when big bass mix with little bass'
Hackney: 'If fishing shallow, let your bait fall to the bottom and drag or short-hop it. If no bites, pull it up to the surface. After a couple catches you can figure things out'

Johnston: At the Bassmaster Classic on Lake Guntersville, I was getting bites on the deeper edge of the eel grass in practice and committed to it. But when the wind blew and everything got muddy, it hurt the deep bite. Having a shallow backup plan is important.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, my bigger fish (5.5 and 6.75 lbs) came shallow. Hit 12 spots and the last one had them — sometimes the late-day push to new water pays off.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence/Lake Ontario system, fish from 10 feet all the way to 45 feet. Run stuff across the full depth range to find where fish are congregated.
Johnston: Did most of his work on an 80-yard stretch of bottom in 33 feet loaded with big smallmouth. Even in 8-10 foot waves, the deep fish were worth the commitment.
Johnston: Pressured St. Lawrence smallmouth can range from 2 feet to 35 feet deep in the same day. Be willing to fish the full water column.
Johnston: At the St. Lawrence, bigger smallmouth (5.5 to 6.75 lbs) often come from shallow water rather than deep.
Johnston: Prefer deeper transitions for St. Lawrence smallmouth. Fish used to be catchable shallow but now go deeper sooner due to pressure.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, the depth of the break doesn't matter much - it might drop 3 feet, 10 feet, or 20+ feet. It's simply a place where fish can push bait up against the ledge so they don't have to chase it.

KVD: Three depth zones need three crankbaits in cold water. Shallow = flatside (Hardliner). Mid-range 6-12ft = round body wide wobble (Gravel Dog). Deep 12-15ft = smaller tight-action diver (3XD). Each zone demands a different action and profile.

KVD: 'Making sure your crankbait is contacting the bottom in any depth zone you're fishing — that is absolutely paramount.' The deflection off rocks, cover, even gravel makes a big difference in triggering cold water fish to bite.

KVD: In summer on grass lakes, big fish move offshore to the middle of the lake where the bait is. They don't stay near shore when it's hot. Look for scattered hydrilla clumps in the middle of the lake.

KVD: Choosing baits that are efficient for the type of cover in that depth zone is a big part of it. When grass is to the surface, a regular crankbait runs too deep — switch to a wake bait version to stay in the strike zone.

KVD: 'Depth is critical in cold water jerkbait fishing — you want the bait slightly above the fish or directly in front of them. They won't chase far when metabolism is low and they certainly don't go down after a bait.'

KVD: In cold water, you can't just cover water. The strike zone is very small — fish won't swim 10 feet to eat your bait. You have to get it right in front of them and 'knock them on the head.' Slow down and be more thorough.

KVD: In clear deep lakes with shad kills, fish often suspend. The higher they are in the water column, the more active they are. Fish off main lake points, humps, drops, centers of big bays — not near the bank. Alabama rig with spinners (keeps rig up in water column) or minnow/swimbait on spotlight head are best for covering water around suspended fish.

KVD: Bass in super cold water are focused on vibration. They use their lateral line to feed — they can feel the presence of shad, the vibration and water displacement of your bait. That's why flatside crankbaits, lipless crankbaits, and bladed jigs all work in extreme cold: vibration is the common thread.

KVD: 'Pick a bait that runs just a little deeper than the depth of water you're fishing — that particular crankbait is going to be at its best.' It contacts bottom with great action. A bait that runs much deeper than the water overpowers the bottom and loses its triggering qualities.

KVD: 'In 99% of circumstances, I want to make sure whatever depth I'm fishing, the bait is getting to the bottom.' Biggest mistake anglers make is using the wrong style crankbait and not contacting the bottom. That's why depth-specific models exist (10XD, 8XD, 6XD, 5XD, 3XD).

KVD: When fishing the Ned Rig on an edge, the main thing is keeping yourself positioned on the edge where the fish are. Spot Lock (trolling motor GPS anchor) is essential — 'without that spot lock, I would never be able to stay positioned or hold or land one.'

KVD: 'Choose a crankbait that runs slightly deeper than the water — that gives best action and triggering when banging off bottom. If water is 10ft, I want bait running 11ft. Manage with bait selection, line size, and rod angle. Multiple rods rigged with different line sizes — smaller for deeper, bigger for shallower. Hold rod up for shallower over ledge tops, lower for depth increases.'

KVD: 'Cold water cranking revolves around three depth zones: (1) Shallow transitions — flat-sided, tight wobble, no rattle; (2) Mid-range 6-12ft — round body, wide wobble, loud rattle; (3) Deep 12-15ft — smaller profile, tight action. Match the crankbait to the zone.'

KVD: 'Summer bass suspend when temps peak, no current, and thermocline forms. See the thermocline as a consistent band on your graph — fish above it, none below. Schooling activity away from bottom and structure is a giveaway. Clouds of suspended baitfish with big marks around them = active fish. Use Neko or drop shot to drop to marked fish — sometimes let it fall to bottom and they'll chase it down.'

KVD: 'In summer, light penetration is a major factor. Bass hold at the lower end of the light penetration spectrum where they can still see but remain concealed from prey. In extremely hot water, they hold near cover or dropoffs for long periods, then feed in short aggressive bursts.'

KVD: When slow rolling, keep the bait ABOVE the bass's line of sight — you're imitating baitfish, not crawfish. Fish cover or fish over the bass's head. Steep rocky banks, laydowns, grass — the bait should be slowly ticking the cover above where bass are positioned.
Palaniuk: 'Fish were in 20-40 feet but 25-32 feet was where the flats set up and held the best average quality fish. Targeted sand-to-rock transitions with gravelly rock and giant isolated boulders.'
Robertson: Fish the mid-section of Kentucky Lake from Blood River to Highway 68 Bridge targeting post-spawn largemouth on structure.
Robertson: On TVA (Tennessee Valley Authority) lakes in summer, you have no choice but to fish offshore. You come to the TVA in the summer, you better get off the bank.

Robertson: On TVA lakes in summer, you have no choice but to get off the bank. 25+ years of experience on ledge fisheries like Kentucky Lake directly translates to success on similar Tennessee River impoundments like Pickwick.
Robertson: On TVA (Tennessee Valley Authority) lakes in summer, you have no choice but to get off the bank. Fish the offshore ledges — that's where the bass live.

Wheeler: On every TN River lake, if you start at the dam and work up, offshore schools are deeper at the lower end and shallower on the upper end. At Guntersville he had schools from 8 to 35 ft deep, with 15-20 ft most common
Wheeler: At Pickwick with heavy current, fish were in 10-30 ft with no depth consistency — depth depended on the area of the lake and the structure in that area
Wheeler: Locating the thermocline eliminates half the water on any given lake in summer because bass and baitfish position just above it where oxygen and cooler temps overlap

Wheeler: On Tennessee River-type lakes, fish lipless-crankbait depth (10 feet or less) near grass. On clear highland reservoirs (Cherokee, Percy Priest, Lanier), fish 25-45 feet in main river channels, deepwater drains, and sharp edges near bait concentrations.
Yamamoto: Has caught fish on the Senko in cold water from as deep as 30 feet at Beaver Lake
Senko on jighead can be fished from 6 inches to 30-40ft of water. Vary jighead size to control depth presentation

electronics.insight: Johnston: On the St. Lawrence River, sonar doesn't always mark bass on drop-offs because river bass are tight to the bottom. An underwater camera with a trolling fin and extra weight lets you get down to depth and get a stable picture of what's happening.

Johnston: Drop-offs are key spots against which bass can push bait. Use an HD underwater camera with on-screen water temperature and depth to verify fish presence on these spots when sonar can't distinguish bottom-hugging bass.
Johnston: Use Garmin LiveScope to track down deep smallmouth and make the perfect cast. In rough conditions with big waves, efficiency drops because it's harder to use LiveScope accurately.
Johnston: I learned a lot from my brother Chris about being patient, slowing down and being methodical when using electronics out deep.
Johnston: The real key on deep sand flats is Garmin LiveScope — without it you're at a huge disadvantage. You can see 90% of the fish way out and cast to them before they're under the boat.
Johnston: Garmin Perspective Mode is critical for bed fishing in low-visibility conditions. Relied heavily on it along with the Power-Pole Move trolling motor for targeting bed fish.
Johnston: In a lake full of timber, idle a bunch and use LiveScope to play a big part in finding fish. The technology is essential when navigating and fishing around heavy timber cover.
Johnston: Watch bed fish on live sonar to monitor their locations and reactions to your presentation. This lets you know when they're cooperative and when to move on.

Johnston: Use Garmin Perspective Mode when seeing bed fish in low-visibility conditions. This was one of two main tools (along with flipping and drop shot baits) for bed fishing at the St. Johns River.
Johnston: Runs a Garmin 1643 for LiveScope (watching fish eat) and 12-inch EchoMap Ultras for the rest of his electronics needs.
Johnston: Uses Garmin LiveScope Perspective Mode to spot individual bed fish in rippled conditions when you can't see them visually. Cast to them and catch on first attempt.

Johnston: Runs 2 twelve-inch units on console -- left unit for left side imaging, right for right side imaging. Gives a bigger picture so you can see things clearer. On bow, 2 ten-inch Garmin units stacked: front is LiveScope, bottom is mapping and 2D.

Johnston: LiveScope wasn't a huge deal for the St. Lawrence Open win. A lot of spots were found by side imaging in practice, then casting to the structure rather than necessarily seeing individual fish.

Johnston: Sets Garmin LiveScope gain at 60-65 and distance at 75 feet for his comfort zone. As soon as a fish appears on screen, he makes a precise cast landing within 5 feet of the fish using muscle memory for the 75-foot range.

Johnston: Forward-facing sonar is particularly important for picking out subtleties like anomalies in grass lines and spotting individual fish. Found a sunken barrel on a featureless Lake St. Clair flat with fish sitting on it -- never would have found it without forward-facing sonar.

Johnston: Fish pinned to the bottom may elude 2D sonar, but with forward-facing sonar you can spot wiggling fins that betray hidden bass. When you throw your bait to them, you see their tail and fins speed up as they race to your bait.
Johnston: With LiveScope at 75 feet, you don't waste time chasing fish or making 2-3 attempts. Just look where the trolling motor is facing and make the cast. Extremely efficient -- no wasted casts.
Johnston: Use Garmin LiveScope to pick apart structure and look for bigger fish when smallmouth are pressured and not in their typical locations.
Johnston: Rely heavily on Garmin Perspective Mode for locating and targeting bed fish in Florida spring runs.

Johnston: Use an Aqua-Vu underwater camera (HD10i Pro with onscreen water temp and depth) to get a true understanding of bottom structure in current. Attach a weight to stabilize it in the current for a clear picture in clear water.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence River, sonar doesn't always mark bass on drop-offs, which are key spots where bass push bait. River bass sit very tight to bottom. The Aqua-Vu HD10i Pro with on-screen water temp and depth, using the XD Trolling Fin and extra XD weight, gets down to depth and provides a stable picture of what's happening.
Palaniuk: 'I caught my biggest bass (7-12) by spotting a brushpile off to the side of my boat with the Humminbird Mega 360. I held my boat 50 feet away and cast a drop shot to it.'

Palaniuk: 'Make a waypoint on the structure you want to hit, then after finding the right cast angle, make another waypoint for boat position. Also pick shore landmarks in front and behind you to stay on line at a glance.'

Palaniuk: 'I set my Humminbird 360 to scan about 150 feet all the way around the boat because thats a little past the distance I can cast. I constantly see bottom and structure in front, to either side, and behind me to determine where to make my next cast.'

Palaniuk: 'When hunting baitfish in fall, use Mega Side Imaging which looks 200 feet from the boat. I can see baitfish, how bass are positioned relative to the school, and which direction the bass are following. I can tell which end is the head and tail to know which end to present the bait to.'

Palaniuk: 'With the 360, I can mark isolated targets I may not have seen with just down scan or side scan. I can even see behind my boat and pinpoint structure that I missed. I can remain on a creek channel or river ledge much better.'

Palaniuk: 'I use four Humminbird Solix 12 units: two on the console (left has mapping + 2D sonar, right has Mega side/down imaging) and two on the casting deck (one on Mega 360 imaging with mapping, one for waypoints). Split screen gives two views per unit.'
Palaniuk: 'The width of the arches tells me how big the fish are, and the length shows how long the bass were in the sonar cone. The thickness of the arch determines the size of the fish.'
Palaniuk: 'Aim Mega Live at your jerkbait even when blind casting to follow it back and see how fish react — it makes you much more efficient and helps dial in the right cadence for the day'

Palaniuk: 'Used Humminbird MEGA Live 2 not to target specific fish but to scan around and see where fish were showing up in groups. If seeing them high in the water column, he knew which bait to pick up. If toward the bottom, he'd pick a crankbait.'

Palaniuk: 'Uses Humminbird to position the boat over individual fish, then drops the lure into its strike zone. Watching the screen shows lure depth and fish response, which helps refine tactics to trigger a reaction strike.'

Palaniuk: 'Humminbird 360 and Ultrex trolling motor were big, especially when the wind was blowing. Could pull up on isolated targets, line up with the 360 and know exactly how far away he was, keep the boat off the fish and make repeated specific casts.'
Palaniuk: 'MEGA Live 2 is way better than version 1. Could tell the detail of what was a bass, what was a gar, what was a crappie -- had a lot to do with how they were positioned and how they moved.'
Palaniuk: 'I couldn't see many bass in down scanning mode while idling, but when I used my bow graph set on 2D sonar in 2X bottom scan mode, I could see individual smallmouths near the bottom.'

Palaniuk: 'With forward-facing sonar I can see in real time how fish react to different colors. I can see how many are looking, chasing, and not reacting. That has changed how I fish jerkbaits and sped up my color bracketing process.'

Palaniuk: 'With forward-facing sonar, look at the distance between head and tail on screen — greater separation often signals a better-quality fish. This allows prioritizing casts and staying efficient, targeting the right fish.'

Palaniuk: 'Make small, deliberate trolling motor movements to get multiple angles on key cover. Small changes in viewing angles may reveal hidden limbs, isolated fish, or changes in bottom composition.'

Robertson: Idle every inch of the lake to find hard spots on the bottom. When you see big bass on electronics over featureless-looking bottom, commit to fishing offshore -- pull out big swimbaits, big spinnerbaits, and big crankbaits.
Robertson: Idle every inch of a lake during practice to find offshore spots. Look for hard-bottom areas where you can see big bass on electronics, even if you can't get them to bite in practice.
Robertson: The Garmin Panoptix LiveScope is essential for relocating offshore fish when they shift position. Just move the trolling motor around to find them again without losing time.
Robertson: Idle every inch of a lake to find offshore hard spots — if you see big bass on electronics but can't get them to bite in practice, commit to the spot in the tournament.

Wheeler: Uses Google Earth aerial imagery to assess vegetation and bank steepness, plus Navionics and Lowrance C-Maps for depth contours; focuses practice on areas holding the biggest concentrations of fish all doing the same thing.

Wheeler: Uses Lowrance HDS LIVE 12 to find bass in deep wintering holes or around brush piles, then drops a Jigging Rap for vertical presentation; targets bass that are grouped together and competing for food.
Wheeler: Lowrance ActiveTarget 2 was a huge player for smallmouth on day 1, giving breathing room to focus on largemouth the rest of the tournament.
Wheeler: Lowrance C-Maps had great detail in deeper stretches and even backwaters for largemouth; showed where deeper reeds and cover were and where to get up on pad for efficiency.

Wheeler: ActiveTarget 2 provides about 20% clearer image and better target separation than AT1 — can see a jighead falling at 90-100 ft away, which lets you see where your cast is in relation to the bass

Wheeler: Keep the bait over the fish's head. He sees about 40% of bass bite on screen but mostly just wants to see where the bait is relative to the fish, then turns away and just fishes — either they bite or they don't
Wheeler: Used FFS less than 2% to find places at Santee Cooper — all places found by side imaging/side scan. FFS was used for making perfect casts to shallow fish, not for finding spots

Wheeler: Swapped from Garmin LiveScope to Lowrance ActiveTarget because he can see fish further — 100+ ft away. That's huge when talking about 1-2 more isolated 3+ lb smallmouth per day, which is the difference between winning and losing

Wheeler: With ActiveTarget can see an 1/8-oz bait at 85 ft and a 1/2-oz at 110 ft. Keeps ranges at 100-120 ft depending on area size. For suspended fish, you can see so much further out with ActiveTarget
