Wheeler: Side imaging is definitely underutilized. Idle through areas before beaming with FFS — you can see groups of bass and bait so much better on side imaging. FFS is better for picking out singles
Wheeler: Swapped one ActiveTarget 2 transducer for a Humminbird 360 — huge deal for Spot Locking on a spot and seeing bottom fish without moving FFS around, being more efficient
Wheeler: Uses a Lowrance Precision 9 heading sensor for precise cast placement in non-FFS periods — rather than being 10-15 ft off target, you're within a foot. Expensive but a fine-tuned deal

Wheeler: When fishing in current for spawning smallmouth, electronics can only show so much — fish are so tight to the bottom that FFS can't really show you anything. More efficient to use your own eyes to spot beds visually

Wheeler: Forward-facing sonar is not the silver bullet people think — it won't close the gap between tour pros and weekend warriors. It's not a substitute for natural intuition and efficient decision-making. Jordan Lee won AOY without any FFS using just 2D, down and side scan

Wheeler: FFS screens show all kinds of blobs, dots, arcs, lines and dashes — you have to decipher size, distance, spacing, species and direction. Much of what he knows about FFS he learned from reading 2D for years, then side and down imaging. It's a building process

Wheeler: FFS shines brightest during offshore/post-spawn periods and in smallmouth country. During spawn and bank-fishing seasons, traditional techniques are more relevant — the technology got overhyped because COVID shifted tournament schedules to offshore/northern events

Wheeler: FFS can cost you hours casting at fish that follow but won't bite — you change lures, sizes, colors, actions, and an hour later they still snub you. When too focused on the screen, you lose awareness of time, weather, current, and fishing pressure that your intuition needs for good decisions

Wheeler: The best use of FFS is not chasing dots but connecting them — taking an attitude reading of the underwater world. Are baitfish up or down? Are bass moving away or toward you? Aggressive, negative, neutral? How close to cover? Every behavior observation feeds the decision-making process

Wheeler: With FFS limited to one period per day, the biggest thing is picking the right time and place. If it's windy or cloudy and fish are biting on reaction baits, maybe don't even turn FFS on that day. Let each day determine what you should be doing rather than overthinking it

Wheeler: On rocky smallmouth structure, 2D sonar couldn't see bass inside the rocks. FFS was required — you'd see them, drop on them, bring the bait above them, and they'd slide up out of those rocks and eat it
Wheeler: The thermocline shows up as a narrow fuzzy band on your locator screen; turn up the gain if it isn't well defined to locate it

Wheeler: Forward-facing sonar has opened up offshore prespawn tactics, but don't overlook traditional fishing techniques — instinct and experience through 'just fishing' will always reveal bass that evade fish finder technology
Wheeler: Maximizing Lowrance electronics usage is critical for finding and catching more bass.

Wheeler: Won on forward-facing sonar but wouldn't have won without spending time with DownScan and SideScan first. You need traditional sonar to find cover among thousands of stumps and irregularities, then FFS to fish them.

Wheeler: Forward-facing sonar has taught him more about jerkbaits than anything else - you can see on screen how fish react to cadence and pauses, how long it takes bass to come up from deeper water to meet the bait.

bait-design.insight: Johnston: All jigs come with rattles, but tear them off if you don't think they'll help. Use a chunk-style trailer that matches the jig skirt color.

Johnston: Developed the SPRO CJ Smasher as a versatile 3-inch worm that is durable (Dura Tuff material), scent-infused (Amino Bite), and can be rigged multiple ways including drop shot, shakey head, Ned rig, and Texas rig.

Johnston: Co-designed the SPRO CJ Smasher with brother Chris. The 3-inch soft plastic uses Dura Tuff material that is super soft yet super tough, floats completely for great action, and features Amino Bite scent with amino acids that create a scent trail through the water column.

Robertson: Adding SuspenDots and heavier aftermarket treble hooks to a walking topwater bait reduces buoyancy so the belly rides lower in the water. This gives the bait a different action and results in better hookup rates because fish get the bait and hooks instead of blowing it out of the water.

Robertson: Modify a Strike King Sexy Dawg by replacing stock hooks with round-bend treble hooks and adding SuspenDots to the belly — this makes it sit lower, improves castability, and prevents fish from popping the bait out of the water.

Wheeler: The Punch Out Craw has five manifestations - full glide bait on 4/0 hook and 1/2-oz sinker, separated claws for football jig or Biffle Hardhead, trimmed small craw for swim jig/flipping jig/chatterbait/shakyhead, shortened torso for even smaller profile, and sleek punch rig with claws removed for matted vegetation.

Wheeler: The importance of showing bass something they haven't seen or heard — a squarebill that combines attributes of a bladed jig (oscillating metal lip with clicking sound) and a crankbait body covers water and triggers reaction strikes
Wheeler: Designed the CrushCity Mayor swimbait with a good wobble that isn't aggressive — subtle changes to action can make a big difference in getting bites
Wheeler: Designed the Freeloader to serve dual roles — as a vibrating jig or spinnerbait trailer and as a cast-and-shake bait on a jighead for suspended fish, reducing the volume of plastics needed
Wheeler: A quality swimbait engages (swims) on the fall and when reeled at the slowest speeds — a constant tail kick mimics shad and generates bites

Wheeler: The Storm Largo Shad has a broader profile than most swimbaits with a slow body roll and exaggerated tail swing. You can customize action by cutting or removing the tail tendon for a wider action. Rig on VMC jighead, heavy duty swimbait hook, weighted willow swimbait hook, or Tokyo rig.

Wheeler: Designed the Punch Out Craw with score lines for tool-free modification into 5 versions: glide (punching), flipping (separated claws), flapping craw (removed spear tail), chunk (jig trailer), and finesse (body and tail only). Full body takes a 4/0 hook; twist off the end segment for a 3/0 hook.
The 3-inch Ika has a 1.5-inch torso shaped like a Senko with six tentacles radiating from one end; salt-impregnated; available in 14 colors

Yamamoto pro Hite: The Zako was designed with restrained 'no-feel' action to mimic how bluegill, green sunfish, and gizzard shad hover and subtly swim; the V-shaped tail with an interior flange minimizes tail movement; a back groove facilitates easy rigging; non-salt-impregnated blend for increased durability
Yamamoto pro Hite convinced Colby to create a non-salt-impregnated plastic blend for the Zako for increased durability — can withstand multiple fish catches without tearing

Yamamoto inducted into Bass Fishing Hall of Fame in 2016; credited with numerous innovations in soft plastic design and manufacturing; the Senko called 'one of the most important lure developments in recent years'; Hula Grub also cited as a mainstay worldwide

Klein: Gary Yamamoto got the density and sink rate just right with the Senko; stickworms must have the right stiffness and fall rate for each end to quiver enticingly on slack line — 'If the plastic is too stiff, there isn't enough action. If it's too soft, the bait just folds in half as it sinks'

Klein: Dense, salt-infused soft plastics sink faster and are stiffer; softer/less-dense plastics are preferred for finesse and have a slower sink rate; when flipping into heavy cover, select dense, tough plastic that withstands abuse — too soft tears up quickly
DeFronzo: The Senko has a unique 'fall away from the angler' action like a Flying Lure Tube — this fall is deadly and unlike any other weightless bait

Sealock: The Senko's weight makes it cast like a rocket even rigged weightless — critical for finesse fishing in clear water where long casts are essential; it skips underneath cover with just a wrist flick
The Yamamoto D Shad is denser than other soft jerkbaits — stays about 1ft below the surface when worked fast and erratically instead of skipping across the top; rig on a 3/0 Offset EWG Worm Hook

The D Shad has a solid diamond-shaped tail instead of a traditional forked tail — the heavier tail causes an even fall through the water column and a shimmying action on slack line similar to a Senko; most bites occur on the fall between twitches

Yamamoto: 'I designed the Senko after trying the Slug-Go which I thought was too hard and bulky. The softer plastic and the way water flows around the lure as it falls gives it a continual vibration on a weightless Texas rig'
Yamamoto: 'I originally wanted to make a twitch bait like the Slug-Go, but after testing it turned out the Senko was better as a finesse bait. I got the idea for the shape from a Bic pen I had'
Yamamoto inducted into Bass Fishing Hall of Fame in 2016; credited with numerous innovations in soft plastic design and manufacturing including the Senko and Hula Grub

Yamamoto pro Shryock: The Yamatanuki is a heavy 'gravity bait' — 3.5 inches at 5/8 oz with a signature side-to-side flutter on the fall. Works from 1 foot to 20+ feet suspended. Extremely weedless. Can replace jigs around heavy cover and grass
The Senko sinks horizontally, which is different from most soft plastics that fall nose-first. This horizontal descent triggers strikes as bass interpret it as dying or injured prey
The Senko is infused with salt which adds weight and a unique taste that encourages bass to hold onto it longer, increasing hook-up ratio
Yamamoto pro Hite: Hite designed the Zako for Yamamoto specifically as a ChatterBait/Jack Hammer trailer. The accordion-style tail collapse on the bite is the key feature for better hookup ratios
Hudson: 'The biggest difference is rate of fall - Senko sinks much faster than Dinger. Faster fall lets you fish faster and deeper, slower fall irritates fish by hanging slowly in their face'

Hudson: 'Senko is more heavily salt infused making fish hold on longer, and way softer feeling more realistic. Softness gives slightly more action and it sinks straight down. Downside is much less durability'
Hudson: 'Dinger sways side to side more than Senko due to slower sink rate, while Senko tends to sink straight down'
Senko falls faster than most soft plastics with a slight tantalizing wiggle due to high salt content. Smallmouth grab them and hold on thanks to salt content and soft texture
Yamamoto: The Senko was initially considered a flop because it did not catch fish when applied the way it was originally intended. Over 17 million Senkos were produced in 2016 alone
Yamamoto Cowboy is very customizable - cut tails, remove appendages, trim profile to change action. Full profile adds bulk as jig trailer, trimmed slicks through cover easier
Hawk (pro): 'Stretch 40 is basically a 4-inch Senko with a small curly tail. The tail is small unlike most baits that size with large curl or ribbon tails - much more lively'
Card (pro): Yamamoto Single Tail Grub is also an excellent spinnerbait trailer

What separates Senko from competitors is the plastic formula - tuned for perfect amount of salt, softness, and natural sink rate. Most competitors fall too fast, too slow, or have other issues. The natural sink and wobble is what makes fish unable to resist
Yamamoto Yama Craw uses entirely different material from Senko - Mega Floater Formula. Softer, spongier, very buoyant. Not as stretchy as ElaZtech but more durable than hand-poured plastics
Yama Craw buoyancy changes presentation for Texas rig, wobble heads, magnum shaky heads, jig trailers, Tokyo rig, and Carolina rig - claws float up in defensive crawfish posture
Yamatanuki is 3.5 inches, weighs 5/8oz without weight. Salt impregnated in proprietary way for heavier, quicker fall with more action. Castable on baitcasting tackle without added weight, skips well
Yamatanuki knobbed tail has a lot of action - always undulating as it skips along bottom or pulls over cover. Rig with hook point out of big end for more action and better hookups

forage-matching.insight: KVD: 'Always look for baitfish in fall — herons, seagulls, terns, bait on the graph, visible bait in the water. Seeing shad on top indicates a really good afternoon bite. From there, look for the type of cover the lake has in the areas the most abundant baitfish want to use.'

KVD: 'During fall, lakes become lower and clearer. Baitfish move shallow — great combination for spinnerbaits. The look of spinning blades and vibration really mimics all types of baitfish. Match weight to blades for the right retrieve speed and depth.'

KVD: In northern natural lakes, perch and gobies are the primary forage — fish spend most of their time feeding on them. Both live on or near rocks. Birds diving on shallow points = alewives spawning (another forage clue).

KVD: 'Knowing the dominant baitfish is critical, especially in fall when bass target the most abundant species to fatten up. Forage varies even between lakes miles apart — bluegill, perch, alewives, smelt, shad, gobies. Regional differences affect coloration, seasonal patterns, migration, and habitat. Study biological reports, ask guides about dominant bait, note coloration and size during practice. Inspect regurgitated baitfish closely.'
KVD: 'It isn't always shad. In northern lakes, bluegills are often the main prey. In the southeast, blueback herring are dominant. Know the habits of the dominant prey species to be successful.'

KVD: 'Fall hotspot indicators: gulls, cormorants, and herons point to baitfish concentrations. Watch for coots — they sit and feed over grass beds, preferring the greener, healthier grass that also attracts bait and bass.'

KVD: 'Great Lakes smallmouth forage varies — yellow perch, crawfish, round gobies. Perch favor grass/sand patches; gobies live in rock. Minnow-imitating lures (jerkbaits, crankbaits) excel on perch bites. Goby-imitating swimbaits and jig combos for goby bites. Identify target forage first — it can change seasonally and day-to-day.'

KVD: The Ned Rig is so popular for smallmouth because it imitates gobies. Gobies stay locked on the bottom on rocks — that's exactly where the Ned Rig sits. Find the rocks, you find the big smallmouth. Gobies have changed these fisheries — fish don't have to move because the food source is so prolific.

KVD: 'Color makes a big difference — match what bass see underwater, not in your hand. Shad have iridescent bluish/grey backs with color variations (green tint, grayish look). As lakes get clearer and pressure increases, your bait has to appear a lot more natural. The right color catches ALL the fish, not just a few.'

KVD: 'Bass are more baitfish-oriented in fall than any other season. Cooling water causes vast shad schools to migrate into tributary arms, bass follow. Match lure size to baitfish size. In lakes without shad, target weedy areas — bass feed on bluegill and shiners.'

lake-reading.insight: KVD: 'Stop signs' = clean areas (hard bottom, rock transitions) that sit just outside major spawning coves. Fish stage on these as they move in and out of spawning areas. Concentrate on high-percentage corners. LakeMaster mapping showing bottom contours was the critical tool — 'the only reason I won.'

KVD: 'Reading grassy lake water: color breaks (darker water = deeper edges), grass clumps (ambush points), wind-driven current seams (concentrate bass). Match bait to time of day AND depth zone: morning/evening = topwater/shallow, midday = soft plastics/spinnerbaits on weed edges, late/low light = crankbaits and deeper jigs.'

KVD: 'Lakes that have grass change the most.' Creek channels don't move and bottom terrain is static, but grass lakes change a lot year to year — grass types, height, density all shift. Different areas of the same lake may have different grass types (hydrilla, eel grass, pepper grass, streaming grass).
KVD: Heavy grass volume makes the water clearer. When grass clumps reach the surface, expect improved water clarity compared to areas without grass.

KVD: 'Look for areas with dark bottom — dead vegetation, pad stems, rocks. Sun radiates anything dark on the bottom. Even in cold water, depth changes, depressions, and little ditches hold fish. Same principle north and south.'

KVD: 'When fishing expansive flats with breaks, mark waypoints at each catch. The pattern often isn't the obvious points — it's tapering breaks with inside turns cutting into the breakline. Those inside turns concentrate fish. Use a crankbait to survey, then switch to a jerkbait to work the productive spots more efficiently.'

KVD: On clear water grass lakes, most anglers fish the inside line where fish spawn. But fish also spawn in the deeper scattered grass patches (7ft+) in the middle of big flats. Nobody else fishes it — that's a competitive advantage. A lot of largemouth have already spawned out there.

KVD: North wind blowing into a bank knocks algae loose and muddies the water, making sight fishing impossible. When this happens, switch from sight fishing to casting Thunder Cricket or fan casting the area. Conditions can clear up fast when wind lays down.

KVD: In practice, keep moving and take notes. Don't fall in love with one area. Stop at many stretches, get a feel for what's there, then move on. 'That's why we call it practice.' Save the commitment for tournament day.

KVD: In clear water slick calm conditions, most anglers reach for finesse. KVD's instinct is to jerk a jerkbait over the top of them — the clear water means they'll see it from a distance. Confidence in the technique matters as much as conditions.

KVD: 'Smallmouth love transitions or changes in bottom composition. Good polarized sunglasses are as critical as electronics — I'm constantly looking for transitions, irregularities, individual pieces of cover, or the fish themselves. Eyes scanning the water catches a pile of smallmouth each year.'

KVD: On Cayuga Lake (northern natural lake), the north end is a huge expanse of flats at 8-12ft, all grass. The key is finding clean holes in the grass at 9-11ft. Good holes have a little bit of rock in them. Each hole has its own ecosystem — bluegill, perch, gobies all live there. Good holes have reloaded with fish all week.

KVD: Guntersville's grass has evolved over the years: milfoil first, then hydrilla, now a lot of eel grass. All three still present. The grass creates great habitat and clear water (filters it). Floating eel grass cut up by wind/waves/boats makes it hard to throw lipless crankbaits and jerkbaits — catches on your line.

KVD: Guntersville breaks into three sections: Lower end = rocks, bluffs, bridges (good for jerkbaits, cranking). Mid-lake = river ledges, miles of grass (always a player). Upper end = shallow creeks, lily pads (Bill Lowen territory — guys who get way back into places others don't look).

KVD: Pre-spawn in Texas (and everywhere), follow the ditches and drains leading into spawning flats. Fish use these as highways. Subtle depressions on LakeMaster mapping concentrate fish, but sometimes the best spots are subtle features you find just by covering water with a Red Eye Shad.
KVD: 'Stained water traps heat much better than clear water, making stained areas the warmest on the lake. It also decreases visibility making bass more receptive to fast-moving presentations.'

KVD: 'Shallow rock traps and holds heat. Fish hold tight to it for comfort and ambush points. Parallel a crankbait down rocky banks, targeting rocks in direct sunlight — extremely consistent winter pattern.'

KVD: 'In cold water, boat position is as critical as lure selection. Position parallel to dropoffs and edges so your bait stays in the proper depth zone throughout the retrieve. Bass won't move far in cold water — if you don't fish parallel, your bait catches a much smaller percentage of the key depth.'

KVD: 'Bluegills spawn midway back in a pocket, not all the way in the back. They like sides of secondary points and shade trees that stick over water — almost guaranteed under a big willow tree. Use Mega 360 or Side Imaging, or find them with your eyes.'

KVD: 'I don't spend more than 10 minutes in the same area doing the same thing without making some kind of change.' Either move, change baits, change presentation — but don't waste time without positive feedback. Any sign (blowup, follow, catch, short bite) is a clue to the puzzle.

KVD: Most important thing arriving at a lake: figure out the seasonal pattern plus current conditions. Even on a known lake, see what's changed. On grass lakes, determine how much grass there is and where. Look for bottlenecks and funnels on your mapping. Cover a lot of water on day 1.

KVD: On grass lakes, look for bottlenecks and funnels on your LakeMaster mapping — cuts and narrows between grass mats. Areas leading in and out of these bottlenecks can be really good, especially with wind creating current.

KVD: 'Bright day, no wind, clear water = fish less aggressive, hugging bottom. Switch to jig, C-rig, drop shot, Neko. With current, wind, cloud cover, or stained water, they'll bite the crankbait. Ultra-clear with wind and clouds = still crankbait but speed up retrieve and use natural colors. Monitor electronics to see how fish relate to bottom.'

KVD: 'In cold water, boat position is as critical as lure selection. Position parallel to dropoffs and edges — if you don't fish parallel, your bait catches a much smaller percentage of the key depth zone. Fish are less likely to move far.'

KVD: 'I begin checking weather conditions for the area I'll be fishing as early as 15 days before a tournament. I want to know warm front or cold front coming in or out. Water temp is critical — sometimes just one degree warmer holds most of the bass.'
KVD: 'It helps to determine what type of lake you're fishing — lowland reservoir, highland reservoir, natural lake, tidewater, or river. Bass use different places in different types of lakes.'

trailer-modification.insight: KVD: Cut the head off the blade minnow trailer to shorten it slightly. The water rushing around the chatterbait blade hits the shortened tail at the perfect distance for lively action. This is his #1 trailer for the Thunder Cricket.

KVD: Bite the head off the KVD Magic trailer — shorten it just a bit. Combined with blue craw Thunder Cricket, it looks like a bluegill. The trailer adds a little flash and keeps the presentation subtle and natural for clear water.

KVD: Always use a plastic trailer when slow rolling — adds bulk and extra action fish home in on. Rage Slim Swimmer is his go-to: subtle, not too bulky (doesn't make bait ride up), has an erratic wag. In coldest months, sometimes removes the skirt entirely and just uses a swimbait as trailer.

hackney.insight: Hackney: 'The end of summer is one of the most difficult times to fish in the South. Downsizing to a compact finesse jig gives pressured bass something they haven't seen.'
Hackney: 'The zero-degree line tie has the same effect as a Texas rig — the line goes straight down making it weedless. Perfect for going through brush and cover without getting stuck.'
Hackney: 'Usually when you get stuck with a jig, it's not because the weed guard is giving, it's because the guard is too far away from the hook point. That's how brush can get up under there.'
Hackney: 'The thinner skirt gives a sleeker presentation for finicky fish. It's also great for smallmouth and spotted bass with their smaller mouths.'

Hackney: 'Round heads are better suited to fishing rock and stumps — less likely to get wedged. Keel-style heads are ideal for fast retrieves and fishing higher in the water column. Bullet heads offer a mix but can get wedged in crevices.'
Hackney: 'Opening the wire spread gives the spinnerbait more lift so you can slow down without cutting vibration. Closing the wire gives more speed and keeps it down.'
Hackney: 'Nine times out of 10 you'll find better success with a spinnerbait fishing around isolated cover like dock posts, stumps, and boulders. Bass position near cover to ambush prey.'
Hackney: 'Split-tail trailers give the most bang for your buck — good action without changing the speed on retrieve or fall. Craw trailers increase profile and slow the bait down.'
Hackney: 'Use a trailer hook slightly smaller than the main hook, oriented the same direction. Run the main hook point through the back of the trailer hook eye.'
Hackney: 'In muddy water, it's more important to increase the size of the blades than to worry about what color they are.'

Hackney: 'I like big poles, big line and big hook sets. It's easier to get a bite on a wacky worm than a 3/4-ounce Hack Attack Jig, but at the end of the day the results are the same. It boils down to how you like to fish.'
Hackney: 'The first six events during the prespawn/spawn period allow power fishing because bass are most active and in the rut.'
Hackney: 'The trailer is usually a Rage Tail Craw. Sometimes I use a small swimbait if the bass are on shad.'
Hackney: 'I don't believe bass are line shy. You need to adjust what line you use based on how a lure performs using the line, not based on water clarity.'
Hackney: 'Tungsten weights are extremely hard with hard edges and are a lot harder on your line than lead, regardless of braid or fluorocarbon. Always use a rubber insert peg to protect the line.'
Hackney: 'Fish will move under mats of grass or cattails in the afternoon after the sun has warmed them up. You can duck hunt in the morning and bass fish in the afternoon.'
Hackney: 'Big females will group up under mats during the winter-to-prespawn transition. That's when I catch some of the biggest fish of the year.'
Hackney: 'During cold fronts, look for old canals, dead-end channels and northern banks sheltered from cold northern winds that retain heat from the sun.'

Hackney: 'I prefer the Strike King Rodent because it penetrates well and doesn't have a lot of unnecessary action. When the water is cold, I don't want a lot of flappy parts on my lure, but I do want a bait with a profile.'
Hackney: 'For primarily flipping (not pitching), I prefer the 7-6 rod over the 7-11. The 7-6 has a softer tip which makes it easier to flip lighter baits.'

Hackney: 'I'll use any color as long as it's black. It's almost impossible to get pure black anymore — it all has flake in it — so I go with the darkest looking stuff I can find. If black isn't working, try Summer Craw or Bama Craw.'
Hackney: 'Any plastic I throw in muddy water will be big and bulky, with a lot of arms and legs to move water. My choice is a Strike King Rage Tail Lobster when the water's real bad.'
Hackney: 'Picking the right lure for muddy conditions begins with helping the bass find it. Four related factors: water movement, noise, size, and color.'
Hackney: 'I don't pick lures based on the size of bass I expect to catch in muddy water. It's all the same whether targeting 2-pounders or 5-pounders. They gotta find it before they can bite it.'
Hackney: 'Uses braid even in clear water when swimming jigs. As long as it is a moving bait, line type doesn't seem to matter — bass chomp regardless.'

Hackney: 'Bass in real muddy water move shallow, about as deep as the light penetrates. They don't like pitch black but also don't like being near the surface. They stay close to the bottom but not on it, right at the point where the light disappears.'
Hackney: 'When the water's a little clearer they relate to cover — around it. When it's real muddy they're right on it, almost touching sometimes. Even more true in strong current.'

Hackney: 'Flooded shallow areas where grass and bushes filter the water are usually your best water. The difference in color might be slight to us, but a bass living in it sees that slight difference as huge.'

Hackney: 'If fishing a river or main lake with good current, the only thing I'm looking for is the current break. Bass have to get out of that moving water to survive. That one factor makes up for anything else.'
Hackney: 'Cold, muddy water is tough but warm, muddy water isn't really that bad. Don't let it get into your head. Think water conditions rather than seasonal pattern.'

Hackney: 'Muddy water bass are target oriented. Look for something shallow, out of the current, with cover in it — stump, laydown, rock, or flooded dock. If there's something that filters the water, so much the better.'

Hackney: 'Matted grass provides excellent surrounding and overhead protection and ambush points while heating up quicker than the surroundings. These factors make them resilient to cold fronts. Rich soils support food webs including crawfish and sunfish.'
Hackney: 'Key tackle for punching mats: 7-11 Heavy rod, high-speed reel, 50-pound braid, 1 to 1.5 oz tungsten, 6/0 flipping hook, Rage Punch Bug in Black Blue Flake.'
Hackney: 'Once you get bit, your job becomes dropping your bait into every nearby nook and cranny. Choose a powerful and balanced flipping rod for effortless and pinpoint pitches and flips.'

Hackney: 'A mat can be made of anything — vegetation, pine needles, leaves piled by wind and current, even discarded lumber or trash. Bass will hold under any mat if it breaks the sunlight and there's a foot of water under it.'
Hackney: 'Mats offer shade, an ambush point and a safe place from predators. The only time bass won't use a mat is when they're actually on the bed — they like sunlight then.'

Hackney: 'You want a heavy action blank with some tip and a parabolic bend from tip to handle. Not a featherweight — you need weight to help set the hook. Close to 8 feet long, never shorter than 7-6. The parabolic bend sets the hook and the strong blank pulls the fish out.'
Hackney: 'Most anglers do not use anything close to what they should for flipping mats. Most of them are carrying a knife to a gunfight.'
Hackney: 'Use a high-speed 7.3:1 gear ratio reel to get slack out quickly. I don't crank fish out — I pull them out with the rod. The reel just manages slack.'
Hackney: 'Only braid for mat flipping, nothing else under any circumstances. 50 or 60 lb test. Go up to 80 lb when you want to slow the fall. No stretch, minimal resistance sliding through the mat.'
Hackney: 'Use a 6/0 heavy flipping hook — no flex. If it flexes, it'll wear a hole in the fish's mouth and you'll lose her.'
Hackney: 'Always use tungsten. I don't have a lead weight in my boat. Tungsten is more sensitive and smaller so it penetrates the mat easier.'

Hackney: 'It's a mistake to only use black and blue for mats. I frequently pick colors like I do for any other lure — match the hatch and pay attention to water color. The more natural a lure looks the more likely a fish will eat it.'

Hackney: 'Do not set the hook with a jerk when flipping mats — the weight and plastic will ball up and you'll never get a good hookset. Reel down until you feel the fish, then use a steady, hard, strong, quick pull back to drive the hook home.'
Hackney: 'Pull the fish back with the rod — don't try to crank her back. The reel's only function is to keep slack out of the line.'
Hackney: 'Flipping mats absolutely requires the right rod, reel and line. Most techniques you can do with several different combinations and still catch fish. Not when you're flipping mats.'
Hackney: 'Visualize the hookset in your mind before every flip. If you don't, you'll reflexively jerk your rod when you get a bite.'

Hackney: 'Fish feed more on crawfish up to the spawn, then switch to shad and bluegill diet. Shad spawns and bluegill spawns occur after the bass spawn, so bass switch because of all that fresh food right in their faces.'

Hackney: 'Changing the sink rate of a jig does not mean tying on a heavier jig. Alter the fall rate by varying the trailer. A bigger, bulkier trailer on a 1/2-ounce jig makes it act like a 3/8 or 1/4 ouncer.'

Hackney: 'I limit myself to just two colors — black/blue for dark water or dark conditions, green pumpkin for clearer water and bright days. These two catch fish from Florida to New York. I may switch during the day — black/blue early morning, green pumpkin when the sun gets up.'
Hackney: 'As the days become shorter and there has been cool weather, bass are very aggressive. I think black is a color that is easily seen by the bass in cooler weather.'
Hackney: 'The frog on a buzzbait floats it, enabling you to reel much slower than with a skirt. A skirted buzzbait sinks when you slow down, but the frog keeps it on the surface.'

Hackney: 'The most consistent spot to find a large bass is where no one else is fishing. If you are more concerned about how you will get a bass out of structure than about a bass biting, that is where you need to fish.'

Hackney: 'I try to fish places in a different way or with a different lure or present my lure in a way other fishermen won't. Fishing weird places with various tactics that others weren't using could pay off with big bass.'

Hackney: 'One of the biggest bass I ever caught was from the center of a giant cypress stump that looked solid. I threw a worm right into the hole in the top and caught a 7.5-pounder. No one else was dumb enough to fish there.'
Hackney: 'Line-tie degrees, the physics of hook penetration, and how and where a jig will outperform plastic are all base-level considerations for every jig flip.'

Hackney: 'There are two phases for prespawn: pre-prespawn right out of winter and then prespawn. These two phases are your best bet at catching a personal best. Fish are stressed from spawning prep - males guard a huge territory and are really aggressive.'

Hackney: 'In the early spawn, nothing beats a piece of plastic. Use baits without a lot of action like a tube or Rodent - the only action is from your rod tip. Be patient with repetitive casts to bedding bass.'
Hackney: 'Once water hits the 70s, fish pay more attention to baits with appendages that swim. Use something like a Game Hawg or Rage Bug. Fish grab it even when reeling back in.'
Hackney: 'Especially in the South, females like to bunch up after spawning. When you find one female, you find a group of them. Use topwater around those groups as females seem to like it more.'

Hackney: 'Quite a few fish live shallow even during the hottest time of year. They hold in shade of boat docks, overhanging trees, lily pads, and grass. Hot weather triggers grass growth that creates shade, oxygen, and ambush points.'
Hackney: 'Bluegill and bream are the bait bass feed on most at this time of year. A bream-colored topwater looks like a bream jumping, skipping, and moving on the surface.'

Hackney: 'One partner needs to do 80 percent of the fishing and the other is the support person - handling the net, unhooking fish, culling. Pride causes more teams to lose buddy tournaments than any other factor.'
Hackney: 'The support person takes the bass off the hook, puts it in the livewell, and handles culling. This lets the leader fish most of the time and catch more bass.'
Hackney: 'I use 55- or 60-pound braided line for flipping. It is low-stretch, cuts through vegetation well with a big bass on, and I can tie on in the morning and never have to retie all day.'

Hackney: 'An 8-foot rod gives better line control than 7 or 7.5-foot. It gives the right amount of tip action and backbone. With braided line you need a parabolic rod with softer action than most flipping sticks because the line does not stretch.'
Hackney: 'Black-and-blue and green-pumpkin are my two favorite colors for punching grass. These two colors you can fish in all 50 states and be very successful.'
Hackney: 'Flippin and pitchin are great techniques but you must be accurate with your bait placement.'
Hackney: 'In early spring or cold water, tip the jig with a KVD Chunk - big profile but less action. A more subtle swimming jig is better in cold water.'
Hackney: 'Once water heats into upper 60s, switch to a Rage Craw with active vibrating legs. The Rage Craw is also his first choice for swimming a jig.'

Hackney: 'Different trailers affect rate of fall. A bulky active trailer makes a jig fall slower. A slender trailer like a Rage Menace causes it to plummet faster. Rate of fall can be critical - experiment with trailers.'
Hackney: 'For casting jigs into deeper water, use a 7-6 with a lighter tip. The longer rod improves hook sets on fish farther away and in deeper water.'
Hackney: 'Go to your favorite lake and carry one rod for jigs. That is the best way to get familiar with jig fishing and resist using other techniques.'

Hackney: 'It was all about creek channels or drains. Where the drain turns, the break is steep and bass relate to that steep break. Bushes were everywhere but the deal was the bottom contour, not the cover.'

Hackney: 'When water gets real high, fish do not go shallow - they go straight up. They sit where they were at normal pool and suspend. Every fish I caught was suspended 1-4 feet under the surface in 8-11 feet of water.'
Hackney: 'I pull a bait through the water and if it looks real, you will get bit. I do not like to fish anything that stands out. I think I am more apt to get bit on something that looks natural.'
Hackney: 'When the water was falling, they were not as aggressive. When it was creeping up, they were aggressive.'
Hackney: 'Key spots did not replenish. You could not go back to the same place and catch another one. Had to keep the jig wet all 8 hours for 5 big bites each day.'
Hackney: 'Fished a 7-foot-11 Hack Attack flip stick, Quantum 200 HD reel (7.3 gear ratio), and 50-lb Gamma Torque braid.'

Hackney: 'Braid carries no light - it is real natural-looking in the water. Fluorocarbon and mono are not natural-looking at all. If fishing cover, the fish sees the jig first, not the line. I have caught them in 18 feet of visible water in cover with braid.'
Hackney: 'Let braid color fade to match cover. If fishing green grass, use green braid. Do not paint it black because that is one of the easiest colors for fish to see.'
Hackney: 'Braid makes me a lot more efficient. I do not have to retie as much. The whole deal about line type is whatever makes the bait act natural - I am not worried about them seeing the line.'

Hackney: 'The 7-foot-11 flip stick was built for braid - not fast enough for fluorocarbon. Very parabolic bend, limber tip, and the whole rod loads. With no-stretch line you need that forgiveness in the rod.'
Hackney: 'You can make a lipless crankbait look and act like a shad, bluegill, or crawfish simply by changing the color.'

Hackney: 'Threadfin shad spawn happens just as bass come off beds. Gizzard shad spawn is later. There is never a gap - it is the perfect storm. Largemouth are run down from the spawn and the shad spawn is a really easy time to gorge.'

Hackney: 'Threadfins spawn all night. The only time to cash in is within the first 45 minutes of daylight or the last 45 minutes of the day. In that span, 20-25 pounds of bass is possible on the right lake.'

Hackney: 'In the South, shad spawn on hard bottoms, matted vegetation, cypress trees, and boat docks. Old Styrofoam marina docks are the best. On the Tennessee River, the better shad spawn is on shell beds and shallow bars in 5-6 feet of water.'
Hackney: 'Shad will not spawn deeper than light penetration - it takes light for their eggs to hatch. Same reason bass do not spawn in 20 feet when water is dirty.'

Hackney: 'The big key during the shad spawn is to be slow and steady and pound the place. It is easy to fish over the bass when there is so much bait. You do not see a lot of chasing because they do not have to.'
Hackney: 'Fish a 6.8:1 reel with 16-lb fluorocarbon on a medium-heavy spinnerbait rod. The slower gear ratio helps maintain the medium to slow retrieve needed.'
Hackney: 'At Stick Marsh with no underwater vegetation, fish were spawning around willow trees and cane on the shoreline - they just wanted to have something to spawn around.'
Hackney: 'In MLF, use Score Tracker to decide if you need to abandon a pattern or ride it out. I never got in a hurry. More slow and steady was the deal because fishing was not through the roof.'
Hackney: 'I got behind in the first round but did not worry because it is FL - the way I was fishing you could catch an 8-10 pounder. I like to go places where they have big ones.'
Hackney: 'Flipped mats with a Rodent (black/blue) and a heavy 1.5-oz weight at Lake Garcia and Kenansville. For hydrilla and hyacinth mats, heavy tungsten is essential.'
Hackney: 'For heavy-duty flipping, use heavier straight-shank hooks to prevent flexing during close powerful sets. The Gamakatsu G Power Flip and Punch Hook works for both braid and fluorocarbon.'
Hackney: 'For casting with plastics, use round bend or EWG hooks. These ensure better hook-ups during the pull set technique which is crucial when the fish is away from the boat.'

Hackney: 'Use the largest hook possible without compromising the natural presentation of the bait. This maximizes hook-up ratio by ensuring the hook occupies the right amount of space within the bait.'
Hackney: 'The size of the hook correlates with the weight being used. For heavier weights and aggressive flipping, a larger hook maintains effective hook penetration.'
Hackney: 'Modern hook coatings like Nano Alpha enhance slickness and penetration capability. The coating aids in better hook sets and offers significant rust resistance.'

Hackney: 'With 1-2 hours left in a tournament, sit down, put the rod down, and mentally pretend the day is over. Critique yourself as if you are driving home. Ask what you would have done differently, then come back and make that change.'
Hackney: 'Many times speeding up and changing lures is not the answer. It is just the opposite - slow down, mentally evaluate, then make a deliberate change.'
Hackney: 'There were more numbers of fish shallow, but bigger ones were on the deeper edge of that grass.'
Hackney: 'Most guys flipping grass were using a punch skirt and plastic or just plastic. But I am a firm believer that when they are biting the jig, it catches the big ones.'
Hackney: 'Anywhere I caught bass, I had to get perch bites. If I fished a stretch of grass and I did not get any perch or bluegill bites, there would not be any bass there.'
Hackney: 'Flipped with a 7-foot-11 rod, EXO PT 100 reel, and 50-pound braid for deep grass jigging.'
Hackney: 'Look for something different in the mat - a yellow algae mat growing in green milfoil will isolate the fish. Fish are never random, NEVER.'
Hackney: 'Fish in mats are school oriented, not isolated. Once you get a bite, it is time to slow down.'
Hackney: 'Shad hang along the outside edge of a mat but never go inside it. Bluegill, sunfish, shiners, and crawfish will go inside. Bass in mats are short-range ambush predators, not bait chasers.'
Hackney: 'An abnormality in vegetation like mixed species can be key. A small area of cabbage growing outside deep milfoil or coontail growing inside will concentrate fish.'

Hackney: 'Birds of a feather flock together. Bigger fish stick together because they are on bigger bait. If you find 3-pounders, a 5-pounder could be nearby. A group of 1-pounders - consider a 3-pounder a gift.'
Hackney: 'Always flip a jig on 50-pound braid for faster fall rate. When flipping an ounce or heavier weight or throwing a frog, use 65-pound test.'
Hackney: 'I will never target smallmouth with a jig. Smallmouth get a soft plastic better than a jig - they are a weird fish.'

Hackney: 'I typically like to go with the biggest hook I can put on a treble hook bait because it increases my chances of catching a fish. A fish on a treble hook bait is harder to land than one caught on a single hook bait.'
Hackney: 'You can manipulate hooks because of water temperature. It is easier to sink a bait in cold water than warm water because air and gas inside the bait are less buoyant in cold water.'

Hackney: 'Adding No.4 2X strong trebles to a flat-sided balsa bait (Lucky Shad) tempers buoyancy and lets it suspend on contact with cover. It acts like a jerkbait but you can crank it and cover more water.'
Hackney: 'Only carry about 10 replacement hooks of each size. Overpacking trays leads to dull hooks as masses of metal bump against one another during long runs.'

Hackney: 'I carry more trailers than I do jigs because conditions sometimes change hourly. I can completely change the look of a jig by changing the trailer - from no action with a Chunk to looking like the trailer is propelling the jig with a Rage Craw.'

Hackney: 'I use a medium-heavy rod instead of a heavy action broomstick for flipping. With braid there is no stretch so you do not need a superstiff rod. The medium-heavy is more accurate, lets me feel the fish before it feels me, and a softer rod is better for fighting fish.'
Hackney: 'I use braid for 90 percent of my flipping. Its smaller diameter and limpness gives the jig more action. I prefer 50-pound red Cajun braid.'
Hackney: 'As bass conclude spawning, big females seek thick heavy grass to rest, recuperate, and feed up on bluegills and crawfish.'

Hackney: 'Use the 7-foot-11 flipping stick for standard flipping and a 7-foot-6 version for different pitching scenarios. Balance rod action, bait weight, and the need for precise efficient hook sets.'

Hackney: 'Sometimes fishing around other people is good because you can see what they are doing differently. Make a point to avoid following anything they might be doing. I have gone in behind other anglers and caught fish doing something completely different.'

Hackney: 'Catch what you believe to be your best weight for the day and get out of there. Do not burn out an area in practice - if you catch a 3-4 pounder in practice you risk not catching it again during the tournament.'
Hackney: 'Disappear from a productive spot when the crowd shows up, then return later. The area needs time to replenish with more bass.'
Hackney: 'Manipulate running depth by varying the trailer. Rage Craw keeps it high, Rage Swimmer keeps it low, Rage Lobster keeps it high with bigger profile, Menace Grub for subtle smaller profile.'
Hackney: 'Use a 7-2 swim jig/frog rod with a 7.5:1 or 8.3:1 reel for fast retrieves. In winter when fishing deep and slow, switch to a 6.4:1 reel.'
Hackney: 'Use 50-pound braid most of the time for snappy response and shallow cover. Switch to 14-20 pound fluorocarbon only for the deep-and-slow presentation.'
Hackney: 'Use 4.75-inch Rage Swimmer for bigger shad or baitfish. Use 3.75-inch in colder water or when fishing deeper. Both keep the swim jig down.'

Hackney: 'I have not changed my fishing strategy at all for MLF. The one thing that will change is you probably will not leave a spot producing lots of bass to go look for bigger ones. But at the end of the day the angler who catches the most big bass will win.'
Hackney: 'I won the MLF World Championship in March 2018 flipping a Rodent. If it is not broken, do not try to fix it.'
Hackney: 'Big fish relate to the perimeter, a creek channel, and hold on an isolated piece of grass on a flat. Little fish run up shallow while big fish satellite around the area.'

Hackney: 'During winter drawdown, larger fish will be on the flat out front holding on a stump, even in 2-3 feet of water, always relating to solid structure like a drop-off, rock pile, piece of wood, or isolated grass.'
Hackney: 'Always upsize hooks with short-shank trebles at least one size bigger than factory. 1/4-ounce gets a No.4, 1/2-ounce gets a No.2, 3/4-ounce gets a No.1.'
Hackney: 'Line test dictates running depth. Lighter line runs deeper. 20-pound fluorocarbon is the mainstay. Use a 7-4 medium-heavy composite rod for flats and a 7-foot rod for the 1/4-ounce model.'
Hackney: 'Use a Lew's 7-2 Hack Attack swim jig/frog rod, Custom Pro reel 8.3:1 gear ratio, and 50-pound braid. Three trailers: Rage Craw, Menace Grub, and Rage Swimmer.'
Hackney: 'Three favorite swim jig colors are green pumpkin, black and blue, and white. Choose color depending on water cover and what the dominant forage is.'
Hackney: 'Slow and steady typically wins the fishing race. When within striking distance of winning, I do not want to fish too hard and spin out.'

Hackney: 'If I am struggling or close to winning and may need one or two more fish, I will slowly fish a shakyhead worm or a wacky-rigged worm thoroughly, working the cover. Time and again this tactic produces more bass than running up and down the lake chucking and winding.'

Hackney: 'You cannot be a one-dimensional bass fisherman. The pros who continue to cash checks are good at every form of bass fishing. I always have a spinning rod and finesse lure on my casting deck.'
Hackney: 'My favorite finesse baits are the wacky worm, the shakyhead worm, and a dropshot worm. I am also learning the Ned Rig and sometimes fish a hair jig.'

Hackney: 'I spend a ton of time watching old tournament videos to learn about structures in the lake, what bass should be doing at that time of year, what portion of the lake produces the biggest bass, and what lures are most productive.'

Hackney: 'Before each tournament I completely reorganize my boat for the expected pattern. Going from Florida spawning bass to Tennessee winter pattern means getting rid of all the heavy tackle and packing smaller tackle.'

Hackney: 'Think of fall as a faster pre-spawn in reverse. Fish migrate back to the exact same cover they use pre-spawn. They get on boat docks, cypress trees, heavy cover just outside spawning areas. They are aggressive and feeding up for winter.'

Hackney: 'Cold fronts in the fall are not as negatively effective as in the spring. I like those cold front days in fall because the cool water triggers them to feed. I do not like warm Indian summer days in the fall.'
Hackney: 'In summer fish swim around in the abyss on nothing, but in fall as water temps drop they get back on cover - brush piles, boat docks, grass edges. Now we can pinpoint and target them.'

Hackney: 'As grass dies and milfoil goes away in fall, it confines bluegill to whatever cover is left. When bluegill concentrate on remaining cover, big largemouth go there too. Everybody has their feed bag on.'

Hackney: 'One big log sticking up on a flat - never run past that in the fall. That is about the keyest place you can find. Isolated cover on flats holds the bigger, quality fish, not the little schoolers chasing shad.'

Hackney: 'The single most useful thing I can say is learn how to find them. The deal is not about our lures, it is about where we are fishing. Two completely different plastics caught fish because the fish were there and we were fishing on the bottom.'
Hackney: 'Largemouth bass do not think. They are simple prehistoric creatures. They eat, rest, and have sex once a year. They do not hide behind rocks because they are afraid.'
Hackney: 'If Rule 1 did not apply, every pro at the top of every tournament would have caught them on the same exact bait. The only thing we have in common is that we found them.'

Hackney: 'The topographical map is the number-one screen on the depth finder I use to find bass. I mark spots where I catch a bass at a certain depth, and it instantly shows me similar depth structure elsewhere on the lake'

Hackney: 'If I lose a big bass today I can get over that tragedy in about 5 minutes. Instead of being upset, I think: you've found the right lure, you're in the correct depth on the best structure, so keep doing the same thing and you will catch another one'

Hackney: 'One of the secrets to winning tournaments is controlling all the variables — sharpen hooks, have fresh line. Once those tasks are done and a bass still gets off, accept the bass just beat you'

Hackney: 'I'm a really good visual learner. As I run down a lake I can see where bass should be. If you've put the time in on the water, your brain identifies the places you need to fish based on past experiences'
Hackney: 'On spinning gear I use 10-pound braid tied to a 3-6 foot fluorocarbon leader material, not casting-style fluorocarbon. The leader material is much stronger and abrasion-resistant'

Hackney: 'I fix little problems before they become major problems. A bass fisherman is only as good as the equipment he uses. I change lower unit fluid regularly and keep tools in the boat to fix almost anything'

Hackney: 'With a single hook I set the hook hard enough the bass shouldn't throw it. I put a lot of pressure on a fish, pulling hard and winding fast to break the fish's will to jump, keeping its head above water like it's on skis'
Hackney: 'A longer rod gives more distance on pitch and flip and more control over the fish. But shorter rods double as heavy casting rods for Texas rigged plastics and suit shorter anglers'

Hackney: 'Beware of lightweight rods — they feel good in the shop but beat you to death with a heavy jig or worm weight. The more the rod weighs, the less stress on your arm, wrist and hand at end of day'
Hackney: 'There is no industry standard for heavy/extra-heavy or fast/extra-fast tip — they are all a little different. Look at and mess with a rod before you buy it'
Hackney: 'During practice I think about almost anything — what to do next, how to fish, where to fish. During the actual tournament, I only think about the next bite'

Hackney: 'In Zone 1 you are dealing with resident fish — they just move in and out of close proximity from summer to fall and winter. As it cools off, fish move into places with less current and less wind, like dead ends'
Hackney: 'Confidence will make you fish a lure better. Without confidence there are casts anglers simply won't make. You'll do things that get you bites when you are confident'

Hackney: 'If you are not getting hung up you are not catching anything. You have to be deflecting that bait — that is what makes a crankbait better on inactive fish than anything. It spurs defensive bites'

Hackney: 'The big thing for me that makes fishing fun is figuring them out — making those changes. Having that weather change and knowing what bait to pick up and catching a big bass. That is the part I like'

Hackney: 'At least 75 percent of catching bass is about fishing in the right spot. All the high-dollar tackle in the world won't help if the fish aren't there. It is rare — like never — that bass are keyed on a specific bait in a specific color'

Hackney: 'I fished cypress trees with a chartreuse-black crankbait, next day another angler caught just as many from same spot on a dark tube, then Aaron Martens caught a mess on a drop shot — the bass were there and feeding, it was location'

Hackney: 'Use your knowledge, your electronics and anything else. Once you find them figure out the details — are they suspended, on the bottom, breaking the surface? Then make an intelligent decision on what bait to throw'
Hackney: 'Don't overcomplicate things. Bass fishing isn't brain surgery'

Hackney: 'After you find the bass, figure out whether they are on the bottom or suspended. At Okeechobee in 2 feet of water, smaller bass were on the bottom and bigger ones were suspended — hitting the lure as soon as it hit the water'

Hackney: 'Sometimes different size fish hold at different depths in the same place. If you are catching smaller bass at one depth, try a different depth. There is only a short time in the spring when big bass mix with little bass'
Hackney: 'When the water is dirty or muddy, bass will suspend more. They don't like to be on the bottom when it is real dark because they can't see. In the end they are sight feeders'

Hackney: 'In cold water under something thick with sun shining, bass are much more likely to be up high under the mat or heavy overhead cover. I have actually seen them touching the underside of a mat — they grab whatever you pitch the second it breaks through the canopy'
Hackney: 'If fishing shallow, let your bait fall to the bottom and drag or short-hop it. If no bites, pull it up to the surface. After a couple catches you can figure things out'

Hackney: 'Most of the time I use a Rage Craw trailer. Sometimes I use a small swimbait if the bass are on shad. I use the standard swim jig in colder water with less cover and the heavy cover jig most other times'
Hackney: 'If you can cast and retrieve you can fish a swim jig. If you are fishing spinnerbaits now you have the ability to fish a swim jig. I always have one tied on'

Hackney: 'After bass finish spawning their primary instinct is to feed. Females shift focus to recovery and nourishment quickly after spawning — sometimes over multiple beds. Males remain shallow longer guarding fry'

Hackney: 'Bass are opportunistic feeders. During post-spawn they target dominant forage — on many lakes bluegill begin spawning right after bass, becoming the primary food source. On other lakes it may be shad, blueback herring, or crawfish'
Hackney: 'Check the mouths of caught fish for signs of their recent meals — shad tails, crawfish antennae, or bluegill fins. That detail often leads to the right bait choice'

Hackney: 'Bass rarely move very far in a short period of time. A far more likely explanation than movement is they simply shut down — ate their fill the day before or environmental factors turned them negative'

Hackney: 'If the water is cold and warms quickly, bass often move vertically into the upper foot or two of the water column, especially in heavily stained or muddy water. SONAR won't find them and you can fish a long time without a bite'

Hackney: 'I fished a tree top three days in a row knowing a 3.5 pounder was in it. First two days no bite. Third day she nearly ripped the rod out of my hand — same jig, nothing different. Sometimes they go negative for reasons we don't understand'

Hackney: 'I let water temperature dictate which jig to throw in shallow cover. Warm water means swim jig, cold water means flipping jig. It only takes a few casts to determine which method works better'

Hackney: 'A 90- or 60-degree line tie covers up half the bite on your hook point. With a big weighted jig like 3/4 oz, the line tie can catch in the roof of their mouth instead of the hook point — feels hooked but the hook never penetrated'

Hackney: 'With low-stretch line, if a lighter-wire hook flexes it will cut its way out of a fish's mouth. A heavy wire hook sticks a lot deeper because it won't flex — it is a low-stretch hook designed for a lot of pressure'
Hackney: 'Flipping gear is a 7-foot 10-inch flipping stick, Quantum 200 HD reel at 7.6 ratio, with 50 or 65-lb braid depending on fall rate desired'
Hackney: 'A bladed jig has the visual appeal of swimming a jig — a very natural look — plus vibration. It does not have rattles, so it is more subtle than a lipless crankbait'

Hackney: 'You can manipulate a bladed jig so much just by changing trailer. A creature bait holds the lure higher, a blade minnow runs deeper. A 1/2-ounce jig with trailer change can fish shallower than a 3/8-ounce with a different trailer'

Hackney: 'Below 55 degrees I switch from a Rage Craw to a KVD plastic chunk. I still want a big profile but not a lot of action — everything bass eat in winter is cold-blooded and moves slower, so a crawfish does not root around and shoot everywhere in the 40s'
Hackney: 'In December-January I target blown-in mats around cypress trees, dead-end canals, oilfield canals. Big females sit in those dead ends because a month later they will be spawning'

Hackney: 'For winter deep-water jig fishing on lowland reservoirs with stumps and channel swings in 6-8 feet, I use 16-20 pound fluorocarbon. On hard structure like rock I go 14-16 pound. Only downsize to 12 in extremely deep clear water'

Hackney: 'In winter it is almost impossible to fish a jig too slow. Use a lighter jig — the bigger jig you throw the quicker it reaches bottom and the tendency is to move it. A lighter one naturally slows you down'
Hackney: 'I always use a super-high-speed reel for jig fishing — 8.3:1 ratio. Super fast and super strong, works with both braid and fluorocarbon'

Hackney: 'There are really three jigs I use year-round: the Hack Attack Flipping Jig for grass, the Structure Jig for casting on fluorocarbon, and the Denny Brauer Premier Pro-Model with a rattle for boat docks and brush piles'

johnston.insight: Johnston: You have to be able to judge the size of bedding fish without catching them to make sure you're going to get the right ones. Spend time gathering waypoints on spawning areas before tournament day.
Johnston: After catching spawners early, chase postspawners in deeper water at spots no one else knows about. Having secret deeper-water spots for postspawn fish can produce multiple 5-pounders.
Johnston: When you have a trusted practice partner, each head in a different direction every day during practice to effectively split the lake in half, giving you double the practice coverage.
Johnston: If I find a pattern, I'll share it so my partner can replicate that pattern somewhere else. We don't split spots or fish, because then we're just competing against each other.
Johnston: When fishing an unfamiliar lake, go in with open minds rather than preconceived notions about what fish should be doing.
Johnston: Run a bunch of isolated spots where there's one or two fish, hitting upwards of 40 different spots per day. Don't be afraid to stop on new water that looks good to test it out.
Johnston: Fish regular rock/weed transition areas, anywhere from 12 feet deep right up to about a foot of water.
Johnston: I need the sun and the calm. Most people want the wind to blow, but I want it to be really tough on everybody because I figured out a method to catch them when it's really slick.

Johnston: On pressured fisheries, if fish see your bait fall they spook every time. Overshoot them and drag your bait to the fish - they'll eat it every time. The key is not letting them see your bait fall.

Johnston: Key on isolated rock piles off the shore in 4 feet or less. Anything that provides a transition - rock pile, patch of sand, clump of weeds - a shallow bass can key on is fair game in summer.
Johnston: When you haven't been to a lake before, you're not going in with a preconceived notion of what the fish were doing last time. You can really figure it out and zone in on them real good.

Johnston: When wind creates waves and stains shallow water, move to sheltered secondary spots that aren't too close to shallow water so they aren't stained. When shallow is blown out, fish a break with a moving bait.

Johnston: Run shallow rock piles starting on the east end of the lake and working west, hitting about 20 isolated piles. Spend the first two days running shallow and adjust deeper only if conditions force it.
Johnston: You don't get a ton of largemouth on northern smallmouth lakes, but when you do, they're big ones. Those largemouth catches are crucial to tournament success.

Johnston: Rod power matters when flipping. I switched from a 4-power GLX flipping stick to a 5-power NRX and the stiffer butt section was so powerful I was ripping holes in fish mouths on the hookset, losing four 4-pounders. Going down to a 4-power NRX solved it - never lost a fish on the jig afterward.

Johnston: On the Potomac, I found fish out in the grass, not in the real thick stuff. I was casting to catch them, not flipping. When that failed due to muddy water from rain, I moved to flipping grass on the Greenway Flats.
Johnston: We're not really guys that do a lot of research on the Internet. We just back the boat in and figure it out.
Johnston: For tossing swimbaits up shallow for smallmouth, use a G.Loomis GLX 892S spinning rod with a Shimano Sustain 2500 reel. For deep drop-shot, use NRX 822S rods.

Johnston: Cover water quickly with a moving bait like a crankbait or swimbait. If you drifted across the whole lake you could catch bass everywhere - that's why you need to cover water and find the sweet spots where they are grouped up.
Johnston: Keep rod choices simple on Lake St. Clair - two swimbaits, a tube, and a drop-shot are all you need on the deck.
Johnston: If shallow water has a chalky tint to it, head back out to the lake. Water color changes indicate you need to move to cleaner water.
Johnston: There are so many smallmouths in St. Clair that you can catch them anywhere, even in shallow pencil reeds where you'd expect largemouth. A 4-pound smallmouth in reeds is not unusual.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence River, sonar doesn't always mark bass on drop-offs because river bass are tight to the bottom. An underwater camera with a trolling fin and extra weight lets you get down to depth and get a stable picture of what's happening.

Johnston: Drop-offs are key spots against which bass can push bait. Use an HD underwater camera with on-screen water temperature and depth to verify fish presence on these spots when sonar can't distinguish bottom-hugging bass.
Johnston: When pre-fishing, very rarely make a cast, because you'd only sting fish you could catch in the tournament. Instead, closely watch sonar looking for the right kind of bottom.
Johnston: You can find smallmouth on rock, you can find them on sand. It doesn't really seem to matter as long as the bottom's not covered in moss or weeds.

Johnston: Where there's a lot of angling pressure, downsize your line and hooks, and try to get off the beaten path. Look for spots on the spot - anything different like rock and weed mixed in, or bigger pockets in grass. It's something small or isolated where people aren't all over it.
Johnston: For smallmouths, use a seven-foot six-inch rod that doesn't have a lot of backbone, allowing long casts with small baits on light line. That's especially important in clear water.

Johnston: Pressured St. Lawrence smallmouth are extremely sensitive. Catch one and they shut down. Time and distance are the best remedy - make a big run away from the spot between catches. It's like night and day from even two years ago.
Johnston: Use Garmin LiveScope to track down deep smallmouth and make the perfect cast. In rough conditions with big waves, efficiency drops because it's harder to use LiveScope accurately.

Johnston: Use a four-bait rotation for picky pressured smallmouth. Figured out something about rigging and presentation that makes the difference - it has to do with how you rig and present the bait, not just what bait you use.

Johnston: Use a Daiwa 7'5" rod designed especially for a mini-jig - lighter tip action but plenty of power to handle a 1.5-ounce weight. Pair with an 8.8:1 reel spooled with 65-pound Seaguar TactX braid or 20-22-pound Seaguar Tatsu fluorocarbon. Cover dictates line choice.
Johnston: Use an EWG-style jig hook with thicker wire for braided line, and a round-bend wide-gap hook for fluorocarbon. The EWG hook is slightly harder to get full penetration on fluoro.
Johnston: Down south with a smaller jig, you will get smallmouth, largemouth and spots. A compact jig gets just as many bites as a full-size jig, if not more, even in Florida.
Johnston: All jigs come with rattles, but tear them off if you don't think they'll help. Use a chunk-style trailer that matches the jig skirt color.
Johnston: Jig box should contain 3/8-, 1/2-, 5/8- and 3/4-ounce jigs for flipping.

Johnston: Consistency and success on tour comes from being a well-rounded angler. I don't feel like there's a technique at which I cannot excel, which means it's a matter of figuring out what I need to do and then executing to the best of my abilities.

Johnston: On Lake Ontario in big waves, I prepared my whole life for that moment. All of my equipment is up to the task. Don't abandon the plan to run to the lake because of wind and waves if you've prepared properly and know what lives on those spots.
Johnston: Won two Elite events in one season on very different fisheries — one largemouth chasing shallow fish, and the other deep smallmouth. Being able to adapt between the two is key.

Johnston: At the Bassmaster Classic on Lake Guntersville, I was getting bites on the deeper edge of the eel grass in practice and committed to it. But when the wind blew and everything got muddy, it hurt the deep bite. Having a shallow backup plan is important.

Johnston: Run a milk run on Lake Ontario starting at the closest spot and working out to the farthest, then come in. Use Garmin LiveScope to cover water while drifting. Pretty simple - just cover a lot of water over rock structure in 18 to 42 feet.
Johnston: Local knowledge is invaluable. Having a lot of spots on Lake Ontario means you only need two days of pre-fishing when you know the place well.
Johnston: On big water, commit to the lake when the wind direction gives you shelter from a point or land feature. Staying sheltered can reduce 8-foot waves to 6-footers, which is fishable.
Johnston: I learned a lot from my brother Chris about being patient, slowing down and being methodical when using electronics out deep.
Johnston: In team tournaments, practice separately and then merge findings for the good of the team. We made a pact to do this from our early teens and it has always worked well.
Johnston: The real key on deep sand flats is Garmin LiveScope — without it you're at a huge disadvantage. You can see 90% of the fish way out and cast to them before they're under the boat.
Johnston: Smallmouth groups on sand flats are transient. You could find a group one day but the next day they were gone. Keep covering water until you come across a group of them.
Johnston: For deep smallmouth drop shot, use a 7-foot medium-light Daiwa Tatula Elite Dropshot rod, size 3000 Daiwa Exist spinning reel, 15-pound braid to 8-pound fluorocarbon leader (10 feet).

Johnston: Everything from your technique to the fish themselves pretty much slows down when it gets cold in that 40-degree range. You have to adapt and change tactics — target the middle to bottom of the water column and work lures with a sluggish style.
Johnston: For smallmouth bass in cold water, they seem to key in on tube jigs or a swimbait better than jerkbaits. For largemouth, suspending jerkbaits and deep-drop jigs are the go-to.

Johnston: In cold water when fishing shallower 2- to 10-foot depths, anchor outside of a sand patch, boulder, or weed edge and work the area hard for multiple hookups. These bass are moving super slow — you really have to work an area over hard with repeated casts to get a strike.

Johnston: During pre-fishing, cruise around the tournament site examining the lake bottom and looking for irregularities that might represent opportunities. Rarely make a cast during these tours — why risk snagging a fish in practice that you could catch in competition?
Johnston: Practice helps, but research makes the difference. Pool knowledge with partners, develop and test theories about how to do things differently.

Johnston: On calm days for deep smallmouth, cast the drop shot out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them. Adjust presentation to match conditions even though the bait stays the same.

Johnston: Targeted an 80-yard stretch of bottom in 33 feet loaded with big smallmouth. Had about three hours of fishing time per day due to the long run, but committed to the spot and made it worth it.

Johnston: Don't abandon the plan to run to Lake Ontario because of 8- to 10-foot waves if you've prepared. Even with only about three hours of fishing time per day, the quality of the spot makes it worth it.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, catch them shallow on sand flats, rockpiles, weed edges all the way to 35-40 feet on little high spots, rocks and deeper weed edges. There's not one specific thing — hit as many spots as you can until you run into them.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, my bigger fish (5.5 and 6.75 lbs) came shallow. Hit 12 spots and the last one had them — sometimes the late-day push to new water pays off.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, 24 pounds really isn't a good day — anything over 25 is what you need to win. It's a special place.

Johnston: When a main pattern starts dwindling, have a backup plan ready. At St. Johns, flipping pads on the main river when the bed fish pattern faded was probably the best decision I could have made — it produced my biggest fish.
Johnston: Garmin Perspective Mode is critical for bed fishing in low-visibility conditions. Relied heavily on it along with the Power-Pole Move trolling motor for targeting bed fish.
Johnston: Use a larger reel like the Daiwa Tatula 150 size for swimbaits and big crankbaits — it holds more line and helps cast big baits further.
Johnston: Fiberglass rods for crankbaits allow bass an extra second to really get the bait, so you hook more crankbait fish compared to graphite.

Johnston: Nobody remembers who finishes second in the Classic, so throw big baits and go for the win. Expect big swimbaits, deep-diving crankbaits, Carolina rigs, and football jigs to be the best chances.
Johnston: In a lake full of timber, idle a bunch and use LiveScope to play a big part in finding fish. The technology is essential when navigating and fishing around heavy timber cover.
Johnston: Where we grew up, it was all largemouth fishing — the smallmouth weren't as relevant. We grew up punching mats, flipping reeds and that kind of stuff.
Johnston: Always have a backup plan ready. At St. Johns, I had one I hadn't needed to use all week but felt the final day would be the day to deploy it — being prepared to pivot is essential.
Johnston: Watch bed fish on live sonar to monitor their locations and reactions to your presentation. This lets you know when they're cooperative and when to move on.

Johnston: Managed fish well and made good decisions across four days. When the main bed-fishing pattern dwindled on the final day, shifting to flipping pads on the main river was the best decision — it produced the biggest fish of the day.
Johnston: Favorite techniques are flipping and sight fishing. Favorite lakes are Lake Simcoe and Lake Ontario.

Johnston: Pro picks from 6th Sense include the Provoke jerkbait series, Slice Blade Bait, Divine Jigging Spoon, Party Minnow (drop shot bait), Crube tube, Stroker Craw, NedFry 4.6, Juggle Minnow, Glitch soft plastic, and Whale Swimbait 6.0 in various shad and natural colors.

Johnston: Use Garmin Perspective Mode when seeing bed fish in low-visibility conditions. This was one of two main tools (along with flipping and drop shot baits) for bed fishing at the St. Johns River.

Johnston: Always verify your time against official tournament clocks, not just your boat graphs or cell phone. A mismatch cost me 7 pounds of penalties (1 pound per minute late) and dropped me from 7th to 60th, likely costing a shot at Angler of the Year and winning the tournament.
Johnston: For big smallmouth drop shot fishing, pairs a Daiwa Ballistic 2500 reel with a Cody Meyer Tatula Elite signature drop shot rod. The parabolic bend and smooth drag are critical on 6-lb test.
Johnston: The soft rod setup was critical to letting him downsize line to get more bites at the event. Having the soft rod to absorb runs and headshakes helped.
Johnston: Fishes two drop shot rods -- a Tatula Elite 7'0" with 8-lb test and a 6'10" light-action BLX with 6-lb test. The EXIST 3000 reel drag is absolutely key when fishing big smallmouth.
Johnston: If you can get through tough southern events and just have a shot at AOY going into the northern swing, be happy. The St. Lawrence and northern fisheries are where you can make up ground.
Johnston: Get me to the St. Lawrence -- familiarity with home waters breeds confidence. Being within striking distance going into familiar fisheries is the ideal AOY scenario.
Johnston: Save certain previously-located fish for when you need a big bite. Start tournament days with known bed fish or saved difference-makers to build an early cushion, then go fishing.
Johnston: Don't burn too many fish -- get a limit, upgrade a little, but save fish for subsequent days. Play the long game over a multi-day tournament.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, fish a mix of rock, sand, and weed in 15 feet or less. Hit as many spots as you can and hope the right ones bite.

Johnston: A Flogger (cone-shaped clear-screen surface viewing device) lets you see your bait, where the fish is, and the sweet spot. You see how the fish reacts to your bait -- it speeds up the process.

Johnston: Zebra mussels clearing the water in the St. Lawrence River system led to goby proliferation, which fueled dramatic smallmouth growth. Clearer water also enabled new sight-based angling techniques.
Johnston: When team fishing with a partner, practice in separate boats on official practice days to cover more water, then combine information for tournament days.
Johnston: Sharing all practice information with a trusted partner doubles your water coverage. If your partner says there are no fish in an area, you don't have to waste time checking it yourself.

Johnston: When you and your practice partner are essentially the same person when it comes to fishing, you know how they'll fish and where they'll go. That eliminates redundancy and maximizes efficiency.

Johnston: In fall, smallmouth group tightly on specific spots and feed heavily before winter. Early fall fish hold on offshore rock piles and summer haunts. Mid to late fall they push shallow to island tips, sand flats, and points with isolated cover.
Johnston: The key to fall smallmouth is finding something different -- a lone weed clump, isolated rock, or small patch of gravel that holds bait and draws the school.

Johnston: Fall smallmouth fishing is all about location and timing. Cover water fast with reaction baits to find active groups, then slow down with finesse presentations to catch the rest. If fish stop biting a tube, switch to Ned rig or drop shot.
Johnston: Leave resting schools of fall smallmouth and return later to catch more. Don't burn through a school in one pass.
Johnston: Use 10-12 lb fluorocarbon for finesse presentations and 15-20 lb braid with fluorocarbon leader for reaction baits when targeting fall smallmouth.
Johnston: For fall smallmouth, use natural shad or baitfish colors for reaction baits, and green pumpkin, brown, or goby for bottom presentations.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence/Lake Ontario system, fish from 10 feet all the way to 45 feet. Run stuff across the full depth range to find where fish are congregated.

Johnston: Prefers fishing Lake Ontario over the St. Lawrence River current. Fish on the lake are more congregated in certain little areas. Even in rough weather with 8-10 foot waves, committed to the lake because he knows what lives out there.

Johnston: In rough weather on big water, limit your spots to a few known groups of fish and rotate them. When you only have 3 hours to fish due to long runs and rough conditions, pick your best 3-4 spots and rotate.
Johnston: Runs a Garmin 1643 for LiveScope (watching fish eat) and 12-inch EchoMap Ultras for the rest of his electronics needs.
Johnston: Drop shot setup uses 16-lb Seaguar JDM PEX8 Braid to 8-lb Seaguar Tatsu fluoro, #2 Gamakatsu drop shot hook, 3/8-oz tungsten weight, Daiwa Exist 3000 reel, 6'10" L Daiwa BLX rod.

Johnston: Early practice in flat calm sunny conditions lets you see bedding fish that later arrivals can't. If wind stains the water later, competitors won't see the fish and fewer boats will fish the area.
Johnston: For bed fishing, uses green pumpkin on everything. Fish bite quicker with a more natural bait. You don't need to see the bait yourself.
