Johnston: The key to winning on bed fish is covering a lot of water -- doing laps -- because new fish are pulling up on beds constantly. Catch them fairly quick when you find one and move on.

Johnston: Flipping setup is a 3.7-inch 6th Sense Bongo craw on a 4/0 Gamakatsu G-Finesse Heavy Cover Hook, 1/4-oz tungsten weight, 20-lb Seaguar Tatsu fluoro, Daiwa Pitch/Flip reel, 7'5" Daiwa Tatula Elite rod.
Johnston: Uses Garmin LiveScope Perspective Mode to spot individual bed fish in rippled conditions when you can't see them visually. Cast to them and catch on first attempt.

Johnston: A quiet trolling motor (Power-Pole Move) is critical for bed fishing. Fish dart off beds if your trolling motor is on full power. Being able to cover more water quicker and quieter is a big key.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, fish a lot of stuff other guys wouldn't fish -- little small subtle things. Spots vary with no similarities: some rocks, some breaks, some little grass patches, depths from 15 to 42 feet.

Johnston: On well-known fisheries, limit practice time to about a day and a half to avoid poachers. If guys see boats in the middle of nowhere, they'll go check it. Rely on experience and knowledge of where fish will be.

Johnston: Runs 2 twelve-inch units on console -- left unit for left side imaging, right for right side imaging. Gives a bigger picture so you can see things clearer. On bow, 2 ten-inch Garmin units stacked: front is LiveScope, bottom is mapping and 2D.

Johnston: LiveScope wasn't a huge deal for the St. Lawrence Open win. A lot of spots were found by side imaging in practice, then casting to the structure rather than necessarily seeing individual fish.

Johnston: Power-Pole Drift Paddles are key for slowing drift over areas. Half paddle slows about 25%, full paddles slow 50-80%. Allows slower drop shot drags which get more bites. Can adjust angle to drift across a shoal at an angle.
Johnston: Drop shot setup is 10-lb Seaguar Smackdown Flash Green Braid to 8-lb Seaguar Tatsu fluoro, #2 Gamakatsu drop shot hook, 3/8-oz Woo Tungsten teardrop weight.

Johnston: Sets Garmin LiveScope gain at 60-65 and distance at 75 feet for his comfort zone. As soon as a fish appears on screen, he makes a precise cast landing within 5 feet of the fish using muscle memory for the 75-foot range.

Johnston: Forward-facing sonar is particularly important for picking out subtleties like anomalies in grass lines and spotting individual fish. Found a sunken barrel on a featureless Lake St. Clair flat with fish sitting on it -- never would have found it without forward-facing sonar.

Johnston: Fish pinned to the bottom may elude 2D sonar, but with forward-facing sonar you can spot wiggling fins that betray hidden bass. When you throw your bait to them, you see their tail and fins speed up as they race to your bait.
Johnston: With LiveScope at 75 feet, you don't waste time chasing fish or making 2-3 attempts. Just look where the trolling motor is facing and make the cast. Extremely efficient -- no wasted casts.

Johnston: Developed the SPRO CJ Smasher as a versatile 3-inch worm that is durable (Dura Tuff material), scent-infused (Amino Bite), and can be rigged multiple ways including drop shot, shakey head, Ned rig, and Texas rig.

Johnston: Learned from a previous mistake of spending too much time in the river. Committed to fishing Lake Ontario exclusively even with only about 3 hours of fishing time per day due to long runs and rough conditions.
Johnston: Did most of his work on an 80-yard stretch of bottom in 33 feet loaded with big smallmouth. Even in 8-10 foot waves, the deep fish were worth the commitment.

Johnston: First angler to earn a Bassmaster Century Club Belt with all smallmouth (100-5 in 2022). Repeated it with 102 lbs in 2024. Key is committing to known big-fish areas even when conditions are tough.
Johnston: When extreme fishing pressure pushes smallmouth off typical spots, hunt individual bass in areas with less pressure on flats with isolated structure.
Johnston: Use Garmin LiveScope to pick apart structure and look for bigger fish when smallmouth are pressured and not in their typical locations.
Johnston: Pressured St. Lawrence smallmouth can range from 2 feet to 35 feet deep in the same day. Be willing to fish the full water column.
Johnston: Heavy recreational and tournament pressure on the St. Lawrence causes fish to behave differently and not go to where they're typically at.
Johnston: This article is identical content to johnston-jumps-into-day-1-lead-at-the-st-lawrence-river.txt, republished on thefishingwire.com. See that extraction for all opinions and knowledge.
Johnston: At the St. Johns River, always look for spawning bass. You can always catch one or two big spawners and fill out a big limit.
Johnston: When your main bed fish pattern dwindles on the final day, make the adjustment to flip pads on the main river. Managing fish well and making good decisions is key.
Johnston: Rely heavily on Garmin Perspective Mode for locating and targeting bed fish in Florida spring runs.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, 24 pounds really isn't a good day and anything over 25 is what you need to win. It's a special place.
Johnston: Catch St. Lawrence smallmouth across a wide variety of habitats in a single day: shallow sand flats, rockpiles, weed edges, and deep high spots, rocks, and deeper weed edges in 35-40 feet.
Johnston: Hit as many spots as possible until you run into the bigger fish. Quality bites often come late in the day after working through many locations.
Johnston: At the St. Lawrence, bigger smallmouth (5.5 to 6.75 lbs) often come from shallow water rather than deep.
Johnston: Having a brother/partner who also competes lets you cover twice as much water in practice. It's like having 6 days on the water with no false information.
Johnston: When one angler is in contention to win, the other should give up their best water. No sense in both trying to do okay if one has a chance to win.
Johnston: Always swing for the win rather than fishing for a check. If you don't make a check, don't let it hinder your next tournament.
Johnston: Try to be good at a lot of things rather than great at one thing and not good with the others. We can catch them deep or shallow, largemouth or smallmouth.
Johnston: Playing the ledge-fishing game on southern reservoirs is very much like graphing structure in search of smallmouth on northern lakes.

Johnston: The most important structure for smallmouth in rivers is breaks - ledges, humps, anything where the depth drops off. These aren't always adjacent to shoreline; they could be a ledge next to the main channel, a hump rising from the channel, or an underwater point extending from shore.

Johnston: Humps in current are unique structure because fish will be in front of them feeding in the current, or resting in the slack water behind. You can catch them in both states, which makes humps especially productive.

Johnston: River smallmouth sit very tight to bottom in current and won't show up on sonar. Always take the time to drop a bait even if you don't mark fish. They're more stationary now due to goby forage - why move when there's so much food right there?

Johnston: Set up a milk run of big boulders for smallmouth. Each boulder typically holds one big smallmouth and maybe one small one. Stop on each, do a few short drifts, and move on. If you catch a big fish, move right away since a boulder only holds one big fish.

Johnston: Boulders used to replenish more quickly with resident gobies keeping smallmouth stationary, but now it takes longer. You generally can't return to boulders during a tournament, so you need to mark as many as possible.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, smallmouth prefer flat bottoms. Look for transitions from hard bottom to soft, or rock to sand, rather than gravel-to-rock transitions. These transitions are easy to spot on Garmin electronics via color change on the bottom.
Johnston: Prefer deeper transitions for St. Lawrence smallmouth. Fish used to be catchable shallow but now go deeper sooner due to pressure.

Johnston: Use an Aqua-Vu underwater camera (HD10i Pro with onscreen water temp and depth) to get a true understanding of bottom structure in current. Attach a weight to stabilize it in the current for a clear picture in clear water.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, the depth of the break doesn't matter much - it might drop 3 feet, 10 feet, or 20+ feet. It's simply a place where fish can push bait up against the ledge so they don't have to chase it.

Johnston: The key to tournament success in Canada was figuring out smallmouth on inland lakes that had been dominated by largemouth. When zebra mussels and gobies began impacting lakes, thinking outside the box and expanding beyond traditional flipping mats, docks and milfoil was critical.
Johnston: Having a sibling rivalry with brother Chris pushes both to be better. They help each other out, which is especially advantageous during practice.
Johnston: Without a doubt, my goal is to win the AOY Title.
Johnston: This article is identical content to meet-elites-cory-johnston.txt, published under a different URL. See that extraction for all opinions and knowledge.

Johnston: Co-designed the SPRO CJ Smasher with brother Chris. The 3-inch soft plastic uses Dura Tuff material that is super soft yet super tough, floats completely for great action, and features Amino Bite scent with amino acids that create a scent trail through the water column.
Johnston: The CJ Smasher is best suited for drop shot, jig head, or Texas rig applications. It works in both freshwater and saltwater.
Johnston: Any time we've been to the St. Johns, my mind goes toward trying to find spawning bass. You can always catch one or two big spawners and fill out a big limit.
Johnston: This is a news roundup article summarizing his 2025 St. Lawrence Open win (74-15 three-day total). No new technique details beyond what is covered in the primary win article.

Johnston: After ice-out, smallmouth come into back bays and shallows to feed. Then big fish disappear - they go back over deep water and suspend to get ready for the spawn. Once the spawn gets close, big females pull back in.
Johnston: Smallmouth start making beds when water reaches the mid-50s. The optimal spawning temperature is between 55 and 65 degrees.
Johnston: A lot of the biggest smallmouth spawn first, but there's no set rule. You can definitely find a few big ones kicking around late into the spawn.

Johnston: Sight fish for bedding smallmouth as deep as 20 feet in clear water, or as shallow as 1 foot in the same area. Start looking in the 8-10 foot range. In tournament practice, just look without throwing so you know what you're getting into.
Johnston: Smallmouth like to spawn on something - rock, gravel, a boulder, patch of weeds, a stick, cracks of slate. Sometimes they'll be on a gravel flat in the middle of nowhere.

Johnston: Unlike largemouth, female smallmouth generally hang out shallow after they spawn because the water is warmer. They feed up post-spawn. Some stay shallow for a month or two; some go straight to deep water depending on the fishery.
Johnston: A late cold spring with high water isn't bad for smallmouth fishing - it can actually make it better.
Johnston: For bedding smallmouth, throw basic baits like a tube, drop shot, or Senko right into the bed.

Johnston: This article is behind a B.A.S.S. membership paywall. The accessible text only states Johnston gives a bait breakdown of the key lure used while battling waves of Lake Ontario en route to his second Elite Series title of 2024. No technique details are accessible.

Johnston: This article is behind a B.A.S.S. membership paywall. The accessible text only states it covers baits used by the Top 10 pros at the 2024 MAXAM Tire Bassmaster Elite at St. Johns River. No technique details are accessible.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence River, sonar doesn't always mark bass on drop-offs, which are key spots where bass push bait. River bass sit very tight to bottom. The Aqua-Vu HD10i Pro with on-screen water temp and depth, using the XD Trolling Fin and extra XD weight, gets down to depth and provides a stable picture of what's happening.

Johnston: Drop-offs are the key structure on the St. Lawrence because bass can push bait up against them. Sonar won't mark these tight-to-bottom fish, making an underwater camera essential for confirming fish presence.

kvd.insight: KVD: 'Fall reservoirs — shad move shallow to warmest water in creek arms. Go as far up a creek as your boat allows. If it has a defined channel, bass use it for ambush points. Pods of bait near surface plus birds indicate a really good afternoon bite as sun warms water. Fall drawdown exposes cover you can't see in summer.'

KVD: 'Natural lakes in fall — start on outside weed edge of biggest flat on main lake. Crank finds irregularities in the weedline, then pitch finesse baits to those spots. Inside turns are where you find concentrations — the closer to the bank that turn is, the better. Bass can stay shallow in milfoil/coontail even in the 40-degree range.'

KVD: 'Fall retrieve progression: go fast and erratic early in fall, less erratic late. The later in fall, the more important it is to hit bottom and keep it there at a slow, steady pace — just enough speed to keep the bill thumping. Use cranks right until ice-up but switch to tighter wobble action below 50 degrees.'

KVD: 'Always look for baitfish in fall — herons, seagulls, terns, bait on the graph, visible bait in the water. Seeing shad on top indicates a really good afternoon bite. From there, look for the type of cover the lake has in the areas the most abundant baitfish want to use.'

KVD: For Knoxville Classic (Fort Loudoun/Tellico, mid-March), the key baits are: Red Eye Shad, flatside crankbait (Hardliner 35), jerkbait, and Thunder Cricket. Power fishing will dominate. Most winning fish will come from Loudoun with largemouth. Fish are staging to spawn but cold winter has them behind schedule. Forward-facing sonar will add 1-2 extra fish per day but won't change the core pattern.

KVD: At Knoxville, running to Tellico costs ~3 hours of fishing time per day. In a 3-day event needing 17-18lbs/day to win, you can't afford that. The winner will likely stay on Loudoun and maximize casts. Time management wins classics.

KVD: Pre-spawn one-two punch strategy: shallow crankbait or jerkbait on deeper edges outside pockets, then Thunder Cricket and Hardliner back in the bays where fish are moving to spawn. The combo covers both staging and migrating fish.

KVD: Three depth zones need three crankbaits in cold water. Shallow = flatside (Hardliner). Mid-range 6-12ft = round body wide wobble (Gravel Dog). Deep 12-15ft = smaller tight-action diver (3XD). Each zone demands a different action and profile.

KVD: In cold water cranking, use slow to medium retrieve. Start and stop often — any time during the retrieve, pausing can trigger a following bass into biting. Parallel the bank for long casts to keep the bait in the zone the entire cast.
KVD: Use lighter line in cold water cranking. Lighter line allows the bait to have its liveliest action and reach maximum depth. 10lb fluoro for the 3XD deep zone.

KVD: CC4 medium-heavy with a soft tip for cranking. 6.2:1 gear ratio reel (Paramag RX braking). The soft tip throws small cold-water crankbaits well and allows long casts for maximum time in the zone.

KVD: Bass in cold water really use their lateral line to hunt. Even in dirty water they can feel the pressure waves of a flatside bait. This is why flatsided crankbaits are 'magical' in cold water — the flat sides displace a lot of water.

KVD: Crawfish patterns dominate cold water. Clear water = natural/ghost crawfish (greens and browns). Stained = fire craw, brighter patterns like spring craw. If you see shad activity, shad patterns work too. Look at what color crawfish the fish are eating — is it green, brown, red, or purple? Match it.

KVD: In cold water, target transition areas where bass will stage before moving to spawning bays. Channel swing ends, flat edges, steeper banks transitioning to flatter areas, where different rock types come together. Chunk rock banks, bluffs, and riprap around bridges are prime for the mid-range crankbait.

KVD: 'Making sure your crankbait is contacting the bottom in any depth zone you're fishing — that is absolutely paramount.' The deflection off rocks, cover, even gravel makes a big difference in triggering cold water fish to bite.

KVD: Red Eye Shad sizes for depth control — 1/4oz for ultra-shallow (1-2ft), 3/8oz for shallow flats, 1/2oz as go-to, 3/4oz for 10-15ft deep drains. Plus tungsten Two Tap in 1/2 and 3/4oz — heavier tungsten fishes slightly deeper. Most anglers get stuck on 1/2oz and don't downsize when fish are shallow.

KVD: Line size is a major depth control tool for lipless crankbaits. Lighter line (10-12lb) = bait gets deeper (use for clear water, deeper grass, northern natural lakes). Heavier line (17-20lb) = bait stays higher and falls slower (use for shallow grass lakes like Guntersville). Rod tip up in shallow water.

KVD: CC3 medium-heavy composite (graphite/glass) cranking rod for lipless crankbaits. 6.2:1 gear ratio reel — not high-speed in cold water. The composite gives feel for detecting grass on hooks while having power to rip free.

KVD: Always change hooks to KVD/Mustad Triple Grip. Upsize: #2 on 1/2oz, #4 on 1/4oz, #1/0 on 3/4oz. Triple grip design locks fish on — critical because lipless crankbaits are known for poor strike-to-land ratio. Won the Classic catching 5-pounders hooked in the back and side thanks to these hooks.

KVD: Crawfish patterns dominate pre-spawn — reds in any water clarity (Rayburn Red, Delta Red, Fire Craw). Clear water = natural craw patterns (dark brown, baby carp, orange brim, bluegill). Stained/dirty = chartreuse, chartreuse sexy shad, brighter colors. Shad-focused systems (Tennessee River) = chrome sexy shad, gold sexy shad, straight sexy shad.

KVD: Fish the lipless crankbait like a jig in cold water. Short hop it along the bottom. Pull it around cover, deflect off rocks. Rip it out of grass, let it flutter down. Use rod tip to manipulate depth — tip up in shallow, down to get deeper. Make the bait fall as many times as possible — that's when bites happen.

KVD: Focus on large flats and spawning flats — where fish are coming and going. Look for deeper depressions, edges, drops, centers of drains within the flat. These subtle depth changes concentrate pre-spawn fish. At places like Toledo Bend and Sam Rayburn, fish deeper drains/ditches leading into spawning bays with 3/4oz.
KVD: 'Spring = crawfish patterns with red. Summer = shad and bluegill patterns. Fall = shad patterns in creek backs. When the 1.5 hits grass, clear hooks with a hard snap of the rod tip.'

KVD: 'Simple rule: lighter spinnerbait with smaller blades in shallow water, larger bait in deep water. The KVD Finesse Spinnerbait (3/8oz, one willow + smaller Colorado) is a great all-around option for weeds, stumps, docks.'

KVD: 'Offshore spinnerbait: 3/4oz, trim wire down a third, single willow blade. Cast, let helicopter down to bottom on tight line. Rip up sharply, let flutter back down. Provokes reaction strikes from suspended bass in summer heat.'

KVD: 'Tandem willow blades for horizontal retrieves through grass — less lift, runs deeper. Single blade for vertical presentations and helicopter flutter. Colorado/willow combo for slower retrieves and longer time in strike zone.'

KVD: 'Position boat to fish parallel to edges and cover. Keeps the bait in the strike zone longer. Short accurate casts (30-40ft) for control in dense cover. Soft underhand presentations so bait lands quietly in shallow water.'

KVD: At Toledo Bend, started each morning shallow on the shad spawn for 1-1.5 hours, then transitioned offshore to deep crank. The biggest fish all came deep cranking with 6XD, 8XD, 10XD. Didn't get a ton of bites but when he got bit, it was a giant.

KVD: 'Stop signs' = clean areas (hard bottom, rock transitions) that sit just outside major spawning coves. Fish stage on these as they move in and out of spawning areas. Concentrate on high-percentage corners. LakeMaster mapping showing bottom contours was the critical tool — 'the only reason I won.'
KVD: With a big lead, 30+ boats follow you. Spectators fish your spots after you leave. 'It's challenging when they're fishing your stuff.' One of the toughest aspects of being the frontrunner.

KVD: 'Grassy lakes have 3 depth zones: Shallow flats (0-4ft) — topwater, spinnerbaits, weedless plastics for sight-feeding fish. Weed edges (4-8ft) — the sweet spot, bass ambush from the edge, use soft plastics, jigs, creature baits. Deeper transitions (8ft+) — where grass tops out, fish suspend, use deep cranks, jigging spoons, blade baits. Cast along features, not straight across flats.'

KVD: 'Reading grassy lake water: color breaks (darker water = deeper edges), grass clumps (ambush points), wind-driven current seams (concentrate bass). Match bait to time of day AND depth zone: morning/evening = topwater/shallow, midday = soft plastics/spinnerbaits on weed edges, late/low light = crankbaits and deeper jigs.'

KVD: Cut the head off the blade minnow trailer to shorten it slightly. The water rushing around the chatterbait blade hits the shortened tail at the perfect distance for lively action. This is his #1 trailer for the Thunder Cricket.

KVD: 'Lakes that have grass change the most.' Creek channels don't move and bottom terrain is static, but grass lakes change a lot year to year — grass types, height, density all shift. Different areas of the same lake may have different grass types (hydrilla, eel grass, pepper grass, streaming grass).

KVD: In summer on grass lakes, big fish move offshore to the middle of the lake where the bait is. They don't stay near shore when it's hot. Look for scattered hydrilla clumps in the middle of the lake.
KVD: Heavy grass volume makes the water clearer. When grass clumps reach the surface, expect improved water clarity compared to areas without grass.

KVD: Choosing baits that are efficient for the type of cover in that depth zone is a big part of it. When grass is to the surface, a regular crankbait runs too deep — switch to a wake bait version to stay in the strike zone.

KVD: 'Drop shot rod: fast-tapering tip with backbone for long casts. Most bend in the first third for sensitivity without sacrificing control. On the bite, don't jerk the rod — just reel in and let the tip load. The sharp hook takes care of the rest. Keep finger on the line to detect strikes.'

KVD: 'The swim jig handles any shallow cover — grass, bushes, lily pads, wood, rocks. Swim it through pads like you're frogging. Skip it under docks. Fish everything from the bank to the outside grass edge. The heavy cover version powers through slime and algae.'

KVD: 'Cold water + no wind + high bright skies = shrunk strike zone, maybe only 1-2 feet. Don't fish fast or randomly. Slow down, be thorough, put the bait right in front of them. Pick small areas apart with repeated precise casts instead of running the lake.'

KVD: 'In stained water on tough days, Gold Sexy Shad is a go-to. High visibility, reflects light as sun gets higher, mimics shad without being too subtle. Stained water creates opportunity even with bluebird skies — bass rely on vibration and contrast for reaction strikes.'

KVD: 'In cold water (40s°F), bass are where the food is — locked into winter areas. Look for depressions, little ditches, anywhere slightly deeper with warm water around it. Graph for bluegill concentrations — if you see wall-to-wall bluegill, the bass will be there.'

KVD: 'Look for areas with dark bottom — dead vegetation, pad stems, rocks. Sun radiates anything dark on the bottom. Even in cold water, depth changes, depressions, and little ditches hold fish. Same principle north and south.'
KVD: 'Bass were locked into one corner of the hole where wind was pushing in. Anything that creates current, a funnel, or an edge leading onto a flat — that's where fish stack in cold water.'

KVD: 'The Rage Swimmer on a jig head, scooting along bottom, kicks up mud and sand. In clear northern lakes, all forage lives on the bottom — perch, bluegill, crawfish. Swim it through the column only when bluegill are suspended.'

KVD: 'For cold water swimbaits and finesse — Custom Lite 200 series shallow spool reel with 8lb braid + fluoro leader. Shallow spool manages line perfectly, no wind knots. Saves line since bass won't strip 100 yards.'

KVD: 'Depth is critical in cold water jerkbait fishing — you want the bait slightly above the fish or directly in front of them. They won't chase far when metabolism is low and they certainly don't go down after a bait.'
KVD: 'Color is crucial for jerkbaits — it's a very visual technique and fish get a really good look as the bait sits still between twitches. You need the right blend of visibility and naturalness.'

KVD: 'Wind and waves make light-line finesse nearly impossible on big water — can't feel bites with bow bouncing and line bowing. That's when crankbaits shine. Use 360 and forward-facing sonar to define bottom composition and find fish. Drift with the wind to conserve battery, make long downwind casts. Use electronics to find irregularities, then cover water with crankbaits.'

KVD: 'Rising water — bass push shallow and spread out. Best spring days can occur when warming water rises fast. Key on runoff areas: backs of creeks, culverts, residential yards, city street drainage. Water temp can vary 5 degrees between pockets — find the warmest runoff. Don't overlook ultra-shallow water — bass will put noses on seawalls and banks in inches of water.'

KVD: 'Cold + rising + muddy is the toughest spring combo. Bass in winter pattern are less likely to move to prespawn areas. Fish a flat-sided crankbait that displaces a lot of water, close to bottom or near objects. Make parallel casts to channel swings, steep banks, and riprap. Some fish always live shallow — target them.'

KVD: 'Deep bream beds are the coral reefs of freshwater — bass attractors all summer. Bream bed in 10-17ft well into August/September. Use side imaging to find them around deep docks, points, shoals, humps, flats, river bars. Full/new moons are peak activity but beds attract bass between spawns too. Mark with waypoints — bream use same beds year after year.'

KVD: 'Cold water jerkbait: solid pull (not snap), pause one second with rod pointed at bait for slack. By pulling short distances the bait runs 3-4 feet deeper. I still cover a lot of water by fishing parallel to edges — keeps the lure in the productive zone throughout the retrieve.'

KVD: 'As water temp drops, a jerkbait that floats in summer will sink in cold water. Switch to three No. 4 Mustad KVD Trebles and test balance — a little sink is fine but nothing rapid or unnatural. 6-10 medium heavy rod, 10lb fluorocarbon for long casts in clear water.'

KVD: 'Cold water jerkbait targets: drop-offs, edges, weedlines, bridge corners, riprap, bluff ends. Fish parallel to these edges and make multiple casts to high-percentage spots like laydown tips or grass points.'

KVD: 'Standard jerkbaits fish 4-5ft and 6-7ft zones. I wanted one that swims down to 12ft — the KVD 300 Series Deep-Diving Jerkbait. Longer bill, dives deeper, suspends, maintains incredible action at depth, and isn't hard to work. Fills a gap where no other jerkbait on the market could reach.'

KVD: 'Fall spinnerbaits: downsize to 3/16oz in clear water with small blades to match young-of-year baitfish. For heavy grass, go bigger with willowleaf blades. To fish fast and deep, put smaller blades on a 3/4oz head — the Scorcher design lets you burn it and keep it down.'

KVD: 'During fall, lakes become lower and clearer. Baitfish move shallow — great combination for spinnerbaits. The look of spinning blades and vibration really mimics all types of baitfish. Match weight to blades for the right retrieve speed and depth.'
KVD: Stained water compensates for tough conditions like no wind and high bright skies — you can still trigger reaction bites. In clear water under those conditions, it would be much harder.

KVD: In cold water, you can't just cover water. The strike zone is very small — fish won't swim 10 feet to eat your bait. You have to get it right in front of them and 'knock them on the head.' Slow down and be more thorough.

KVD: 'You've got to fish the conditions and dictate your lure choice, your patterns, your style, your speed to those conditions — and as they change, you want to adjust.' This applies to any tournament or fishing day.

KVD: 'When fishing expansive flats with breaks, mark waypoints at each catch. The pattern often isn't the obvious points — it's tapering breaks with inside turns cutting into the breakline. Those inside turns concentrate fish. Use a crankbait to survey, then switch to a jerkbait to work the productive spots more efficiently.'

KVD: '45 degree water at Sam Rayburn Texas is a lot different than 45 degree water in Michigan.' Shallower natural lakes and lowland reservoirs with vegetation — fish don't go deep regardless of cold, they stay shallow. Highland reservoirs (Table Rock, Dale Hollow) — fish live deeper, suspend, roam following shad. Approach must match lake type.

KVD: When water temps drop into the 30s, threadfin shad start dying off and bass gorge. Can be the best fishing you've ever had. Signs: watch for birds (seagulls, herons) hovering over the middle of the lake on frosty mornings — birds tell you where the bait is, and that's where bass will be.

KVD: In clear deep lakes with shad kills, fish often suspend. The higher they are in the water column, the more active they are. Fish off main lake points, humps, drops, centers of big bays — not near the bank. Alabama rig with spinners (keeps rig up in water column) or minnow/swimbait on spotlight head are best for covering water around suspended fish.

KVD: In extreme cold, downsize everything. Clearer the water = smaller the bait. Coffee tube, Bitsy tube (Zona taught him this at ice-out in Michigan — 'absolute money'). Small compact minnow baits. Use just heavy enough weight to reach the zone.

KVD: 'The common misnomer is that bass slow down and don't bite in cold water. That is absolutely not true.' Growing up in Michigan, fish live under ice all winter and still feed actively. Cold water fishing is nothing to be intimidated by — it can be some of the best fishing of the season.

KVD: On lowland reservoirs with grass (Guntersville, Toledo Bend, Okeechobee), bass don't leave shallow areas even in extreme cold. They stage in slightly deeper areas adjacent to spawning flats — drains, ditches, depressions, holes in the grass. Old dead vegetation holds heat and draws bass. Fish caught in 1-2ft of water in upper 30s temps.

KVD: Bass in super cold water are focused on vibration. They use their lateral line to feed — they can feel the presence of shad, the vibration and water displacement of your bait. That's why flatside crankbaits, lipless crankbaits, and bladed jigs all work in extreme cold: vibration is the common thread.

KVD: 'Fall transition signals: cooling nights drop water temps below summer highs, baitfish begin transitioning. Watch for pods schooling near surface, shad flipping on top, dive-bombing gulls, multiple herons on points or in creek backs. On natural lakes, bluegills transition from bottom to surface — bass may be on top of grass or suspended around deep humps, feeding UP not on bottom. Switch from bottom-bouncing to mid-range presentations.'
KVD: 'On a frog bite, pause a split second before the hookset. With braid and no stretch, you want to be sure the bass gets it all the way in its mouth before setting the hook.'

KVD: 'Target the greenest mats — they're untreated, most fertile, and hold the most bass. Avoid brown dying mats that lack ecosystem. Noise isn't a concern when punching — the loud impact of your weight often attracts fish rather than spooking them. Mats are naturally noisy environments.'

KVD: 'Punching mats is especially effective during cold fronts (bass bury deeper under mats for stable conditions) and heat of summer (mats provide cooler shade). Aggressive fish hit on the drop. If bites are sluggish, pause and jig longer. This technique is known for producing trophy bass.'

KVD: 'Shad spawn happens around 72 degrees, after bass/crappie spawn — Mother Nature helping them recover by providing a feast. Spikes on full moon, lasts 2-4 weeks. Starts in one area of lake — keep moving until you see activity. Most happens at night; first hour of daylight is crucial. Shady areas or cloud cover extend it. Look near deep-water access: rocks, riprap, laydowns, flooded trees, grass edges, floating docks.'

KVD: 'Four depth finders on the boat: rear has MEGA side imaging (both sides while scouting), down imaging (under boat), and LakeMaster Maps (1-foot contours). Front has split screen (side + down imaging) plus a dedicated unit for MEGA 360 scanning around the boat. 360 on trolling motor lets me see structure and bass before I arrive.'

KVD: Bill angle determines everything. Straighter bill = tighter action + steeper dive angle + deeper running. More angle from the body = wider wobble + more displacement + more vibration + quicker recovery after deflection. The line tie position relative to the bill matters too.

KVD: Flatside crankbaits send off a lot of pressure waves because flat sides displace a ton of water. Best in cold water (bass rely on lateral line to hunt) and dirty water any season (visibility is down). Round body crankbaits vary widely depending on bill angle — the body shape alone doesn't tell the whole story.

KVD: 'Pick a bait that runs just a little deeper than the depth of water you're fishing — that particular crankbait is going to be at its best.' It contacts bottom with great action. A bait that runs much deeper than the water overpowers the bottom and loses its triggering qualities.

KVD: The 10XD is unique for a straight-body deep diver — it has a slightly wider wobble than expected, giving it great deflection even in 12-15ft of water. Most deep divers with straight bills lose themselves completely when hitting cover. The 10XD doesn't — that's why it's a premier deep cranking bait.

KVD: Square bills are round-bodied with a steep angle and a square bill — this gives them a wide wobble and the best deflection qualities of any crankbait. Most weedless crankbait you can throw. Good through grass, brush, gnarly rocks, riprap. Works in cold or warm water. Triggers neutral/negative fish with deflection.

KVD: 'In 99% of circumstances, I want to make sure whatever depth I'm fishing, the bait is getting to the bottom.' Biggest mistake anglers make is using the wrong style crankbait and not contacting the bottom. That's why depth-specific models exist (10XD, 8XD, 6XD, 5XD, 3XD).

KVD: 'For lipless on flats: 7-foot glass rod, 5:1 gear ratio reel. Slower retrieve keeps bait down in the strike zone. Line controls depth — 20lb test for shallow water above grass, 8-10lb fluorocarbon for deeper to maximize depth and bottom contact.'

KVD: 'On large flats, the key is contact, depth, and placement. Vary retrieve speed with stop-and-go movements. Let the bait sink periodically. Always aim for the bait to be hitting something — if it's not interacting with cover, it loses effectiveness.'

KVD: 'For lipless crankbaits, use a long composite rod (part glass, part graphite). Graphite gives sensitivity to feel tiny bits of weed on hooks. Glass slows the action for better deflection off cover. Longer rod gives depth control — rod tip up for shallow flats (2-4ft), rod tip down for deeper areas. Always fluorocarbon — sinks, low stretch, abrasion resistant.'

KVD: 'Early spring lipless colors: stick to natural patterns mimicking what bass already feed on — bluegill and crayfish in clear water. Stained or murky water, switch to brighter colors — chartreuse, fluorescent reds, anything that stands out more.'

KVD: 'Efficiency means keeping your bait in the strike zone as long as possible. Cast straight along any edge — drop-offs, grass lines, weed lines, seawalls, sandbar drop-offs. Position yourself to fish parallel so the bait stays in the zone the entire retrieve. Choose the most efficient bait for the cover and clarity, then maximize every cast.'

KVD: On clear water grass lakes, most anglers fish the inside line where fish spawn. But fish also spawn in the deeper scattered grass patches (7ft+) in the middle of big flats. Nobody else fishes it — that's a competitive advantage. A lot of largemouth have already spawned out there.

KVD: North wind blowing into a bank knocks algae loose and muddies the water, making sight fishing impossible. When this happens, switch from sight fishing to casting Thunder Cricket or fan casting the area. Conditions can clear up fast when wind lays down.

KVD: In northern natural lakes, perch and gobies are the primary forage — fish spend most of their time feeding on them. Both live on or near rocks. Birds diving on shallow points = alewives spawning (another forage clue).
KVD: 'So important in clear water lakes that you've got good polarized glasses.' They let you see green grass clumps further out so you can pitch to them without getting right on top of spooky fish.

KVD: In practice, keep moving and take notes. Don't fall in love with one area. Stop at many stretches, get a feel for what's there, then move on. 'That's why we call it practice.' Save the commitment for tournament day.

KVD: 'Punching matted grass setup: 7-10 heavy rod, 8.3:1 high-speed reel (for making more casts, not fighting fish), 1.5oz tungsten weight pegged with bobber stop, 65lb braid, 4/0 hook with Punch Bug. Tungsten's density penetrates dense mats better than lead. The loud impact often attracts fish rather than spooking them.'

KVD: 'Cold water Thunder Cricket: cast toward structure or transitions, let sink just above cover/grass, lift-and-drop retrieve with brief pauses. The key isn't speed — it's triggering instinctive reaction bites. Slow enough for blade to stay in strike zone but fast enough for vibration.'

KVD: 'Medium-heavy casting rod with backbone, 6.4-7.1:1 reel, fluorocarbon leader. Match blade color to water — gold in stained, chrome in clear. Keeping hookup ratio high is essential when fish are only following.'

KVD: 'Cold water bass bites get tougher below 55°F. Morning — wind blows into shallow flats, bass edge up. Afternoon — fish move to outside bends and deeper transition breaks. Target these transition zones for reaction strikes.'

KVD: 'Knowing the dominant baitfish is critical, especially in fall when bass target the most abundant species to fatten up. Forage varies even between lakes miles apart — bluegill, perch, alewives, smelt, shad, gobies. Regional differences affect coloration, seasonal patterns, migration, and habitat. Study biological reports, ask guides about dominant bait, note coloration and size during practice. Inspect regurgitated baitfish closely.'

KVD: 'Fishing in the moment — apply your strengths and trust instincts as you see different water, weather, pressure, and wildlife changes, then adapt instantly. Start with basics of where bass should be on seasonal migration, then apply search baits to find schools and choose the right lure to make them bite. Don't rely on past reports or history — conditions change overnight.'

KVD: 'First thing at a new lake: observe water clarity and check water temperature. Combine that with where bass should be according to seasonal patterns. The angler who figures out where bass are and how to catch them fastest wins. I'm not afraid to totally change technique, lure, and approach when conditions have changed.'

KVD: 'Summer topwater triggers: bluegill spawn and mayfly hatches. Popper has tremendous drawing power during bluegill spawn — great hook-up ratio even with smallmouth. Use larger popper in wind, dirtier water, or around big bass for more commotion. Fish topwater all day long in summer — produces some of the biggest bass of the year.'

KVD: In clear water slick calm conditions, most anglers reach for finesse. KVD's instinct is to jerk a jerkbait over the top of them — the clear water means they'll see it from a distance. Confidence in the technique matters as much as conditions.

KVD: 'Jerkbait cadence: always start and finish with slack in your line — this gives the bait its best side-to-side action. Experiment every day and let bass tell you what they want. Cold water = longer pauses. Warm water = more active and erratic retrieve. The clearer the water, the more action in the retrieve.'

KVD: 'Jerkbait rod is the most critical piece — shorter handle, longer rod, graphite with extra fast tip and sturdy backbone. Never pull the jerkbait toward the boat with the reel — work it with the rod, use reel only to pick up slack. Fast 7.5:1 gear ratio. Fluorocarbon is critical — 14lb is my favorite, still makes bait jump on long casts into wind. Below 12lb, too much stretch hurts action.'

KVD: 'I want minimum 10 sites on a tournament day, prefer 100. Bass quit biting then start again 1-2 hours later — current replenishes a spot within an hour, weather changes need 2-3 hours. If a spot produces 5-6 bass in 45 min, rotate back every 1-2 hours. Only 1-2 bass in 30-45 min? Move on. Also factor in size — 10 fish at 1.5lbs, leave for a spot producing 3-4lb fish.'

KVD: 'When I pinpoint a good school of big bass, I have to maximize the number from that school. Use a search bait to pinpoint them, determine if they're actively feeding and what lures to fish, then slow down my presentation to catch as many as possible before they quit biting or move. My top finesse baits: tube, shaky head, drop shot, Ned rig, wacky-rigged worm, and Neko rig.'

KVD: 'Sexy Frog technique: reel through tall grass with enough momentum to spray water, then slow down at the grass edge — walk it side to side. Watch fish come up and nail it at the edge. Also target docks: put on a skipping reel and throw it way back under coverage. The hollow soft body skips well. Fish often follow it out of thick cover into open water.'

KVD: During pre-spawn, when you find one fish on a spot, they're usually grouped. Got a bite every single cast on one little spot on a point. They stage together before moving up to spawn. Mark the spot and work it thoroughly.

KVD: Clear water on grass lakes = fish can see the bait from farther away (benefit) but can also be spooky (challenge). Fish may be timid — bites are subtle, just bumping the bait. Slow down and keep working the area.

KVD: 'For all four finesse techniques (drop-shot, Neko, shaky head, Ned): 10lb braid to 8lb fluorocarbon leader with FG knot as baseline. Go heavier for cover or colored water. Longer 7-2 to 7-4 rods for open water long casts, shorter 6-10 to 7-foot for tight target work.'

KVD: 'After scouring a productive spot with power approaches, drop down to lighter line and finesse to pick up the scraps. Cleaning up with finesse has become a regular part of my game — these techniques revitalize a worn-out fishing spot.'

KVD: Bite the head off the KVD Magic trailer — shorten it just a bit. Combined with blue craw Thunder Cricket, it looks like a bluegill. The trailer adds a little flash and keeps the presentation subtle and natural for clear water.
KVD: CC5 composite rod with heavy action (e-glass/graphite blend) — you feel everything with the bladed jig and it absorbs shock on the bite. 7.5:1 KVD reel, 20lb fluorocarbon.

KVD: 'I fish faster and make more casts than other anglers — but my lure presentation isn't always fast. This enables me to find bass quicker and locate productive areas faster, spending more time in the most productive zones. I analyze weather and water conditions every minute on the lake and base fishing on current seasonal patterns, not where I caught fish last year.'

KVD: 'For northern smallmouth in clear water, use longer-than-usual rods — never shorter than 7 feet. Long casts are extremely important. Light line and long rod keep bait far from the boat and help move more line on hookset. I designed a 7-4 spinning rod specifically for finesse applications.'

KVD: 'Smallmouth love transitions or changes in bottom composition. Good polarized sunglasses are as critical as electronics — I'm constantly looking for transitions, irregularities, individual pieces of cover, or the fish themselves. Eyes scanning the water catches a pile of smallmouth each year.'

KVD: 'Pump and stop retrieve — rod tip to the side, sweep and follow the bait back down keeping tension. Let the Red Eye Shad do its thing and swim to bottom. Works over grass, rocks, deeper flats. I probably catch more fish this way than any other.'

KVD: 'I throw the Red Eye Shad on a 7-7'10" fiberglass composite rod. Gives me graphite feel with the slow reaction time of glass. Enough power to snap it free from grass. Long casts cover more water. Always fluorocarbon — it sinks, great feel, good hook sets on long casts.'
KVD: 'I use the 1/2oz Red Eye Shad in the 7-12 foot zone and the 3/4oz for deeper — 15-20 feet on ledges. Use lighter line to help keep the bait down.'

KVD: On Cayuga Lake (northern natural lake), the north end is a huge expanse of flats at 8-12ft, all grass. The key is finding clean holes in the grass at 9-11ft. Good holes have a little bit of rock in them. Each hole has its own ecosystem — bluegill, perch, gobies all live there. Good holes have reloaded with fish all week.

KVD: For pitching into grass holes — 3/8oz tungsten weight with a 3/0 Mustad grip pin hook (the pin keeps the bait from sliding down). 5-inch Ocho worm. Blue craw or green pumpkin. Making it fall straight is critical — the Texas rig glides down clean into the holes.

KVD: 'Summer natural lakes: weeds are where you find the majority of largemouth. Look for prime structure or irregular features — dropoff close to dense vegetation, creek channel, or where covers combine (thick weeds against a dock, two weed types mixing). Bluegill colors on all lures in summer, especially up north where bluegills are primary forage.'

KVD: 'Dense vegetation techniques (frogs, flipping, swim jigs): 50lb braid on lakes where bass max around 6lbs. Lakes with 10-pounders, use 65lb braid. 7-4 rod for frogs and jigs with high-speed reel. 7-10 rod for flipping and punching.'

KVD: 'Skip casting with baitcasters: sidearm cast low to water that turns into a tight, quick roll cast. Lure should travel 70-80% of the cast just above water, skip 2-3 times, then land quietly. First cast is critical — a bad cast spooks fish and your one shot is over. It's a deadly combination of stealth and accuracy for pressured fish.'

KVD: 'Power-finesse: cover water fast but use finesse baits to trigger inactive fish. Adjust weight on shaky head, drop shot, or tube to control fall rate. Tube and drop shot are the most versatile power-finesse baits. Won Smith Mountain Lake Elite with this — 61lbs over 4 days in a bed fishing event.'

KVD: Position boat just outside the grass line. Cast at 45° quartering angle into shallower grass. Crank down until you hit grass, then slow up. If it won't float free, snap it out. The bait follows the contour of the grass as it comes back to the deeper edge where your boat sits. This is the most effective way to cover a lot of grass quickly.

KVD: When crankbait sticks in grass — if it won't come through, snap it free. Otherwise let it float off. The moment it tears free or floats off is when you get the bite. 'A crankbait through the grass is almost as good as a finesse bait like a worm at triggering strikes from inactive fish.'

KVD: Fluorocarbon is mandatory for cranking grass — low stretch helps rip bait free. Soft-tip composite rod (glass/graphite blend) for feel and power. Multiple rods with different line sizes. Series 5 crankbait runs 10-12ft — wants a bait that runs to the edge of the grass, not one that buries into it.

KVD: The crankbait is a search tool for reading the grass line — you can feel where grass comes out farther, where it's taller, subtle points and cuts. Fish relate to irregularities in the grass edge. On natural lakes where structure is subtle, the crankbait finds what your eyes can't.

KVD: 'I look for a 10-degree drop from average summer water temps to trigger the fall pattern. On Ozarks reservoirs that's when temps drop from low-mid 80s to mid 70s. Length of day is the most important determinant — shorter days trigger change.'
KVD: 'You don't have to be out at dawn in fall. The best shad activity often comes in the afternoon — especially later into fall, the shad seem more active when it warms up a bit.'
KVD: 'It isn't always shad. In northern lakes, bluegills are often the main prey. In the southeast, blueback herring are dominant. Know the habits of the dominant prey species to be successful.'

KVD: 'Bass don't go extremely deep immediately after spawning. They stop at secondary points, road beds, shallow ledges, main lake points in the 8-10ft range. Many anglers pull out deep divers too soon — a medium-diving crankbait in Sexy Shad matches the shad-gorging post-spawn phase better.'

KVD: 'Not every quality bass follows bait to the back of a creek in fall — there's still plenty of good fish eating bait on flat, shallow points along the main lake too. Target shallow flat main lake secondary points.'

KVD: 'Upgrade treble hooks one size on favorite crankbaits and jerkbaits. No. 6 factory → No. 4 replacement. No. 4 factory → No. 2. Keep checking hook points throughout the season. Also keep a variety of replacement spinnerbait blades with split rings pre-rigged for fast swaps — different sizes, shapes, and colors.'

KVD: Guntersville's grass has evolved over the years: milfoil first, then hydrilla, now a lot of eel grass. All three still present. The grass creates great habitat and clear water (filters it). Floating eel grass cut up by wind/waves/boats makes it hard to throw lipless crankbaits and jerkbaits — catches on your line.

KVD: Guntersville breaks into three sections: Lower end = rocks, bluffs, bridges (good for jerkbaits, cranking). Mid-lake = river ledges, miles of grass (always a player). Upper end = shallow creeks, lily pads (Bill Lowen territory — guys who get way back into places others don't look).

KVD: 'One of the things when I'm preparing for a big tournament is I really want to follow the weather — not only current conditions but what's been happening leading up to the event.' When lakes are off-limits for a month+, weather tracking is how you prepare. Water clarity, temp trends, rain history all matter.

KVD: Mild winters at Guntersville keep water in low 50s with grass still green and water exceptionally clear. Cold winters push shad into creek centers with wolf packs of bass around them. Both scenarios produce but require completely different approaches.

KVD: 'Smallmouth crankbait color: go to one extreme or the other. Start super bright — Chartreuse Sexy Shad, Powder Blue Back Chartreuse. Smallmouth are sight feeders, want a bait they can see from far off. On slick calm sunny days or when they won't commit to bright, switch to natural — Green Gizzard Shad, bluegill, or perch pattern.'

KVD: 'Biggest smallmouth cranking difference: it's NOT about bottom contact. Keep crankbait a few feet off bottom — smallmouth in clear water love to feed looking up. It's about speed and covering water, casting all directions to find roaming aggressive fish. Burns a meal over their heads and they can't resist.'

KVD: 'Daylight length and time of year affect spawning movements as much as anything, but conditions must be right too. Snowmelt runoff is colder AND clearer than normal spring rain — moves slower and drops water temps faster. In prespawn, target pockets and bays near creek channels or deep water access. Northerly flats with dark/muck bottoms, rock, dying vegetation are degrees warmer.'

KVD: 'The KVD 1.5 can run almost 6 feet deep with light line (10-12lb fluoro) — deeper than you'd think for a shallow runner. Put it on 20lb and it runs shallow. Rod height controls depth during retrieve — tip up to stay high, tip down to dig deeper.'

KVD: 'What I look for in a squarebill is erratic swimming — kick around, don't run straight. Old wood baits had this but broke fast. The body and weighting design make every single one swim erratically out of the package. It looks like an injured baitfish — catches a lot of smallmouth over deep water.'

KVD: 'I've weighted crankbaits for a long time. Adding weight helps in three ways: extra depth, better castability (flat-sided cranks cast like potato chips without it), and allows slower retrieve speed. In winter you want to crawl a crankbait as slow as possible.'

KVD: 'The balance point on a crankbait is right by the throat or just forward of the front hook hanger — that's where you add weight. Storm SuspenDots for small amounts, clip-on lead weights on the front hook hanger for more. Open the eye of a lead drop-shot weight as alternative.'

KVD: 'Buoyancy is determined by water temperature and elevation above sea level. Tuning at home doesn't translate to the lake. Start with a fairly heavy weight, check in the water, clip off weight with side cutters until you hit that sweet spot between suspending and a very slow fall.'
KVD: 'I'm a big believer in flat-sided crankbaits in winter — the KVD 1.5 Flat and Lucky Shad for cold water. Throw Lucky Shad on spinning equipment with 8lb fluoro for extra distance and depth.'

KVD: 'Fall hotspot indicators: gulls, cormorants, and herons point to baitfish concentrations. Watch for coots — they sit and feed over grass beds, preferring the greener, healthier grass that also attracts bait and bass.'

KVD: Three weight tricks for Neko rigs: (1) Superglue all tungsten Neko weights — they won't fly out when fish jump. (2) Wrap lead wire from fly tying department (Bass Pro) around the hook shank for subtle extra weight without bulk. (3) Put weights in both ends of creature baits like the Sexy Bug — hook in the middle, weight in both nose and tail.

KVD: GS1 spinning rod, 6'10" medium action for accuracy around docks. Longer spinning rod for open water distance. 8lb braid to 8lb fluoro leader. Mustad Apex weedless hook for cover/docks, exposed hook in open water. Mustad Apex drop shot hook (wide gap) for creature baits.

KVD: Dice/bug baits on a drop shot are a new frontier. Lighter weight = better (1/8oz ideal, 1/4oz max in deeper water). The slow sinking action is the key — once on bottom you can still shake it traditionally, but most bites come on the fall. Game-changer for clear water smallmouth and during Mayfly hatches.

KVD: Neko-rigged creature baits are 'deadly springtime bait around the spawn.' In clear water, most fish bite on the initial fall — if they're on a bed, the bait lands next to them and they eat it immediately. Works for both largemouth and smallmouth/spots.

KVD: Match the forage and water clarity. Spring in Texas = red (crawfish coming out, dark red). Stained water = brighter reds like fire craw or chili craw. Gold has great flash in stained water (gold sexy shad). General rule: 'Pick something that's natural and something that's visible.' Match bluegill, crawfish, or shad based on the system.

KVD: As water warms in pre-spawn, fish scatter out into big spawning bays. Try to follow ditches but sometimes you just have to fan cast randomly. Wind or clouds help position fish better and make them more aggressive.

KVD: 'Great Lakes smallmouth forage varies — yellow perch, crawfish, round gobies. Perch favor grass/sand patches; gobies live in rock. Minnow-imitating lures (jerkbaits, crankbaits) excel on perch bites. Goby-imitating swimbaits and jig combos for goby bites. Identify target forage first — it can change seasonally and day-to-day.'

KVD: 'Deep jerkbait for smallmouth: slower-action medium-heavy rod (not snappy like standard jerkbait rod), fast reel, 12-14lb fluorocarbon. The slower rod action works better for the deep-diving versions.'

KVD: Day 1 of practice = get a big picture view of the whole lake. Day 2 = dial in and refine. Find areas and patterns that fit your strengths. Once you find the best area, everything else is just a secondary pattern.

KVD: 'You got to find areas and patterns that fit your strengths.' As a power fisherman, KVD looks for areas where power techniques (crankbaits, bladed jigs) can produce quality bites, not just numbers.
KVD: 7'2" medium light with extra fast tip (GS4 LSE KVD rod). Uses braid for maximum feel — 'with that braid, you can feel everything.' Bites can be very subtle.

KVD: Don't set the hook traditionally on a Ned Rig. Reel into them fast — let the rod load, then pull. If you miss one, immediately drop it right back down because they'll come right back and bite it.

KVD: The Ned Rig is so popular for smallmouth because it imitates gobies. Gobies stay locked on the bottom on rocks — that's exactly where the Ned Rig sits. Find the rocks, you find the big smallmouth. Gobies have changed these fisheries — fish don't have to move because the food source is so prolific.
KVD: Use the lightest Ned head you can get away with. 1/16 oz in shallow, 3/16 oz in deep water or heavy wind. Lighter head = better action on the bait.

KVD: When fishing the Ned Rig on an edge, the main thing is keeping yourself positioned on the edge where the fish are. Spot Lock (trolling motor GPS anchor) is essential — 'without that spot lock, I would never be able to stay positioned or hold or land one.'

KVD: For lipless crankbaits in shallow grass (3-7ft): CC5 7'4" heavy action composite rod, 7.5:1 reel, 20lb fluoro. Multiple cranking rods with 14, 17, and 20lb line for different depths. Heavy line keeps the bait up and slows it down in shallow grass. The heavy composite rod has backbone to rip through grass.

KVD: Pre-spawn in Texas (and everywhere), follow the ditches and drains leading into spawning flats. Fish use these as highways. Subtle depressions on LakeMaster mapping concentrate fish, but sometimes the best spots are subtle features you find just by covering water with a Red Eye Shad.

KVD: 'Cranking system: composite cranking rods for casting distance and lure response. Wide-spool reel for more line/better casting. Low 5.3:1 gear ratio for power to horse fish from cover — handles everything from Series 3 to 10XD. Fluorocarbon 10-17lb for sinking and abrasion resistance. Always upgrade crankbait hooks to heavier gauge short shank trebles — stock hooks often lack stiffness and foul on each other.'

KVD: 'Sexy Shad color system: Sexy Shad for slightly stained to clear (first choice most situations). Chartreuse Sexy Shad for dirtier water. Sexy Blue Back Herring for herring-forage lakes. Sexy Ghost Minnow for real clear/bright days when you don't want a loud profile (great for topwaters). Chrome Sexy Shad for max flash (lipless, jerkbaits). Sexy Sunfish for bluegill-dominant lakes.'

KVD: 'Color makes a big difference — match what bass see underwater, not in your hand. Shad have iridescent bluish/grey backs with color variations (green tint, grayish look). As lakes get clearer and pressure increases, your bait has to appear a lot more natural. The right color catches ALL the fish, not just a few.'

KVD: 'Power finesse: use heavier weights (3/8 to 1/2oz) on finesse rigs to fish them faster. Heavier sinker keeps line tight, puts more action in lure, and triggers reactionary strikes. Start heavy and work down. On tubes, I'll throw 1/2 to 3/4oz heads — sinks like a bowling ball but still produces erratic fall. Cover a ton of water.'

KVD: 'For power finesse, use the heaviest gear you can get away with. 10lb fluorocarbon instead of 6lb — far less visible, better hooksets, land more quality fish around cover. The lack of stretch in heavier fluoro gives better hook penetration on long casts. Fish don't notice the line with a fast-falling heavy weight.'

KVD: 'Don't assume you must finesse spawning bass. At the 2011 Classic, 4 of top 7 finishers power fished spinnerbaits and crankbaits on the same 150-yard spawning flat. Key conditions: hard bottom flat with stumps + grass, 10-degree water temp rise in a week, full moon. Day 1 spinnerbait (fish aggressive), Day 2 crankbait (fish on beds, needed triggering). Dirty water helped crankbait effectiveness.'

KVD: 'Winter shallow power fishing is often best on calm bluebird days with little traffic. While these conditions are typically unfavorable, they allow fish to move shallow and bask in the sun. Target stained water and shallow rock — both hold heat.'
KVD: 'Stained water traps heat much better than clear water, making stained areas the warmest on the lake. It also decreases visibility making bass more receptive to fast-moving presentations.'

KVD: 'Shallow rock traps and holds heat. Fish hold tight to it for comfort and ambush points. Parallel a crankbait down rocky banks, targeting rocks in direct sunlight — extremely consistent winter pattern.'

KVD: 'Hot water dog days: bait and bass suspend off structure over deep points and humps. Clear water makes them selective and easily spooked — odds of catching several from one spot are minimal. Hit a lot of spots, catch a few, move on. Go back to the same area 3-4 times during the day while rotating with other spots. Give edgy bass time to forget what just happened.'

KVD: 'I power fish year-round, even in cold water. Bass are far more active than I once believed. Strike zone is smaller but they'll crush a power bait worked in their immediate area. Power fishing is the foundation of everything I do — it helps locate fish and dial in patterns more efficiently than finesse.'

KVD: 'In cold water, boat position is as critical as lure selection. Position parallel to dropoffs and edges so your bait stays in the proper depth zone throughout the retrieve. Bass won't move far in cold water — if you don't fish parallel, your bait catches a much smaller percentage of the key depth.'

KVD: 'Cold-water bass respond to weather changes like warmer seasons. Strike zone largest on cloudy, rainy frontal days with wind — move lures faster. High pressure/no wind — slow down to bottom-bouncing baits but still fish faster and erratically. 2008 Classic at Hartwell: hammered them cranking Day 1 in brutal cold, switched to stained water with flat-sided cranks Day 2 when front passed.'

KVD: 'There is no magic temperature that triggers the hot bite — it's about transition and rising temperatures, not a set number. The moon has greater influence over spawning than temperature. Stable weather + full moon + water in the 50s = very active bass, some spawning. Don't wait for the 60s. Northern bass spawn at lower temperatures than southern fish.'

KVD: 'Peak prespawn: concentrate on fish staging near spawning areas — they're the most aggressive. Cover water with fast baits searching for the sweet spots. I've hit one little stretch where a school of hungry big ones pulled up and caught four or five in a row. Dragging soft plastics, I might have missed those opportunities.'

KVD: 'Winter bass move shallower than most realize — if you can reach them with crankbait, jerkbait, or slow-rolled spinnerbait in 10ft or less, power baits are first option. Fish avoid expansive flats unless warming trend pushes them to gravel points. Rocky banks and riprap are ideal — hold crawfish (primary winter forage). Big pods of shad = jerkbait or spinnerbait. No baitfish = crankbait rooting on bottom.'

KVD: 'Hybrid approach: use jerkbait to cover water and find fish, then go back through with drop shot, tube, or jig to pick off more. The power bait located the school and gave confidence to slow down. You can no longer fish one way in most tournaments and win — use both power and finesse to maximize fish off each spot.'
KVD: Texas pre-spawn isn't just about Rayburn and Toledo Bend. Many other Texas lakes have big fish. Pre-spawn in Texas = follow ditches and drains leading to spawning flats.

KVD: 'Conditions over calendar. High, off-colored water sets the stage for shallow bass even in summer. More structure in shallows means more bass. The bluegill spawn is a coast-to-coast pattern — bass target spawning panfish for big meals.'

KVD: 'Bluegills spawn midway back in a pocket, not all the way in the back. They like sides of secondary points and shade trees that stick over water — almost guaranteed under a big willow tree. Use Mega 360 or Side Imaging, or find them with your eyes.'

KVD: 'For skip casting, use the heaviest line you can get away with — heavier line makes line management easier. 50lb braid for frogs and swim jigs in colored water. 20lb fluorocarbon for sparser cover or clearer water. 14lb fluoro for finesse jigs on deep docks in clear water. Less line on the spool is better for skip casting.'

KVD: 'Jerkbait setup: high-speed reel (not for pulling bait, just picking up slack fast). All-graphite rod with snappy tip for sharp darting action, even at end of long casts in wind. Fluorocarbon line — lighter line runs deeper, heavier stays shallow. Line choice controls bait depth with precision.'

KVD: 'I don't spend more than 10 minutes in the same area doing the same thing without making some kind of change.' Either move, change baits, change presentation — but don't waste time without positive feedback. Any sign (blowup, follow, catch, short bite) is a clue to the puzzle.

KVD: Most important thing arriving at a lake: figure out the seasonal pattern plus current conditions. Even on a known lake, see what's changed. On grass lakes, determine how much grass there is and where. Look for bottlenecks and funnels on your mapping. Cover a lot of water on day 1.

KVD: On grass lakes, look for bottlenecks and funnels on your LakeMaster mapping — cuts and narrows between grass mats. Areas leading in and out of these bottlenecks can be really good, especially with wind creating current.

KVD: 'When choosing any lure for any situation, account for: water clarity, depth, time of year, water temp, type of cover, current, rising or falling water, weather conditions, and potential mood of the fish. Example: you might throw a frog over grass mats, but after a cold front with high pressure and 10-degree temp drop, punch through instead. Every lure is a tool — use the right one for the conditions.'

KVD: Keeps 3/8, 1/2, and 5/8 oz Thunder Crickets on the deck during pre-spawn. Uses lighter weight (3/8oz) to slow down in tough conditions. Tungsten Thunder Cricket has smaller blade with tighter/subtler vibration and runs deeper in the same size — better for finicky fish.

KVD: In cold/post-frontal pre-spawn, don't just wind the Thunder Cricket. Work it like a jig — hop, twitch, pull-and-stop. Get it down in the grass and on the bottom. Bites are extremely subtle: the bait just gets heavy or the vibration stops. 'I always move faster than the fish' — force yourself to slow down.

KVD: 'Three swimbait styles for different conditions: Bulkier hollow-body for slower presentations — great prespawn and stained water for extra vibration. Subtle finesse swimbaits (like Swimming Shiner) for clear water and tough bites on bright days. Mid-action swimbaits like Caffeine Shad as all-around choice — weedless through grass on screw-lock hook, vary head weight from 1/8oz shallow to 3/8oz deep. If bass follow but won't bite, adjust style, action, color, or speed.'

KVD: 'Choose a crankbait that runs slightly deeper than the water — that gives best action and triggering when banging off bottom. If water is 10ft, I want bait running 11ft. Manage with bait selection, line size, and rod angle. Multiple rods rigged with different line sizes — smaller for deeper, bigger for shallower. Hold rod up for shallower over ledge tops, lower for depth increases.'

KVD: 'Bright day, no wind, clear water = fish less aggressive, hugging bottom. Switch to jig, C-rig, drop shot, Neko. With current, wind, cloud cover, or stained water, they'll bite the crankbait. Ultra-clear with wind and clouds = still crankbait but speed up retrieve and use natural colors. Monitor electronics to see how fish relate to bottom.'

KVD: 'Cold water crankbait colors: crawfish patterns are top choice. Red/fire craw in stained water, natural greens/browns in clear water, shad patterns when baitfish are present. Pro tip: if you catch a bass with a crawfish in its mouth, note the color and match it.'

KVD: 'Cold water cranking setup: 6.2:1 gear ratio reel, 10lb fluorocarbon, slow to medium retrieve with frequent pauses. Make long casts, parallel the bank, and let the bait reach max depth. Bottom contact is non-negotiable.'

KVD: 'Cold water cranking revolves around three depth zones: (1) Shallow transitions — flat-sided, tight wobble, no rattle; (2) Mid-range 6-12ft — round body, wide wobble, loud rattle; (3) Deep 12-15ft — smaller profile, tight action. Match the crankbait to the zone.'

KVD: 'Summer bass suspend when temps peak, no current, and thermocline forms. See the thermocline as a consistent band on your graph — fish above it, none below. Schooling activity away from bottom and structure is a giveaway. Clouds of suspended baitfish with big marks around them = active fish. Use Neko or drop shot to drop to marked fish — sometimes let it fall to bottom and they'll chase it down.'

KVD: 'I power fish year-round, even in cold water. Bass are far more active in cold water than I once believed. The strike zone is smaller but they'll crush a power bait worked in their immediate area. Power fishing is the foundation of everything I do — it helps locate fish and dial in patterns.'

KVD: 'In cold water, boat position is as critical as lure selection. Position parallel to dropoffs and edges — if you don't fish parallel, your bait catches a much smaller percentage of the key depth zone. Fish are less likely to move far.'

KVD: 'Cold-water bass respond to weather changes like in warmer seasons. Strike zone is largest on cloudy, rainy frontal days with wind — move lures faster. On high-pressure/no-wind days, slow down to bottom-bouncing baits but still fish them faster and erratically.'

KVD: '2008 Classic at Hartwell — brutal cold Day 1, hammered them cranking a weighted-to-suspend Series 5 over 15-25ft with stop-and-start retrieve. Day 2, post-frontal sunny skies killed that bite. Ran to the upper end with stained water and fished flat-sided cranks shallower — caught 12lbs in 2 hours.'

KVD: 'Summer Sexy Shad vs Sexy Shad: if creeks are clearer than normal from lack of rain or zebra mussels, Summer Sexy Shad is the better choice. It's a subtler version for clear water fall conditions.'

KVD: 'My Seasonal Guide helps me make educated guesses about where bass are most likely at any given time. It quickly eliminates unproductive water. The concept: at any given time, the majority of bass in a lake will be on certain key types of structure.'

KVD: 'Water clarity is a major determinant of bass depth in winter. In clear lakes, bass can go 50-60 feet deep. In murky lakes, they'll be much shallower. Bass often suspend in clear water rather than hold tight to cover.'

KVD: 'Once the water hits 50°F, bass are definitely prespawn. They follow migration routes — ditches, depth contours, tapering points, fallen trees on sloping banks — toward shallow water. Northern banks are warmer and very important now.'

KVD: 'I pick up my fishing pace considerably as the water hits around 52°F. I may run down a bank quickly throwing a lipless crankbait or spinnerbait, then pause to pitch a jig at submerged logs or brushpiles.'

KVD: 'Many bass move onto spawning beds around the new or full moon, or when the sun comes out after prolonged cloudy weather. Not all spawn at once — biggest bass are first. Personally, I stay with prespawn bass as long as I can because they're more aggressive and weigh more.'

KVD: 'Postspawn bass hang around their spawning grounds near cover sticking up vertically — dock pilings, submerged bushes, standing tree trunks. Keep your presentation up high in the water column, not bumping bottom.'

KVD: 'In summer, light penetration is a major factor. Bass hold at the lower end of the light penetration spectrum where they can still see but remain concealed from prey. In extremely hot water, they hold near cover or dropoffs for long periods, then feed in short aggressive bursts.'

KVD: 'Bass are more baitfish-oriented in fall than any other season. Cooling water causes vast shad schools to migrate into tributary arms, bass follow. Match lure size to baitfish size. In lakes without shad, target weedy areas — bass feed on bluegill and shiners.'

KVD: 'I begin checking weather conditions for the area I'll be fishing as early as 15 days before a tournament. I want to know warm front or cold front coming in or out. Water temp is critical — sometimes just one degree warmer holds most of the bass.'
KVD: 'It helps to determine what type of lake you're fishing — lowland reservoir, highland reservoir, natural lake, tidewater, or river. Bass use different places in different types of lakes.'
KVD: 'In winter, fish parallel to the structure — this keeps your lure deeper in the water column longer. Use search lures that draw strikes without moving fast.'

KVD: '7.5-foot rod, 3000 series spinning reel, 10lb braid with a 20-foot leader of 8lb fluorocarbon. Long leader and small-diameter braid means long casts with no twist. Critical for clear water where long casts matter.'
KVD: '1/2oz tungsten for 10-40ft depth range with the 7-6 rod. Shallow water gets 1/8 or 3/16oz weight. Use just heavy enough weight to keep good bottom contact and line tight.'

KVD: 'On a long cast, let it sink then tightline the last bit so the Baby Z-Too swims like a real minnow on the way down. Shake 3-4 times, reel a little, shake again. If you find concentrated fish, work it longer through the cast.'

KVD: 'Match bait to fish mood: super tough = wacky-rigged Ocho or finesse worm. Power fishing = something with lots of wiggle like Baby Z-Too or nose-hooked Dream Shot. Figure out the mood and give them action that generates extra strikes.'

KVD: 'For vertical deep water (50ft on Table Rock, fishing standing timber at 30ft down), use a short rod. Video gaming — looking at graph, dropping to the fish, watching bait on sonar, trying to talk them into biting.'

KVD: 'Wake Shad runs only about 3 inches deep — perfect for burning over grass flats like hydrilla without getting snagged. Reel it right through the crisp green grass, mimicking a wounded baitfish just above the vegetation. Skimming the surface while creating enticing movement triggers aggressive strikes.'

KVD: 7'4" medium-heavy rod (KVD Elite GC7), 7.5:1 gear ratio reel, 20lb fluorocarbon. Heavy line does NOT affect spinnerbait action — it's one of the few lures where heavy line doesn't hurt. Allows accurate casts tight to cover.

KVD: Barely turn the handle. When the bait hits a limb, speed the reel up one fast turn to force the bait over the limb keeping it up and down (not turning over and hanging). The flutter on the other side of the limb is when they bite. Even without cover, intermittently speed up for a quick flare of the skirt and blades to trigger following fish.

KVD: Always use a plastic trailer when slow rolling — adds bulk and extra action fish home in on. Rage Slim Swimmer is his go-to: subtle, not too bulky (doesn't make bait ride up), has an erratic wag. In coldest months, sometimes removes the skirt entirely and just uses a swimbait as trailer.

KVD: Bright sunny + clear water = natural colors (bluegill, spot remover). Dirty water = brightest or darkest colors (chartreuse/white or black/blue) to maximize silhouette. Shad patterns (white/chartreuse) are day-in day-out staples. Spring around grass in Texas = red crawfish patterns. Match the forage and water clarity.

KVD: When slow rolling, keep the bait ABOVE the bass's line of sight — you're imitating baitfish, not crawfish. Fish cover or fish over the bass's head. Steep rocky banks, laydowns, grass — the bait should be slowly ticking the cover above where bass are positioned.
KVD: Spinnerbait is one of the few lures where heavy line (20lb fluoro) does NOT hinder the action at all. On a small crankbait or finesse jig, heavy line hurts the action — but not on a spinnerbait.

KVD: 'I don't cast finesse baits until I know I'm in a good area holding bass. Three triggers to go finesse: (1) really clear water where I can see bass, (2) when bass quit biting power baits to pick up a few more, (3) when a bass blows up on or short-strikes my power bait — I know exactly where it is and cast a finesse worm right there. Go-to finesse: drop shot with Half Shell or finesse worm, then Ocho stick worm, then small jig.'

palaniuk.insight: Palaniuk: 'Uses a 7-foot-6 medium-heavy Abu Garcia Fantasista Regista rod with Revo MGXtreme reel spooled with 15-pound Trilene 100% fluorocarbon for summer worming.'
Palaniuk: 'I like to fish offshore and target shell bars or brushpiles with a 10-inch worm in summer.'

Palaniuk: 'My offshore rotation goes: 1) deep-diving crankbait to cover water fast and fire up the school, 2) swimbait for a slower moving presentation once crankbait bite dies, 3) big flutter spoon for vertical reaction bites on fish seen on electronics, 4) big worm dragged on bottom to soak bait, 5) neko rig or drop shot as last resort.'

Palaniuk: 'No two schools are the same, and no two fish in a school are the same. Each one is sitting down there with a little bit different personality. You have to figure out which bait pushes their buttons enough to get them to bite.'

Palaniuk: 'When you change from horizontal action (crankbait/swimbait) to vertical action (spoon), it makes fish react. There are two things that trigger a fish to react: a fleeing baitfish or a dying baitfish. You get both with the retrieve on a spoon.'

Palaniuk: 'After you burn a crankbait through and catch a few, the ones that dont want to bite kind of move out of the way and break up. A swimbait allows them to settle back in but still gives them a moving target to chase.'

Palaniuk: 'I dont start with the spoon because the landing ratio is poor. Bass can gain leverage and throw the spoon on the jump or tear off underwater. But it triggers additional strikes once they slow down on crankbait and swimbait.'

Palaniuk: 'Setup 1 (nose-hooked): X-Zone 4-inch Original Slammer, VMC Drop Shot Hook size 2, VMC Tungsten Teardrop weight 1/8 to 3/4 oz, 6-foot-10 medium Alpha Angler DSR spinning rod, Daiwa Exist 3000, 15-lb Seaguar Smackdown braid to 6-12 lb Seaguar Gold Label fluoro leader.'

Palaniuk: 'Setup 2 (threaded): X-Zone 6-inch MB Fat Finesse Worm, VMC Finesse Neko Hook size 2, VMC Tungsten Teardrop weight 1/8 to 3/4 oz, same rod/reel, 15-lb Smackdown braid to 6-12 lb Seaguar Tatsu fluoro leader.'
Palaniuk: 'Drop shot can be fished shallow to deep, in open water or around cover, and in clear or dingy water by making slight changes to leader length, hook style, plastics, and weight size.'

Palaniuk: 'In the fall, plan to fish everything in your tackle box. Bass are moving between deep and shallow water. Not all bass will be on the same cover or depth at the same time. You must be willing to adapt and adjust to various water and wind conditions.'
Palaniuk: 'I caught my biggest bass (7-12) by spotting a brushpile off to the side of my boat with the Humminbird Mega 360. I held my boat 50 feet away and cast a drop shot to it.'
Palaniuk: 'For the Santee Cooper drop shot, I used a green/pumpkin/blue X-Zone Deception Worm 12 inches above the weight on an Alpha Angler DSR 6-foot-10 medium rod with a Daiwa Exist 3000 reel.'
