Palaniuk: 'During the late-summer/fall transition, bream and shad hold around and under grass mats. Bigger largemouths are ambush feeders and move under those mats for a safe haven and to locate ambush points.'

Palaniuk: 'For punching mats, use a 7-foot-9 extra heavy Alpha Angler Mag Hitter rod with Daiwa Zillion HD 7.3:1 reel. The X-Zone Muscle Back Finesse Craw in black-blue-laminate with 1-1/4 oz weight gets through heavy mats.'

Palaniuk: 'Dont hesitate to fish a spot just because another competitor fished it before you. Another smallmouth will start holding in that same site before long. Decisions on how long you fish and how quickly or slowly you move from spot to spot make the difference.'

Palaniuk: 'Set up a milk run starting with 50 different rocks/boulders in the area with the most baitfish. Over the tournament, learn which rocks and boulders consistently hold good-sized smallmouths.'
Palaniuk: 'Some fish caught off rocks are resident fish, but a larger majority are roaming and following schools of baitfish like perch and alewives.'

Palaniuk: 'Use a longer than average rod for deep cranking. A 7-foot-6 medium-heavy cranking rod with a 5.4:1 retrieve reel and 10-lb fluorocarbon helps make long casts and get the lure as deep as possible.'

Palaniuk: 'Make a waypoint on the structure you want to hit, then after finding the right cast angle, make another waypoint for boat position. Also pick shore landmarks in front and behind you to stay on line at a glance.'

Palaniuk: 'When idling over an area and you find structure, idle past it. The prop leaves a bubble trail on the surface. Cast back to that bubble trail and retrieve through it to speed up finding the right cast line.'
Palaniuk: 'The more you fish a lure, the more you understand its vibration pattern. That helps you know if there is grass on the lure or a fish has subtly struck it.'
Palaniuk: 'Deep diving crankbaits are really one of the few ways we have of generating any kind of reaction strike through much of the year on tour.'

Palaniuk: 'I set my Humminbird 360 to scan about 150 feet all the way around the boat because thats a little past the distance I can cast. I constantly see bottom and structure in front, to either side, and behind me to determine where to make my next cast.'

Palaniuk: 'When hunting baitfish in fall, use Mega Side Imaging which looks 200 feet from the boat. I can see baitfish, how bass are positioned relative to the school, and which direction the bass are following. I can tell which end is the head and tail to know which end to present the bait to.'

Palaniuk: 'With the 360, I can mark isolated targets I may not have seen with just down scan or side scan. I can even see behind my boat and pinpoint structure that I missed. I can remain on a creek channel or river ledge much better.'

Palaniuk: 'I use four Humminbird Solix 12 units: two on the console (left has mapping + 2D sonar, right has Mega side/down imaging) and two on the casting deck (one on Mega 360 imaging with mapping, one for waypoints). Split screen gives two views per unit.'
Palaniuk: 'The width of the arches tells me how big the fish are, and the length shows how long the bass were in the sonar cone. The thickness of the arch determines the size of the fish.'
Palaniuk: 'During the last days of practice I just idle around dropping GPS pins in areas rather than catching fish. I use mapping clarity and LakeMaster charts to dial in the pattern.'

Palaniuk: 'Even on lakes I have fished many times, I keep an open mind and try not to fish the same places where I caught bass in past years. I keep tabs on water and weather conditions each day to determine where bass might be.'

Palaniuk: 'Braided line has the most sensitivity compared to any other line — having 15-lb braid mainline to an 8-lb fluoro leader with a Crazy Alberto knot creates a nearly same-diameter connection that runs free through the guides'

Palaniuk: 'Play a 4-5 pound smallmouth for 1-2 minutes before bringing it to the boat. When they jump, don't try to pull them down or you'll pull the hooks out. Pick them up by the belly like a football — they act paralyzed'
Palaniuk: 'The more fishing pressure the bass get, the more effective finesse fishing is with little baits, light line and spinning tackle'

Palaniuk: 'My low-light system: 1) topwater walker for aggressive fish and covering water, 2) jerkbait at 5-6 feet when bass wont surface, 3) weightless wacky-rigged stick bait as finesse to slow down for non-aggressive bass. Use all three early morning, late afternoon, and on cloudy/overcast days.'

Palaniuk: 'A stick bait with a forked tail gives a slightly different action and more shimmy on the fall than regular stick baits. I believe it draws bass from further away. Rig it wacky on a No. 2 VMC hook.'

Palaniuk: 'In fall, cooling temps trigger bass to feed heavily for winter and the spawn. Bass prefer to eat a bigger bait so they dont have to chase as much small bait. A big swimbait moves slowly through the water column so bass can track it easier. Thats why I start with a giant swimbait in October.'
Palaniuk: 'In fall, bigger bass set up to ambush bait on isolated cover in creek backs and adjacent to channel swings with big flats. Match jig color to water color.'
Palaniuk: 'For flipping jig in fall: 7-foot-6 heavy-action Alpha Angler Hitter rod, Daiwa Steez A reel, 20-pound Seaguar Tatsu fluorocarbon line.'
Palaniuk: 'Uses a specific braid to fluorocarbon leader knot for combining braided mainline with a fluorocarbon leader.'

Palaniuk: 'On a 170,000-acre lake with only three practice days, do as much online research as possible studying past tournament results from the same time period to determine where the most productive water will be'
Palaniuk: 'On large lakes you must eliminate a lot of water quickly — use past tournament results to guide your practice days to the most productive sections, then verify under current conditions'
Palaniuk: 'In the fall, weather can be a big factor in where you'll pinpoint bass and how you'll catch them — chilly mornings warming through the day will shift fish locations'

Palaniuk: 'With 15 minutes left and no keeper, don't rush — you still need to fish your lure thoroughly. Switch to power baits (jerkbait, crankbait, spinnerbait) to cover water efficiently but not frantically'
Palaniuk: 'If you haven't had bites in a while, run to productive water closer to the boat ramp — being close to the ramp lets you push the clock longer and fish until the last possible moment'
Palaniuk: 'You can't get in a hurry and attempt to force a bass to bite. Very rarely will you get a bite if you're rushing through your cast and working your bait faster than you should'

Palaniuk: 'Big fish that haven't made the full transition to main summertime structure are singles or doubles on brushpiles or standing timber with a bottom composition change (mud to hard), on straight banks leading from spawning areas to points — a midway transition point'
Palaniuk: 'Standing-timber fish sit in 18-28ft suspended 5-6ft off the bottom, relating to bottom composition changes even though they aren't on the bottom'

Palaniuk: 'The same principles applied from 4ft to 30ft: it had to have a good straight steeper edge close to a main channel — main-lake fish near the main river channel, grass fish near a creek channel'
Palaniuk: 'Parallel the edges where grass falls off to a straight wall to keep the bait in the strike zone the longest — fish roam and sit on the edge for an ambush point'

Palaniuk: 'When marking fish on electronics that won't bite big cranks or football jigs, drop a Neko rig — the first fish was a 9+. When I kept it in my hand I could go to every single brushpile and catch one'

Palaniuk: 'Use a balancing beam to compare your smallest livewell fish against each new catch — whichever side goes down stays, the other goes back. Knowing your exact weight is a real secret to winning'

Palaniuk: 'I dedicated myself to catching five big smallmouths each day rather than splitting time between largemouth and smallmouth. Consistent 19-20 pound bags beat the gamble of one big largemouth bite per day'

Palaniuk: 'Fish an 8-mile stretch moving between rock piles and boulders — build confidence on spots that reload with big smallmouths daily. I had 50 spots and was sure to catch a 4-pounder on 20 of them'

Palaniuk: 'Look for a mixture of different grass types in mats — different species grow and die at different rates creating better canopy. Always seek out the cleanest, greenest grass which produces more oxygen and is crucial in summer'
Palaniuk: 'Matted vegetation provides warmth for bass early and late in the year and helps them cool down in summer — no other type of cover can do that'

Palaniuk: 'In reeds and cattails, focus on places where they create little debris mats — grass blown in, or where some have folded over. Also look for tighter clumps and areas where contour lines show quick access to deeper water'

Palaniuk: 'In lily pads, look for tight clumps and pay attention to where you flip so the line doesn't get in the cracks between pad stems — braid slices through pads but stems and stalks hang you up'

Palaniuk: 'In hydrilla and milfoil, use contour lines to find where bottom tightens from deep to shallow or makes a point in the grass. A harder edge congregates fish better than sparse grass — find subtle points or ditches in the grass'

Palaniuk: 'Drop to 50-lb braid for frog fishing on thin cheese mats — the smaller pound test gives much better casting distance and helps in pressured areas. If you can cast farther than others, you reach unpressured fish in the back of the mats'
Palaniuk: 'Use 10-12lb Tatsu fluorocarbon for jerkbaits which sinks and helps the bait stay down, but switch to 8-10lb monofilament when pausing more than 8 seconds to keep a direct line to the bait'
Palaniuk: 'Aim Mega Live at your jerkbait even when blind casting to follow it back and see how fish react — it makes you much more efficient and helps dial in the right cadence for the day'
Palaniuk: 'Match the hatch in terms of size and color with jerkbaits. Want the lure to sit right above the bass or at their level — adjust suspension with SuspenDots placed toward the front'

Palaniuk: 'In soft cover like grass, use braid. In harder cover like wood, use fluorocarbon because braid digs in. 22-lb Tatsu fluorocarbon is a good in-between — 20 is too light for big fish in heavy cover, 25 struggles with lighter weights'
Palaniuk: 'Use a 7ft 6in heavy-fast rod for leverage and control, with an 8.1:1 gear ratio reel. Offset the power loss from high gear ratio with a bigger 100mm handle with oversized knobs'
Palaniuk: 'Shake the bait in place using your forearm, not your wrist, to create subtle action while keeping it in the strike zone'

Palaniuk: '90 percent of people say they're flipping but are actually pitching. Flipping uses a fixed amount of line, never releasing more. Pitching picks a target and creates a cast while releasing line'

Palaniuk: 'The Percentage Triangle: the wide base is the spawning flats where contour lines are widest, the point is where contours are steepest. It's all based off the spawn — the larger the triangle, the more fish it holds, and fish use contour lines like highways'

Palaniuk: 'Pull out a paper map and reduce the playing field by identifying percentage triangles. When you find where the fish are in one triangle, run to other triangles and focus on the same section — it eliminates home lake advantage'

Palaniuk: 'Florida is the hardest place to apply the Percentage Triangle because it lacks distinct contour changes. Grand Lake is one of the best — I could look at a map and tell you where I was going to catch a bass'

Palaniuk: 'The only constant we have in fishing is change. The Percentage Triangle gives you baseline confidence to pivot from practice findings when conditions change — it tells you about fish movements so you can alter your game plan quickly'

Palaniuk: 'Prefer wacky rig over Neko mainly when fishing shallow around docks or laydowns or when fish are suspended under deeper docks. Use Crossover Rings to quickly switch between Neko and wacky by pulling the weight out and switching hook direction'

Palaniuk: 'The Damiki Rig (jighead with fluke-style bait) works best when fish are in a negative mood or suspended 25-30ft off the bottom, or locked to cover. The bait just sits there and piques their curiosity because it's doing nothing'

Palaniuk: 'Use a 1/4 or 3/8-oz Neon Moon Eye Jig Head for the Damiki Rig — originally designed for walleye but the bait sits perfectly horizontal at rest and the lighter wire hook hooks fish well even when they're just nipping'

Palaniuk: 'Late August and early September are the worst times for most anglers. Bass need cooler, well-oxygenated water — find it in three places: water running into the lake (upper river/creeks), deep cover (brush/rock piles), and thick vegetation providing shade'
Palaniuk: 'Bass hold just off the current behind structure — trees in the water, rock piles coming up off the bottom, or other cover that breaks the current'

Palaniuk: 'Find good, healthy, green vegetation that looks vibrant — it produces the most oxygen and shade for bass and baitfish. The thickest part of the grass that makes a canopy over the water is where bass hold'
Palaniuk: 'Walk the frog on top of thick grass to get bass to blow through it, or fish it on edges and points of the grass. Walk it 90% of the time like a Zara Spook'

Palaniuk: 'I call it floating a crankbait -- pick a crankbait that dives deeper than the depth you're fishing, reel it so it crawls up and over rocks, then back down the other side. It looks like a crawfish slowly scooting.'

Palaniuk: 'Red is the first color in the light spectrum to disappear. Bass and bait in the spring tend to be real milky or void of color. If you're looking for something muted, red, the first color to disappear, looks the most that way.'
Palaniuk: '12-pound fluorocarbon is the optimal line for the Z2 -- the lure is sensitive to different diameters. There are occasions for 10 or 15, but 12 is the sweet spot.'

Palaniuk: 'Find the perfect mixture of a particular level of visibility mixed with a particular type of rock or rock transitions. Focus on areas where contours change from tight to wide, with something unique -- a laydown, small rockpile, or bottom composition change that concentrates the strike zone.'

Palaniuk: 'As fish progress toward the spawn, you want things to be as stable as possible. On large waters, wind might be the biggest factor. On rivers, it might be water levels and current. Find the places that are most stable and least affected by conditions.'

Palaniuk: 'If fish are high in the water column, pick up a jerkbait. If they're toward the bottom, pick up a crankbait. Use forward-facing sonar to see where fish are positioned and choose accordingly.'

Palaniuk: 'Throws the Big-M 4.0 on 15-lb Tatsu fluorocarbon -- 15 is the perfect balance of diving ability and action, though not a stretch to go to 20. Uses 12-lb Tatsu with the Kanata and mid-depth crankbaits as his go-to mid-depth zone line.'

Palaniuk: 'Used Humminbird MEGA Live 2 not to target specific fish but to scan around and see where fish were showing up in groups. If seeing them high in the water column, he knew which bait to pick up. If toward the bottom, he'd pick a crankbait.'

Palaniuk: 'I was popping the rod almost like stroking a jig. The crankbait was trying to dive but the line was pulling up at the same time -- like a teeter-totter or a bull bucking. I don't think that fish would have hit otherwise.'

Palaniuk: 'When water is still really low and clearer than normal in the spring, fish want to go shallow but there is nothing for them to get on -- they are just sitting and waiting on those transitions, the first breaks. Shallow crankbaits become the top producer.'

Palaniuk: 'When the water hits the upper 40s and low 50s, fish start getting more active and willing to eat a big bait. Northern strain bass get much more aggressive sooner in the year since they are used to living in a colder climate.'
Palaniuk: 'I fish the same areas that I throw a crankbait or jerkbait, but use the glide bait when I am after the really big ones.'

Palaniuk: 'Uses a 7-foot medium-action spinning rod, spinning reel with 8-lb Nanofil to 8-lb fluorocarbon leader. Ties on a snap swivel to prevent line twist and give the Jigging Rap more freedom -- the smaller the swivel the better, as large snaps may get caught in the lure's hooks.'

Palaniuk: 'Uses Humminbird to position the boat over individual fish, then drops the lure into its strike zone. Watching the screen shows lure depth and fish response, which helps refine tactics to trigger a reaction strike.'

Palaniuk: 'Fish isolated rockpiles, boulders and grass patches anywhere from 8 to 22 feet. It just has to be the right-looking stuff -- depth didn't really seem to matter. Had a milk run of places to hit and cycle through, and at some point during the day would run into a group of fish actively feeding.'
Palaniuk: 'Uses a Super Fluke (minnow-style bait) on the dropshot with a 2/0 straight shank hook and 3/8-oz weight. 15-lb Smackdown braid to 8-lb Tatsu fluorocarbon leader.'

Palaniuk: 'Humminbird 360 and Ultrex trolling motor were big, especially when the wind was blowing. Could pull up on isolated targets, line up with the 360 and know exactly how far away he was, keep the boat off the fish and make repeated specific casts.'

Palaniuk: 'The spot had a riprap bank with a steep drop, then a trough that came up onto a hard bottom ridge, tapered back off into another trough. The trough made a horseshoe. A 40-yard section where the fish wanted to live, with a 25-foot hole the trough dumps into.'
Palaniuk: 'MEGA Live 2 is way better than version 1. Could tell the detail of what was a bass, what was a gar, what was a crappie -- had a lot to do with how they were positioned and how they moved.'
Palaniuk: 'If seeing fish high in the water column on MEGA Live 2, pick up a jerkbait or big bait. If seeing them toward the bottom, pick up a crankbait.'

Palaniuk: 'When 3 other competitors were already on his spot, he adapted by using forward-facing sonar to gauge fish interest and differentiated his approach with oversized crankbaits and jerkbaits rather than downsizing to jig and minnow like the others.'

Palaniuk: 'Big M 4.0: 15-lb Tatsu fluoro, Daiwa Steez A reel (6.3:1), 7-foot 6-inch MH rod. Prototype IPX: 12-lb Tatsu fluoro, Daiwa Zillion reel (7.1:1), 7-foot MH Mod rod. Kanata: 12-lb Tatsu fluoro, Daiwa Steez CT SV reel, 6-foot 10-inch rod.'

Palaniuk: 'First, there has to be a decent population of 4-pounders. Second, the bass need to be feeding on larger forage -- gizzard shad, trout, or perch. Third, fish should be in less than 10 feet of water for it to be consistently good, which favors February through May.'

Palaniuk: 'In the spring you want more warm sunshine, maybe even calmer water, so it pushes big bass to docks and laydowns. In August, you might still want sunshine, but a little more wind or current will generally make things better.'

Palaniuk: 'A 7-inch glide is the sweet spot. With a 6-inch Magdraft you'll catch 2 and 3-pounders galore. Going up in size doesn't always mean less bites -- sometimes fish just want the most bang for their buck.'
Palaniuk: 'At Chickamauga, I had to switch from a 7-inch glide to the 8-inch Magdraft. The fish had switched from wanting a side-to-side action to something straight and more methodical.'

Palaniuk: 'Uses 22-lb Seaguar Tatsu for big baits -- small diameter, high strength, and supple double structure. Suppleness is particularly important with glide baits because it allows them to be fluid. Most bites come on the pause, so if line coils up when you pull it, the lure won't look natural.'

Palaniuk: 'Pairs the swimbait rod with a Daiwa Tatula 300 with 7.1:1 gear ratio -- faster than most would use, but the 110mm handle, bigger gears and oversized knobs make up for any loss in power. In tournaments, big bait casts are typically target-oriented, not long retrieves.'

Palaniuk: 'At Champlain, one big-bait bite on the way to weigh-in gave me confidence to start on largemouths the next day. I saw a weather system coming in, so I tied on multiple swimbaits and glide baits.'

Palaniuk: 'When it slicked off, the bait would roam wherever in the lake. But when the wind blew it pushed bait to the edges of deep flats, and smallmouth would pull up and get on rocks and stuff on those flats.'
Palaniuk: 'Fish were in 20-40 feet but 25-32 feet was where the flats set up and held the best average quality fish. Targeted sand-to-rock transitions with gravelly rock and giant isolated boulders.'

Palaniuk: 'On the last day, realized smallmouth were just following bait across 40-foot sand flats. Made the decision to jump downwind to intercept the same group of fish again -- first drop caught almost a 4.5-pounder.'
Palaniuk: 'Dropshot: 3/8-oz VMC tungsten weight, #2 hook, 8-lb Tatsu fluoro leader (15 feet) to 15-lb Smackdown Flash Green braid. 15-lb braid is super small, 6-lb equivalent diameter.'

Palaniuk: 'Used LakeMaster charts to drive to spots -- based on contours could idle over a spot, see fish, and catch them almost every single stop. Spent the third practice day just driving and marking spots rather than fishing.'
Palaniuk: 'Standard glide bait retrieve is a 3/4 handle turn that pulls the bait to the side, then a 1/4 turn and stop, which allows it to glide.'

Palaniuk: 'Took time to do research online, looking at areas that continually came up in past tournament results no matter what time of year. That way he knew a population of fish lived in that area, then could see how they were using those places that week.'

Palaniuk: 'Strategy was to figure out how to get one big bite and then fill out a limit. That one big bite was a difference-maker for guys cutting checks. The guy who'd win would get 2-3 of those in a day vs just getting 1.'

Palaniuk: 'Read the entire vegetation management plan document to figure out where grass was and wasn't. Found only a 50-yard section of submerged grass. Because it was the only submerged stuff within miles, it held bait and fish. It wasn't far off the main lake so bigger fish could move in and out easily.'

Palaniuk: 'Punch rig: Muscle Back Finesse Craw (black/blue), 3/0 VMC Heavy Duty Flippin Hook, 1.25-oz tungsten weight, VMC Sinker Stop, 65-lb Smackdown Stealth Grey braid, 7-foot 11-inch Heavy rod. Best mats were 1.5-3 foot hyacinth with giant salvinia blown up against them.'

Palaniuk: 'Dropshot at Santee: 6-foot 10-inch M rod, 15-lb Smackdown Flash Green braid to 10-lb Seaguar Gold Label fluoro, 1/4-oz VMC tungsten teardrop weight, #1 VMC Finesse Neko Hook, 6-inch Deception Worm (GP blue).'

Palaniuk: 'Cast the soft swimbait out and let it hit bottom. Reeled at a fairly decent pace in 4-6 feet of water, trying to keep it right on the bottom and bounce it off shell or stumps. When it deflected off something, he'd get bites.'

Palaniuk: 'For river fishing, I want a lure that will be appropriate for the type of cover I expect to find. My top three are a jig, crankbait, and spinnerbait because each can be fished in nearly any river situation.'
Palaniuk: 'I couldn't see many bass in down scanning mode while idling, but when I used my bow graph set on 2D sonar in 2X bottom scan mode, I could see individual smallmouths near the bottom.'

Palaniuk: 'I looked for the largest spawning areas near deep water and the best smallmouth habitat. The bass were on points and steep drops near those points in 20 to 35 feet. They were relating to sand and gravel seams on some spots and boulders on others.'
Palaniuk: 'I often watched a bass on my graph move up to my bait as it was sinking and then followed it to the bottom. Whenever that happened, a strike was almost sure to follow.'
Palaniuk: 'If you slow down and work topwater in the strike zones, you'll get more bites and the bites you do get hook up a lot better.'

Palaniuk: 'The right rod for topwater needs a good backbone and a good light tip that allows you to control the bait very well. That creates a system where you can stay in the strike zone and take your time.'

Palaniuk: 'For southern regions the key player is the shad — I lean towards colors with more flash, silver or white hues with a touch of color. For northern regions, fish follow perch on outside weedlines and transitions to deep water, so I go more towards greenish hues, often with vertical bars to imitate perch.'

Palaniuk: 'Fall is when the difference between primary forage up north vs down south is most noticeable. The baitfish start to make their fall transitions, with bass soon to follow or already set up and waiting.'
Palaniuk: 'The palomar knot is my favorite knot for any given technique.'

Palaniuk: 'Start with 4-5 baseline colors based on conditions and experience. If the baseline isn't working, bracket — go more natural/transparent one direction, more opaque/bright the other. Identify the strongest response, make that your new baseline, and bracket again. It may take 6-7 color switches to dial in.'

Palaniuk: 'With forward-facing sonar I can see in real time how fish react to different colors. I can see how many are looking, chasing, and not reacting. That has changed how I fish jerkbaits and sped up my color bracketing process.'

Palaniuk: 'I always change cadence first, then color, then profile. Cadence is the easiest thing to change quickly. Color becomes the deciding factor when cadence adjustments don't generate the bite.'

Palaniuk: 'Jerkbaits aren't merely a seasonal tool for the coldest months. I've made them 12-month options. Color selections aren't typically seasonal but relate to water conditions, sunlight, wind, and forage.'

Palaniuk: 'My baseline jerkbait colors are Mat Shad, Ito Wakasagi, GP Pro Blue, HT Ito Tennessee Shad, and Table Rock SP. Rather than copying my colors, adopt the bracketing system for your specific waters.'

Palaniuk: 'The largest bass prefer cooler water. During late fall, they get more active since the water is cooling and shorter days trigger them to feed more. Big bass are very efficient at eating an easy, large meal. They usually won't waste a lot of energy chasing something down.'

Palaniuk: 'A glide bait's slow side-to-side motion has drawing power that will pull fish out of deeper water and cover like submerged brush piles or bluff ends. You can fish it fast or slow with 3/4-turns and a pause. Definitely a big fish bait.'
Palaniuk: 'For glide baits, use a 7-foot-9 mag/heavy action rod paired with 20-pound Seaguar Tatsu fluorocarbon. You want some shock absorption when fishing this bait.'

Palaniuk: 'For winter jerkbaits, use a smaller 70-size reel with 7.1:1 gear ratio in cold water, then bump up to 8.1:1 as it warms into spring. The smaller spool means even a faster ratio isn't too fast.'

Palaniuk: 'For jighead minnow in winter, I use a 3000-size spinning reel. Smoothness of the drag from start to finish is critical, and the smoothness of the retrieve impacts your technique, especially when constantly shaking your rod and reeling in slack.'

Palaniuk: 'For winter cranking, I prefer a 7.1:1 gear ratio with Hyperdrive gears. Since you usually retrieve a crankbait fairly slowly in winter, smooth gears let you feel everything your crankbait is doing. You can lose sensitivity if gears are not smooth.'

Palaniuk: 'If you can still find vegetation in winter, get there as fast as possible. On places like Okeechobee, big prespawn females will be under those mats. Use a heavy-duty reel with bigger spool, handles, and gears to winch big ones out of cover.'

Palaniuk: Won Bull Shoals by capitalizing on a specific spot — flooded roadbed and bridge pilings in West Sugarloaf Creek — catching 20-pound bags the first two days and 19 pounds on Day Three. He was the 99th boat to launch on Day One, so he took advantage of a spot others passed over.

Palaniuk: 'At temperature extremes (below 42F or above 90F), bass metabolism is either too slow or they conserve energy by sitting in shade. An opportunistic feeder will still take one more bite if it's an easy meal — a finesse bait is ideal.'
Palaniuk: 'Keep a tight drag on a 7-foot medium-action spinning rod with 8- to 10-pound fluorocarbon leader on 8-pound braid. Never ventures above 10-pound test even fishing heavy brush.'

Palaniuk: 'In fall I operate under the theory that you downsize your baits. Baitfish born in spring have grown but are still smaller. These smaller baitfish school in large numbers making them easy pickings for bass. Downsizing to match the hatch ensures more consistent bites and might be your best shot at a true giant.'
Palaniuk: 'Always have multiple sizes of baits in fall. A fish's mood can change day to day, even hour to hour. Having multiple offerings to present can pay off with extra bites throughout the day.'

Palaniuk: 'All the things we thought were black-and-white rules about how to work a jerkbait during different times of year are not always the case. It doesn't always have to be a fast pace — you just have to read each fish. If you don't have forward-facing sonar, the general rule of thumb is move the jerkbait faster.'

Palaniuk: 'Use a glidebait to draw fish and identify interest, then follow up with a large jerkbait to convert followers. The jerkbait has erratic dying action that glidebaits can't replicate, triggering bites from fish that won't fully commit to the swimbait.'
Palaniuk: 'I fish all jerkbaits on an Alpha Angler Slasher casting rod with a Daiwa 70-size reel and 12-pound Seaguar Tatsu fluorocarbon. The small reel palms well, reducing forearm fatigue.'

Palaniuk: 'A baitfish stuck in the gullet of a bass provides valuable information. Bait color and size are key factors when choosing jerkbaits. Mat shad works well for largemouth; in clear northern waters, half-tone translucent colors like pro blue and HT Tennessee shad are good starting points.'

Palaniuk: 'With forward-facing sonar, look at the distance between head and tail on screen — greater separation often signals a better-quality fish. This allows prioritizing casts and staying efficient, targeting the right fish.'

Palaniuk: 'Make small, deliberate trolling motor movements to get multiple angles on key cover. Small changes in viewing angles may reveal hidden limbs, isolated fish, or changes in bottom composition.'
Palaniuk: 'Spotted bass often group by size, varying from section to section. Recognizing these patterns lets you shift quickly between areas and be selective.'

robertson.insight: Robertson: During prespawn at Chickamauga, fish an offshore pattern with a lipless crankbait in grass, then go headhunting with a big swimbait for kicker bites.
Robertson: On heavily-pressured lakes, wind is the great equalizer -- when the wind blows, the fish bite. Want it to blow hard.
Robertson: After a tough start to a season, commit to fishing your strengths rather than adjusting to what others are doing. Fish for five bites and don't care if you blank -- fish how you want.
Robertson: A cold snap during prespawn can work in your favor by slowing fish down and keeping them in their prespawn staging areas longer.
Robertson: After the spawn on Kentucky Lake, target points at the mouths of bays where fish move to after spawning. High water can bust up schools, requiring an open mind.
Robertson: Fish move a lot with rising water -- keep an open mind and don't get stuck on previous spots. Be willing to search for new schools.
Robertson: Fish the mid-section of Kentucky Lake from Blood River to Highway 68 Bridge targeting post-spawn largemouth on structure.
Robertson: High water busts up schools of post-spawn bass. Keep an open mind, don't get stuck on previous spots, and be willing to search for new fish.
Robertson: As a ledge/Tennessee River angler, partnered with Outkast Tackle for jigs, bringing an offshore jig perspective to their lineup.
Robertson: When you stop caring too much about the outcome and just go fishing, good things happen. Assumed he'd weigh 6 pounds and stopped pressing -- ended up catching 25-8.
Robertson: Sometimes the bite doesn't develop until afternoon. All five keepers caught after 12:50 PM on Lake Murray in April -- don't give up when the morning is slow.
Robertson: At the 2021 Classic on Ray Roberts, fished a 3/8-oz Outkast Swim Jig (white) with Berkley Chigger Craw (white), 50-lb Berkley X5 Braid, Abu REVO STX 6.6 reel, 6'8" MH Ugly Stik rod.

Robertson: Fish exclusively offshore by idling every inch of a lake to find hard-bottom spots in 9-10 feet with no visible structure. Use electronics to see big bass stacked on these areas -- spots as small as 40x50 yards can hold massive numbers.

Robertson: Idle every inch of the lake to find hard spots on the bottom. When you see big bass on electronics over featureless-looking bottom, commit to fishing offshore -- pull out big swimbaits, big spinnerbaits, and big crankbaits.
Robertson: If you're going to fail, fail doing what you do well. When a shallow approach produces only 11 pounds, switch to offshore fishing with big baits if that's your strength.
Robertson: Loves throwing hollow-bodied frogs almost any time of year. The Scum Frog Launch Frog and Trophy Series Frog are his go-to frog baits.

Robertson: Spring crankbait progression -- 1) Prespawn: slow-roll lipless on flats near channel banks in 1-6 feet; 2) Postspawn: big-bodied squarebill on tapering points near spawning flats in under 10 feet; 3) Late postspawn: deep diver following ditches and drainages 10-15 feet out to summer structure.

Robertson: On lakes without heavy vegetation (like Kentucky Lake), slow-roll the lipless just off the bottom rather than ripping it through grass. Move the bait only 12-18 inches at a time horizontally, almost like yo-yoing.
Robertson: Postspawners don't immediately go deep. They hold on small tapering points and bars about 150 yards from spawning flats for about three weeks. New fish keep replenishing the spot.
Robertson: Use shad-type colors for lipless crankbaits on lakes without vegetation -- not the reds and oranges that work in grass.
Robertson: Uses 12-lb Berkley Big Game mono for all crankbaits. 7-foot Ugly Stik (MH parabolic) for squarebills and lipless; 7'11" Abu Garcia Winch (MH parabolic) for deep divers.

Robertson: When following postspawn bass down ditches with a deep diver, start by casting ahead and up on top, then bring the crankbait down the side of the ditch at about a 45-degree angle. The crankbait must hit bottom.

Robertson: On fisheries known for shallow grass fishing like the Harris Chain, look for offshore hard-bottom spots 150 yards from shore in 9-10 feet of water. Idle over the area and use electronics to spot big bass — a 40x50 yard hard spot can hold fish that replenish daily.

Robertson: If you're an offshore fisherman, commit to your style even on fisheries known for shallow patterns. When a shallow approach fails, go back to what you do well — if you're going to die, die doing what you do well.
Robertson: Idle every inch of a lake during practice to find offshore spots. Look for hard-bottom areas where you can see big bass on electronics, even if you can't get them to bite in practice.
Robertson: Hollow-bodied frogs like the Scum Frog Launch Frog and Trophy Series Frog can be thrown almost any time of year, not just during typical frog season.
Robertson: The Ugly Stik Carbon rod offers lightweight sensitivity combined with legendary toughness, proving that an accessible working-man's rod can compete at the Bassmaster Elite Series level.
Robertson: As a ledge and Tennessee River specialist, bringing an offshore jig-fishing perspective to jig design helps create products tuned for deep structure fishing.
Robertson: On TVA (Tennessee Valley Authority) lakes in summer, you have no choice but to fish offshore. You come to the TVA in the summer, you better get off the bank.
Robertson: On offshore ledges, you won't get a lot of bites, but figuring out one or two little things to trigger bigger bites can make the difference. It's about quality over quantity.
Robertson: On ledge fisheries, rotate through multiple spots rather than hammering one area. This keeps the fish from getting too pressured and ensures fresh opportunities throughout the day.

Robertson: Sometimes when practice goes poorly, the best approach is to stop caring so much about the outcome. Just go fishing and let things happen rather than forcing it — this mental shift can unlock better bites.
Robertson: Don't give up on a tournament day even with a rough morning. All five keepers in a 25-pound bag can come after 12:50 PM when you stop overthinking and just go fishing.

Robertson: On heavily pressured lakes, wind is the great equalizer. When the wind blows, the fish bite — especially on a lake with significant fishing pressure. The only thing that equalizes the pressure is the weather.

Robertson: During prespawn, a cold snap that slows bass from moving shallow is beneficial for offshore prespawn fishing. If fish are still wanting to spawn, cold weather keeps them staged offshore longer.
Robertson: Commit to fishing your own way. If you come in with no fish, that's fine because you're fishing how you want. Fish for five bites and don't worry about the outcome.
Robertson: After a tough start to a season, make the decision to fish more to your strengths rather than trying to adapt to every situation. Leaning into what you do best produces better results.
Robertson: Being self-employed (pressure cleaning business) provides the schedule flexibility needed to fish multi-day tournaments and pursue a professional fishing career through the Opens path.

Robertson: Winning offshore areas can be essentially featureless — no defined contour, just a hard bottom with 'sort of a point but not really.' The distinguishing feature is simply hard bottom 150 yards from shore.

Robertson: On offshore hard-bottom spots, bass move to the outside edge in bright sun but hold 40 yards closer to shore under cloud cover. When you hit a lull in the action, it often means they've relocated.
Robertson: The Garmin Panoptix LiveScope is essential for relocating offshore fish when they shift position. Just move the trolling motor around to find them again without losing time.

Robertson: After catching your limit quickly on an offshore spot, stay on the spot to protect it for the rest of the day rather than risking someone else finding it. This was a key strategic move that preserved fish for the next day.

Robertson: On fisheries known for shallow-water patterns, offshore fish have likely never seen a 7-inch swimbait or a big spinnerbait. Throwing big baits that fish haven't been exposed to is a major advantage.

Robertson: When fishing 10-foot depths offshore, 20-pound fluorocarbon works for swimbaits, crankbaits, and spinnerbaits. The heavier line won't spook fish at that depth and prevents break-offs. Normally he'd use mono for swimbaits and lighter fluoro for cranking.

Robertson: A wind direction change can destroy a deep-water pattern by muddying previously clear water. When visibility drops from 6 feet to 2 feet, bass stop moving through the area and the deep bite dies. Be ready to switch to a shallow backup plan.
Robertson: In fall, smallmouth stack up on boulder fields in 20 feet of water where baitfish are concentrated. The fish can be found within a very small 60-foot area when they're grouped up.

Robertson: Adding SuspenDots and heavier aftermarket treble hooks to a walking topwater bait reduces buoyancy so the belly rides lower in the water. This gives the bait a different action and results in better hookup rates because fish get the bait and hooks instead of blowing it out of the water.

Robertson: The prespawn period is one of Robertson's strongest seasonal patterns, complementing his offshore fishing identity. He considers himself a 'heavyweight angler' who prefers big-fish fisheries.
Robertson: When fishing eelgrass flats, the big bites come from areas where the grass is not dense but more isolated. Concentrate on those isolated clump areas rather than the thick grass mats.
Robertson: On lakes with big fish like Santee Cooper, commit to fishing for big bites rather than playing it safe for consistency. Fish to win rather than just survive.

Robertson: The shad spawn is a morning-only pattern. Spend 1-1.5 hours on it maximum, then transition to other patterns. Having limited shad-spawn locations means you must have a backup plan for the rest of the day.
Robertson: The Ugly Stik Carbon provides lightweight sensitivity with legendary toughness, proving an Ugly Stik can be both your first rod and your last rod, even at the Elite Series level.

Robertson: On TVA lakes in summer, you have no choice but to get off the bank. 25+ years of experience on ledge fisheries like Kentucky Lake directly translates to success on similar Tennessee River impoundments like Pickwick.
Robertson: On offshore ledges you won't get a lot of bites, but one or two subtle adjustments that other anglers aren't doing can trigger the bigger bites that make the difference.

Robertson: Don't avoid community holes on ledge fisheries. Fish some community spots and let other anglers fish ahead of you, but also have your own spots where you catch the bulk of your weight undisturbed.
Robertson: When idling offshore, look for hard-bottom spots that hold big bass even with no visible structure. A 40x50 yard hard spot in 9-10 feet can hold large concentrations of quality fish.
Robertson: Idle every inch of a lake to find offshore hard spots — if you see big bass on electronics but can't get them to bite in practice, commit to the spot in the tournament.
Robertson: If you're going to fail, fail doing what you do well. Commit to your strengths — for Robertson, that's fishing offshore.
Robertson: Loves throwing hollow-bodied frogs almost any time of year — not just a summer-only technique.
Robertson: Considers the Scum Frog Launch Frog and Trophy Series Frog legendary baits for catching giant bass.
Robertson: On heavily pressured Tennessee River lakes, wind is the great equalizer — when the wind blows, the fish bite. Root for bad weather on pressured fisheries.
Robertson: A cold snap during prespawn can work in your favor by keeping fish offshore and slowing the spawn, extending the prespawn feeding window.
Robertson: Commit to fishing your strengths — if you come in with no fish, that's fine because you fished how you wanted. Don't chase other patterns.
Robertson: Wind equalizes fishing pressure on Tennessee River lakes — the only thing that overcomes heavy pressure is weather. Root for it to blow hard.
Robertson: During prespawn, a cold snap slowing fish from spawning keeps them in the offshore staging pattern longer, which benefits offshore anglers.
Robertson: Sometimes the best thing is to stop caring too much about results — when he stopped overthinking and just went fishing, he caught 25 pounds including a 6-14 kicker.
Robertson: Don't panic if the morning is slow — all five of his weigh-in fish were caught after 12:50 PM after boat problems forced him to restart his day.
Robertson: Fish more deliberately and stop second-guessing. Go with your gut instead of constantly questioning whether to move.
Robertson: On TVA (Tennessee Valley Authority) lakes in summer, you have no choice but to get off the bank. Fish the offshore ledges — that's where the bass live.

Robertson: On offshore ledges, you won't get a lot of bites, but there are little things you can do to trigger bigger bites that other anglers aren't doing — subtle presentation adjustments make the difference.
Robertson: Don't avoid community holes on ledge fisheries — let other anglers fish through first, then work behind them. But save your best weight-producing spots where you won't have competition.

Robertson: A lifetime of experience on Tennessee River ledge fisheries (Kentucky Lake, Pickwick) is invaluable — knowing how fish set up on ledges from years of fishing them gives you an edge others can't replicate.

Robertson: On Lake Okeechobee during prespawn, quantity comes first and quality follows — Day 1 produced 60 fish of lesser quality, Day 2 produced 40, and Day 3 produced fewer but bigger fish as spawning females moved in.
Robertson: Staying in a 600-yard area all three days can pay off if the fish are the right caliber — commit when practice shows big bites and wait for weather to stabilize post-cold-front.
Robertson: Use 65-pound braid in heavy Florida vegetation even when fluorocarbon might get more bites — avoiding heartbreak in the grass is more important than getting extra bites.
Robertson: In Florida grass (pencil reeds, flat reeds, Kissimmee grass, hyacinth), drop the trolling motor and cover water with swimbaits rather than sitting on one spot.
Robertson: Won the Cherokee Lake Open with a drop shot and topwater combination during post-tropical-storm, post-frontal fall conditions.

Robertson: Modify a Strike King Sexy Dawg by replacing stock hooks with round-bend treble hooks and adding SuspenDots to the belly — this makes it sit lower, improves castability, and prevents fish from popping the bait out of the water.
Robertson: Look for bass hunting bait in small feeding groups called 'wolf packs' — these aggressive groups will respond well to topwater presentations in pockets and around scattered rock.
Robertson: Be willing to completely scrap your game plan midday if the pattern dies. Set a deadline (e.g., noon) and if it's not producing, switch to something in your wheelhouse.
Robertson: Fish more deliberately and stop second-guessing. Have faith in yourself and roll with your decisions instead of bouncing between spots.
Robertson: Fishing rods don't have to cost a fortune to compete at the highest level — Robertson finished 7th at the 2021 Bassmaster Classic using Ugly Stik Carbon rods that retail under $80.
Robertson: Fishes the Bassmaster Elite Series with Ugly Stik Carbon rods, proving that the working man's rod can compete at the highest level of professional bass fishing.

wheeler.insight: Wheeler: The Punch Out Craw has five manifestations - full glide bait on 4/0 hook and 1/2-oz sinker, separated claws for football jig or Biffle Hardhead, trimmed small craw for swim jig/flipping jig/chatterbait/shakyhead, shortened torso for even smaller profile, and sleek punch rig with claws removed for matted vegetation.
Wheeler: When deadsticking a creature bait on a football jig or Hardhead, the claws and spear-like appendage rise to almost a 90-degree angle off the bottom and quiver and undulate.
Wheeler: Uses a curly-tail worm Texas-rigged on a keel-weighted swimbait hook as a more subtle alternative when everyone else is throwing swimbaits; the keel sinker makes it run straight as an arrow.
Wheeler: Carolina rig setup uses 7.5-foot medium-heavy rod, 7.0:1 reel, 17-lb fluorocarbon mainline to barrel swivel, then 14-lb fluorocarbon leader of 22 inches (18-26 inch range).

Wheeler: Respool with fresh line at midday on the last practice day, not the night before the tournament; this gives you the afternoon to adjust to the feel of new line and fix any issues like too much or too little line on a spinning reel before competition.

Wheeler: If limited to three baits for any season or condition, picks a DT-6 crankbait, a buzzbait, and a Biffle Bug creature bait; prefers moving/reaction baits over finesse and likes to force reflex bites.
Wheeler: Prefers to force fish into biting out of reflex rather than teasing them with finesse; a bait suddenly getting in their space triggers a reaction bite.
Wheeler: Usually fishes the DT-6 on 12-lb Sufix Siege monofilament but varies line size to control depth; heavier mono adds buoyancy for shallower running, lighter line increases diving depth.

Wheeler: Invests a full week or more before arriving at a lake for practice; prepares roughly 35 pre-rigged Duckett rods with fresh Sufix line and baits matched to the seasonal pattern, spending 1-2 full days just on rod prep.

Wheeler: Uses Google Earth aerial imagery to assess vegetation and bank steepness, plus Navionics and Lowrance C-Maps for depth contours; focuses practice on areas holding the biggest concentrations of fish all doing the same thing.
Wheeler: For a spring pre-spawn event limited to three rods, picks a jerkbait/crankbait for deeper staging fish, a 1/2-oz jig for versatility, and a ChatterBait for stained shallow water.
Wheeler: Learned to pattern bass at age 16 - the ability to go to an entirely new lake and figure it out rather than just fishing spots or chasing distant memories.

Wheeler: Keeps a list in his phone of things he's weak at or needs to clean up each fall; believes you always have to challenge yourself to be better because the fish adjust and you must adjust your techniques and strategy.

Wheeler: The difference between top anglers and those getting relegated is minimal now; it comes down to minute details like the difference between 8- or 10-lb braid on a spinning rod, or a knot or hook choice.

Wheeler: Never stops thinking about the next best technique; challenges himself on pitching, flipping, boat setup, and preparation; the offseason doesn't begin until November with boat prep and recon trips to next year's venues.
Wheeler: Gets very motivated by little things - a poke from a competitor or something he hears - that he channels out on the water.

Wheeler: Goal on day 1 of qualifying is to catch as much as possible and build a cushion; if the cut weight is about half his day-1 total, he uses day 2 to practice and find new areas for later rounds.
Wheeler: On large open fisheries, wind dictates where you can fish; plans areas for different wind directions and uses calm days to explore the whole fishery.

Wheeler: Pre-practice information becomes irrelevant during long events; having a cushion over the cut and a chance to look around on the second qualifying day provides the most up-to-date information possible.
Wheeler: Events on big fisheries won't be won catching big numbers of 1-8 and 1-9 bass; you need mid-grade fish between 4 and 6 pounds to win.

Wheeler: On flat fisheries bass stay in the shallow zone a long time before spawning, but on highland reservoirs they may stage in 6-10 feet before moving up; crankbait selection should match the lake type and depth zone.
Wheeler: Bass don't all spawn at once; they spawn in waves so some will be in pre-spawn and some will be done, and both times are perfect for crankbaits.

Wheeler: At the end of the day crankbait selection comes down to trial and error; sometimes everything says throw a bright squarebill shallow but someone is catching them on deep-diving baits in shad patterns. Always have a starting point with favorite baits in a few colors but be willing to experiment.

Wheeler: The 7'1" MH casting rod is great for jigs, spinnerbaits, and single-hook baits around tight cover like docks, laydowns, and overhangs; the 7'4" MH moderate-fast casting rod is great for offshore cranking and big swimbaits with its parabolic bend that increases landing percentage on treble hook baits.
Wheeler: In late February/early March, bass group up in underwater ditches 30-40 feet deep; targets them with underspin or small swimbait on light fluorocarbon.

Wheeler: In fall, bass follow shad to backs of creeks onto flats, but the biggest misconception is only focusing on creeks; don't overlook main lake flats, flat points on the main lake, and mouths of creeks.
Wheeler: In fall, fish school up in ditches, on little flats in 2-4 feet, and especially around grass; on grassless lakes, key on shallow ditches with stumps that let bass ambush shad.
Wheeler: Constantly moves boat in 4-8 feet of water casting shallower; covers a lot of dead water but once you find them you catch a lot of fish.

Wheeler: Uses 7-foot medium-action rod with tip and backbone, 7.3:1 high-speed reel (no disadvantage to high-speed for topwater - ability to catch up to fish), and 17-lb Sufix monofilament that floats and has slight stretch.

Wheeler: This is the bass rut - they're heading to the bank and you need to intercept them in transit; stresses interpreting water temperature and clarity, reading the bank (different rock types), and figuring out the depth zone bass are hanging.

Wheeler: Multiple patterns usually happen simultaneously on complex reservoirs; anglers tend to rush the bass in spring - just because it's warm out doesn't mean they're all on the bank; there's a significant lag between air and water temperature so don't overlook deeper main lake bass.
Wheeler: Many bass spawn on the main lake, not just up in creek arms and pockets.
Wheeler: Targets bluff ends and transitional banks leading into pockets; looks for distinct areas with shale rock and a creek or river channel nearby.

Wheeler: If struggling to find something, just drop your trolling motor and run the bank with a handful of favorite crankbaits to contact your first fish; the first bass is an essential clue to start refining your day.
Wheeler: Jerkbait setup uses 6'10" medium Duckett rod, 7.5:1 reel, and 12-lb Sufix Advance fluorocarbon; crankbait setup uses 7'2" MH composite rod with 6.8:1 reel.

Wheeler: Won the 2012 Forrest Wood Cup at Lanier going shallow while the field fished deep for spotted bass; saw cruising largemouth around bluegill beds in pockets and committed to the shallow pattern with almost no competition.
Wheeler: Saved mid-lake pockets for the final day of competition; the strategy of not burning through all areas early preserved fresh spots when he needed them most.
Wheeler: Set a specific goal weight of 12 lbs on the final day knowing his competitor would need over 18 to beat him; worked methodically toward that target rather than fishing recklessly.
Wheeler: When a bass dogs a prop bait without striking, pause it for as long as 6 seconds; the fish finally inhaled the topwater after the extended pause.

Wheeler: Uses Lowrance HDS LIVE 12 to find bass in deep wintering holes or around brush piles, then drops a Jigging Rap for vertical presentation; targets bass that are grouped together and competing for food.
Wheeler: Uses a 7-foot medium-heavy baitcasting rod with 7.3:1 reel; 14-lb Sufix fluorocarbon when jigging around brush, 12-lb in open areas to help the bait sink faster.

Wheeler: Uses a come-and-get-it approach by aggressively snapping the Jigging Rap well above fish rather than dropping it in their face; forces an interested bass to commit by swimming up, making it more likely to strike.
Wheeler: Played a 1-2 punch of smallmouth on calm days using forward-facing sonar and largemouth in reeds; caught 50+ lbs of smallmouth day 1 then pivoted to largemouth to lock up AOY.
Wheeler: Left his best largemouth stretch on the final day to let it rest, fished other areas, then returned to find the fish had reloaded and repositioned; caught 6 fish in 10 bites to win.
Wheeler: In 14-16 inches of clear water, hooking one bass spooks 4-5 more in the area; they need time to set back up and reposition before you can catch them again; requires long casts.
Wheeler: Lowrance ActiveTarget 2 was a huge player for smallmouth on day 1, giving breathing room to focus on largemouth the rest of the tournament.
Wheeler: Lowrance C-Maps had great detail in deeper stretches and even backwaters for largemouth; showed where deeper reeds and cover were and where to get up on pad for efficiency.
Wheeler: Power-Poles are the most underrated piece of equipment; locked in at 1-2 feet of water in 20+ mph wind, inching up 20-30 feet at a time and dropping Poles for stealth.
Wheeler: Wind direction changes on the Great Lakes change the way smallmouth position big time; a shift from practice to tournament conditions can mean never relocating them.

Wheeler: On TN River lakes, post-spawn fish move to offshore spots in waves — first a few show up, then a few more, then suddenly 100 bass are there 5 days later. If you can be the first one to find them arriving, it can be a lot of fun

Wheeler: Community areas are big giant points and lead-out places where ditches and creek channels come out — the highway bass use to exit spawning areas. Sneakier spots are along straight ledges where bass set up due to current swing or a little hard spot, and these change year to year

Wheeler: On every TN River lake, if you start at the dam and work up, offshore schools are deeper at the lower end and shallower on the upper end. At Guntersville he had schools from 8 to 35 ft deep, with 15-20 ft most common

Wheeler: Catch fish fast enough early each day to use the remaining periods for graphing and finding new schools — that's the way to win offshore TN River events. Finding fresh fish others haven't located is the biggest advantage
Wheeler: With a big lead, stop setting the hook on 4.5-lb fish on community holes — those fish might be the ones that win the tournament for you on the final day

Wheeler: ActiveTarget 2 provides about 20% clearer image and better target separation than AT1 — can see a jighead falling at 90-100 ft away, which lets you see where your cast is in relation to the bass

Wheeler: Keep the bait over the fish's head. He sees about 40% of bass bite on screen but mostly just wants to see where the bait is relative to the fish, then turns away and just fishes — either they bite or they don't

Wheeler: Bass don't see line as well as we think — the drag of heavier line diameter negatively impacts bait action, which is why fish don't bite as well. 8-lb Nanobraid has smaller diameter, better action, equals more bites

Wheeler: Don't dial things in during practice. Half the tournaments I've won I figured it out during the tournament. Practice really hard daylight to dark but don't fish as much — just bebop around. Events are 8-9 days long so you have to adapt

Wheeler: When a community area has boats everywhere and other anglers are cannibalizing each other, ignore it entirely — spend time in different zones with fewer bass where you can manage your own fish with less pressure
Wheeler: When wind picks up, bass move from the top of brush/stumps down to the base of the cover to use it as a current break. When it goes slick calm, they slide back up higher in the cover
Wheeler: Used FFS less than 2% to find places at Santee Cooper — all places found by side imaging/side scan. FFS was used for making perfect casts to shallow fish, not for finding spots
Wheeler: At Santee Cooper, pre-spawn keys were 10 ft and shallower, hard places and rock (hard bottom, rock, gravel), plus brush and stumps. Better if you had both rock and wood but didn't need both

Wheeler: Had 7-8 spots he refused to hit before Championship Day — knew he couldn't win the tournament if he burned them early. Made gametime decisions with 25 minutes left, running 15 minutes across the lake to a fresh brushpile for the winning fish

Wheeler: Post-spawn offshore on Table Rock, the 3 key elements were brushpiles, isolated cedar trees, and bluff end points/big long main-lake points where schools related to current and hard bottom — mixed species (smallmouth, largemouth, spotted bass) depending on lake section
Wheeler: One of his best spots was a cedar tree in the middle of a creek channel — in 40 ft of water but came up to 12-15 ft with fish suspended in the top of it
Wheeler: Pumped fluorocarbon leader from 8-lb to 10-lb on dropshot because when you fire up a school you don't want to retie constantly — broke off a few fish earlier in the week on 8-lb

Wheeler: Northern smallmouth fisheries like Champlain only have a few months to do their deal — 1 week up north is like a month down south. Water temps went from 68 to 75 degrees during the event and fish transitioned dramatically from 12-16 ft to 18-30 ft

Wheeler: Key structure elements were hard gravel places, small isolated grass patches, boulders, and some fish on nothing — just suspended and nomadic. There wasn't one pattern, you had to keep things in rotation

Wheeler: Swapped from Garmin LiveScope to Lowrance ActiveTarget because he can see fish further — 100+ ft away. That's huge when talking about 1-2 more isolated 3+ lb smallmouth per day, which is the difference between winning and losing

Wheeler: Experimented with fluoro leaders from 4-lb up to 12-lb on smallmouth dropshot. Going down to 4-lb to check if fish were line-shy made no difference — it's trial and error figuring out what bass fishing is

Wheeler: A big key because MLF events are so long is not dialing it in during practice. Don't commit to any one thing. Have pre-spawners, spawners, fish around brush, fish suspended around bait, fish around bluff walls — lots of options

Wheeler: On highland reservoirs, 15-25 mph wind with 40 mph gusts blows bait and nutrients into certain areas. This gets bass to group up more in schools and actually hunt — wind can amplify a pattern you thought was going away
Wheeler: It's amazing how much pressure changes fish behavior — even when a competitor fishes an area before you, the fish act weird and won't bite. They know what's up

Wheeler: When leading by 30 lbs with the 2nd-place angler nearby, it's better to share the area and split the fish (he catches one, I catch one, I stay up) rather than leave for a distant spot and risk the comeback

Wheeler: With ActiveTarget can see an 1/8-oz bait at 85 ft and a 1/2-oz at 110 ft. Keeps ranges at 100-120 ft depending on area size. For suspended fish, you can see so much further out with ActiveTarget

Wheeler: Side imaging is definitely underutilized. Idle through areas before beaming with FFS — you can see groups of bass and bait so much better on side imaging. FFS is better for picking out singles
Wheeler: Any highland reservoir is more of a FFS lake because bass sit on bait a lot and suspend. Dale Hollow fish ranged from 1 ft to 20-30 ft deep

Wheeler: When current runs around the clock at high levels on the TN River, fish have no rest period and change positions — they move to secondary places where they can get out of the main channel. Secondary spots become the key

Wheeler: Snapping a football jig is different from stroking — stroking is for suspended fish, snapping is a small sharp motion that makes bottom-hugging fish in heavy current react. The higher-frequency kick action of the Cleanup Craw trailer complements the snap

Wheeler: Without FFS, angles are everything on an offshore school. Changing the angle on a group of fish several times let him catch 2-3 more each time. Old-school triangulating on the bank with a heading sensor to position within 1 ft of target
Wheeler: Swapped one ActiveTarget 2 transducer for a Humminbird 360 — huge deal for Spot Locking on a spot and seeing bottom fish without moving FFS around, being more efficient
Wheeler: Uses a Lowrance Precision 9 heading sensor for precise cast placement in non-FFS periods — rather than being 10-15 ft off target, you're within a foot. Expensive but a fine-tuned deal

Wheeler: On the TN River, when you leave a spot someone might follow you and catch your fish. If you can get away with not showing your hand to 20-30 spectator boats, it makes sense not to fish your best stuff — very strategic, not emotion-based

Wheeler: In heavy TN River current, fish don't set up consistently — sometimes behind a stump, sometimes on the front edge of the current. The pattern is never always the same, so the goal is to find as many places as possible
Wheeler: Fish trying to move offshore can get held up by the shad spawn in 4-8 ft of water. In years with floods and cold fronts, the post-spawn transition can run behind schedule
Wheeler: At Pickwick with heavy current, fish were in 10-30 ft with no depth consistency — depth depended on the area of the lake and the structure in that area

Wheeler: Smallmouth spawn starts in creeks and flat areas with no current, then they transition toward the main river/lake — same as largemouth. Look for spawners in 10-15 ft rather than the obvious 4-8 ft most people target

Wheeler: On pressured fisheries like St. Lawrence, look deeper (10-15 ft) for spawners rather than the obvious 4-8 ft zone. Beds are harder to see — look for a couple cleared-off white shells on darker bottom. Troll for miles on big flats to find congregated areas with enough current
Wheeler: Prefers 2 sneaky schools no one else has found that he can manage himself over community schools you can never get on. Key is to find fish where you're the only person fishing them

Wheeler: When fishing in current for spawning smallmouth, electronics can only show so much — fish are so tight to the bottom that FFS can't really show you anything. More efficient to use your own eyes to spot beds visually

Wheeler: Practice in the wind when other competitors won't — found 25 fresh spawners in 2.5 hours that likely wouldn't be pressured by Championship Day because no one else was looking in those windy areas

Wheeler: When making a 150-mile run through unfamiliar saltwater, accept the risks — 'I knew the dangers of making that kind of run.' Self-reported a rule violation for having his marshal exit the boat on a sandbar, resulting in a DQ, because it was the right thing to do

Wheeler: Prefers monofilament over braid for topwater because braid's zero stretch risks jerking the bait out of the fish's mouth when anglers instinctively jerk on the blowup. Mono's stretch provides forgiveness. Only exception is Florida heavy grass where 30-lb braid with a mono leader is needed

Wheeler: Even when using braid main line for topwater in heavy cover, always connect to the bait with a 12-inch monofilament leader (Sufix Elite) — thinner, more supple, and adds stretch at the connection point

Wheeler: Forward-facing sonar is not the silver bullet people think — it won't close the gap between tour pros and weekend warriors. It's not a substitute for natural intuition and efficient decision-making. Jordan Lee won AOY without any FFS using just 2D, down and side scan

Wheeler: FFS screens show all kinds of blobs, dots, arcs, lines and dashes — you have to decipher size, distance, spacing, species and direction. Much of what he knows about FFS he learned from reading 2D for years, then side and down imaging. It's a building process

Wheeler: FFS shines brightest during offshore/post-spawn periods and in smallmouth country. During spawn and bank-fishing seasons, traditional techniques are more relevant — the technology got overhyped because COVID shifted tournament schedules to offshore/northern events

Wheeler: FFS can cost you hours casting at fish that follow but won't bite — you change lures, sizes, colors, actions, and an hour later they still snub you. When too focused on the screen, you lose awareness of time, weather, current, and fishing pressure that your intuition needs for good decisions

Wheeler: The best use of FFS is not chasing dots but connecting them — taking an attitude reading of the underwater world. Are baitfish up or down? Are bass moving away or toward you? Aggressive, negative, neutral? How close to cover? Every behavior observation feeds the decision-making process

Wheeler: Great fishing innovations are double-edged swords — like Power-Poles making you fish too slow, FFS can trap you into casting at visible fish that won't bite while your overall game plan suffers. It's up to the angler to decide when too much of a good thing is really too much

Wheeler: At Heavy Hitters on Toho, saved brushpiles with biggest dots for the final day but they wouldn't bite. Got so consumed trying to catch dots that it bogged down his intuition and locked up his decision flow — should have relied on gut and weather, not a screen

Wheeler: With FFS limited to one period per day, the biggest thing is picking the right time and place. If it's windy or cloudy and fish are biting on reaction baits, maybe don't even turn FFS on that day. Let each day determine what you should be doing rather than overthinking it

Wheeler: With 2/3 of the day spent without FFS, expect many more traditional patterns in play — buzzbait, cranking, conventional bass fishing. Two-thirds of the day goes back to how he's fished his whole life, and he's excited about that

Wheeler: Don't waste FFS time panning and looking for fish. Don't stop a working pattern (like cranking rocky banks) just because you haven't used your FFS period yet. Assessing when to use it will be part of practice strategy
Wheeler: It's time that the Carolina rig made a comeback — a technique worth revisiting

Wheeler: The importance of showing bass something they haven't seen or heard — a squarebill that combines attributes of a bladed jig (oscillating metal lip with clicking sound) and a crankbait body covers water and triggers reaction strikes

Wheeler: On highland reservoirs with clean water, fall bass may be in 15-30 feet in creek pockets and drains; on lowland reservoirs like Tennessee River lakes, fish are shallower (3-5 feet) focused on gizzard shad requiring bigger baits
Wheeler: Designed the CrushCity Mayor swimbait with a good wobble that isn't aggressive — subtle changes to action can make a big difference in getting bites
Wheeler: In fall, find the baitfish first — bass constantly move and chase baitfish, so you might catch them one day and nothing the next because they've moved on

Wheeler: Use a 6.6:1 gear ratio reel for spinnerbaits, chatterbaits, and swimbaits; a 7.3:1 is the best all-around ratio if you can only have one; and 8.1:1 is ideal for flipping, frogs, and getting fish out of cover fast
Wheeler: Designed the Freeloader to serve dual roles — as a vibrating jig or spinnerbait trailer and as a cast-and-shake bait on a jighead for suspended fish, reducing the volume of plastics needed

Wheeler: On heavily pressured community holes, a bait with an action bass haven't seen produces dramatically better results — the Freeloader's unique shaking action caught fish from Guntersville's most pressured spots
Wheeler: Target smaller isolated schools rather than mega-schools that attract most anglers — the bigger fish often live in the smaller isolated schools with less pressure
Wheeler: Look for small subtle spots off the beaten path that other anglers miss, found during low-water periods; save your best spot for when you need it most in competition
Wheeler: When fishing ledges, keep baits on bottom but often burn them fast then kill the retrieve to trigger reaction strikes — this produced key fish at Chickamauga

Wheeler: On highland reservoirs like Cherokee, smallmouth live around holes/depressions (20 ft around the side) — if rock is around them the smallies live there. If a rock vein goes into the middle, they live on those veins. The holes harbor the bait with schools in the 20-30 ft range at the bottom

Wheeler: Big fish spots are big giant flats in 22-29 ft with tiny isolated rockpiles — a boulder in the middle of a rocky flat, or a hard-bottom spot. Only good for 1-2 fish and time-consuming to find, but that's what separates you

Wheeler: Smallmouth want to be related to and adjacent to gravelly bottom (flat or chunk rock) near some depth change — even a 1 ft change matters. One spot was just hard bottom from 40 to 37 ft with isolated rock spots coming up half a foot more
Wheeler: Cherokee smallmouth were feeding up, not down — a dropshot dropped in front of them wouldn't get bit. Had to keep the bait 2 ft above their heads for them to come up off the bottom and eat

Wheeler: On rocky smallmouth structure, 2D sonar couldn't see bass inside the rocks. FFS was required — you'd see them, drop on them, bring the bait above them, and they'd slide up out of those rocks and eat it

Wheeler: The jighead/swimbait combo is the #1 deal in winter on highland reservoirs like South Holston, Cherokee, and Norris — vertical presentation targeting smallmouth in depressions and on rockpiles
Wheeler: The thermocline shows up as a narrow fuzzy band on your locator screen; turn up the gain if it isn't well defined to locate it
Wheeler: Locating the thermocline eliminates half the water on any given lake in summer because bass and baitfish position just above it where oxygen and cooler temps overlap
Wheeler: Look for structure like drop-offs, humps, ledges and cover like brush, rockpiles, and timber that intersect the thermocline depth
Wheeler: The thermocline sets up deeper (25-30 feet) in clear-water reservoirs and shallower (8-10 feet) in murky waters; rivers generally don't have consistent thermoclines due to flowing water
Wheeler: Concentrations of baitfish gather right above the thermocline with bass feeding on them — that's the ideal summer setup
Wheeler: Determine the thermocline depth early in summer practice because it gives you a starting point for where fish can live and sets a maximum effective fishing depth

Wheeler: Bass are often most active on wind-blown spots when the food chain is riled up; less pressure, bass are less spooky, and they're often more aggressive, making the inconveniences worth the effort
Wheeler: Does not use a trailer hook on spinnerbaits; instead relies on a trimmed skirt with soft plastic trailer for better hookup ratio

Wheeler: As temps flirt with the 50s, target traditional staging areas close to spawning pockets; deeper staging fish focus more on feeding than spawning activities, making prespawn the most consistent action of the year
Wheeler: Transitional banks and creek arms are prime prespawn locations; use electronics to scan deeper pockets and channel swings near spawning grounds for actively feeding fish
Wheeler: Be versatile during prespawn — cycle through finesse swimbaits, Ned rigs, and heavy gliding jigs to determine the best technique for current conditions

Wheeler: Forward-facing sonar has opened up offshore prespawn tactics, but don't overlook traditional fishing techniques — instinct and experience through 'just fishing' will always reveal bass that evade fish finder technology
Wheeler: For prespawn swimbaits use a 7' medium-fast spinning rod with 12-pound Sufix NanoBraid mainline and 8-pound fluorocarbon leader

Wheeler: In spring, start with a prop bait (X-Rap Prop) in colder pre-spawn water when fish aren't as active, then transition to a frog when water temps hit low 60s and fish move off beds into heavier cover
Wheeler: When walking a popping frog, snap the rod to throw slack back in the line — the slack allows the frog to turn; if the line is tight it will only move forward and won't walk
Wheeler: For frog fishing use a 7'6" heavy-action rod with 65-pound braid and an 8:1 gear ratio reel to pick up line quickly and set the hook in heavy cover
Wheeler: Post-spawn bass push fry into grass mats for protection — target those grass mats with a frog, and also skip under bushes, docks, and overhangs on shallow flats
Wheeler: First cast to each area with a swimbait to catch easy grouped-up fish, then switch to drop shot to pick off individuals — this two-pronged approach maximizes catches on offshore structure
Wheeler: For drop shot in heavy cover use a 7'2" medium-heavy spinning rod, 3000-size reel, 8-pound NanoBraid mainline with 10-pound fluorocarbon leader and 1/4-ounce cylinder weight
Wheeler: For offshore swimbaits (3-4 inch Largo Shad) targeting suspended fish around trees and on points, use a 7' medium-heavy casting rod with a slow 6.3:1 reel and 12-pound fluorocarbon

Wheeler: His top 5 swimbait rigs are: 3-inch on ball head jig (spinning), 4-inch on jig head (casting), Tokyo rig, belly-weighted weedless (Texas rig), and as a trailer on moving baits — each serves a distinct depth and cover scenario
Wheeler: Do not overwork a jig — the slower you go the more bites you get; less is always more when fishing a jig on the bottom
Wheeler: For general jig fishing use a 7'1" heavy-action fast-tip casting rod with an 8.5:1 reel and 15-pound fluorocarbon for sensitivity and getting fish out of cover
Wheeler: When fishing a football jig on deep rock, make a long cast, let it hit bottom, and drag it slowly — most bites come when the bait pops off a rock as bass follow it on the drag
Wheeler: For swim jigs use the same 7'3" medium-heavy moderate-fast tip rod used for spinnerbaits and chatterbaits — the moderate-fast action absorbs the shock of aggressive swim jig strikes
Wheeler: Always use a trailer on jigs — without a trailer a jig doesn't look like anything natural; match trailer size to jig size to increase bites
Wheeler: Always check your line while jig fishing as it constantly rubs against bottom structure and invasive species like zebra mussels cause more abrasion than ever — re-tie often
Wheeler: A quality swimbait engages (swims) on the fall and when reeled at the slowest speeds — a constant tail kick mimics shad and generates bites
Wheeler: Has a quick method for switching out hooks on crankbaits to improve hookup ratios and land more bass.
Wheeler: Maximizing Lowrance electronics usage is critical for finding and catching more bass.
Wheeler: When flipping mats, select appropriate weights and baits to punch through vegetation cover effectively.
Wheeler: Regardless of what equipment you have, it doesn't make you who you are - if you work hard you can truly accomplish great things. It ain't the boat that catches them, it's the fisherman.
Wheeler: Learning electronics and bass behavior from experienced anglers, plus fishing pot tournaments alone, helped build confidence in decision-making that translated to professional success.
Wheeler: Casting angles are critical for deep cranking offshore structure - varying your angle of retrieve relative to the cover triggers reaction strikes from bigger fish.
Wheeler: Reading the mood of offshore bass is essential - sometimes a deep crankbait triggers reaction bites from bigger fish when finesse approaches are dominating.
Wheeler: Pitching is for being very specific with bait placement - the strike zone may be only 6-8 inches wide. Casting is for covering water, pitching is for fishing one particular thing.
Wheeler: Aggressive bass bite on the initial fall. Sometimes the strike comes after hopping a couple times. During the spawn or heavy pressure, let the bait sit still for 5+ seconds.

Wheeler: Uses a 7'3" heavy action rod with parabolic action for 5/16-1/4 oz weights, and a 7'6" heavy action rod for heavier sinkers in thick cover. Pairs with 8.1:1 high-speed reel to quickly retrieve slack when bass bites and runs toward you.
Wheeler: Uses 14-pound fluorocarbon for pitching to rock banks or bluffs, switches to 17-pound fluorocarbon when pitching into heavy cover.

Wheeler: The Storm Largo Shad has a broader profile than most swimbaits with a slow body roll and exaggerated tail swing. You can customize action by cutting or removing the tail tendon for a wider action. Rig on VMC jighead, heavy duty swimbait hook, weighted willow swimbait hook, or Tokyo rig.
Wheeler: One swimbait (Storm Largo Shad) can target spotted bass, largemouth, and smallmouth across the country - a single bait that does it all.

Wheeler: Designed the Punch Out Craw with score lines for tool-free modification into 5 versions: glide (punching), flipping (separated claws), flapping craw (removed spear tail), chunk (jig trailer), and finesse (body and tail only). Full body takes a 4/0 hook; twist off the end segment for a 3/0 hook.

Wheeler: Uses four Ned rig methods - traditional exposed jig head, drop shot Ned (best for smallmouth), Texas rigged swinging Ned (for snaggy cover), and wacky rigged drop shot Ned (for deep individual fish targeting).
Wheeler: Fishes Ned rigs on a 7'2" medium heavy spinning rod with a Shimano Vanford 2500 reel.
