Wheeler: Bladed jigs are go-to lures for exploring new water because you can fish them at various speeds and they come through cover well. Sound and vibration add fish-drawing power, widening the strike window.

Wheeler: Uses a longer medium-heavy rod with a slower (moderate fast) taper to balance power and forgiveness on hooksets. Pairs with 17-pound fluorocarbon for strength and durability without losing sensitivity.
Wheeler: ChatterBait color system is simple - black/blue for stained water, green pumpkin for slightly clearer conditions and bluegill waters, shad patterns for clear water and shad-based fisheries.

Wheeler: Graph offshore spots during practice even if they have zero or few fish - bass may show up during the tournament. By the end of a Guntersville event, he had 26 schools (double what he found in practice) because fresh fish moved in that hadn't been pressured.

Wheeler: In qualifying rounds, fish hard for just the first period and a half to secure advancement, then spend remaining time keeping tabs on other spots and discovering new ones as the tournament progresses.

Wheeler: Lighter braid (8-pound NanoBraid) results in better bait action because heavier braid has more drag in wind and current, negatively impacting swimbait action. Estimates 20% more bites with small-diameter braid vs 20-pound braid.
Wheeler: Being too versatile can hurt - trying to do too much instead of committing to one pattern leads to bad positioning. After worst tournaments, the next one is usually pretty solid.
Wheeler: Best offshore spots included areas adjacent to grass lines, actual holes in the grass, and hard-bottomed shell beds in 8-35 feet of water.

Wheeler: Find the most obscure offshore areas that might only have a few fish but you can manage yourself. Popular areas where multiple anglers graph will cannibalize each other - you can't win in those places.

Wheeler: Won on forward-facing sonar but wouldn't have won without spending time with DownScan and SideScan first. You need traditional sonar to find cover among thousands of stumps and irregularities, then FFS to fish them.
Wheeler: Has become a bigger fan of scent recently - adding extra Bait Pop Sonar Intensifier gel when fish are inactive and won't commit helps trigger bites.
Wheeler: If you're not enjoying it, you don't have the right mindset. Focusing on having fun rather than standings leads to better performance.
Wheeler: When you get the first rain in fall and water temps drop, fish reset into winter patterns. Temperature thresholds vary: 40s-30s up north, around 50 degrees in the south.

Wheeler: Forward-facing sonar has taught him more about jerkbaits than anything else - you can see on screen how fish react to cadence and pauses, how long it takes bass to come up from deeper water to meet the bait.

Wheeler: Flat main-lake drains near island clusters are bass magnets - the islands funnel baitfish and the flatter topography serves as spawning territory. Current flow through island cut-throughs makes them prime prespawn/postspawn staging areas.

Wheeler: Keeps an array of spinning rods rigged with jigheads from 1/8 to 5/8 oz. The right weight for the right scenario is about efficiency - heavier gets to fast-moving fish, lighter gets better action for inactive singles.

Wheeler: Spent as much time as possible during qualifying rounds in practice mode, scouting new areas and tracking where fish were moving. When wind blew and concentrated bait, groups of 100 bass schooled up in windblown offshore areas.

Wheeler: Wind congregates bait in protected areas like island funnels, which causes bass to school up in massive numbers. A spot with a few small groups can explode to groups of 100 bass after a windy day.

Wheeler: In winter, bass target the prior year's threadfin shad hatch, the most abundant and smallest forage. The majority of fish target the smaller profile shad, making finesse swimbaits the right match.

Wheeler: On Tennessee River-type lakes, fish lipless-crankbait depth (10 feet or less) near grass. On clear highland reservoirs (Cherokee, Percy Priest, Lanier), fish 25-45 feet in main river channels, deepwater drains, and sharp edges near bait concentrations.

Wheeler: On deep clear reservoirs, look for sharp edges near bait concentrations - points going into creek arms, where creek channels run into bluffs, channel turns. The bait congregates there. Bass follow food and don't move much in winter because deep areas are more stable.

Wheeler: In shallow winter situations, fish push into backs of pockets and creek arms to depths as little as 2 feet, but deeper water (8-12 feet) is always close by. Target ditches, drains, secondary points, and grass lines.
Wheeler: For deep water swimbait, uses 8-pound Sufix NanoBraid main line with 6-7 foot leader of 8-pound fluorocarbon. For shallow rock or cover, switches to 10-12 pound fluorocarbon leader.

yamamoto.insight: The 3-inch Ika has a 1.5-inch torso shaped like a Senko with six tentacles radiating from one end; salt-impregnated; available in 14 colors

Yamamoto pro Hite: The Zako was designed with restrained 'no-feel' action to mimic how bluegill, green sunfish, and gizzard shad hover and subtly swim; the V-shaped tail with an interior flange minimizes tail movement; a back groove facilitates easy rigging; non-salt-impregnated blend for increased durability
Yamamoto pro Hite convinced Colby to create a non-salt-impregnated plastic blend for the Zako for increased durability — can withstand multiple fish catches without tearing

Cast an unweighted Senko on a 4/0 wide-gap hook and retrieve with a hopping/skipping cadence across the surface — faster than a floating worm but not burning it; the wide-gap hook acts as a keel to keep the Senko running straight and prevents line twist; 3- or 4-inch size works best
The surface Senko technique is a year-round pattern — works whenever there are fish up shallow, and according to this angler there are always fish up shallow

Yamamoto pro Yelas: The Cowboy is a 4-inch 'power soft plastic' — big, bulky, salt-laden with a powerful double tail and two side appendages; always rig it perfectly straight to get the most action out of it
Yamamoto pro Yelas: 'A lot of times I will make my first few casts with the Cowboy to catch the most aggressive fish and then if the bite slows down, I'll downsize my lure'
Yamamoto: A Senko rigged on a weighted screw-lock hook is a simple system that covers water faster than wacky or weightless while maintaining skipping ability

Yamamoto inducted into Bass Fishing Hall of Fame in 2016; credited with numerous innovations in soft plastic design and manufacturing; the Senko called 'one of the most important lure developments in recent years'; Hula Grub also cited as a mainstay worldwide

Wilson: Split-shotting a 4- or 5-inch stickbait in 3-20ft is excellent for prespawn and high-pressure situations; vary the leader length by depth — deeper water gets a longer leader (up to 2ft) so the bait sinks the last 2ft with a slow tantalizing fall after the weight hits bottom; put the shot right against the hook when sight fishing beds

Wilson: 'If it's breezy, the wind will pull your line and the lure so you don't get the slow fall — a split shot crimped above your line will help you stay in contact with the bait and help it fall straighter'

Yamamoto: Gary Yamamoto's key technique on Lake Champlain was dragging a weighted Texas-rigged Senko across the bottom rather than the standard lift-reel retrieve — the dragging motion was irresistible to smallmouth, possibly imitating crawfish

Klein: Gary Yamamoto got the density and sink rate just right with the Senko; stickworms must have the right stiffness and fall rate for each end to quiver enticingly on slack line — 'If the plastic is too stiff, there isn't enough action. If it's too soft, the bait just folds in half as it sinks'

Klein: Dense, salt-infused soft plastics sink faster and are stiffer; softer/less-dense plastics are preferred for finesse and have a slower sink rate; when flipping into heavy cover, select dense, tough plastic that withstands abuse — too soft tears up quickly
Prochnow (Berkley): Bass are more inclined to spit out a hard soft plastic bait faster than a softer one — softness increases how long a bass holds the bait
Prochnow (Berkley): Some colorants harden the plastic while others do not — Berkley adjusts the plastic formula by colorant to ensure consistent action, density, and texture across colors

DeFronzo (via Yamamoto staffer Jerry Puckett): Do not set the hook on wacky-rigged Senko — apply slow steady pressure instead; the hook will penetrate the corner of the bass's mouth; switching from a straight-shank to a splitshot hook and using pressure instead of a hookset drastically reduced lost fish

DeFronzo: Crimp a small split shot 18 inches above a size 1 hook with a 3-inch Ika Texas-rigged; keep a slow steady retrieve; effective up to 20ft — deeper than that you lose contact; add a grub guard for bottom snags and weed penetration; a glass bead above the weight adds noise and reflects light
DeFronzo: The Senko has a unique 'fall away from the angler' action like a Flying Lure Tube — this fall is deadly and unlike any other weightless bait

DeFronzo: Use a 6ft or 6'6" ML IM8 rod with a solid backbone for Senko and Ika finesse work; 6lb Silver Thread line for almost all applications; light wire hooks reduce durability problems with soft Yamamoto plastics by not tearing the bait as much; skin-hook the baits to get multiple fish per bait

Yamamoto pro Fukae: The Free Rig is a cross between a Texas Rig and Carolina Rig — the line runs freely through the weight's eye so the weight falls first and the soft plastic flutters down behind it with an unpredictable slow fall; use a 'lift and fall' retrieve; 1/4-oz tungsten weight is the all-around top choice
Yamamoto pro Fukae: Rigging a 3-inch Fat Senko Texas-rigged on a drop-shot with a 3-inch leader replicates a Ned Rig presentation but avoids bottom hang-ups around cover
Yamamoto pro Fukae: Uses 14-lb YGK sinking braid to an 8-lb fluorocarbon leader for spawn-time drop-shot and Ned Rig presentations on a Shimano Expride 170M with 2500-size spinning reel

Sealock: When the flipping/pitching bite gets tough, switch to a wacky-rigged Senko on a spinning rod with 8-lb test — fish it in the same areas (under docks, laydowns, boulders, grass lines); hook through the middle, make a long cast, let fall on slack line, pop the slack occasionally

Sealock: The Senko's weight makes it cast like a rocket even rigged weightless — critical for finesse fishing in clear water where long casts are essential; it skips underneath cover with just a wrist flick
The Yamamoto D Shad is denser than other soft jerkbaits — stays about 1ft below the surface when worked fast and erratically instead of skipping across the top; rig on a 3/0 Offset EWG Worm Hook

The D Shad has a solid diamond-shaped tail instead of a traditional forked tail — the heavier tail causes an even fall through the water column and a shimmying action on slack line similar to a Senko; most bites occur on the fall between twitches
If a bass short-strikes the D Shad, immediately 'kill' it and let it sink on slack line — the level fall looks like a dead shad and prompts follow-up strikes on the same cast

The D Shad's extra density allows use on casting gear (15-lb fluorocarbon, medium-heavy rod) unlike most soft jerkbaits — this opens up fishing thick cover without breakoffs, and enables skip casting under docks and overhangs

Yamamoto: 'I designed the Senko after trying the Slug-Go which I thought was too hard and bulky. The softer plastic and the way water flows around the lure as it falls gives it a continual vibration on a weightless Texas rig'
Yamamoto: Use 10-14lb Sugoi Fluorocarbon leader for stained water, 6-10lb for clear water
Yamamoto: 'Salt-and-pepper is probably a sleeper color — a forgotten color, but I can take it any place in the world and fish will bite it'
Yamamoto: 'I originally wanted to make a twitch bait like the Slug-Go, but after testing it turned out the Senko was better as a finesse bait. I got the idea for the shape from a Bic pen I had'
Yamamoto: The Senko works all year round but shines spring to fall. In colder months, fish deeper water on long underwater structure such as points with a small weight added
Yamamoto: Has caught fish on the Senko in cold water from as deep as 30 feet at Beaver Lake

Yamamoto: 'When the fish hear the splash, they look to see what it is. When they see the worm, they may not grab it right away, but go over and inspect it. When they get a good look at it and it moves again, that's when most strikes occur'

Yamamoto: 'The most common mistake is fishing too fast. This bait is very subtle. It needs to be worked slowly. From the time it splashes and falls, it only needs to be moved a little bit to get good action'
Yamamoto inducted into Bass Fishing Hall of Fame in 2016; credited with numerous innovations in soft plastic design and manufacturing including the Senko and Hula Grub

Yamamoto pro Shryock: The Yamatanuki is a heavy 'gravity bait' — 3.5 inches at 5/8 oz with a signature side-to-side flutter on the fall. Works from 1 foot to 20+ feet suspended. Extremely weedless. Can replace jigs around heavy cover and grass

Yamamoto pro Shryock: 'It's a power-finesse approach — the power part comes in your tackle. You don't have to downsize. You can throw it on a spinning rod but it's almost borderline heavy for a soft spinning rod. I like using a baitcaster'
Yamamoto pro Shryock: Unlike a Senko, the Yamatanuki has a fast fall rate so it excels in nearly any depth. It's non-disruptive like a Senko in shallow water but can also target deep suspended fish

Bank fishing: 'Shooting' technique from crappie fishing — point rod at target, pull bait toward you to load rod tip with downward arc, release like bow and arrow. Great for docks, laydowns, gaps in weeds

Adding accent color to Senko's tail can turn lookers into biters. Chartreuse mimics bream fin tips. Red and orange also work. Rubbing the tail vigorously lightens the plastic as a field-expedient color change

Adding a tail spinner blade to a Senko via Owner Flashy Spin on a centering pin is effective in darker water — swimming the bait through sunlit areas throws attention-getting flashes into shadow areas where predators lurk
For bank fishing Senkos: 7-foot medium-heavy spinning outfit. Use Tactical Anglers Power Clip to swap rigs without re-tying

Yamamoto: The Chicken Rig is a modification of the Neko rig using a 7.75-inch Kut Tail worm rigged backward with a 4/0 or 5/0 straight-shank hook inserted about an inch behind the egg sack, threaded on Texas-rig style, with a drywall screw inserted in the meaty tail end for weight

Yamamoto: 'Cast it out and let it fall on slack line. Once it hits bottom, lift it up a couple feet and let it drop again. If I haven't gotten bit, I reel it back up and throw again. It's more like a power fishing tool than finesse'
Yamamoto: 'When a fish bites, they are almost always swimming with it. If you jerk really hard, you'll likely lose fish. Just reel down and pull with hard steady pressure'
Yamamoto: Fish the Chicken Rig on medium-heavy spinning tackle, 15-20lb braid mainline with 16 or 20lb Sugoi Fluorocarbon leader

Yamamoto: The Chicken Rig is weedless unlike the Neko rig so it can be fished through brush, rocks, and docks. It has big bass appeal with the large Kut Tail profile. It's durable because the worm slides up the line on hookset
Yamamoto: Fish the Chicken Rig around bridge pilings, barge tieups, docks, riprap, and other hard cover. Used it to finish 5th at Rayovac FLW Series on Upper Mississippi

Yamamoto: 'We as anglers need to show the bass something different. When that backwards weighted Kut-Tail falls in front of them, it presents an image they haven't seen before that looks really natural. They have no choice but to strike'
Yamamoto: In 2011, broke the FLW single day weight record for Lake Champlain with 24lb 4oz stringer skipping docks with a Senko tail-weighted with a No. 7 finishing screw

Yamamoto: Won the US Open of Bass Fishing in 1995 at Lake Mead. Competed on Bassmaster and FLW tours after moving east in the late nineties, found major success on both tours and internationally in Japan and Europe
Yamamoto: 'At 67 I still know I can do real well. If I didn't think I could win this event I wouldn't be here'

Weighted wacky hooks and wacky jig heads (flick shake) take the double-ended wiggle straight to the bottom. FLW pro Roy Hawk: 'Throw it at anything — grass lines, pockets, docks, single rocks, shade lines. Vary the head weight depending on wind, current and depth'
FLW pro Roy Hawk prefers nail-weighted Neko over flick shake in deeper waters because the rig falls faster with less lateral resistance — straight down

Rubbing the tail of a Senko releases more salt and subtly changes the color — an on-the-fly alteration when fish have seen enough of one color. Chartreuse tail dip gets most carries; orange works where bluegill abound
Kevin Hawk: Owner Flashy accent (Indiana or willow blade on barrel swivel slipped onto hook bend) used in murky water or high pressure situations to draw more attention to wacky-rigged Senko
A stick bait on a skirtless football head is a productive look for walking down bluff ends
The Senko sinks horizontally, which is different from most soft plastics that fall nose-first. This horizontal descent triggers strikes as bass interpret it as dying or injured prey
The Senko is infused with salt which adds weight and a unique taste that encourages bass to hold onto it longer, increasing hook-up ratio
Yamamoto pro Hite: Hite designed the Zako for Yamamoto specifically as a ChatterBait/Jack Hammer trailer. The accordion-style tail collapse on the bite is the key feature for better hookup ratios
Yamamoto pro Shryock: The ultra-soft PsychoDad excels over standard jig trailers when fishing pressured bass and around lighter cover where good hookups are critical
Yamamoto pro Hite: Use Kut Tail Worm on Neko rig post-spawn through summer for more aggressive action in warmer water. Switch to standard Senko on Neko when cold or when subtle action is needed
Yamamoto pro Hite: Standard Zako for ChatterBaits (subtle natural action), Kickin' Zako (paddle tail) for deep heavy ChatterBaits on ledges with poor light penetration and for swim jigs exclusively

Yamamoto pro Hite: The Fat Ika rigged backward weightless on EWG hook backslides like the old 'Flying Lure' — heavy salt-loaded plastic sinks on its own, and backward rigging makes it slide under docks and away from you

Line choice affects stickbait presentation: monofilament makes the bait float more, fluorocarbon makes it sink quicker. Braid is often overlooked but useful for fishing weightless stickbaits over thick matted weeds
Wacky rig stickbait works best for a slightly more aggressive retrieve. Weedless/Texas rig with no weight is a much more subtle approach with a very subtle wiggle
Hudson: 'The biggest difference is rate of fall - Senko sinks much faster than Dinger. Faster fall lets you fish faster and deeper, slower fall irritates fish by hanging slowly in their face'

Hudson: 'Senko is more heavily salt infused making fish hold on longer, and way softer feeling more realistic. Softness gives slightly more action and it sinks straight down. Downside is much less durability'
Hudson: 'Dinger sways side to side more than Senko due to slower sink rate, while Senko tends to sink straight down'
Hudson: 'Senko is best option if you are searching for bites and not on a pattern, or when fish are overly color sensitive or not giving solid hookups'
Hudson: 'Prefer Dinger in post-spawn pitching at finicky recovering bass hanging near the surface'
Yamamoto: 'One of the best colors that is probably a sleeper is salt-and-pepper. I think it is a forgotten color, but I can take that color any place in the world and fish will bite it'
Yamamoto: 'Employs a simple lift-and-drop retrieve - it is like working a plastic worm but not quite as fast because it takes longer for it to fall'
Yamamoto: 'Prefers 3/0 Gamakatsu EWG on weightless Texas rig. Smaller light-wire hooks increase tail action and bait durability'
Senko falls faster than most soft plastics with a slight tantalizing wiggle due to high salt content. Smallmouth grab them and hold on thanks to salt content and soft texture
Howell (pro): 'Just throw them out and let them sink. They cast a mile, ideal in heavily pressured or clear water. Smallmouth come from a long way to eat one'
Howell (pro): 'Prefers wacky rig when fish are less active and less eager to chase. Texas rig when bass are more active, working it faster'
Heavy-gauge hooks pull the Senko down more quickly; fine-wire hooks allow slower sink. Vary hooks to control sink rate
Scott (pro): 'Fish Senkos with Power Pro braided line tipped with fluorocarbon leader because braided line floats, acting like a bobber to detect strikes on the fall'

Scott (pro): 'Wacky rigged 5-inch Senko with 1/0 Owner Mosquito hook through egg sack is ideal for heavily pressured smallmouth. Caught 20-pound bag while everyone else throwing tubes and drop shots caught few fish'
Howell (pro): 'When Texas rig end gets torn up, flip the Senko and use the other end - no difference in catch rates. Wacky rig lures too torn on ends'

Most effective wacky style retrieve mimics crippled shad - raise and lower stick worm a couple feet, never letting it touch bottom. Pop it a couple times for herky-jerky action then return to subtle fall
Wacky rig causes Senko to fall sideways, gliding under pier platforms or foam flotation of docks where bass lurk in shadows. Especially effective when bass suspend under docks
Senko on jighead can be fished from 6 inches to 30-40ft of water. Vary jighead size to control depth presentation
Key to catching fish on Yamamoto 10-inch grub is delayed hookset with EWG hook and screw-in weight. Using straight shank hook and standard hookset results in many lost fish
Yamamoto 10-inch grub requires Gamakatsu EWG Superline 5/0 hook with screw-in 1/4oz weight (F rig). Wrong hook and rigging method causes constant missed fish

Becker (MLF pro): Uses 7ft 2in MH spinning rod, 20lb braid mainline (abrasion resistance around docks), 12-15lb fluorocarbon leader. TroKar TK137 Finesse Hook 1/0 with unique bend that locks and holds fish
Becker (MLF pro): 'Bites commonly go undetected while Senko sinks on slack. Watch line movement and use bright-colored braid to see line bites'
Becker (MLF pro): 'Heavier fluorocarbon leader (15lb vs 12lb) facilitates faster sink rate in addition to better durability around docks'
Yamamoto: The Senko was initially considered a flop because it did not catch fish when applied the way it was originally intended. Over 17 million Senkos were produced in 2016 alone
Book 'Gary Yamamoto: My Life with Fish' by Steve Price contains one-page tips at end of each chapter on fishing the Senko, Hula Grub, dropshot rigs, flipping, and Neko rig
Yamamoto Cowboy is very customizable - cut tails, remove appendages, trim profile to change action. Full profile adds bulk as jig trailer, trimmed slicks through cover easier
Cowboy legs are more solid and durable than most flipping baits and jig trailers, yet still pliable enough to move water well

Yamamoto: 'Started using braided line but lost numerous fish because glass-type guides cracked from it. Had six fish in a Bassmaster tournament, only brought in two. Switched to titanium guides and solved the problem'
Yamamoto: 'Every year you fish you learn a few more tricks and learn how to solve some of the problems you had before. You add a few more pieces to the puzzle'

Yamamoto: 'Using 4lb test and 1/16-1/32oz jigheads with tiny grubs for crappie, caught 7-pound bass instead. Makes me think we are missing opportunities to catch fish that are there but just won't eat a big bait'
Yamamoto: 'I like open practices where you can go whenever you can. If somebody can afford to spend a month practicing, more power to them. That is how people get good'
Hawk (pro): 'Stretch 40 is basically a 4-inch Senko with a small curly tail. The tail is small unlike most baits that size with large curl or ribbon tails - much more lively'

Card (pro): 'Single Tail Grub works all year but great during pre-spawn for smallmouth. Fish it like a small swimbait, reeling slow enough to just tick rocks. Also reel-reel-reel then let fall on tight line'
Card (pro): 'Reel slow enough that it just hits bottom every once in a while, barely ticking the rocks. Can also drag on bottom like a jig, or reel-reel-reel and let it fall on tight line'
Howell (pro): 'Daiwa/Yamamoto Neko worms are made of same material and formula as Senko but unique designs give different action and fall rate'
Card (pro): Yamamoto Single Tail Grub is also an excellent spinnerbait trailer

What separates Senko from competitors is the plastic formula - tuned for perfect amount of salt, softness, and natural sink rate. Most competitors fall too fast, too slow, or have other issues. The natural sink and wobble is what makes fish unable to resist
Best way to see Senko's fish-catching power is wacky rig - cast out, let it sink, twitch rod to make worm shake then return to sinking. Catches fish from 6 inches to over 5 pounds
Senko can be rigged at least seven ways: Texas rig, weightless Texas rig, jig worm, drop shot, wacky rig, Neko rig, and Carolina rig. Keep one or more rigged up at all times
For wacky rig Senko use #1 VMC Neko Hook. For Texas rig use 3/0 Gamakatsu EWG Hook
Yamamoto Yama Craw uses entirely different material from Senko - Mega Floater Formula. Softer, spongier, very buoyant. Not as stretchy as ElaZtech but more durable than hand-poured plastics
Yama Craw buoyancy changes presentation for Texas rig, wobble heads, magnum shaky heads, jig trailers, Tokyo rig, and Carolina rig - claws float up in defensive crawfish posture
Yama Craw will work wonders during spawn with claws floating right up in bedding bass's face
Yamatanuki is 3.5 inches, weighs 5/8oz without weight. Salt impregnated in proprietary way for heavier, quicker fall with more action. Castable on baitcasting tackle without added weight, skips well

Yamatanuki catches fish on initial fall like Senko and equally well dragged on bottom. Bites are very easy to detect due to added weight. Fish it on MH baitcasting with 15-17lb Seaguar InvizX fluorocarbon
Yamatanuki knobbed tail has a lot of action - always undulating as it skips along bottom or pulls over cover. Rig with hook point out of big end for more action and better hookups

Best for pitching to isolated cover - docks, logs, laydowns, boulders, stumps. A little tough to sink into thick cover. Effective on both smallmouth and largemouth, spawning, cruising, and fry guarding fish
Profile matches what Dr. Keith Jones proved was the preferred size and profile bass wanted in testing. Heavy plastics gaining popularity in US after years of use in Japan on lakes like Biwa
Yamamoto: Video-only content about his sweep hook set technique. No text transcript available beyond title
